Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Neville Prescott has a second-hand saloon car displayed on the Prescott Motors forecourt on Bath Road with a price tag of £8,500. Rachel Okonkwo enters the showroom and says ‘I would like to buy that car for £8,500.’ Neville checks his records and tells her the price is now £9,200 because the tag had not been updated. Rachel insists she is entitled to the displayed price. Which of the following best describes the legal position?

options.A: A contract has been formed at £8,500 because Rachel accepted the displayed price.
options.B: Rachel is entitled to the £8,500 price under the Consumer Rights Act 2015, which requires traders to honour displayed prices.
options.C: No contract has been formed because the forecourt display is an invitation to treat, not an offer.
options.D: No contract has been formed because Rachel’s statement was a counter-offer, which Neville rejected.

Contract Law.explanation: A shop or window display is an invitation to treat, not an offer: Pharmaceutical Society of GB v Boots Cash Chemists [1953]. Rachel’s statement constitutes the offer, which Neville Prescott has not yet accepted. Option A is wrong because there was no offer capable of acceptance — the forecourt display merely invited customers to make offers to buy. Option B is incorrect: the CRA 2015 does not require traders to honour displayed prices; an incorrect price display may raise a Trading Standards issue but does not create a binding contract. Option D mischaracterises Rachel’s statement — she was making an offer, not a counter-offer. A counter-offer responds to a prior offer; no prior offer existed here.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: On Monday, Neville Prescott posts an offer to sell his personal car to Rachel Okonkwo for £4,000, requesting a reply by post. Rachel posts her acceptance on Wednesday. On Thursday, Neville phones Rachel to withdraw the offer. Rachel’s letter arrives on Friday. Has a contract been formed?

options.A: No contract was formed because Neville withdrew the offer before Rachel’s acceptance was received.
options.B: No contract was formed because revocation by telephone overrides a postal acceptance.
options.C: A contract was formed on Wednesday when Rachel posted her acceptance.
options.D: A contract was formed on Friday when Neville received Rachel’s acceptance.

Contract Law.explanation: Under the postal rule established in Adams v Lindsell (1818), acceptance is complete and binding on the offeror at the moment of posting. Rachel’s acceptance was therefore complete on Wednesday. Neville’s Thursday phone call came after the contract had already been formed and cannot revoke an already-accepted offer. Option A is wrong because the revocation came after formation on Wednesday. Option B is incorrect: revocation is effective when communicated, but is irrelevant here because the contract existed before Neville’s call. Option D is wrong because receipt is not the operative moment under the postal rule.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: James Ashworth offers to sell his motorbike to Priya Mehta for £3,500. Priya replies: ‘I reject your price — I will pay £3,000.’ James says: ‘No.’ Priya then says: ‘Fine — I accept your original offer of £3,500.’ Has a contract been formed?

options.A: Yes, because Priya’s final acceptance matched the terms of James’s original offer.
options.B: Yes, because James’s rejection of £3,000 implicitly reaffirmed the original offer.
options.C: No, because Priya’s offer of £3,000 was a counter-offer that extinguished James’s original offer.
options.D: No, because Priya’s change of mind showed she lacked the necessary intention to contract.

Contract Law.explanation: A counter-offer operates as a rejection of the original offer and substitutes a new one: Hyde v Wrench (1840). Priya’s offer of £3,000 was unambiguously a counter-offer, extinguishing James’s offer of £3,500. There was no original offer capable of acceptance when Priya said ‘I accept.’ Option A is wrong because the original offer no longer existed. Option B is a common trap: James’s rejection of £3,000 was a rejection of Priya’s counter-offer, not a reinstatement of his original offer. To revive it, James would need to make a fresh offer. Option D misidentifies the reason: the issue is destruction of the original offer, not intention to contract.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Last year, Marta Kowalski spent three hours searching for Dr Felix Unwin’s lost dog and returned it safely. Moved by her kindness, Dr Unwin later promises to pay Marta £200 for what she did. Dr Unwin never pays. Can Marta enforce Dr Unwin’s promise?

options.A: Yes, because Marta’s act of finding the dog was good consideration for Dr Unwin’s promise.
options.B: Yes, because Dr Unwin’s promise was made in recognition of a benefit he received.
options.C: No, because Marta acted gratuitously and cannot claim payment for voluntary acts.
options.D: No, because Marta’s act was past consideration, which is not valid consideration.

Contract Law.explanation: Consideration must be provided in exchange for the promise and cannot be past: Re McArdle [1951]. Marta found the dog before Dr Unwin made his promise; her act therefore cannot support his subsequent promise. Option A is wrong: a past act cannot constitute consideration for a later promise, however beneficial it was. Option B reflects a moral argument but not the law — past consideration does not become binding merely because the promisor acknowledges a benefit. This option conflates past consideration with the narrow exception in Lampleigh v Braithwait (1615), which applies only where the prior act was performed at the promisor’s request and payment was always contemplated; neither condition is met here. Option C is a distractor: the legal objection is timing (past consideration), not the voluntary character of the act.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Owen Davies, of Atlas Contracts Ltd, agrees to renovate Wharton Bakery for Alek Kowalski for £40,000. Halfway through, Owen tells Alek he has underestimated costs and will not finish unless Alek pays an extra £5,000. Alek agrees because he has a corporate catering booking in three weeks. Owen completes the work. Can Owen enforce the additional £5,000?

options.A: Yes, because completing on time gave Alek a practical benefit, which constitutes good consideration.
options.B: No, because Owen was only doing what he was already contractually bound to do, which is not good consideration.
options.C: No, because the promise to pay more was made under duress and is therefore unenforceable.
options.D: Yes, because both parties agreed to the variation, making it contractually binding.

Contract Law.explanation: Where a party performs an existing contractual duty but doing so confers a practical benefit on the promisor, that benefit constitutes good consideration for the variation: Williams v Roffey Bros [1991]. Alek received the practical benefit of timely completion, avoiding loss of his corporate catering booking. Option B states the traditional rule in Stilk v Myrick (1809) but Williams v Roffey qualifies this rule where a genuine practical benefit is obtained. Option C is a distractor: while economic duress can vitiate contracts where pressure is illegitimate, the question is about enforceability of consideration, and on these facts Owen’s threat to stop is not necessarily illegitimate pressure. Option D is incorrect: mutual agreement alone does not make a modification binding without consideration; agreement and consideration are separate requirements.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Frank Hollis and his wife Sandra agree that Frank will pay Sandra £50 per week for household expenses while she manages the family home. Frank stops paying. Sandra seeks to enforce the agreement in court. Which of the following best describes the legal position?

options.A: The agreement is binding because it is supported by good consideration on both sides.
options.B: The agreement is not binding because agreements between spouses about household arrangements are presumed not to be legally binding.
options.C: The agreement is binding because it was made with clear agreement between both parties.
options.D: The agreement is not binding because household management is not recognised as good consideration.

Contract Law.explanation: There is a rebuttable presumption that domestic and family agreements are not intended to create legal relations: Balfour v Balfour [1919]. Frank and Sandra’s arrangement is a classic domestic agreement that the courts consistently hold to be outside legal enforcement. The presumption is not rebutted on these facts — there is no indication of a separated couple formalising arrangements or a commercial context. Option A is a distractor: even if consideration exists, a binding contract requires both consideration and intention to create legal relations; these are separate requirements. Option C is wrong: agreement alone does not create legal relations in a domestic context. Option D is incorrect: the reason for non-enforceability is lack of ICLR, not absence of consideration; household management may in principle constitute consideration.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd sends an order for steel components on its standard purchase order form, which limits Hollowell’s liability for defective goods. The supplier responds with a sales acknowledgment on its own standard form, which excludes Hollowell’s liability limit and imposes the supplier’s terms. The components are delivered and accepted by Hollowell without further correspondence. On whose terms is the contract formed?

options.A: Hollowell’s terms, because Hollowell made the first offer.
options.B: The supplier’s terms, because the supplier’s acknowledgment was a counter-offer accepted by Hollowell taking delivery.
options.C: Both parties’ terms apply in equal measure, with conflicting terms cancelling each other out.
options.D: No contract is formed because the parties never agreed on terms.

Contract Law.explanation: In a battle of the forms, English law applies the ‘last shot’ doctrine: the final set of terms put forward before performance is treated as a counter-offer, and a party who performs without objection accepts those terms: Butler Machine Tool Co v Ex-Cell-O Corp [1979]. The supplier’s acknowledgment on its own terms was a counter-offer, and Hollowell Engineering accepted it by taking delivery without objection. Option A is wrong because the first shot does not win in a battle of forms — it is displaced by subsequent counter-offers. Option C is incorrect: English law does not apply a ‘knock-out’ rule blending conflicting terms; conflicting terms are resolved by the last shot. Option D is wrong because performance resolves the battle — delivery and acceptance confirm the parties proceeded on the supplier’s last-stated terms.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Kieran Hollis, who is 17, buys a professional racing bicycle from a sports shop on the High Street in Wharton Magna for £800 and uses it for competitive cycling training. He attempts to return it the following week, claiming he lacks capacity to contract. Which of the following best describes the legal position?

options.A: The contract is void because minors lack capacity to enter contracts.
options.B: The contract is void because the purchase price exceeds an amount a minor can legally spend.
options.C: The contract is voidable at Kieran’s option because he is a minor.
options.D: The contract is binding on Kieran because it is a contract for necessaries.

Contract Law.explanation: A minor is bound by contracts for necessaries: Sale of Goods Act 1979 s.3. Necessaries are goods suitable to the minor’s condition in life and to his actual requirements at the time of sale. A professional racing bicycle for a minor engaged in competitive cycling training qualifies as a necessary. The contract is therefore binding and Kieran cannot repudiate it. Option A is wrong: minor’s contracts are generally voidable, not void. For necessaries, they are fully binding. Option B is incorrect: English law imposes no financial threshold on minor’s contracts; the test is the category of goods relative to the minor’s lifestyle, not price. Option C is wrong on these facts: while non-necessary contracts are voidable at the minor’s option, the necessaries doctrine defeats that right here.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Marcus Bridgewater emails Victoria Ashworth: ‘I offer you the development plot at the corner of Bath Road and Orchard Road for £2.5m, subject to contract.’ Victoria immediately replies: ‘I accept and consider myself legally bound.’ Marcus later sells the plot to a third party. Victoria claims a binding contract was formed. Which of the following best analyses the position?

options.A: A contract was formed because Victoria’s immediate acceptance was communicated to Marcus.
options.B: A contract was formed because the postal rule applies to email communications.
options.C: No contract was formed because ‘subject to contract’ negatives the intention to be legally bound at this stage.
options.D: No contract was formed because contracts for the sale of land must be in signed writing under s.2 LP(MP)A 1989.

Contract Law.explanation: ‘Subject to contract’ is a well-recognised phrase in English property dealings indicating that the parties do not intend to be legally bound until formal contracts are exchanged: Eccles v Bryant [1948]. This qualification negatives the intention to create legal relations at the offer stage, regardless of Victoria’s purported acceptance. Option A is wrong: even if acceptance was effectively communicated, there was no binding offer to accept because Marcus’s communication was not intended to create legal relations. Option B is a trap: the postal rule applies to acceptance by post, and its application to email is uncertain; in any event the issue is the ‘subject to contract’ qualification, not the mechanics of communication. Option D is a distractor: s.2 LP(MP)A 1989 does require contracts for sale of land to be in signed writing, but the correct primary reason here is the ‘subject to contract’ qualification operating at the ICLR level, before formality requirements are even reached.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd and Wharton Foods Ltd enter a supply agreement for food-grade metal containers where the price is ‘to be agreed between the parties at the start of each month.’ Six months of supply proceed smoothly. In month seven, the parties cannot agree a price. Hollowell Engineering claims a binding contract exists and seeks payment at the previous month’s price. Which of the following best states the legal position?

options.A: A binding contract exists and the court will imply a reasonable price under the Sale of Goods Act 1979 s.8.
options.B: The contract is void for uncertainty because the price was never definitively fixed.
options.C: The contract is binding for months one to six but there are no obligations for month seven because agreement has broken down.
options.D: Hollowell Engineering can enforce payment at the previous month’s price because the parties’ course of dealing implies that term.

Contract Law.explanation: Where a price is not fixed but goods are supplied, the Sale of Goods Act 1979 s.8 implies a reasonable price unless the contract shows a contrary intention. Six months of consistent performance is strong evidence that a binding framework contract exists, and the statutory mechanism provides the fall-back for pricing disputes: Foley v Classique Coaches [1934] (distinguishing May & Butcher v R [1934] where there had been no performance). Option B overstates the effect of ‘to be agreed’: this formulation does not automatically void a contract where consistent performance and a statutory fall-back exist. Option C imposes an artificial split between months 1–6 and month 7 that does not reflect how courts treat framework contracts with a statutory pricing mechanism. Option D goes further than the law allows: the SGA 1979 s.8 supplies a reasonable price — not necessarily the previous month’s price — and cannot be displaced by merely invoking a course of dealing without more.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Rachel Okonkwo books a parking space at the Market Square car park by telephone. On arrival, she sees a prominent sign at the entrance stating: ‘Parking at owner’s risk. All liability excluded.’ Her car is damaged due to the negligence of car park staff. Is the exclusion clause incorporated into the contract?

options.A: No, because the contract was formed by telephone before Rachel Okonkwo saw the sign, so the clause was brought to her attention too late.
options.B: No, because exclusion clauses must be contained in a signed document to be incorporated.
options.C: Yes, because the sign is prominently displayed and Rachel Okonkwo had constructive notice of its contents.
options.D: Yes, because exclusion clauses in commercial car parks are always incorporated as standard industry terms.

Contract Law.explanation: An exclusion clause must be incorporated before or at the time the contract is made: Olley v Marlborough Court [1949]. The contract was formed when Rachel Okonkwo booked by telephone. The sign was not brought to her attention until she arrived — after the contract was concluded. The clause therefore cannot be incorporated. Option B overstates the requirement: exclusion clauses can be incorporated by notice without a signature, provided notice is given in time: Parker v South Eastern Railway (1877). Option C is wrong in this context: even prominent notice cannot incorporate a term into a contract already formed — the prominence of the sign is irrelevant to the timing point. Option D is incorrect: there is no industry convention that automatically incorporates exclusion clauses; incorporation always requires reasonable notice given to the specific party before or at contracting.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Frank Hollis, who runs The Malt & Mattock as a sole trader, buys a commercial coffee machine from a trade supplier under a contract excluding all liability for defects. The machine fails within a month due to a manufacturing defect. Frank challenges the exclusion clause. Which legal regime governs his challenge?

options.A: The Consumer Rights Act 2015, because Frank is an individual purchasing goods.
options.B: The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, because Frank is contracting in the course of a business.
options.C: The common law reasonableness test only, because statutory controls apply solely to consumers.
options.D: Neither statute: exclusion clauses between businesses are entirely a matter of freedom of contract.

Contract Law.explanation: The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (UCTA) applies to business-to-business contracts. Frank Hollis, as a sole trader buying equipment for his pub business, is contracting in the course of a business and is not a consumer. UCTA s.6(3) subjects clauses excluding liability for breach of the SGA 1979 implied terms in non-consumer sales to the reasonableness test. Option A is wrong: the Consumer Rights Act 2015 applies to contracts between a ‘trader’ and a ‘consumer’ (an individual acting outside their trade, business, craft or profession). Frank is acting within his business. Option C is incorrect: UCTA applies to B2B contracts and the reasonableness test is the statutory test under UCTA s.11, not a purely common law device. Option D is wrong: UCTA specifically restricts freedom of contract in B2B contexts involving exclusion of the SGA implied terms.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A software company provides bespoke practice management software to Crane & Pelletier Solicitors under a contract limiting the supplier’s liability for all losses to £5,000. A serious software bug causes Crane & Pelletier to lose £250,000 in client data. The supplier relies on the limitation clause. Under UCTA 1977, which factor is most relevant to the reasonableness assessment?

options.A: Whether Crane & Pelletier read and understood the clause before signing.
options.B: Whether the parties had equal bargaining power and whether the supplier was insured against the risk capped by the clause.
options.C: Whether the limitation clause was prominently displayed in the contract.
options.D: Whether £5,000 was a reasonable estimate of likely loss at the time the software failed.

Contract Law.explanation: The UCTA 1977 Schedule 2 guidelines direct courts to consider, among other things, the relative bargaining strength of the parties and whether the supplier was insured for the risk: George Mitchell (Chesterhall) Ltd v Finney Lock Seeds [1983]. Whether the software supplier was insured and whether Crane & Pelletier had equal bargaining power to negotiate the cap are central to whether the clause is reasonable. Option A is wrong: incorporation and reasonableness are separate tests under UCTA. Reasonableness is assessed objectively against the Schedule 2 guidelines, not by the customer’s subjective knowledge. Option C conflates incorporation (where prominence assists notice: Parker v South Eastern Railway) with reasonableness — prominence is relevant to notice, not to the fairness of the substantive term. Option D misapplies the test: reasonableness is assessed at the time of contracting, not at the time of loss, and focuses on the overall allocation of risk, not merely whether the cap matched eventual loss.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A delivery contract between Northgate Couriers and Wharton Foods Ltd guarantees delivery of food-grade packaging components ‘by Friday.’ The Northgate van arrives on Saturday. The cargo is slightly delayed but undamaged. Wharton Foods refuses to pay, claiming the delivery term is a condition and any breach entitles termination. Which of the following best describes how a court will approach the classification?

options.A: As a condition, because ‘guaranteed’ language signals strict compliance and any breach entitles the innocent party to terminate.
options.B: As a warranty, because the breach caused no serious loss to the buyer.
options.C: As an innominate term, because the court should assess the seriousness of the consequences of the actual breach.
options.D: As a condition, because time of delivery is always a condition in commercial contracts under the Sale of Goods Act 1979.

Contract Law.explanation: Where a term does not fall clearly within a recognised category, the court may treat it as an innominate (intermediate) term and assess the consequences of the particular breach: Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha [1962]. Whether the innocent party can terminate depends on whether the breach deprived them of substantially the whole benefit of the contract. A one-day delay causing no spoilage is unlikely to meet that threshold. Option A is wrong: ‘guaranteed’ language is persuasive but not determinative; courts look to consequences of breach, not the label used. Option B reverses the analysis: classification is not determined by the loss caused. For an innominate term, the loss caused determines the available remedy — but the reasoning must flow from classification first. Option D is incorrect: the SGA 1979 makes certain implied terms conditions (ss.12–15) but does not make every delivery time term a condition in all commercial contracts; time is of the essence only by agreement or in specific commercial contexts.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A software company contracts to develop bespoke case management software for Crane & Pelletier Solicitors. The contract is silent on quality. The delivered software contains numerous bugs rendering it unfit for the firm’s purposes. Under which basis is a quality term most likely to be implied?

options.A: A term implied in law under the Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982 s.13, requiring reasonable skill and care.
options.B: A term implied in fact under the business efficacy test, because the contract cannot work without quality software.
options.C: A term implied by custom, because it is standard in the software industry to warrant fitness for purpose.
options.D: A term implied in law under the Sale of Goods Act 1979 s.14, requiring satisfactory quality.

Contract Law.explanation: A contract to develop bespoke software is primarily a contract for services, not the sale of goods. The Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982 s.13 implies a term that the supplier will carry out the service with reasonable skill and care. Delivering bug-ridden software unfit for Crane & Pelletier’s purposes is likely to breach this. Option B is incorrect: a term implied in fact under business efficacy (The Moorcock (1889)) fills specific gaps necessary to give effect to the parties’ obvious intention. The statutory implied term is the more direct and appropriate mechanism. Option C is wrong: custom requires proof of an established and notorious trade usage — general expectations in the software industry do not automatically constitute a legally recognised custom. Option D is wrong: the SGA 1979 s.14 applies to contracts for the sale of goods. A bespoke software development contract is primarily a service contract governed by SGSA 1982, not the SGA 1979.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Neville Prescott sells a second-hand car to James Ashworth and tells him it has never been in an accident. Neville genuinely believes this, but in fact the car had a minor collision before he acquired it for resale. James purchases the car and later discovers the accident history. Which type of misrepresentation has Neville made?

options.A: Fraudulent misrepresentation, because Neville should have checked the car’s history before making the statement.
options.B: Negligent misrepresentation under s.2(1) MA 1967, because he had no reasonable basis for his belief.
options.C: Innocent misrepresentation, because Neville genuinely believed the statement and had no reason to know otherwise.
options.D: No misrepresentation, because the statement was an opinion rather than a statement of fact.

Contract Law.explanation: An innocent misrepresentation is one made without fault — the representor had reasonable grounds to believe the statement was true and made it honestly. On these facts, Neville Prescott genuinely believed the car had never been in an accident and had no reason to know otherwise; he therefore made the statement innocently. Option A is wrong: fraudulent misrepresentation requires knowledge of falsity, absence of belief in truth, or recklessness as to truth: Derry v Peek (1889). Mere failure to check does not amount to fraud. Option B is wrong: s.2(1) MA 1967 requires that the defendant lacked reasonable grounds for their belief. The facts state Neville genuinely believed and had no reason to doubt — on these facts he may have had reasonable grounds, so s.2(1) is not clearly established. Option D is wrong: the factual history of a car (‘never been in an accident’) is a statement of fact, not an opinion.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Graham Grange tells Marcus Bridgewater that Wharton Foods Ltd’s annual turnover is £500,000. Relying on this, Marcus pays £1.2m for a controlling stake. After completion, the true turnover is found to be £310,000. Graham made the statement believing it was correct but had not verified the figures. Marcus claims damages under s.2(1) of the Misrepresentation Act 1967. Which of the following best states Graham’s position?

options.A: Graham is liable for damages assessed on a fraudulent basis unless he can prove he had reasonable grounds to believe the statement was true.
options.B: Marcus must prove Graham was negligent in making the statement for s.2(1) to apply.
options.C: Graham is not liable because he made the statement honestly and without intent to deceive.
options.D: Graham is liable only if Marcus can show the misrepresentation induced the contract.

Contract Law.explanation: Section 2(1) MA 1967 reverses the burden of proof: once the claimant proves a misrepresentation that induced the contract, the defendant is liable unless they can prove they had reasonable grounds for their belief up to the time the contract was made. Moreover, damages under s.2(1) are assessed on the tortious fraud measure: Royscot Trust Ltd v Rogerson [1991]. Graham’s failure to verify the turnover figures may mean he cannot discharge the burden of proving reasonable grounds. Option B is wrong: the burden is reversed under s.2(1) — Graham must disprove negligence, not Marcus prove it. Option C is wrong: honesty without reasonable grounds is not a defence under s.2(1); the test is objective reasonable grounds, not subjective belief. Option D identifies a genuine requirement — inducement is necessary for all misrepresentation claims — but it does not address Graham’s specific position under s.2(1) and is therefore incomplete as an answer.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: In 2021, James Ashworth bought shares in Wharton Foods Ltd after Graham Grange falsely told him the company had no outstanding debts. James was aware ‘something was not right’ but purchased anyway, continued holding the shares and claiming dividends until 2024, when he discovered the true position and sought rescission. Which factor is most likely to bar rescission?

options.A: Lapse of time only, because James took three years to bring his claim.
options.B: Affirmation only, because James continued to hold shares and claim dividends after becoming suspicious.
options.C: Both affirmation and lapse of time, because James’s conduct evidenced acceptance and the delay is also relevant.
options.D: Neither: rescission for fraudulent misrepresentation cannot be barred by affirmation or lapse of time.

Contract Law.explanation: Two bars to rescission are engaged. First, affirmation: a representee who continues to perform the contract with knowledge of the misrepresentation (or facts putting them on notice) affirms it and loses the right to rescind: Long v Lloyd [1958]. James Ashworth continued holding shares and claiming dividends while aware ‘something was not right’ — strong evidence of affirmation. Second, lapse of time: while for fraud the limitation period runs from discovery, the equitable remedy of rescission can be lost by laches (unreasonable delay making rescission inequitable) even in fraud cases, particularly where third-party interests or unjust enrichment concerns arise. Option A is incomplete: lapse alone is uncertain without more on equitable laches; affirmation is the stronger and clearer bar on these facts. Option B is also incomplete: affirmation is clearly made out, but lapse is also a relevant equitable consideration. Option D is wrong: rescission can be barred even for fraud by affirmation, laches, impossibility of restitution, and third-party rights.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Priya Mehta and James Ashworth contract for James to sell his vintage motorbike to Priya for £8,000. Unknown to both parties, the motorbike had been destroyed in a fire the previous week. What is the legal effect on the contract?

options.A: The contract is void under the doctrine of common mistake because the subject matter did not exist at the time of contracting.
options.B: The contract is voidable at Priya’s option because she was the party misled.
options.C: The contract is valid and James must pay damages to Priya for failure to deliver.
options.D: The contract is frustrated because the motorbike was destroyed after the agreement was made.

Contract Law.explanation: Where both parties share a fundamental mistake about the existence of the subject matter at the time of contracting (res extincta), the contract is void: Sale of Goods Act 1979 s.6. Neither Priya nor James knew of the destruction; the mistake is shared and goes to the root of the contract. Option B is wrong: voidable contracts arise in cases of unilateral mistake or misrepresentation. Common mistake as to res extincta renders the contract void ab initio, not merely voidable at one party’s option. Option C is wrong: James cannot be in breach for failing to deliver goods that did not exist at formation; there is no valid contract to breach. Option D is wrong: frustration applies to supervening events occurring after the contract is formed. The motorbike was destroyed before contracting, making this a case of res extincta at formation, not frustration.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A fraudster visits Prescott Motors claiming to be Owen Davies, the well-known local contractor, with an excellent credit record. Neville Prescott makes reasonable checks (which have been faked) and sells a car on credit. The fraudster immediately sells the car to an innocent third party. When the fraud is discovered, Neville seeks to recover the car from the third party. Which principle determines whether Neville can recover?

options.A: Neville can recover because unilateral mistake as to identity renders the contract void, and a void contract passes no title.
options.B: Neville cannot recover because the third party gave value and is protected by a general bona fide purchaser defence.
options.C: Neville can recover under the Misrepresentation Act 1967 by rescinding the contract against the third party.
options.D: Neville cannot recover because he dealt face-to-face with the fraudster, and the law presumes the seller intended to contract with the person physically present, making the contract voidable not void.

Contract Law.explanation: The critical distinction is between contracts made at a distance — where mistake as to identity can void the contract (Cundy v Lindsay (1878)) — and face-to-face dealings — where the presumption is that the seller intends to contract with the person physically present, making the contract voidable not void: Lewis v Averay [1972]; Phillips v Brooks [1919]. A voidable contract can pass valid title before rescission, and once an innocent third party acquires for value, the right to rescind is lost. Neville Prescott therefore has only a voidable contract; the fraudster passed title to the third party before rescission, extinguishing Neville’s right to recover. Option A is wrong: in face-to-face dealings the contract is voidable, not void — the void/voidable distinction turns on the face-to-face rule, not merely on the existence of a mistake. Option C is wrong: even analysed as misrepresentation, the MA 1967 cannot assist because the right to rescind is barred by the third party’s acquisition for value. Option B partially captures the outcome but identifies the wrong mechanism — it is not a general bona fide purchaser doctrine but the specific operation of the voidable contract rule in face-to-face dealings.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Atlas Contracts Ltd threatens to walk off a major fit-out project for Bridgewater Developments unless Marcus Bridgewater agrees to pay an additional £100,000. Marcus has a non-negotiable opening date two weeks away and no realistic prospect of finding a replacement contractor. Marcus agrees under protest and, after the project completes, seeks to set aside the additional payment. What elements must Marcus establish?

options.A: That Atlas Contracts’ threat was unlawful, and that the threat caused Marcus to agree.
options.B: That Atlas Contracts made a fraudulent misrepresentation about its ability to complete the contract in order to extract the extra payment.
options.C: That Atlas Contracts exploited a relationship of confidence and trust to induce the payment.
options.D: That Atlas Contracts’ pressure was illegitimate, that it left Marcus with no practical alternative, and that it caused the agreement.

Contract Law.explanation: Economic duress requires: (i) illegitimate pressure (which need not be unlawful in a criminal sense — threatening to breach a contract can suffice: DSND Subsea Ltd v Petroleum Geo Services ASA [2000]); (ii) which left the claimant no practical alternative; and (iii) which caused the agreement: Atlas Express Ltd v Kafco [1989]. Option A is too narrow: ‘unlawful’ in the criminal sense is not required; illegitimate pressure includes a threat to breach contract. Option C describes undue influence (exploitation of a relationship), which is a distinct doctrine not engaged here — there is no relationship of trust and confidence between a contractor and a commercial client. Option B introduces fraudulent misrepresentation, which is not on the facts: Atlas Contracts’ threat was about future conduct, not a false statement of existing fact.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Betty Huxley signs a deed of gift transferring her house at 6 Church Lane, Wharton Magna, to her adult son Robert, who has managed her finances for three years and to whom she ‘always deferred.’ She now seeks to set aside the gift claiming undue influence. Which of the following best describes how her claim would be approached?

options.A: Betty must prove actual undue influence by showing Robert applied specific pressure to obtain the gift.
options.B: The presumption of undue influence cannot apply because Betty is an adult with full legal capacity.
options.C: Robert must prove there was no undue influence, because parent-child relationships always raise a presumption of undue influence.
options.D: The relationship raises a presumption of undue influence; Betty need only show the transaction calls for explanation.

Contract Law.explanation: Undue influence can be presumed (Class 2B) where a claimant proves a relationship of trust and confidence and shows the transaction calls for explanation: Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No 2) [2001]. Robert managed Betty’s finances for three years and she ‘always deferred’ to him — capable of establishing a Class 2B relationship. Transferring the family home at 6 Church Lane is a transaction that plainly calls for explanation, shifting the burden to Robert to rebut. Option A is too onerous: once a Class 2 presumption arises, actual pressure need not be proved. Option C is wrong: a parent-child relationship is not a Class 2A (automatically recognised) category; it must be established as Class 2B on the specific facts. Option B is wrong: capacity and undue influence are separate doctrines; having legal capacity does not prevent a finding of undue influence.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A band is booked by James Ashworth, an events manager, to perform at an outdoor festival on the Ashworth Hall grounds on a specific date for £15,000. Before the event, the government declares a national emergency and bans all public gatherings, making the festival impossible. Which of the following best describes the legal effect?

options.A: The contract is voidable at the festival organiser’s option because performance has become impractical.
options.B: The contract is discharged by frustration because a supervening event has made performance impossible without the fault of either party.
options.C: The contract is void because the parties failed to provide for government intervention in their agreement.
options.D: The band can claim £15,000 because the organiser must bear the risk of cancellation.

Contract Law.explanation: A contract is frustrated where a supervening event, beyond the parties’ control, makes performance impossible, illegal, or radically different from what was contemplated: Davis Contractors Ltd v Fareham UDC [1956]; Taylor v Caldwell (1863). A government ban makes performance physically impossible without fault on either side. Option A is wrong: frustration terminates the contract automatically by operation of law; it does not create a voidable contract at one party’s option. Option C is wrong: frustration does not require the contract to be silent on the supervening event — it applies unless the parties have specifically allocated the risk of that event; no such allocation appears here. Option D is wrong: upon frustration, the contract is discharged and the contractual fee cannot be claimed; financial adjustment is governed by the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943 instead.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Alek Kowalski prepays £3,000 to The Malt & Mattock for a private banquet function room. The contract is subsequently frustrated by a fire destroying the function room. Alek had also incurred £1,500 in preparatory catering costs before the frustrating event. The Malt & Mattock had spent £500 acting on the agreement. Under the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943, what is the most likely outcome?

options.A: Alek recovers £3,000 minus an allowance for The Malt & Mattock’s expenses up to the cap of the sum paid; the preparatory costs are not recoverable.
options.B: Alek recovers the full £3,000; The Malt & Mattock cannot retain any sum because the contract is frustrated.
options.C: Alek recovers £3,000 and can also claim the £1,500 preparatory costs from The Malt & Mattock as unjust enrichment.
options.D: Losses lie where they fall: neither party can recover anything after frustration.

Contract Law.explanation: Under the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943 s.1(2), prepayments are recoverable but the court may allow the payee to retain or recover expenses incurred before the frustrating event, up to a cap equal to the sum paid. The Malt & Mattock may therefore retain up to £500 of the £3,000, and Alek Kowalski will recover the remainder. As for the £1,500 preparatory costs: s.1(3) allows a party to claim a just sum for a valuable benefit conferred on the other party — but Alek’s preparatory catering costs were for his own performance and did not benefit The Malt & Mattock, so s.1(3) does not assist. Option B is wrong: s.1(2) permits the payee to retain an expense allowance; a blanket recovery ignores this. Option C is wrong: s.1(3) requires a valuable benefit to have been conferred on the other party; Alek’s own preparation did not benefit The Malt & Mattock. Option D states the common law position before the 1943 Act, which the Act was enacted to replace.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: In January, a sports equipment manufacturer contracts to deliver 500 football kits to Wharton Athletic FC by 1 August. In March, the manufacturer calls the club and says: ‘We have taken on another client and will not be fulfilling your order.’ The club immediately sources replacement kits at higher cost in April. Which of the following best describes the club’s position?

options.A: The club must wait until 1 August to determine if the manufacturer actually breaches, then claim damages.
options.B: The club has no claim because the manufacturer may change its mind before the delivery date.
options.C: The club can treat the contract as discharged but must wait until 1 August to assess its loss for damages.
options.D: The club can treat the contract as discharged immediately, accept the repudiation, and claim damages.

Contract Law.explanation: Where a party clearly and unequivocally renounces a contract before performance is due, this constitutes anticipatory breach: Hochster v De la Tour (1853). The innocent party may accept the repudiation immediately, treat the contract as discharged, and sue at once without waiting for the performance date. Wharton Athletic FC was entitled to source replacement kits in April. Option A is wrong: under anticipatory breach, the innocent party need not wait until the performance date. Option C is wrong: damages are assessed from the date of accepted repudiation, not the original performance date; there is no need to wait until August. Option B is wrong: a clear, unequivocal renunciation in March is anticipatory breach; the manufacturer’s theoretical right to change its mind does not prevent the innocent party from accepting the breach and mitigating.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd contracts to deliver specialist metal processing machinery to Wharton Foods Ltd by 1 June. On 15 May, Hollowell says it will be unable to deliver by 1 June. Wharton Foods responds: ‘We’ll give you more time — deliver by 30 June.’ Hollowell delivers on 30 June, but the machinery is defective. Wharton Foods wishes to terminate for both the late delivery and the defective machinery. Which of the following best analyses Wharton Foods’ position?

options.A: Wharton Foods can terminate because the original failure to meet the 1 June deadline was itself a repudiation.
options.B: Wharton Foods cannot terminate for late delivery because it affirmed the contract by granting an extension, but may be able to terminate for defective machinery if the defect goes to the root of the contract.
options.C: Wharton Foods can terminate for both the late delivery and the defective machinery, because both arose from the same breach.
options.D: Wharton Foods has no right to terminate because it is estopped from relying on the delivery terms having varied them.

Contract Law.explanation: By agreeing to an extension on 15 May, Wharton Foods elected to affirm the contract rather than accept the repudiation. Affirmation waives the right to terminate for that particular breach. Wharton Foods therefore cannot retrospectively terminate for late delivery. The defective machinery is an independent, subsequent breach. Whether Wharton Foods can terminate for it depends on the classification of the term: if delivery of conforming goods is a condition, any breach entitles termination; if an innominate term, termination requires a breach depriving Wharton Foods of substantially the whole benefit. Option A is wrong: Wharton Foods affirmed the contract on 15 May, waiving the right to terminate for late delivery. Option C is wrong: affirmation cuts off the right to terminate for the 1 June breach; subsequent breaches are assessed independently. Option D misstates the position: estoppel prevents enforcement of strict delivery by 1 June during the extension, but does not extinguish all rights — Wharton Foods retains the right to enforce the varied date and to rely on subsequent defects.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A publisher contracts to pay Clive Dring, a local journalist, a £20,000 advance on publication of his memoir. The publisher breaches by refusing to publish. Clive had spent £3,000 preparing the manuscript and expected royalties of at least £30,000 over the life of the book. The primary measure of damages in contract law aims to put the claimant in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed. Which measure best applies?

options.A: Clive should recover the £3,000 in wasted preparation costs under the reliance measure.
options.B: Clive should recover the £20,000 advance only, because that is the benefit promised by the publisher.
options.C: Clive should recover damages based on the expectation interest, including the £20,000 advance and any lost profits, subject to remoteness and mitigation.
options.D: Clive should recover nothing because the royalty projections were speculative.

Contract Law.explanation: The primary rule in contract damages is the expectation measure: the court aims to put the claimant in the same financial position as if the contract had been performed: Robinson v Harman (1848). This covers the promised advance and lost profits, subject to remoteness and mitigation. Option A is wrong as a primary answer: the reliance measure (recovering wasted expenditure) is an alternative the claimant may elect, typically where expectation loss is difficult to quantify. It is not the primary measure. Option B is incomplete: the expectation interest is not limited to the advance; it includes loss of profit subject to remoteness. Option D is wrong: courts will assess damages for uncertain future profits where there was a real chance of earning them; speculative projections do not defeat the claim where a reasonable expectation of profit existed.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Northgate Couriers is contracted to deliver a replacement part for Wharton Foods Ltd’s production machine. Northgate is told the delivery is ‘urgent’ but not told the machine is broken and production has stopped. Northgate delays delivery by three days. During those three days Wharton Foods loses £50,000 in production profit. Under the remoteness rule in Hadley v Baxendale, can Wharton Foods recover the £50,000?

options.A: No, because loss of production profit from a broken machine was not within the reasonable contemplation of Northgate at the time of contracting.
options.B: Yes, because Northgate was told the delivery was urgent.
options.C: Yes, because lost profits are always recoverable in contract as consequential losses.
options.D: No, because Northgate was merely negligent rather than in deliberate breach.

Contract Law.explanation: Under the second limb of Hadley v Baxendale (1854), special losses are only recoverable if the party in breach was aware of the special circumstances at contracting, such that the loss was within their reasonable contemplation. Northgate Couriers was told delivery was ‘urgent’ but not that the machine was broken and production had stopped. The £50,000 production loss is a special loss not arising naturally from a delivery delay. Without knowledge of the specific machine-breakdown context, this loss was not in Northgate’s reasonable contemplation. Option B is wrong: ‘urgent’ does not communicate the specific circumstances causing the lost profit and does not bring the £50,000 within the second limb. Option C is wrong: consequential losses are only recoverable if within the reasonable contemplation of the parties at contracting — there is no general rule that lost profits are always recoverable. Option D is wrong on the facts: a courier who delays delivery is in breach of contract; the issue of damages arises only once breach is established.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A logistics contract between Northgate Couriers and Wharton Foods Ltd states: ‘In the event of late delivery, Northgate shall pay £10,000 per day of delay as liquidated damages.’ Northgate delivers two days late, causing Wharton Foods actual losses of £3,000. Wharton Foods claims £20,000. Under which principle is the clause most likely to be challenged, and what is the correct statement of current law?

options.A: The clause is void as a penalty under the rule that liquidated damages clauses must be a genuine pre-estimate of loss.
options.B: The clause is enforceable because the parties freely agreed to it and freedom of contract prevails.
options.C: The clause is void as a penalty because £10,000 per day is extravagant compared to £3,000 actual loss.
options.D: The clause will be assessed under Cavendish Square Holding BV v Makdessi: it is void only if it imposes a detriment out of all proportion to any legitimate interest the innocent party has in enforcing the obligation.

Contract Law.explanation: The Supreme Court in Cavendish Square Holding BV v Makdessi [2015] reformulated the test for penalty clauses. The old ‘genuine pre-estimate of loss’ test has been superseded: a clause is a penalty only where it imposes a detriment on the contract-breaker that is out of all proportion to any legitimate interest of the innocent party. Commercial parties with equal bargaining power have considerable latitude to agree deterrent clauses with legitimate interests in securing performance. Option A misstates current law: the ‘genuine pre-estimate of loss’ test from Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co v New Garage [1915] is no longer the primary test after Makdessi. Option B overstates freedom of contract: the courts retain equitable jurisdiction to strike down genuine penalties even in commercial contracts, albeit on a narrower basis post-Makdessi. Option C applies the pre-Makdessi Dunlop test, which focused on disproportion between the stipulated sum and actual loss; post-Makdessi, the question is whether the clause is out of all proportion to a legitimate interest — a wider and different analysis.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Wharton Foods Ltd contracts with an IT company for the IT company to install a practice management system at the offices of Wharton Foods Distribution Ltd, Wharton Foods’ subsidiary. The contract states: ‘This contract is for the benefit of Wharton Foods Distribution Ltd.’ The installation is negligently performed and Wharton Foods Distribution suffers loss. Can Wharton Foods Distribution enforce the contract against the IT company under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999?

options.A: No, because Wharton Foods Distribution was not a party to the contract and privity prevents third-party enforcement.
options.B: Yes, because the contract expressly identifies Wharton Foods Distribution as a beneficiary, satisfying s.1(1)(b) of the Act.
options.C: Yes, but only if Wharton Foods Distribution can show it provided consideration to the IT company.
options.D: No, because the Act only applies to contracts for the direct supply of goods or services to the third party.

Contract Law.explanation: Under s.1(1)(b) of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, a third party may enforce a contractual term if the contract purports to confer a benefit on them and they are expressly identified by name, class, or description. The contract here expressly states it is ‘for the benefit of Wharton Foods Distribution Ltd’ — Wharton Foods Distribution is identified by name and a benefit is conferred. Unless s.1(2) applies (i.e. it appears the parties did not intend the term to be enforceable by the third party), Wharton Foods Distribution may enforce. Option A is wrong: the 1999 Act is the statutory exception to the common law privity rule. Option C is wrong: the Act specifically removes the requirement for the third party to provide consideration — this was the fundamental change the Act made. Option D is wrong: the Act contains no limitation restricting it to direct supply contracts; it applies to any contract satisfying s.1(1)(a) or s.1(1)(b).

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Neville Prescott posted an offer to Alek Kowalski on Monday to sell a commercial van for £8,000, stating the offer was open for seven days. On Wednesday, Prescott sold the van to another buyer and posted a revocation letter to Kowalski. On Thursday morning, before receiving the revocation, Kowalski posted an acceptance. When Kowalski's acceptance arrived on Friday, Prescott refused to proceed. Is a contract formed?

options.A: Yes — the postal rule applies to acceptances, so the contract was formed on Thursday when Kowalski posted his acceptance.
options.B: No — revocation of an offer is effective when posted, so Prescott's Wednesday revocation ended the offer before Kowalski accepted.
options.C: No — Prescott had already sold the van to a third party, making performance impossible and the offer automatically lapsed.
options.D: Yes — a seven-day offer cannot be revoked within that period without paying consideration for the option.

Contract Law.explanation: A is correct. Under the postal rule (Adams v Lindsell [1818]), acceptance by post is effective when the letter is posted, not when it arrives. Kowalski posted his acceptance on Thursday before receiving the revocation, so the contract was formed at that moment. B is wrong: revocation is effective on communication to the offeree (Byrne v Van Tienhoven [1880]), not on posting — Kowalski had not received the revocation when he accepted. C is wrong: automatic lapse by impossibility does not apply unless the offeree knows of it. D is wrong: a gratuitous promise to keep an offer open for a fixed period is not binding without consideration; Prescott could revoke, but only by effective communication before acceptance.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Prescott Motors placed a sign in its window: 'We will pay £200 to any customer who finds a fault we missed on a pre-sale inspection — claim within 30 days of purchase.' Rachel Okonkwo bought a car from Prescott Motors and, within 14 days, discovered a brake defect that had not been identified on the pre-sale inspection report. Prescott refuses to pay, arguing the sign was merely an advertisement. Is Rachel entitled to £200?

options.A: No — a sign in a shop window is an invitation to treat, not an offer capable of acceptance.
options.B: Yes — the sign constitutes a unilateral offer accepted by Rachel's performance of the condition (purchasing and discovering the fault within 30 days).
options.C: No — Prescott's offer required Rachel to notify acceptance in writing before purchasing the car.
options.D: Yes — but only because Rachel has a separate statutory claim under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 that overrides the contractual analysis.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Following Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893], a promise to pay upon performance of a specified act is a unilateral offer, not a mere invitation to treat. No prior communication of acceptance is required; acceptance occurs through performance of the condition. Rachel purchased the car and discovered a qualifying defect within the stated period, completing performance. A is wrong: the advertisement/ITT principle applies to bilateral contracts; a clear unilateral promise with definite conditions is an offer. C is wrong: unilateral offers do not require prior notification of acceptance. D incorrectly conflates contractual and statutory claims.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Owen Davies owed Hollowell Engineering £12,000 for steelwork on a completed project. Pat Hollowell, facing a cash-flow problem, wrote to Davies: 'If you pay £9,000 now, I will not pursue the remaining £3,000.' Davies paid £9,000 in reliance on this promise. Six months later, Hollowell Engineering sought to recover the outstanding £3,000. Can it do so?

options.A: Yes — Foakes v Beer establishes that an agreement to accept part payment does not extinguish the balance, and Davies provided no fresh consideration.
options.B: No — the agreement to accept £9,000 is a binding variation supported by the consideration of early payment.
options.C: No — Hollowell is estopped from going back on the promise, as Davies relied on it to his detriment by paying immediately.
options.D: Yes — promissory estoppel can only be used as a shield in existing litigation, not to permanently extinguish a debt.

Contract Law.explanation: C is correct. Promissory estoppel (Central London Property Trust v High Trees House [1947]) prevents Hollowell from going back on its clear promise where Davies acted in reliance on it. The detriment is paying early and restructuring financial plans accordingly. A states the common law rule from Foakes v Beer [1884] correctly but ignores how equity modifies it through promissory estoppel. B is not the right basis — early payment alone may not be fresh consideration for part payment of an existing debt (Re Selectmove [1995]), but estoppel achieves the same outcome. D misstates the doctrine — while estoppel is typically suspensory, the circumstances may make it inequitable to revert; here Davies has fully performed the agreed arrangement.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering owed Bridgewater Developments £20,000 for professional fees under a construction contract. The parties agreed in writing that Hollowell would pay £16,000 in full satisfaction of the debt. Hollowell paid £16,000. Bridgewater later claimed the remaining £4,000. What is the legal position?

options.A: Bridgewater cannot recover — the written agreement constitutes a binding accord and satisfaction.
options.B: Bridgewater can recover — under Foakes v Beer, part payment of an existing debt, even if agreed, does not extinguish the balance without fresh consideration.
options.C: Bridgewater cannot recover — the written form of the agreement satisfies the variation requirements under LP(MP)A 1989.
options.D: Hollowell can rely on promissory estoppel to prevent recovery even without proving detrimental reliance.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. The rule in Foakes v Beer [1884] (affirming Pinnel's Case [1602]) holds that payment of a lesser sum cannot be good consideration for the promise to forgo the balance — Hollowell provided nothing new beyond what it already owed. A is wrong: accord and satisfaction requires fresh consideration, and a mere agreement to accept less without more does not supply it. C is wrong: LP(MP)A 1989 applies to contracts for the sale or disposition of land, not to ordinary debt compromises. D is wrong: promissory estoppel requires detrimental reliance on the promise — merely paying what one already owed is not itself detrimental reliance sufficient to prevent recovery of the balance.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Marta Kowalski set up an online ordering system for Wharton Bakery. A wholesale customer clicked 'Confirm Order' on the website at 11.58pm on Friday for a large catering order. The website displayed: 'Your order is received and will be confirmed by email.' At 9am Saturday, the bakery sent an email stating it could not fulfil the order. When was the contract, if any, formed?

options.A: At 11.58pm Friday — a contract formed when the customer clicked 'Confirm Order', which constitutes acceptance of the bakery's standing offer.
options.B: No contract was formed — the 'Confirm Order' button is only an application to purchase; the contract requires the bakery's confirmation email.
options.C: At 9am Saturday — the bakery's email refusing the order constitutes a counter-offer that the customer must now accept or reject.
options.D: At 11.58pm Friday — the Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002 deem the contract formed at the moment of clicking regardless of any further steps.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Under the Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002, a website displaying goods is typically an invitation to treat, not an offer; the customer's order is the offer and the contract is formed on the trader's acknowledgement of acceptance — not mere receipt notification. The website message 'will be confirmed by email' expressly reserved the right to confirm, meaning no contract was formed at the point of clicking. A wrongly treats the click as acceptance of an offer. C mischaracterises the bakery's refusal — it is a rejection of the customer's offer, not a counter-offer. D misapplies the Regulations, which permit businesses to structure when acceptance occurs.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: In May, Prescott Motors offered to sell a minibus to Rachel Okonkwo for £15,000. Rachel said she would 'think about it.' In November — six months later — Rachel wrote accepting the offer. Prescott refused to proceed. Is there a contract?

options.A: Yes — there is no time limit stated, so the offer remains open indefinitely until expressly revoked.
options.B: No — an offer lapses after a reasonable time, and six months is unreasonable for a commercial transaction of this nature.
options.C: Yes — Rachel's acceptance within the same calendar year cannot constitute lapse.
options.D: No — Rachel's failure to respond immediately constituted a counter-offer by conduct, ending the original offer.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Where no time limit is specified, an offer lapses after a reasonable time (Ramsgate Victoria Hotel v Montefiore [1866]). What is 'reasonable' depends on context — for a commercial vehicle in a fluctuating second-hand market, six months is clearly unreasonable. A is wrong: offers do not remain open indefinitely; lapse by passage of time is a recognised termination event. C applies no recognisable legal rule. D is wrong: silence or delay by the offeree does not constitute a counter-offer — it simply means no acceptance has been communicated.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: James Ashworth agreed to sell his vintage motorcycle to his friend for £1, described in the contract as 'nominal consideration for a valuable asset.' The motorcycle was worth approximately £4,500. James later refused to complete the sale, arguing the consideration was inadequate. Can James avoid the contract on this basis?

options.A: Yes — consideration must be adequate and reflect the true value of what is exchanged.
options.B: No — consideration must be sufficient (real) but need not be adequate; courts will not assess whether it is commercially fair.
options.C: Yes — the gross undervalue means there was no genuine intention to create legal relations.
options.D: No — but James can seek rectification of the contract price to reflect the true market value.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. The rule is that consideration must be sufficient (legally recognisable) but need not be adequate (Thomas v Thomas [1842]; Chappell v Nestlé [1960]). £1 is good consideration — it is real value freely given. Courts do not inquire into the commercial equivalence of the bargain. A incorrectly requires adequacy. C confuses adequacy of consideration with ICLR — the friends may well intend to be bound despite the low price. D: rectification requires a mistake in recording the parties' common intention; here the price was intentional.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Atlas Contracts Ltd was engaged to fit out Wharton Foods' new distribution depot. Midway through the project, Owen Davies told Graham Grange that Atlas would not complete unless Wharton Foods paid an extra £15,000. Grange, having no realistic time to engage another contractor, agreed. Atlas completed the work. Wharton Foods now refuses to pay the extra £15,000. Is the promise to pay enforceable?

options.A: No — Atlas was only doing what it was already obliged to do under the original contract; performing an existing duty is not good consideration.
options.B: Yes — Atlas's continued performance conferred a practical benefit on Wharton Foods, which can constitute good consideration (Williams v Roffey).
options.C: Yes — the agreement to pay extra was a written variation and therefore automatically binding.
options.D: No — the agreement was obtained by economic duress, rendering the promise to pay unenforceable.

Contract Law.explanation: D is correct on the facts as stated. While Williams v Roffey Bros [1991] established that practical benefit can constitute consideration for paying extra under an existing contract, that principle operates only where the agreement to pay more is freely reached. Here, Owen Davies's ultimatum — refusing to complete unless paid more — is economic duress (threatening to breach without legitimate justification). The agreement was not freely entered into. A states the old rule (Stilk v Myrick) which Williams v Roffey partially modified but ignores duress. B correctly states the Williams v Roffey principle but fails to apply the duress analysis. C is wrong: written form does not cure absent or tainted consent.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Priya Mehta entered a written supply contract with a fabric manufacturer. The written contract stated delivery would be 'within 30 days.' Before signing, the manufacturer's representative orally promised Priya that delivery would be within 10 days 'as a priority customer.' Delivery took 25 days, causing Priya to lose a major order. Can Priya rely on the oral promise?

options.A: No — the parol evidence rule prevents any oral agreement made before or at the time of a written contract from adding to or varying its terms.
options.B: Yes — the oral promise can be relied on as a collateral contract, provided it was intended to have legal effect and was the reason Priya entered the main contract.
options.C: Yes — pre-contractual oral statements always take precedence over inconsistent written terms as they reflect the true agreement.
options.D: No — the oral promise was merely a sales puff and cannot form the basis of any contractual or misrepresentation claim.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. The parol evidence rule (option A) is subject to an important exception for collateral contracts. Where one party makes a specific oral promise that induces the other to enter the main contract, and that promise was intended to have contractual effect, it can bind as a collateral contract alongside the written agreement (City and Westminster Properties v Mudd [1959]). Here, the priority delivery promise induced Priya to sign — she can recover the loss caused by the broken collateral promise. A overstates the parol evidence rule — it is not absolute. C is wrong: oral terms do not automatically override written ones; they may be incorporated via collateral contract. D is wrong: a specific factual promise about delivery time is not a mere puff.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Rachel Okonkwo engaged Prescott Motors to service her car under a written contract. A clause stated: 'Prescott Motors accepts no liability whatsoever for damage caused to the vehicle during servicing.' During a routine service, a Prescott employee negligently scratched Rachel's bonnet. Prescott relies on the clause. Can it do so?

options.A: Yes — the clause was included in a written contract, signed by Rachel, and clearly brought to her attention.
options.B: No — under s.65 of the Consumer Rights Act 2015, a trader cannot exclude liability to a consumer for negligence causing loss or damage to property.
options.C: No — only under UCTA 1977, which renders such a clause unreasonable in a consumer contract.
options.D: Yes — the clause is effective because it satisfies the UCTA 1977 reasonableness test for a standard-form consumer contract.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Section 65 of the Consumer Rights Act 2015 (CRA 2015) provides that a trader cannot exclude or restrict liability to a consumer for negligence that causes death, personal injury, or (under s.65(2)) loss or damage to property — subject to the transparency and fairness requirements of Part 2. The clause as drafted is void as against a consumer such as Rachel. C is wrong: UCTA 1977 has been largely superseded by CRA 2015 for B2C contracts. A is wrong: notice and signature are not sufficient to save a clause rendered ineffective by statute. D is wrong: the CRA 2015 reasonableness test in Part 2 applies to other terms, but s.65 establishes a near-absolute exclusion of liability for negligence-caused damage.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd contracted with Atlas Contracts Ltd for the supply of specialist steel frames. The contract contained a clause limiting Atlas's liability for defective goods to £5,000, regardless of the actual loss suffered. The frames were defective and Hollowell suffered £40,000 in consequential losses. Which statutory regime governs this clause?

options.A: Consumer Rights Act 2015 — which applies to all contracts involving a business.
options.B: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 — which applies to business-to-business contracts and subjects the clause to a reasonableness test.
options.C: Consumer Rights Act 2015 Part 2 — which applies to all limitation clauses regardless of the parties.
options.D: Neither statute — limitation clauses in commercial contracts between equal businesses are fully enforceable without restriction.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. UCTA 1977 applies to business-to-business (B2B) contracts. Section 3 of UCTA 1977 subjects liability limitation clauses in standard-form B2B contracts to the reasonableness test under Schedule 2. A is wrong: CRA 2015 applies only where one party is a 'consumer' (individual acting outside their business) and the other is a trader; both Hollowell and Atlas are businesses. C is wrong: CRA 2015 Part 2 applies only to consumer contracts. D is wrong: UCTA 1977 applies and the clause must satisfy reasonableness.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Wharton Foods contracted with a haulage company requiring delivery of perishable goods 'in refrigerated vehicles at no more than 5°C.' On one occasion, the vehicle's refrigeration unit malfunctioned partway through a journey; the goods arrived at 9°C. The goods were slightly warm but perfectly edible and only marginally below specification. Wharton Foods purported to terminate the contract for breach. Can it do so?

options.A: Yes — the temperature specification is a condition, so any breach, however minor, entitles termination.
options.B: No — the temperature specification is a warranty; Wharton Foods can only claim damages, not terminate.
options.C: No — the temperature specification is likely an innominate term; the right to terminate depends on whether the breach substantially deprives Wharton Foods of the whole benefit of the contract.
options.D: Yes — food safety regulations impose a strict duty on temperature compliance, automatically entitling termination for any breach.

Contract Law.explanation: C is correct. Unless the parties have expressly labelled the term a 'condition,' courts apply the test from Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha [1962]: a term is innominate if consequences of breach can range from trivial to severe. The right to terminate arises only if the breach goes to the root of the contract. Here, a marginal temperature breach on a single occasion causing no damage does not substantially deprive Wharton Foods of what it contracted for — termination would be disproportionate. A wrongly classifies the term as a condition without express designation. B wrongly classifies it as a warranty. D: statutory compliance duties do not directly determine contractual termination rights.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: For three years, Hollowell Engineering had ordered steel from the same supplier on standard terms that included a clause excluding liability for late delivery. The terms were printed on the supplier's delivery notes, which Hollowell signed on each occasion. On the fourth year's order, agreed verbally, the supplier delivered four weeks late, causing significant losses. The supplier relies on the exclusion clause. Is it incorporated?

options.A: No — the clause was not on the contract document itself, only on delivery notes received after the contract was formed.
options.B: Yes — the consistent course of dealing over three years means the standard terms, including the exclusion clause, are incorporated into all contracts by course of dealing.
options.C: No — an exclusion clause can only be incorporated if it is specifically drawn to the other party's attention before each contract.
options.D: Yes — but only if the exclusion clause satisfies the UCTA 1977 reasonableness test.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. A consistent course of dealing over a substantial period can incorporate standard terms, including onerous exclusion clauses, into subsequent contracts even without express reference (Hollier v Rambler Motors [1972] — though there the course was insufficient; here three years and multiple signed occasions suffices). Hollowell is a business that has repeatedly signed documents containing the clause. A is wrong: incorporation can operate by prior course of dealing even if the clause was on post-contractual documents, provided the history is consistent and the party had reasonable notice. C is wrong: specific notice per transaction is not required once incorporation by course of dealing is established. D: while UCTA reasonableness is separately relevant, the issue asked is incorporation.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.subtopic: Implied Terms — Business Efficacy / Officious Bystander
Contract Law.stem: Crane & Pelletier Solicitors engaged a building firm to refurbish their offices. The contract specified the scope of works but was silent on what would happen to furniture and equipment during the works. The builders left all furniture in situ, causing significant damage to computer equipment from dust. Crane & Pelletier claims the contract included an implied term to protect equipment. On what basis might the court imply such a term?

options.A: Under the officious bystander test — both parties would have agreed it went without saying that equipment would be protected.
options.B: Under the business efficacy test — the contract cannot work at all without the implied term.
options.C: As a term implied by statute — the Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982 imposes a duty of reasonable care.
options.D: A is the correct primary basis, supported by C — the officious bystander test applies to fill gaps, and the statutory duty of care reinforces the analysis.

Contract Law.explanation: D is correct. The officious bystander test (Shirlaw v Southern Foundries [1939]) implies terms that 'go without saying' — both parties would obviously have agreed to protect equipment if asked. That is the primary basis here. Additionally, s.13 of the Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982 implies a term that a service provider will exercise reasonable care and skill — which itself encompasses taking care of the client's property. B is wrong on its own: business efficacy implies terms necessary to make the contract work, not merely convenient ones; the contract could technically work (badly) without the term. A alone is incomplete as it ignores the statutory route. D correctly combines both.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Rachel Okonkwo bought a new laptop from an electronics retailer for £1,200 for personal use. After five weeks, the screen developed a permanent fault. The retailer offered a repair. Rachel refused, insisting on a refund. Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015, what are Rachel's rights?

options.A: Rachel must accept a repair — a refund is only available if the repair fails.
options.B: Rachel is entitled to a full refund within 30 days of purchase without needing to give the trader a chance to repair.
options.C: Rachel must first allow one repair attempt; if that fails, she may claim a price reduction or replacement but not a full refund.
options.D: Rachel is entitled to a final right to reject — giving a full refund — because the short-term right to reject (30 days) has expired and the repair option applies first.

Contract Law.explanation: D is correct. Under CRA 2015, the short-term right to reject under s.20 is available for 30 days from delivery for goods that are not of satisfactory quality. Rachel's fault appeared at five weeks — just outside the 30-day window. After 30 days, the consumer must first allow one repair or replacement (s.23). If that first repair fails (or is disproportionate), the consumer can then exercise the final right to reject under s.24 (which may include a deduction for use). B is wrong: the 30-day short-term right has expired. A is wrong: it suggests repair is always the first remedy — correct post-30 days — but misstates the consequence of repair failure. C is incorrect in excluding a refund as a possible outcome after failed repair.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Neville Prescott told James Ashworth that a van he was selling had 'only had one owner and never been in an accident.'
