In fact, Prescott knew the van had had three owners and had been in a recorded collision. James bought the van in reliance on these statements. On discovering the truth, James seeks rescission and damages. Under which head of misrepresentation can James recover damages as of right without needing to prove loss was foreseeable?

options.A: Innocent misrepresentation under s.2(2) of the Misrepresentation Act 1967.
options.B: Negligent misrepresentation under s.2(1) of the Misrepresentation Act 1967.
options.C: Fraudulent misrepresentation under the tort of deceit (Derry v Peek).
options.D: Both B and C — damages under both s.2(1) MA 1967 and the tort of deceit allow recovery without foreseeability.

Contract Law.explanation: D is correct. Both fraudulent misrepresentation (Derry v Peek [1889]) and negligent misrepresentation under s.2(1) MA 1967 allow recovery of all consequential losses without the remoteness limitation of the tort of negligence — foreseeability is not required. Under s.2(1), the 'fiction of fraud' means the representee is treated as if the representation were fraudulent for the purposes of the damages measure (Royscot Trust v Rogerson [1991]). James can claim under either or both heads. A is wrong: innocent misrepresentation under s.2(2) gives the court discretion to award damages in lieu of rescission only, and foreseeability still applies. B and C alone each correctly state the rule but D is more complete.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Priya Mehta entered a fabric supply contract after the supplier made an innocent misrepresentation about the fabric's fire-resistance rating. Six months after discovering the truth, Priya continued to order fabric from the same supplier under the same terms and only sought rescission eight months after discovery. The supplier argues rescission is no longer available. Is the supplier correct?

options.A: No — the right to rescind for innocent misrepresentation is unlimited in time once the misrepresentation is discovered.
options.B: Yes — Priya has affirmed the contract by continuing to perform after discovering the misrepresentation.
options.C: No — rescission is available as a matter of right for any misrepresentation; affirmation is irrelevant.
options.D: Yes — but only because more than six months have passed since discovery, which is the statutory limitation period for rescission.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Affirmation is one of the recognised bars to rescission. A party who, with knowledge of the misrepresentation, continues to perform the contract or acts consistently with treating it as valid affirms it and thereby loses the right to rescind (Long v Lloyd [1958]). Priya continued ordering fabric from the same supplier on the same terms after discovering the truth — this constitutes affirmation. A is wrong: lapse of time and affirmation both bar rescission even post-discovery. C is wrong: affirmation, lapse of time, third-party rights, and restitutio in integrum impossibility all bar rescission. D is wrong: there is no six-month statutory limitation period for rescission specifically.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Owen Davies agreed to pay Prescott Motors £2,000 to help him repaint a van so it could be passed off as a different vehicle to defraud Davies's insurer. Prescott completed the work but Davies refused to pay. Prescott sues for the £2,000. What is the outcome?

options.A: Prescott can recover — it performed the work and is entitled to payment regardless of how the van is used.
options.B: Prescott cannot recover — the contract was formed to carry out an illegal purpose (insurance fraud) and is unenforceable.
options.C: Prescott can recover — only Davies is the wrongdoer; Prescott's participation was innocent as a service provider.
options.D: Prescott cannot recover the contractual price but may recover the reasonable value of its services in quantum meruit.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. A contract formed for an illegal purpose — here, facilitating insurance fraud — is void on grounds of illegality (ex turpi causa non oritur actio). The courts will not assist a party seeking to enforce a contract whose performance requires or promotes an illegal act. A is wrong: the nature of the purpose taints the contract regardless of performance. C is wrong: Prescott's knowledge of the illegal purpose (repainting to defraud the insurer) means it is a knowing participant, not an innocent party. D is wrong: quantum meruit is equally unavailable where both parties participated knowingly in the illegal purpose (Parkinson v College of Ambulance [1925]).

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A fraudster impersonating 'Graham Grange of Wharton Foods' contacted Prescott Motors by email, negotiating to buy a fleet of vans on credit. Prescott, believing it was dealing with the well-known local businessman, agreed. The vans were delivered and the fraudster sold them to an innocent third-party buyer. When the fraud was discovered, Prescott sought to recover the vans or their value from the third party. What is the legal position?

options.A: Prescott can recover — the contract with the fraudster was void for unilateral mistake as to identity, so the fraudster had no title to pass to the third party.
options.B: Prescott cannot recover — where parties contract at a distance, there is a presumption the offeror intends to contract with whoever responds, making the contract voidable, not void.
options.C: Prescott can recover — the Consumer Rights Act 2015 protects sellers where fraud is involved.
options.D: Prescott cannot recover — the third-party buyer's good faith defeats any title claim by Prescott.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Following Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003], where parties contract at a distance (here by email), the identity of the contracting party is usually that of whoever is dealing — the fraudster. The contract is therefore voidable for fraudulent misrepresentation, not void for mistake. A voidable contract is valid until rescinded; the fraudster could pass a voidable title to the third party before rescission, and the third party's good faith purchase under s.23 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979 defeats Prescott's claim. A applies the rule from Cundy v Lindsay [1878] (face-to-face / written contracts with known third party) but Shogun distinguishes distance dealings. C: the CRA 2015 is irrelevant. D reaches the right conclusion but misstates the mechanism — the third party is protected because the fraudster had voidable (not void) title.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Betty Huxley, aged 82 and in poor health, charged her home to secure a personal loan taken by her granddaughter. Gerald Crane, acting for both Betty and the lender, did not advise Betty independently about the risks. Betty's granddaughter chose the property for her. Betty now seeks to set the charge aside on grounds of undue influence by her granddaughter. What must Betty establish?

options.A: Betty must prove actual undue influence — specific acts of pressure or coercion by her granddaughter.
options.B: Betty can rely on a presumption of undue influence arising from the grandmother-granddaughter relationship, and must show the transaction calls for explanation.
options.C: Betty must show that the transaction was manifestly to her disadvantage and that there was no independent legal advice.
options.D: Betty can only set aside the charge if the lender had actual knowledge of the granddaughter's undue influence.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Class 2B undue influence arises where the relationship between the parties gives rise to a presumption of influence (not limited to the fixed categories in Class 2A). A close family relationship between an elderly vulnerable person and a younger relative can raise such a presumption (Etridge (No 2) [2001]). Betty must show: (1) a relationship in which one placed trust and confidence in the other, and (2) a transaction that calls for explanation (is not readily explicable by ordinary motives). A requires proof of actual pressure — that is Class 1 undue influence. C overstates the requirements (manifest disadvantage is relevant but not a standalone test post-Etridge). D is relevant to the lender's position but not to what Betty must establish against her granddaughter.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Atlas Contracts Ltd agreed to fit out Priya Mehta's expanded workshop, with work to start on 1 March. On 10 February, Owen Davies told Priya: 'We won't be doing the job — we've taken on something bigger.' Priya immediately engaged another contractor at a higher cost. Can Priya claim for the extra cost without waiting until 1 March?

options.A: No — Priya must wait until 1 March to see if Atlas actually fails to perform before she can sue.
options.B: Yes — Owen's statement is an anticipatory breach that Priya is entitled to accept immediately, treating the contract as discharged and claiming damages.
options.C: No — Priya must first serve a notice to perform under the contract before bringing a claim.
options.D: Yes — but only if Priya can show she made reasonable efforts to persuade Atlas to reconsider before engaging another contractor.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. An unequivocal statement by one party that it will not perform constitutes an anticipatory breach (Hochster v De la Tour [1853]). The innocent party may accept the breach immediately, treat the contract as discharged, and sue for damages — they need not wait until the performance date. Priya was entitled to engage a replacement contractor at once and claim the extra cost as damages. A is the position if Priya elected not to accept the anticipatory breach — she can wait, but she is not required to. C: no notice to perform is required in this context. D is wrong: there is no rule requiring persuasion attempts before accepting anticipatory breach.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Atlas Contracts fitted out The Malt & Mattock for Frank Hollis under a contract worth £30,000. On completion, Atlas demanded full payment. Frank identified 14 defects, mostly cosmetic, costing approximately £1,800 to remedy. Frank refused to pay anything, arguing Atlas had not completed the works. Is Frank entitled to withhold payment entirely?

options.A: Yes — the contract is an entire contract, and Atlas must complete perfectly before being entitled to any payment.
options.B: No — Atlas has substantially performed the contract and Frank must pay the contract price less the cost of remedying the defects (£28,200).
options.C: Yes — the 14 defects mean the contract has not been completed, entitling Frank to treat the contract as still executory.
options.D: No — Frank must pay the full £30,000 and bring a separate claim for the cost of remedying the defects.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. The doctrine of substantial performance (Hoenig v Isaacs [1952]) permits recovery of the contract price where the work has been substantially completed, subject to a deduction for the cost of completing or remedying minor defects. Here, Atlas completed the main works; 14 mostly cosmetic defects costing £1,800 on a £30,000 contract do not prevent substantial performance. Frank must pay £28,200. A is wrong: the substantial performance doctrine qualifies the harshness of the entire obligations rule. C is wrong: minor defects do not entitle the innocent party to treat the contract as unperformed. D is wrong: Frank is entitled to deduct the remedy cost rather than pay in full and counter-sue.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: James Ashworth contracted to manage a corporate event for Hollowell Engineering at a venue on 15 September. In August, James accepted another booking for the same date that paid more, making himself unavailable. He told Hollowell the event was 'frustrated by a scheduling conflict beyond his control.' Can James claim the contract is frustrated?

options.A: Yes — supervening impossibility caused by a scheduling conflict is a recognised frustrating event.
options.B: No — frustration cannot be self-induced; where the event is caused by the party's own act or choice, the doctrine does not apply.
options.C: Yes — frustration applies whenever performance becomes impossible, regardless of the reason.
options.D: No — James should have served a force majeure notice within a reasonable time to preserve the right to claim frustration.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. The doctrine of frustration does not apply where the frustrating event is caused or contributed to by one of the parties — it must be truly supervening and outside the parties' control (Maritime National Fish v Ocean Trawlers [1935]). James's scheduling conflict is entirely self-induced — he chose to accept a competing booking. He cannot rely on frustration. A applies the correct test but ignores self-inducement. C is too wide — impossibility caused by the party's own choice is not a frustrating event. D: force majeure is a contractual mechanism and irrelevant where frustration is unavailable as a matter of law.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Atlas Contracts failed to deliver specialist timber for Priya Mehta's shopfront refurbishment by the agreed date. The delay was three weeks. Priya had a bespoke order from a large retailer that depended on completing the refurbishment by a certain date. She lost that order, worth £25,000. At the time of contracting, Atlas did not know about the retailer order. Can Priya recover the £25,000?

options.A: Yes — all losses flowing directly from Atlas's breach are recoverable.
options.B: No — the loss of the retailer order is not in the reasonable contemplation of Atlas as a likely consequence of late delivery, as Atlas had no knowledge of that special circumstance.
options.C: Yes — but only if Priya can show that Atlas would have accepted the risk had it known about the retailer order.
options.D: No — Priya must claim under her insurance policy before seeking contract damages.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Under Hadley v Baxendale [1854], damages are limited to losses that were in the reasonable contemplation of both parties at the time of contracting as a probable consequence of the breach. The second limb covers special losses only where the defendant had knowledge of the special circumstances. Atlas did not know about the retailer order, so the £25,000 loss of that order is not recoverable under the contract — it falls outside both limbs. A is wrong: not all direct losses are recoverable — remoteness limits recovery. C conflates knowledge with consent, which is not required. D is wrong: insurance is not a prerequisite to contractual recovery.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Neville Prescott failed to deliver a van he had contracted to sell to Donna Pearce for £12,000. Identical vans were available from three other dealers in Northampton at £12,500 each. Donna, upset by Prescott's conduct, declined to buy a replacement and instead temporarily hired vans for four months at a total cost of £6,000, then claimed that total from Prescott. How much can Donna reasonably recover?

options.A: The full £6,000 hire cost — this is the actual loss she suffered.
options.B: Only the £500 difference between the contract price and the cost of a replacement van — she was obliged to mitigate by buying a replacement.
options.C: Nothing — Donna failed entirely to mitigate and cannot recover any loss.
options.D: The £6,000 hire costs minus any hire costs she would have incurred even if the van had been delivered.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. The duty to mitigate requires the claimant to take reasonable steps to reduce their loss (British Westinghouse v Underground Electric Railways [1912]). Replacement vans were readily available at a £500 premium — Donna was required to take that reasonable step. She cannot recover losses she could easily have avoided. The measure of damages is the additional cost of mitigation: £500. A is wrong: failing to mitigate prevents recovery of avoidable losses. C is wrong: total failure would only apply where no loss at all was unavoidable. D introduces a complexity not relevant here.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Atlas Contracts' contract with Hollowell Engineering contained a clause: 'For every week of delay in completion beyond the agreed date, Atlas shall pay £5,000 to Hollowell.' Atlas was six weeks late. Hollowell's actual loss from the delay was £8,000. Atlas argues the clause is an unenforceable penalty. Is Atlas correct?

options.A: Yes — the clause is a penalty because the stipulated sum (£30,000) greatly exceeds Hollowell's actual loss (£8,000).
options.B: No — under Cavendish Square v Makdessi [2015], the clause will be enforceable if it represents a legitimate business interest and is not extravagant or unconscionable.
options.C: Yes — any liquidated damages clause that produces a sum greater than actual loss is void as a penalty.
options.D: No — the clause is enforceable because Hollowell, as a commercial party, is presumed to understand the clause and accepted the commercial risk.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Cavendish Square Holding v Makdessi [2015] reformulated the penalty clause test: a clause is not a penalty merely because it exceeds actual loss. The question is whether it represents a legitimate business interest and is not extravagant or unconscionable having regard to that interest. Hollowell has a legitimate interest in timely completion (business disruption, production delays, subcontractor commitments). Whether £5,000 per week is proportionate is a factual question — it is not automatically unenforceable. A applies the old Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre test (now superseded) which focused on genuine pre-estimate of loss. C overstates the rule. D is relevant as context but not the primary test.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: James Ashworth contracted to sell a rare vintage motorbike — one of only 12 known to exist in this condition — to a collector for £18,000. He then sold it to a third party for £22,000. The collector seeks specific performance. Is this remedy available?

options.A: No — specific performance is not available for the sale of personal property, only for land.
options.B: Yes — specific performance may be granted for unique chattels where damages are not an adequate remedy.
options.C: No — the motorbike has already been transferred to a third party; specific performance is therefore impossible.
options.D: Yes — but only if the collector can prove James acted in bad faith by accepting a higher offer.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct as a statement of principle. Specific performance is available for contracts involving unique goods where money damages would be inadequate (Cohen v Roche [1927] distinguished — Heirloom items and truly unique goods justify specific performance). A vintage motorbike of which only 12 exist in this condition is likely sufficiently unique. However, C raises a practical point: if the third party now has legal title (having purchased for value without notice), the court may be unable to order specific performance against James directly — damages would be the remedy instead. On the bare principle of availability, B is correct. C describes a real-world complication but does not defeat B as the answer to availability as a matter of principle.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Alek Kowalski entered a catering contract for a large corporate event. In preparation, he incurred pre-contractual costs of £3,000 (promotional materials, specialist ingredients). The other party cancelled in breach. Kowalski cannot prove what profit he would have made. Can he recover the £3,000?

options.A: No — pre-contractual expenditure is not recoverable; damages are limited to post-formation losses.
options.B: Yes — reliance damages cover wasted expenditure incurred in anticipation of performance of the contract, even if incurred before the contract was formed.
options.C: No — Kowalski must prove his expected profit; without that evidence, he recovers nothing.
options.D: Yes — but only the proportion of expenditure incurred after the contract was signed.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Reliance damages allow recovery of wasted expenditure incurred in anticipation of a contract's performance, including pre-contractual expenditure reasonably incurred after the contract was entered — or incurred in preparation for it that was within the parties' reasonable contemplation (Anglia Television v Reed [1972]). The innocent party may elect between expectation damages (profit) and reliance damages (wasted expenditure) when expectation damages are uncertain or unquantifiable. A is wrong: Anglia Television confirmed pre-contractual expenditure is recoverable under reliance measure. C is wrong: when profit cannot be proved, the claimant may elect the reliance measure. D is incorrect: Anglia Television extended recovery to pre-formation expenditure within reasonable contemplation.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Atlas Contracts began fitting out new offices for Hollowell Engineering. After completing half the work (estimated value £14,000), Hollowell wrongfully terminated the contract. No price had been agreed for partial completion. Owen Davies claims payment for the work done. On what basis can Atlas recover?

options.A: Atlas cannot recover — no price was agreed for partial completion, so no contractual claim arises.
options.B: Atlas can recover on a quantum meruit basis — the reasonable value of the work done — because Hollowell's wrongful termination prevents Atlas from completing the contract.
options.C: Atlas can recover the full contract price as damages for Hollowell's wrongful termination.
options.D: Atlas can only recover if it had substantially completed the contract before termination.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Where a contract is wrongfully terminated by the other party before completion, the innocent party can claim a quantum meruit — the reasonable value of work done — as an alternative to or in addition to contractual damages. This rests on the unjust enrichment principle: Hollowell has received the benefit of half the works and should not be unjustly enriched. A is wrong: absence of an agreed partial price does not prevent quantum meruit recovery. C is wrong: the full contract price would overcompensate; Atlas can claim the difference between the contract price and its remaining costs of performance, or quantum meruit. D applies the substantial performance doctrine, which is a different rule for completed (rather than terminated) contracts.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering contracted with Atlas Contracts for the construction of a new loading bay. The contract stated: 'This agreement is made for the benefit of Hollowell's subtenants at Unit 4 who shall have the right to use the loading bay.' One subtenant, whose name was not in the contract, suffered loss when Atlas failed to complete. Can the subtenant enforce the contract against Atlas?

options.A: No — only named third parties can enforce a contract under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999.
options.B: Yes — s.1(1)(b) of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 permits enforcement by a third party where the contract purports to confer a benefit on them, provided they are identified as a member of a class.
options.C: No — the subtenant is not a party to the contract and privity of contract prevents any enforcement right.
options.D: Yes — but only if the subtenant can demonstrate they have provided consideration to Hollowell under the head lease.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Section 1(1)(b) of the C(RTP)A 1999 provides that a third party may enforce a contractual term where the contract purports to confer a benefit on them. Section 1(3) requires the third party to be expressly identified by name, as a member of a class, or answering a particular description. 'Hollowell's subtenants at Unit 4' identifies a class — the subtenant qualifies. A is wrong: identification by name is not required; class membership suffices under s.1(3). C states the common law privity rule but ignores the statutory exception. D is wrong: the C(RTP)A 1999 specifically removes the consideration requirement for third party enforcement.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering assigned the benefit of a debt owed to it by Atlas Contracts (£20,000 for completed steelwork) to a finance company, giving written notice to Atlas. Atlas refused to pay the finance company, arguing it had not consented to the assignment. Is Atlas correct?

options.A: Yes — the benefit of a contract cannot be assigned without the debtor's consent.
options.B: No — the benefit of a contract (such as a debt) can be assigned without the other party's consent; only the burden requires consent.
options.C: No — provided written notice was given, Atlas must pay the finance company and has no ground to refuse.
options.D: Yes — Atlas can refuse because the assignment changes the nature of the obligation it originally undertook.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. The benefit of a contract — including a right to receive payment — can be assigned without the debtor's consent (Tolhurst v Associated Portland Cement [1902]). Once valid written notice of the legal assignment is given under s.136 Law of Property Act 1925, the assignee (finance company) acquires the right to sue in its own name. Atlas must pay the finance company. A is wrong: assignment of benefit does not require consent; only the burden of a contract cannot be assigned unilaterally. C is broadly correct but B is the more precise legal basis. D is wrong: the assignment does not change Atlas's obligation — it still pays the same sum for the same work.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Wharton Foods contracts with Atlas Contracts for fit-out works. Partway through, Atlas wishes to transfer all its obligations under the contract to a different construction firm. Graham Grange refuses to agree. Without Grange's consent, can Atlas transfer its contractual obligations?

options.A: Yes — the burden of a contract can always be transferred by written assignment.
options.B: No — the burden of a contract cannot be transferred without the consent of the other party; novation requires all parties' agreement.
options.C: Yes — Atlas can delegate performance of its obligations to a subcontractor without novation.
options.D: No — Atlas must apply to court for a substitution order before transferring its obligations.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Unlike the benefit, the burden of a contract cannot be assigned unilaterally — this would allow a party to escape its obligations without the counterparty's agreement. To transfer both benefit and burden to a third party, a novation is required: a tripartite agreement where the original contract is extinguished and replaced by a new one between the creditor and the new obligor. Grange must consent. A is wrong: burdens cannot be transferred by mere assignment. C is a partial point — delegation of performance is permitted but Atlas remains liable to Grange if the delegate fails; delegation is not the same as transferring the burden. D: no such court procedure exists for commercial contracts.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Neville Prescott supplied a defective vehicle to Alek Kowalski under a written commercial contract. The defect became apparent to Kowalski after five years and six months. Kowalski brings a breach of contract claim. Is the claim time-barred?

options.A: Yes — the Limitation Act 1980 provides a three-year limitation period for breach of contract claims.
options.B: No — the Limitation Act 1980 provides a six-year limitation period for simple contract claims; the claim was brought within time.
options.C: Yes — the six-year period runs from when the defect was discovered, and five years six months is within time.
options.D: No — for contracts for sale of goods, the limitation period is ten years from delivery.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Section 5 of the Limitation Act 1980 provides a six-year limitation period for simple (non-deed) contract claims, running from the date of breach (not discovery). The breach occurred at or around delivery of the defective vehicle. If Kowalski brings his claim within six years of that breach, it is within time. Five years and six months is within the six-year period. A is wrong: three years is the personal injury limitation period, not for contract. C confuses the accrual date — for breach of contract the period runs from breach, not discovery (unless latent damage provisions apply under the Latent Damage Act 1986, which covers negligence, not contract). D is wrong: no ten-year period applies to sale of goods contracts.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Prescott Motors agreed to sell a fleet of vans to Northgate Couriers with delivery 'on or before 30 April, time being of the essence.' Prescott delivered one day late, on 1 May. Donna Pearce refused to accept the vans and cancelled the contract. Prescott argues the one-day delay is trivial. Is Donna entitled to reject?

options.A: No — a one-day delay is so minor that it cannot justify rejection; Donna must accept and claim nominal damages.
options.B: Yes — where time is expressly made of the essence, any failure to perform by the stipulated date entitles the innocent party to treat the contract as repudiated.
options.C: Yes — but only if Donna first served a notice making time of the essence before the original deadline.
options.D: No — 'time of the essence' clauses only apply to land contracts, not contracts for the sale of goods.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Where the parties have expressly agreed that time is of the essence, the deadline is a condition of the contract. Any breach, however minor, entitles the innocent party to terminate — the court will not inquire into whether the breach was trivial (Bunge Corporation v Tradax Export [1981]). Donna is entitled to reject. A is wrong: the express 'time of the essence' designation makes the timeline a condition, removing any argument about triviality. C: a 'notice making time of the essence' is needed where time was not originally of the essence and a party needs to set a new deadline — not required here where it was originally express. D is wrong: 'time of the essence' can be express in any commercial contract.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A supply contract between Wharton Foods and a packaging supplier contained a force majeure clause: 'Neither party shall be liable for failure to perform due to circumstances beyond its reasonable control, including acts of God, government action, or industrial action.' A lorry drivers' strike prevented delivery for three weeks. Graham Grange seeks damages for the delay. Can he succeed?

options.A: Yes — force majeure clauses are void under UCTA 1977 in commercial contracts.
options.B: No — an industrial strike (lorry drivers' action) falls within 'industrial action' in the clause; the supplier is protected from liability.
options.C: No — the doctrine of frustration automatically discharges the contract, making the force majeure clause redundant.
options.D: Yes — force majeure only covers permanent inability to perform; a temporary three-week delay does not trigger the clause.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Force majeure clauses operate as a matter of contract rather than common law doctrine. Courts interpret them strictly according to their wording (Channel Island Ferries v Sealink [1988]). 'Industrial action' clearly covers a lorry drivers' strike — the supplier's performance was prevented by a circumstance expressly within the clause. The supplier is not liable for the delay. A is wrong: force majeure clauses in B2B contracts are not automatically void under UCTA 1977; they are subject to the reasonableness test only in some contexts. C is wrong: force majeure and frustration are different mechanisms; a force majeure clause addresses agreed risk allocation and does not require the contract to be frustrated. D is wrong: force majeure clauses typically apply to temporary as well as permanent events unless the clause says otherwise.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Prescott Motors sold a car to Rachel Okonkwo after innocently (but incorrectly) representing that it had a full-service history. The car had in fact missed two services. The car is otherwise in excellent condition and the missing services make no material difference to its value or safety. Rachel seeks rescission and return of her £11,000. The court exercises its discretion under s.2(2) Misrepresentation Act 1967. What is the likely outcome?

options.A: The court must order rescission — rescission is an automatic right for misrepresentation and cannot be denied.
options.B: The court awards damages in lieu of rescission — rescission would be inequitable given the minor nature of the misrepresentation and the absence of any material loss.
options.C: The court awards nothing — innocent misrepresentation carries no remedy if no loss is proved.
options.D: The court orders rescission plus damages — both remedies are available simultaneously for innocent misrepresentation.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Section 2(2) of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 gives the court a discretion to award damages in lieu of rescission for innocent or negligent misrepresentation where it would be equitable to do so, having regard to the nature of the misrepresentation, the loss caused, and the position of the parties if rescission were ordered. Here, the misrepresentation was innocent, the car is otherwise excellent, and the missing services cause no material loss — rescission of an £11,000 purchase would be disproportionate. A is wrong: rescission is not automatic; s.2(2) specifically creates a judicial discretion. C is wrong: s.2(2) allows damages as a substitute remedy even without provable loss. D is wrong: s.2(2) awards damages in lieu of (replacing) rescission, not in addition to it.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Atlas Contracts, knowing Hollowell Engineering was in the final week before a critical manufacturing deadline, threatened to stop all site work unless Hollowell paid an uncontested additional £30,000 for 'increased material costs' not provided for in the contract. Hollowell paid under protest and immediately instructed Crane & Pelletier. What must Hollowell establish to recover the £30,000?

options.A: That it had no practical alternative to payment, and that the threat amounted to illegitimate pressure that was a significant cause of the payment.
options.B: That the threat was accompanied by violence or the threat of physical harm.
options.C: That Hollowell lacked independent legal advice at the time of payment.
options.D: That the £30,000 was not a genuine pre-estimate of increased material costs.

Contract Law.explanation: A is correct. Economic duress requires: (1) illegitimate pressure (a threat to breach a contract without lawful justification is illegitimate — DSND Subsea v Petroleum Geo-Services [2000]); (2) that the pressure was a significant cause of the claimant's decision to pay; and (3) that there was no reasonable practical alternative (protesting and paying immediately preserves the right to recover). Hollowell's payment under protest is strong evidence. B is wrong: duress of the person (violence or threats of violence) is a separate, older doctrine — economic duress does not require physical threat. C is wrong: independent legal advice is relevant to undue influence, not duress. D is irrelevant to the duress analysis.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: James Ashworth contracted to enter a classic car for a prestigious auction managed by a Northampton auction house. Due to the auction house's breach, the car was withdrawn from the auction at the last minute. Evidence showed the car had a 65% chance of selling at its reserve price of £25,000, and a 20% chance of selling at £35,000. James claims £25,000. What is the correct measure of his damages?

options.A: £25,000 — James would have received the reserve price and that is the value of his loss.
options.B: Nothing — damages for loss of chance are too speculative to recover.
options.C: A sum reflecting the value of the lost chance — calculated by reference to the probability of each outcome occurring.
options.D: £35,000 — the highest possible sale price, to put James in the best possible position.

Contract Law.explanation: C is correct. Where a claimant loses a chance to achieve a financial outcome, damages are assessed as the value of the chance itself — not on an all-or-nothing basis (Chaplin v Hicks [1911]; Allied Maples Group v Simmons & Simmons [1995]). The court values the probability-weighted outcomes: (65% × £25,000) + (20% × £35,000) = £16,250 + £7,000 = £23,250 (simplified — the full analysis also considers costs and the probability of the car not selling at all). The key principle is that James recovers for the lost prospect at its assessed probability. A awards the full reserve price as if sale were certain. B is wrong: loss of a chance is a recognised head of damage. D overclaims by ignoring probability.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Bridgewater Developments and Wharton Magna District Council signed a document headed 'Heads of Terms' stating the parties would work together to develop the old goods yard, with 'all terms to be finalised in a formal agreement to be agreed.' No formal agreement was ever signed. Marcus Bridgewater spent £120,000 on planning and survey work. Is there an enforceable contract?

options.A: Yes — the Heads of Terms and subsequent expenditure demonstrate a binding intention to contract.
options.B: No — the document expressly contemplates a future formal agreement; without it, there is no binding contract (subject to contract).
options.C: Yes — Bridgewater's reliance expenditure of £120,000 creates a binding obligation on the council through proprietary estoppel.
options.D: No — local authorities cannot enter binding contracts without full council approval under statutory procedures.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Where the parties have expressly contemplated that their arrangement will only become binding on execution of a formal agreement, neither is bound until that agreement is signed (Winn v Bull [1877]; RTS Flexible Systems v Molkerei Alois Müller [2010] explains when this can be rebutted). The 'subject to contract' principle applies — the Heads of Terms are not a binding contract. A is wrong: expenditure in anticipation of a contract does not create one. C is wrong: proprietary estoppel applies to property rights, not to commercial contract situations of this kind; there is no representation that a contract exists. D raises a valid public law point but is not the primary contractual reason.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering's standard-form supply contract with a new customer contained a clause limiting its liability for defective goods to the contract price (£50,000). The customer suffered losses of £200,000 from defective goods. The clause had not been specifically negotiated. In assessing reasonableness under UCTA 1977 Schedule 2, which factor most strongly supports enforceability of the clause?

options.A: The clause was printed in small font and not drawn to the customer's attention.
options.B: The customer is a business that could have sought insurance against this risk, and similar limitation clauses are common in the industry.
options.C: The customer had no realistic alternative supplier for the goods.
options.D: The limitation amount (£50,000) bears no relation to the likely loss in the event of defective goods.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Schedule 2 of UCTA 1977 sets out guidelines for reasonableness. Relevant factors include: the parties' bargaining positions; whether the customer received an inducement to agree; whether the customer knew or ought to have known of the term (custom/trade practice); whether the goods were specially manufactured; and the practicability of insurance. Availability of insurance to the customer and industry standard practice both support enforceability — if the customer could reasonably have insured against this type of loss, the clause is more likely reasonable. A points to a factor that would undermine enforceability (poor notice). C (no alternative supplier) would suggest weaker bargaining position — against enforceability. D suggests the limit is disproportionately low — against enforceability.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.subtopic: Acting for Both Parties — Conflict of Interest
Contract Law.stem: Gerald Crane is approached by both Priya Mehta and her landlord Marcus Bridgewater to advise them on the terms of a new commercial lease for Stitched Up's premises. There is already a dispute about the service charge. Under the SRA Code of Conduct for Solicitors, can Gerald act for both?

options.A: Yes — provided both clients give informed consent in writing, a solicitor may always act for both parties to a commercial lease negotiation.
options.B: No — there is a current, actual conflict of interest between the parties on the service charge dispute; Gerald must not act for both.
options.C: Yes — commercial property transactions are expressly exempt from the conflict of interest rules under the SRA Code.
options.D: No — solicitors can never act for landlord and tenant in any lease transaction, even without a dispute.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. The SRA Code of Conduct (para 6.2) provides that a solicitor must not act for two or more clients where there is a conflict of interest or a significant risk of one arising. Here, there is a live dispute on the service charge — this is an actual conflict, not a mere risk. Informed consent cannot cure an actual conflict (only a 'significant risk' of conflict can be managed with consent under a narrow exception). A is wrong: consent cannot override an actual conflict. C is wrong: there is no commercial property exemption. D overstates the rule — acting for both on a non-contentious lease is permissible in some circumstances if conflicts are properly managed, but not here.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Ravi Sharma, a trainee at Crane & Pelletier, discovers during a commercial contract matter for Hollowell Engineering that the other party (Atlas Contracts) has just entered administration — information not yet public. Crane & Pelletier also acts for a third-party creditor of Atlas Contracts on an unrelated matter. Can Ravi share the administration information with the firm's other client?

options.A: Yes — information obtained in the course of one matter can always be shared between clients of the same firm if it is relevant to them.
options.B: No — the duty of confidentiality under the SRA Code prevents sharing confidential information about one client with another, even within the same firm.
options.C: Yes — because the information was obtained through a commercial transaction, the duty of confidentiality does not apply.
options.D: No — but Ravi may share the information with Diana Pelletier as supervising partner, who must then decide.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. The SRA Code of Conduct (para 6.3–6.4) imposes a strict duty of confidentiality — a solicitor must keep confidential information relating to a client's matter, even after the retainer ends, unless the client consents or disclosure is required by law. Information about Atlas's administration obtained via Hollowell's instructions cannot be used to benefit another client (the creditor). This would be a breach of confidentiality and misuse of information. A is wrong: sharing confidential client information without consent is a breach of the Code. C is wrong: there is no commercial transaction exception to confidentiality. D is wrong: escalation to a supervisor does not resolve the confidentiality issue — it remains confidential.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.subtopic: Duty to Advise on Consumer Rights
Contract Law.stem: Diana Pelletier is instructed by Prescott Motors to draft standard-form terms of sale for vehicles sold to members of the public. She includes a clause excluding liability for any defects discovered after 14 days of purchase. Under her professional duties, what must Diana do before finalising the terms?

options.A: Include the clause as instructed — a solicitor's role is to carry out client instructions without question.
options.B: Advise Prescott Motors that the clause is likely to be void under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 as against consumers, and that including it could constitute a misleading practice.
options.C: Refuse the instruction — including potentially unlawful clauses means the firm cannot act for Prescott Motors.
options.D: Include the clause but add a disclaimer noting that its validity is uncertain.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. A solicitor's duty to act in the client's best interests (SRA Code para 1.2) includes advising the client when instructions would expose them to legal risk. A clause purporting to exclude liability for defects after 14 days in a consumer contract directly conflicts with the Consumer Rights Act 2015 (ss.9–11 require goods to be of satisfactory quality; s.20 gives a 30-day short-term right to reject; s.65 limits exclusion of liability for negligence). Such a clause is void as against consumers. Diana must advise Prescott that the clause is unlawful and risks regulatory action or claims — acting blindly on the instruction would breach her duty. A is wrong: the duty to follow instructions is qualified by the duty to advise on legal risk. C is wrong: Diana need not refuse; she must advise. D is inadequate and potentially misleading.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Owen Davies instructs Diana Pelletier to draft a commercial contract which, on Diana's analysis, is structured to allow Atlas Contracts to misrepresent completion dates to an insurer in order to trigger an early payment under a performance bond. Diana is concerned the contract is designed to commit fraud. What should Diana do?

options.A: Draft the contract as instructed — it is for the court, not the solicitor, to determine whether a contract is illegal.
options.B: Refuse to continue with the matter, as a solicitor must not assist a client in conduct that amounts to a criminal offence or fraud.
options.C: Draft the contract but include a disclaimer stating that the firm accepts no responsibility for the client's use of it.
options.D: Report the matter to the SRA immediately without first raising it with Owen Davies.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. The SRA Code of Conduct (para 1.2 and 6.1) requires a solicitor to act only within the law and not to assist a client in unlawful conduct. Deliberately drafting a contract to facilitate insurance fraud is assisting in a criminal offence. Diana must decline to act on these instructions and may withdraw from the matter. A is wrong: a solicitor cannot draft documentation knowing it is intended to facilitate fraud simply because no court has yet condemned it. C is wrong: a disclaimer does not cure participation in a fraudulent scheme. D is wrong: before reporting to the SRA, Diana should raise her concerns with Owen Davies and, if necessary, terminate the retainer — immediate SRA reporting is not required unless there is an ongoing harm that cannot be prevented otherwise.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Alek Kowalski instructed Crane & Pelletier in a breach of contract dispute against a supplier. Gerald Crane allowed the matter to drift and failed to issue proceedings before the six-year limitation period expired. Kowalski now brings a negligence claim against the firm. What is the basis of the firm's liability?

options.A: None — the limitation period is a procedural bar, not a substantive obligation, and solicitors are not liable for clients' procedural failures.
options.B: Professional negligence — a solicitor owes a duty of care to advise on and observe limitation periods; failing to do so is a breach of that duty causing loss.
options.C: Breach of contract only — the solicitor-client relationship is purely contractual and negligence principles do not apply.
options.D: None — Kowalski should have monitored the limitation period himself.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. A solicitor owes both a contractual duty (retainer) and a tortious duty of care to the client (Henderson v Merrett Syndicates [1995]). Failing to identify and observe the limitation period is a well-established head of solicitor negligence (see Daniels v Thompson [2004]). The loss is the value of the claim that Kowalski has lost the ability to pursue. A is wrong: monitoring and advising on limitation is a fundamental part of litigation conduct. C is wrong: solicitors owe concurrent contractual and tortious duties. D is wrong: the burden of managing limitation falls on the instructed solicitor, not the lay client.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Diana Pelletier gave a written undertaking to the other side's solicitors in a commercial dispute that Crane & Pelletier would 'ensure that Atlas Contracts does not dissipate the disputed funds pending resolution.' Atlas Contracts subsequently transferred the funds to a third party without Crane & Pelletier's knowledge. What is the consequence for Diana?

options.A: No consequence — solicitors are not responsible for their clients' unilateral actions after an undertaking is given.
options.B: Diana may face professional disciplinary action — a solicitor's undertaking is a personal professional obligation; breach, even if caused by the client's action, puts the solicitor in breach.
options.C: Diana must indemnify the other party from her personal funds — an undertaking is personally binding and the solicitor must make good any loss caused by breach.
options.D: Diana must report Atlas Contracts to the police for frustrating the undertaking.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. A solicitor's undertaking is a personal professional obligation — the SRA Code requires solicitors to fulfil any undertaking they give in the course of practice (SRA Code para 1.3). If the undertaking cannot be fulfilled, the solicitor is in breach regardless of the reason, including their client's unilateral action. This does not automatically mean personal financial liability to the other party (that would depend on the undertaking's terms and whether loss results), but it does expose Diana to professional regulatory consequences. Before giving an undertaking, a solicitor should ensure they have the ability and authority to perform it. A is wrong: the personal nature of undertakings means the solicitor cannot escape liability by blaming the client. C overstates the automatic financial consequence without examining the undertaking's terms. D is not a required regulatory response.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Crane & Pelletier has entered an arrangement with a local insurance broker under which the broker pays the firm £200 for each commercial client referred to them for business insurance. Clients are not told about the arrangement. Under the SRA Code, is this arrangement permissible?

options.A: Yes — referral fee arrangements between solicitors and introducers are freely permitted without disclosure.
options.B: No — the SRA Code requires transparency about referral arrangements; undisclosed payments from a third party for referrals breach the duty to act in clients' best interests and the transparency obligations.
options.C: Yes — provided the amount (£200) is below the SRA's de minimis threshold for financial interests.
options.D: No — any referral fee arrangement is banned under the Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. The SRA Code of Conduct (paras 5.1–5.2) requires solicitors to act in clients' best interests and to be transparent about any financial arrangements with third parties that could affect their advice. Receiving an undisclosed referral fee from an insurer creates a financial interest that may compromise the solicitor's independence when recommending insurance. The firm must disclose the arrangement to clients. A is wrong: disclosure is mandatory. C: there is no de minimis threshold for financial interest disclosure. D overstates the prohibition — LASPO 2012 bans referral fees in personal injury cases, not all referral arrangements; however, the SRA transparency rules still apply to all undisclosed arrangements.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.subtopic: Frustration — Effect of Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943
Contract Law.stem: Alek Kowalski paid £5,000 in advance to a catering equipment supplier. Before delivery, the contract was frustrated when the supplier's premises were destroyed by fire. The supplier had incurred £2,000 in pre-frustration preparation costs. Under the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943, what is Kowalski entitled to recover?

options.A: The full £5,000 — frustration automatically restores all pre-paid sums without deduction.
options.B: The £5,000 less a deduction for the supplier's pre-frustration expenses of up to £2,000, at the court's discretion — so Kowalski recovers between £3,000 and £5,000.
options.C: Nothing — frustration discharges the contract and pre-paid sums are lost as the risk passes to the buyer.
options.D: £5,000, and the supplier cannot deduct any expenses — the Act only allows expense deductions for partial performance.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Section 1(2) of the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943 provides that money paid before frustration is recoverable, but the court has a discretion to allow the payee to retain an amount not exceeding their pre-frustration expenses incurred in performance of the contract. The supplier can retain up to £2,000 of the £5,000 prepayment to cover its reasonable costs, at the court's discretion — so Kowalski recovers between £3,000 and £5,000. A is wrong: the Act does not guarantee full repayment; the expense allowance can reduce it. C is wrong: the Act was passed precisely to avoid the harsh common law rule (Chandler v Webster [1904]) that left pre-paid sums irrecoverable. D is wrong: the expense deduction in s.1(2) applies to pre-frustration costs generally, not only to partial performance situations.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Atlas Contracts deliberately breached its contract with Hollowell Engineering to take on a more profitable project elsewhere, making £80,000 profit from the substitute project. Hollowell's actual loss from the breach was only £12,000 in extra procurement costs. Can Hollowell claim an account of Atlas's profits of £80,000?

options.A: Yes — deliberate breach of contract always entitles the innocent party to an account of profits.
options.B: No — damages for breach of contract are limited to compensating the claimant's actual loss; the defendant's profit is irrelevant.
options.C: Possibly — an account of profits is an exceptional remedy available for breach of contract where the claimant has a legitimate interest in preventing the defendant's profit that is not adequately protected by compensatory damages (Attorney General v Blake).
options.D: Yes — the deliberate nature of the breach means Hollowell is entitled to punitive damages equal to Atlas's profit.

Contract Law.explanation: C is correct. In Attorney General v Blake [2001], the House of Lords held that an account of profits for breach of contract is an exceptional remedy, available only where the claimant has a legitimate interest in preventing the defendant's profit and compensatory damages are inadequate. Ordinarily, breach of a commercial supply contract — even deliberate — would not justify an account of profits; compensatory damages suffice. Hollowell's £12,000 loss adequately compensates it. A overstates Blake — not every deliberate breach justifies an account of profits. B states the general rule but ignores the Blake exception. D: English contract law does not award punitive damages for breach; exemplary damages are available only in tort.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Prescott Motors agreed to sell a 'restored 1960s campervan' to James Ashworth for £18,000. Unknown to either party, the van had been destroyed in a fire at the storage facility two days before the contract was signed. What is the effect on the contract?

options.A: The contract is voidable — the mistake as to the existence of the van makes it voidable at James's option.
options.B: The contract is void — under s.6 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979, where specific goods have perished before the contract is made, the contract is void.
options.C: The contract is valid — Prescott bears the risk of loss and must compensate James for breach.
options.D: The contract is frustrated — subsequent destruction of the goods frustrates performance.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Section 6 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979 provides that where there is a contract for the sale of specific goods, and the goods have perished without the seller's knowledge before the contract is made, the contract is void. Both parties believed the van existed; it had already perished before the contract was formed — this is the res extincta principle (Couturier v Hastie [1856]) codified in s.6. A is wrong: the contract is void, not voidable; mistake as to the existence of the subject matter is a total failure of the basis of the contract. C is wrong: Prescott cannot be in breach of a contract that never existed. D is wrong: frustration applies to supervening events after a valid contract is formed; here the goods had perished before formation.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering repudiated its contract with a steel supplier by refusing to accept any further deliveries. The supplier chose not to accept the repudiation and continued to stand ready to perform. One month later, the contract became impossible to perform due to a government trade restriction (not caused by either party). The supplier now claims damages for breach. What is the outcome?

options.A: The supplier can claim full damages — it kept the contract alive and is entitled to compensation for Hollowell's repudiation.
options.B: The supplier cannot claim damages — by not accepting the repudiation, it took the risk of the contract becoming frustrated, which extinguishes both parties' obligations.
options.C: The supplier can claim damages — keeping the contract alive is always permissible and the subsequent frustration is irrelevant.
options.D: The supplier can claim damages for losses suffered before the frustrating event only.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. This is the scenario from Avery v Bowden [1856] and White & Carter (Councils) v McGregor [1962]. An innocent party who elects not to accept an anticipatory repudiation keeps the contract alive — for better or worse. The risk of a subsequent frustrating event falls on the party who elected not to accept the breach. The government restriction frustrated the contract, discharging all obligations under the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943 regime. The supplier's potential damages claim is extinguished by the frustration. A is wrong: keeping the contract alive means frustration can intervene. C is wrong: the innocent party's election to affirm does not immunise them from supervening frustration. D is not the correct outcome — the frustration discharges all obligations, not just prospective ones.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.subtopic: Intention to Create Legal Relations — Commercial
Contract Law.stem: Wharton Foods issued a press release announcing: 'We commit to sourcing 100% of our packaging from local suppliers within three years.' A local packaging supplier claims this is a binding contractual commitment entitling it to Wharton Foods' business. Is the press release an enforceable contract?

options.A: Yes — a public commitment made by a commercial entity is binding as a unilateral offer accepted by local suppliers who continue trading.
options.B: No — a press release is a public statement, not an offer capable of acceptance; it lacks the necessary certainty of terms and bilateral agreement for a contract.
options.C: Yes — commercial parties are presumed to intend legal relations for all their public statements.
options.D: No — press releases are protected from legal consequences by freedom of speech principles.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. A press release is a public relations statement, not a contractual offer. It lacks the specificity needed for an offer (no identified offeree, no price, no mechanism for acceptance). The presumption that commercial parties intend legal relations applies to negotiated agreements — not to aspirational public statements. Furthermore, there is no consideration from the packaging supplier. A confuses public statement with unilateral offer; a unilateral offer requires a specific act to be performed in exchange. C overstates the commercial ICLR presumption — it applies to bilateral agreements, not all public statements. D: freedom of speech is irrelevant to contractual analysis.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Before entering a supply contract, Neville Prescott told Graham Grange that a fleet of vans he was selling 'would be ideal for the cold chain — perfect for frozen food transport.' The written contract made no mention of this. The vans were not suitable for frozen food transport. Grange seeks remedies. Is Prescott's statement a term of the contract or a mere representation?

options.A: It is a term — verbal assurances given before a contract become terms automatically when they induce entry into the contract.
options.B: It is a representation only — because the written contract is silent on suitability, the parol evidence rule prevents any pre-contractual statement from being a term.
options.C: Whether it is a term or representation depends on factors including the importance of the statement, the relative expertise of the parties, and whether the maker invited verification.
options.D: It is a representation only — terms must always be in writing to be enforceable in commercial contracts.

Contract Law.explanation: C is correct. Whether a pre-contractual statement is a term or mere representation depends on the parties' objective intention, assessed using factors from Heilbut Symons v Buckleton [1913] and Bannerman v White [1861]: (1) how important was the statement to the contract? (2) did the maker have special knowledge or skill? (3) did the maker say the counterparty could verify it? (4) how much time passed between statement and contract? Here, Prescott specialises in vans and made a specific fitness-for-purpose claim — this points toward it being a term. A is wrong: not all inducing pre-contractual statements become terms. B overstates the parol evidence rule — it does not absolutely prevent pre-contractual terms. D is wrong: commercial terms need not be in writing.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Marta Kowalski, aged 17, contracted on behalf of herself (not the family business) to buy a professional-grade commercial espresso machine worth £3,500 on credit for a planned business venture. She has not yet started the business. She refuses to pay. Is the contract enforceable against her?

options.A: Yes — contracts for goods necessary for a business are binding on minors.
options.B: No — a contract for goods is only binding on a minor if the goods are 'necessaries' within the meaning of the Sale of Goods Act 1979 s.3; a commercial espresso machine for a not-yet-started business venture is not a necessary.
options.C: Yes — a credit agreement is always binding on a minor if it relates to a commercial purpose.
options.D: No — all contracts entered by minors are void and entirely unenforceable.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Under s.3 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979, a minor is only bound to pay a reasonable price for 'necessaries' — goods suitable to the minor's condition in life and actually needed at the time of sale. A professional commercial espresso machine purchased for a business that does not yet exist is not a necessary; it is a capital item for a speculative commercial venture. The contract is voidable at Marta's option. A is wrong: business goods are not automatically necessaries for a minor. C is wrong: the capacity rules apply regardless of commercial purpose. D is wrong: the common law and Sale of Goods Act provide that contracts for necessaries are enforceable; other contracts by minors are voidable (not void).

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering and Atlas Contracts orally agreed to vary their written construction contract by extending the completion date by four weeks and adjusting the payment schedule. Both parties relied on the variation. Atlas later seeks to hold Hollowell to the original completion date. Is the oral variation binding?

options.A: Yes — an oral variation of a written contract is binding if supported by consideration from both parties.
options.B: No — the original written contract contained a clause requiring all variations to be in writing and signed; the oral variation is ineffective.
options.C: Yes — a no-oral-modification clause can be overridden by a subsequent agreement even if not in writing.
options.D: No — variations to construction contracts must comply with the Housing Grants, Construction and Regeneration Act 1996 requirements.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct, if the contract contained a 'no oral modification' (NOM) clause. Following MWB Business Exchange Centres v Rock Advertising [2018], the Supreme Court held that NOM clauses are effective in commercial contracts. A party cannot rely on an oral variation if the contract requires written form. The oral variation between Hollowell and Atlas is ineffective if the written contract required written variations. A states the general rule from before MWB but ignores the NOM clause. C: MWB directly held that NOM clauses can only be overridden by a variation that itself complies with the agreed formality — a simple informal oral agreement does not suffice. D: the Housing Grants Act 1996 deals with adjudication rights and payment notices, not the formality of contract variations.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.subtopic: Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 — Exclusion
Contract Law.stem: A written supply contract between Wharton Foods and Hollowell Engineering stated: 'Nothing in this agreement shall confer any right on any person who is not a party to it.' A Wharton Foods employee suffered loss due to defective goods supplied by Hollowell. Can the employee enforce the contract?

options.A: Yes — the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 overrides any contractual exclusion of third-party rights.
options.B: No — s.1(2) of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 allows parties to contract out of the Act; the exclusion clause is effective.
options.C: Yes — employees always have standing to enforce supply contracts made for their benefit.
options.D: No — the employee's only remedy is in tort (negligence), not contract.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Section 1(2) of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 provides that the Act does not apply where the contract expressly provides that the third party shall not be entitled to enforce the term in question. The clause in the Wharton Foods/Hollowell contract expressly excludes third-party rights — this is a valid and effective contracting-out of the Act. A is wrong: the Act expressly permits parties to contract out of it. C is wrong: employees have no general right to enforce their employer's supply contracts. D describes the employee's position correctly — the tort route may be available — but it does not directly address the contract question asked.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Neville Prescott sold a commercial van to Donna Pearce. Before the sale, Prescott knew the van had been used as a recovery vehicle and had significantly higher mileage than the odometer showed (it had been clocked). Prescott said nothing about this. Donna only discovered the true history after purchase. Is Prescott liable for misrepresentation?

options.A: No — there is no general duty of disclosure in contract; silence cannot amount to misrepresentation.
options.B: Yes — Prescott's silence about the clocked odometer constitutes a fraudulent misrepresentation because a half-truth or active concealment can amount to misrepresentation.
options.C: No — unless Donna specifically asked about the mileage, Prescott had no obligation to volunteer the information.
options.D: Yes — consumer protection legislation requires full disclosure of vehicle history in all second-hand vehicle sales.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. While mere silence does not generally constitute misrepresentation, there are recognised exceptions: (1) where a statement made is a half-truth (true but misleading by omission); (2) where facts are actively concealed; (3) where a true statement later becomes false before contract. A clocked odometer is a form of active concealment — the odometer positively represents a false mileage. Prescott did not merely stay silent; the falsified reading made an implicit representation (the displayed mileage is accurate) which was false to Prescott's knowledge. This is fraudulent misrepresentation (Derry v Peek). A correctly states the general rule but fails to apply the half-truth/concealment exception. C is wrong for the same reason. D: while consumer protection law may be relevant, the question targets misrepresentation doctrine.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering's offer to Atlas Contracts stated: 'This offer must be accepted by fax or by hand-delivered letter only.' Atlas sent acceptance by email. Pat Hollowell claims no contract was formed. Is a contract formed?

options.A: Yes — an email is an equivalent modern method to fax and satisfies the offer's prescribed method.
options.B: No — where an offer prescribes the method of acceptance, acceptance by any other method is generally ineffective unless the prescribed method is merely directory.
options.C: Yes — the Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002 require offerors to accept electronic acceptances.
options.D: No — only a hand-delivered letter is effective because fax and email are equivalent, cancelling each other out.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. An offeror may prescribe the method of acceptance, and acceptance by a non-prescribed method is generally ineffective (Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial and General Investments [1970]). However, the court will consider whether the prescribed method was mandatory (no other method permitted) or merely directory (prescribing a preferred method while leaving others open). If the offer was mandatory ('must be accepted by fax or hand-delivered letter only'), an email acceptance does not comply. A is wrong: modern equivalence does not override an express prescriptive term. C is wrong: the E-Commerce Regulations do not override express contract terms about acceptance method. D's reasoning is incorrect — there is no 'cancellation' principle.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Donna Pearce entered a two-year exclusive services agreement with Wharton Foods under which Northgate Couriers would be the sole provider of courier services for the factory. Six months in, Graham Grange told Donna he was switching to a cheaper national carrier. Donna seeks an injunction restraining Wharton Foods from using another carrier during the contract period. Is an injunction available?

options.A: No — injunctions are not available to enforce contracts for services; specific performance is the only equitable remedy.
options.B: Yes — a prohibitory injunction restraining a party from breaking a negative covenant in a contract (i.e. not using another carrier) may be granted to protect the benefit of the exclusivity agreement.
options.C: No — damages would be an adequate remedy as Donna can calculate her lost revenue precisely.
options.D: Yes — but only as a final injunction after trial; interim injunctions are not available in contract disputes.

Contract Law.explanation: B is correct. Where a contract contains an express or implied negative covenant (here, an obligation not to use any other courier — the flipside of the exclusivity), a prohibitory injunction may be granted to prevent its breach (Warner Bros v Nelson [1937]). The court applies the American Cyanamid test at the interim stage. Courts are more willing to grant injunctions to enforce negative covenants (not to do something) than mandatory orders to compel positive performance. A is wrong: injunctions can enforce negative contractual obligations even in service contracts. C is a relevant argument at the American Cyanamid 'adequacy of damages' stage but does not categorically bar injunctive relief. D is wrong: interim injunctions are available in contract disputes.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Wharton Magna District Council invited tenders for cleaning services, stating in the invitation: 'The council is not bound to accept the lowest or any tender.' Three companies submitted tenders. The council accepted the tender of a company that had submitted its bid after the deadline. One of the on-time bidders, Rachel Okonkwo's cleaning company, challenges the decision. What is Rachel's legal position?

options.A: Rachel can sue the council for breach of contract — the council was contractually bound to consider only on-time tenders.
options.B: Rachel has no claim — the invitation to tender was not an offer, and the disclaimer allows the council to accept any tender including a late one.
options.C: Rachel can judicially review the decision — local authority procurement decisions must comply with public law fairness principles.
options.D: Rachel has a contractual claim for a reasonable chance of winning the tender, but cannot claim loss of profit.

Contract Law.explanation: C is correct in this context. The invitation to tender is not an offer and created no binding contractual obligation on the council to follow any particular process. B is correct as a pure contract analysis — the disclaimer prevents contractual liability. However, Wharton Magna District Council is a public body and its procurement decisions must comply with public law obligations, including procedural fairness and potentially the Public Contracts Regulations 2015. Accepting a late tender when the rules of the process prohibited it could be challenged by judicial review. A is wrong: the disclaimer prevents a contractual obligation to accept any tender. B is partially correct but misses the public law dimension. D describes the Blackpool Aero Club v Blackpool Borough Council [1990] principle (implied contractual obligation to consider conforming tenders) but that would require tender rules that expressly excluded late tenders — here the question is about a local authority, making judicial review the stronger route.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Which of the following best describes the 'neighbour principle' established in Donoghue v Stevenson [1932]?

options.A: A duty of care is owed to those so closely and directly affected by your acts that you ought reasonably to have them in contemplation.
options.B: A duty of care is owed only to those with whom the defendant has a contractual relationship.
options.C: A duty of care is owed to all members of the general public regardless of foreseeability.
options.D: A duty of care is owed only in cases where there has been physical contact between parties.

Tort Law.explanation: Option A is correct. The neighbour principle (Lord Atkin, Donoghue v Stevenson) established that a duty of care is owed to those so closely and directly affected by acts that they ought to be in contemplation. Option B is incorrect because the neighbour principle explicitly moved beyond contractual relationships. Option C overstates the duty (foreseeability is required). Option D is too narrow and ignores the principle's breadth.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: In Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018], the Supreme Court clarified the role of the Caparo test. When does Caparo apply?

options.A: Caparo applies only to novel situations where the facts do not fall within an established duty category.
options.B: Caparo applies in every negligence case without exception.
options.C: Caparo applies only when the claimant is a professional.
options.D: Caparo applies only where the defendant has acted intentionally.

Tort Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Robinson clarified that Caparo's three-stage test applies only to novel situations; where facts fall within established categories (road users, employers, manufacturers, occupiers), the duty exists without applying Caparo. Option A is incorrect (Caparo is not universal). Option C wrongly limits Caparo to professionals. Option D confuses Caparo with intentional acts.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: What are the three stages of the Caparo test from Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990]?

options.A: Foreseeability, proximity, and fair, just and reasonable.
options.B: Intention, causation, and remoteness.
options.C: Breach, damage, and proof of loss.
options.D: Assumption of responsibility, reliance, and knowledge.

Tort Law.explanation: Option B is correct. The Caparo test requires: (1) reasonable foreseeability of risk to the claimant; (2) sufficient proximity between claimant and defendant; (3) fairness, justice and reasonableness in imposing a duty. Option A confuses elements of other torts. Option C lists elements of proving negligence, not the duty test. Option D relates to negligent misstatement, not the general Caparo framework.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.subtopic: Duty of Care — Omissions & Liability for Acts of Third Parties
Tort Law.stem: Wharton Magna District Council maintains a public riverside walkway along the River Weld near Church Lane. Rachel Okonkwo is attacked by a third party in a poorly-lit stretch of the path. The council had not installed additional lighting despite previous complaints about that location. Is the council liable in negligence for failing to prevent the third-party attack?

options.A: Yes, because the council should have foreseen the risk of attack and must prevent all third-party crimes.
options.B: No, because the council has no general duty to prevent crimes committed by third parties.
options.C: Yes, if the council owed a duty to path users (as occupier) and the failure to light was a breach that contributed to the foreseeable risk.
