options.D: Hollowell has a complete defence because the injury was triggered by Sharma's drone, making Sharma solely liable.

Tort Law.explanation: Under Animals Act 1971 s.2(2), a keeper of a non-dangerous species animal is strictly liable if three conditions are met: (i) damage of the kind caused was likely, or likely to be severe if caused; (ii) the likelihood/severity was due to abnormal characteristics; and (iii) the keeper knew of those characteristics. Bulls can exhibit aggressive behaviour in particular circumstances (e.g., when startled), satisfying condition (ii). Cattle are not a dangerous species under s.6(2), so A is wrong. Option C imposes a negligence standard which does not apply to s.2(2) strict liability. Option D is incorrect: a third party's act may be relevant to contributory fault but does not automatically extinguish the keeper's liability.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Alek Kowalski keeps a pet capuchin monkey at his flat. The monkey bites his neighbour Priya Mehta. Priya sues. Alek argues the monkey had never bitten anyone before. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Alek is liable only if the monkey had previously shown aggressive tendencies.
options.B: Alek is strictly liable under Animals Act 1971 s.2(1) because capuchin monkeys are a dangerous species not commonly domesticated in Great Britain.
options.C: Alek is liable in negligence only, as exotic pets attract a higher duty of care.
options.D: Priya must prove Alek knew of the particular propensity of this specific monkey to bite.

Tort Law.explanation: Under Animals Act 1971 s.2(1), a keeper of an animal belonging to a dangerous species is strictly liable for any damage it causes. Section 6(2) defines a dangerous species as one not commonly domesticated in the British Isles and whose fully grown animals have characteristics which, unless restrained, are likely to cause severe damage. Capuchin monkeys satisfy this test. There is no need to show prior dangerous behaviour or knowledge of a specific propensity — that is the advantage of strict liability under s.2(1). Options A and D incorrectly import the s.2(2) knowledge requirements. Option C is wrong: negligence is not required.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Hassan Nazari owns Wharton Magna Industrial Estate. A telecoms company erects a mobile phone mast on adjacent land; the support cables run at 25 metres above Nazari's property without consent. Nazari seeks damages. On which basis is his claim most likely to succeed?

options.A: Private nuisance, because the cables interfere with his quiet enjoyment.
options.B: Trespass to land, because the cables constitute a direct, unauthorised physical intrusion into the airspace above his land.
options.C: Rylands v Fletcher, because the cables represent a non-natural use of land.
options.D: Public nuisance, because a structure at that height creates a risk to the public generally.

Tort Law.explanation: Trespass to land extends to airspace above the land to the height necessary for the ordinary use and enjoyment of the land and structures on it (Bernstein v Skyviews [1978], Anchor Brewhouse v Berkley House [1987]). A permanent cable at 25m directly above the land constitutes a direct and intentional intrusion, satisfying the requirements for trespass. Private nuisance (A) covers indirect or continuing interference with use/enjoyment and is less apt than trespass for physical permanent intrusion. Rylands (C) requires an escape from the defendant's land — the cables are static. Public nuisance (D) requires interference with the public at large and there is no evidence of that here.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Donna Pearce verbally granted Owen Davies permission to use a shortcut across her garden for three months. After six weeks, Pearce verbally revoked the permission. Davies continued using the shortcut the following day. Pearce claims in trespass. Davies argues the licence was irrevocable for the agreed three months. Is Davies correct?

options.A: Yes — a licence granted for a fixed period cannot be revoked early.
options.B: No — a bare licence is revocable at any time on reasonable notice; Davies became a trespasser once reasonable notice had elapsed.
options.C: No — trespass requires proof of damage, which Pearce cannot show.
options.D: Yes — because verbal licences for land use are subject to part performance and cannot be revoked without compensating Davies.

Tort Law.explanation: A bare licence — permission without consideration — is revocable at any time, subject only to giving reasonable notice (Robson v Hallett [1967]). Once reasonable notice has been given and expired, entry becomes trespass. The three-month term creates no binding obligation without consideration, so A is wrong. Trespass to land is actionable per se — no proof of damage is required (C is wrong). Part performance and compensation (D) are equitable doctrines relating to contracts for the sale/disposition of land, not bare licences.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Victoria Ashworth owns a rival recruitment agency to Crane & Pelletier. Knowing that Crane & Pelletier has an exclusive services contract with Northgate Couriers Ltd, Ashworth approaches Northgate's managing director and persuades him to terminate the contract early, causing Crane & Pelletier financial loss. Which tort has Ashworth most likely committed?

options.A: Passing off.
options.B: Unlawful means conspiracy.
options.C: Inducing breach of contract (OBG Ltd v Allan [2008]).
options.D: Negligence.

Tort Law.explanation: The tort of inducing breach of contract requires: (i) knowledge of the contract; (ii) intention to induce its breach; (iii) an actual breach by a third party; and (iv) resulting damage to the claimant (OBG Ltd v Allan [2008]). Ashworth knew of the contract, intentionally persuaded Northgate to breach it, and Crane & Pelletier suffered loss. Passing off (A) protects goodwill/trade reputation and is not relevant. Unlawful means conspiracy (B) requires two or more persons combining and the use of unlawful means — the facts show Ashworth acting alone and the primary mechanism is persuasion/inducement, not unlawful means. Negligence (D) does not capture the intentional element here.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Clive Dring runs a local bakery he calls 'The Wharton Bake House'. A new trader sets up a competing stall and calls it 'Wharton Bake House' using almost identical branding and packaging. Dring has not registered any trade mark. What cause of action is available to Dring?

options.A: Dring has no remedy because he has not registered a trade mark.
options.B: Dring can sue for passing off, provided he establishes goodwill, misrepresentation and damage (the classic trinity from Reckitt & Colman v Borden [1990]).
options.C: Dring can sue under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 for misleading practices.
options.D: Dring's only remedy is to apply for an injunction under the Human Rights Act 1998.

Tort Law.explanation: Passing off protects unregistered goodwill. The classic trinity from Reckitt & Colman v Borden [1990] (the Jif Lemon case) requires the claimant to establish: (i) goodwill or reputation attached to their goods/services; (ii) a misrepresentation by the defendant leading (or likely to lead) the public to believe the defendant's goods/services are those of the claimant; and (iii) damage resulting. Dring need not have a registered trade mark to bring a passing off claim. The CRA 2015 is consumer protection legislation, not a remedy between traders. The HRA 1998 does not provide this type of private law remedy.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Priya Mehta is injured in a road accident caused jointly by Donna Pearce and Alek Kowalski. She obtains a judgment against Pearce for the full sum of £90,000. Pearce wishes to recover a contribution from Kowalski. Under the Civil Liability (Contribution) Act 1978, which statement is correct?

options.A: Pearce cannot recover any contribution because she was also at fault.
options.B: Pearce can recover a contribution from Kowalski in such proportion as the court considers just and equitable having regard to the extent of Kowalski's responsibility for the damage.
options.C: Pearce can recover from Kowalski only if Kowalski was more than 50% responsible.
options.D: Pearce must first obtain a separate judgment against Kowalski before seeking contribution.

Tort Law.explanation: Under the Civil Liability (Contribution) Act 1978 s.2, a person liable for damage may recover a contribution from any other person liable for the same damage. The amount is such as may be found by the court to be just and equitable having regard to the extent of that person's responsibility for the damage. There is no threshold of 50% (C is wrong). Contribution proceedings are commenced by Pearce against Kowalski directly — there is no requirement for a prior separate judgment against Kowalski (D). Pearce's own fault is relevant to apportionment within the original claim under the Law Reform (Contributory Negligence) Act 1945, not to her right to seek contribution (A is wrong).

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.subtopic: Publication on a matter of public interest — s.4 DA 2013
Tort Law.stem: The Wharton Magna Gazette publishes an article alleging that Councillor Stubbs improperly awarded a council contract to a company in which he has a financial interest. The allegation is not fully proven but the editor reasonably believed publication was in the public interest and acted responsibly in verifying sources. Stubbs sues for defamation. What is the newspaper's best defence?

options.A: Absolute privilege — reports of council proceedings are absolutely privileged.
options.B: Publication on a matter of public interest under s.4 Defamation Act 2013, provided the defendant reasonably believed publication was in the public interest.
options.C: Honest opinion under s.3 DA 2013.
options.D: Truth under s.2 DA 2013, because the allegation is substantially true.

Tort Law.explanation: Section 4 DA 2013 provides a defence of publication on a matter of public interest where: (i) the statement was, or formed part of, a statement on a matter of public interest; and (ii) the defendant reasonably believed that publishing the statement was in the public interest. The court must have regard to all circumstances including editorial judgement. This replaced the old Reynolds qualified privilege. Absolute privilege (A) applies to parliamentary proceedings and court proceedings, not newspaper reporting of council activities. Honest opinion (C) requires the statement to be clearly identifiable as an opinion, not a factual allegation. Truth (D) requires proving the substantial truth of the imputation — if the allegation is not fully proven, truth is unavailable.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A user posts a defamatory comment about Clive Dring on an online forum hosted by a company. The company does not author the comment but hosts the platform. Dring notifies the company and asks it to remove the post. The company ignores the notification. Which statement best describes the company's legal position?

options.A: The company has no liability because it is merely a passive host and did not author the statement.
options.B: The company is potentially liable as a publisher once it has been notified of the defamatory post and fails to act, losing the operator defence under s.5 DA 2013.
options.C: Only the original poster can be sued; platform operators are immune under the E-Commerce Regulations 2002.
options.D: The company is liable only if it edited or moderated the content before publication.

Tort Law.explanation: Section 5 DA 2013 provides a defence for operators of websites who did not post the statement, but the defence can be lost if the operator fails to respond properly to a notification of complaint. Regulation 19 of the E-Commerce Regulations 2002 provides a separate hosting defence but that too is lost where the operator has actual knowledge of the unlawful activity and fails to act expeditiously. Once notified, a passive host that ignores a defamatory post may lose its immunity and be treated as a publisher. A is therefore incorrect. C incorrectly states absolute immunity — the E-Commerce Regs hosting defence is conditional. D imports an editorial/moderation test which is not the governing principle.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: James Ashworth has, on five separate occasions over three months, sent aggressive emails to Rachel Okonkwo demanding repayment of a disputed debt, using threatening language. Okonkwo has told Ashworth to stop. Ashworth argues the emails are legitimate debt collection. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Okonkwo cannot bring a civil claim because harassment is a criminal offence only.
options.B: Okonkwo can bring a civil claim under s.3 Protection from Harassment Act 1997; Ashworth's course of conduct (at least two occasions) amounts to harassment and debt collection is not a reasonable excuse for threatening language.
options.C: Okonkwo can only claim if she proves psychiatric injury.
options.D: Ashworth's conduct is lawful because creditors are entitled to pursue debtors robustly.

Tort Law.explanation: The Protection from Harassment Act 1997 s.1 prohibits a course of conduct (conduct on at least two occasions) which the person knows or ought to know amounts to harassment. Section 3 creates a civil remedy. The reasonableness defence in s.1(3)(c) requires that the pursuit of debt collection be conducted reasonably — threatening language takes the conduct outside what is reasonable in the circumstances. Option A is wrong: s.3 explicitly creates a civil cause of action. Option C is wrong: the civil claim under s.3 does not require psychiatric injury (though s.3(2) permits damages for anxiety caused). Option D is wrong: there is no absolute right to harass debtors.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.subtopic: Provision of safe system of work
Tort Law.stem: Pat Hollowell's employee, Dave Sutton, is injured when using an unguarded grinding machine at Hollowell Engineering. Hollowell argues he provided written safety instructions and that Sutton ignored them. Which statement best describes Hollowell's position?

options.A: Hollowell is not liable because he provided written safety instructions.
options.B: Hollowell may still be liable because the duty to provide a safe system of work includes not merely issuing instructions but also ensuring they are followed and that equipment is safe.
options.C: Hollowell's liability is extinguished by Sutton's contributory negligence in ignoring the instructions.
options.D: Hollowell is exempt from liability because the Employers' Liability (Compulsory Insurance) Act 1969 transfers risk to the insurer.

Tort Law.explanation: An employer's non-delegable duty at common law encompasses: (i) safe plant and equipment; (ii) competent co-workers; (iii) safe premises; and (iv) a safe system of work (Wilsons & Clyde Coal v English [1938]). Merely issuing safety instructions does not discharge the duty if the system is not properly enforced and machinery remains unguarded. The ELCIA 1969 (D) requires employers to hold insurance — it does not transfer the underlying liability. Sutton's contributory negligence (C) may reduce damages but does not extinguish Hollowell's liability entirely. Option A overstates what written instructions achieve.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Donna Pearce works as a dispatcher at Northgate Couriers. She tells her line manager that she is struggling with an overwhelming workload and fears a mental breakdown. The manager takes no action. Pearce subsequently suffers a clinical depressive episode. She claims against Northgate. Which factor is most decisive in establishing employer liability?

options.A: Whether the psychiatric harm was reasonably foreseeable given Pearce's warning to management.
options.B: Whether Pearce had a pre-existing vulnerability to psychiatric illness.
options.C: Whether Northgate's business is inherently stressful.
options.D: Whether Pearce's GP had previously notified Northgate of her mental health condition.

Tort Law.explanation: In Hatton v Sutherland [2002] the Court of Appeal set out that an employer's duty to take steps to prevent psychiatric harm arises only where harm is reasonably foreseeable. The key question is whether the claimant showed signs of impending harm. Pearce's warning to management is a significant indicator that psychiatric harm was foreseeable, making it the most decisive factor. A pre-existing vulnerability (B) may affect quantum (thin skull rule) but is not the primary test for the duty to arise. The general stressfulness of the job (C) is a background factor, not the primary trigger. GP notification (D) is not required — internal notice to management suffices.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Which of the following correctly states all three elements of the Caparo Industries v Dickman [1990] test for establishing a duty of care in negligence?

options.A: Foreseeability of damage; a relationship of proximity between the parties; and it is fair, just and reasonable to impose a duty.
options.B: Foreseeability of damage; reliance by the claimant on the defendant; and absence of a voluntary assumption of risk.
options.C: A special relationship; foreseeability of damage; and causation.
options.D: Proximity; causation; and remoteness.

Tort Law.explanation: Caparo Industries v Dickman [1990] established the three-stage test: (i) it must be reasonably foreseeable that a failure to take care would cause damage to the claimant; (ii) there must be a relationship of sufficient proximity between the parties; and (iii) it must be fair, just and reasonable to impose a duty. Reliance (B) is a relevant factor in some contexts (especially pure economic loss/negligent misstatement) but is not a universal element of Caparo. Options C and D conflate duty with causation and remoteness, which are separate elements of a negligence claim.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Dr. Felix Unwin, a GP, refers Priya Mehta to a specialist two weeks after she first presents with symptoms that, with hindsight, suggested a serious condition. A body of responsible GPs would have referred immediately. A second body of responsible GPs would have waited up to three weeks before referring. Mehta sues. Is Unwin in breach of duty?

options.A: Yes — Unwin is in breach because some GPs would have referred immediately.
options.B: No — Unwin is not in breach because his practice is supported by a responsible body of medical opinion (Bolam v Friern HMC [1957]).
options.C: Yes — Unwin is in breach because the balance of medical opinion favours immediate referral.
options.D: No — Unwin is not in breach because the two-week delay caused no additional harm.

Tort Law.explanation: The Bolam test holds that a doctor is not negligent if he acts in accordance with a practice accepted as proper by a responsible body of medical men skilled in the relevant area, even if a different body of practitioners would adopt a different practice. Since a responsible body of GPs would have waited up to three weeks, Unwin's two-week referral is not a breach. Option A would incorrectly require the defendant to meet the highest standard of any reasonable practitioner. Option C inappropriately applies a majority-view test — Bolam does not require the majority view. Option D conflates breach with causation; breach is assessed on conduct, not outcome.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.subtopic: Material increase in risk — Bonnington Castings
Tort Law.stem: Pat Hollowell's factory employee is exposed to silica dust partly from an innocent source and partly from negligently maintained equipment. The employee develops pneumoconiosis. Medical evidence cannot establish which source predominantly caused the disease. Hollowell argues causation fails on the balance of probabilities because the innocent source may have been the dominant cause. Which principle best resolves this?

options.A: The but-for test; the claim must fail because causation cannot be established on the balance of probabilities.
options.B: The material contribution to damage principle (Bonnington Castings v Wardlaw [1956]); Hollowell is fully liable if his breach materially contributed to the disease.
options.C: The Fairchild principle; the claimant must apportion damages between the innocent and negligent sources.
options.D: Loss of a chance; damages are reduced by the percentage probability that the innocent source caused the disease.

Tort Law.explanation: In Bonnington Castings v Wardlaw [1956] the House of Lords held that where a defendant's breach materially contributed to cumulative damage (as opposed to divisible damage), the claimant need not show the breach was the sole or dominant cause — a material contribution suffices. This was appropriate because both sources were acting cumulatively on the same disease process. The but-for test (A) would defeat the claim on the facts but Bonnington provides a recognised exception. Fairchild (C) applies to divisible risk scenarios (e.g., mesothelioma from multiple employers) and involves an apportionment rule from Barker v Corus [2006] modified by Compensation Act 2006. Loss of a chance (D) was rejected in clinical negligence in Gregg v Scott [2005].

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.subtopic: Heads of damage — general and special
Tort Law.stem: Priya Mehta is injured in a road accident caused by Alek Kowalski. She sustains a broken leg and cannot work for four months, losing £15,000 in earnings. She also suffers ongoing pain. Which of the following correctly categorises these losses?

options.A: The lost earnings are general damages; the pain and suffering are special damages.
options.B: The lost earnings are special damages; the pain and suffering are general damages.
options.C: Both are general damages because they both flow from the same tortious act.
options.D: Both are special damages because they are quantifiable in monetary terms.

Tort Law.explanation: Special damages are pecuniary losses that are specifically quantifiable and have already accrued by the date of trial — such as lost earnings, medical expenses, and travel costs. General damages are losses that are not precisely calculable and are assessed by the court, including pain, suffering and loss of amenity (PSLA), and future losses. Lost earnings of £15,000 are pre-trial pecuniary loss = special damages. Pain and suffering are non-pecuniary = general damages. Options A and C are incorrect categorisations. Option D is wrong because not all quantifiable losses are special damages — future loss of earnings is general damage, for example.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A court awards significant future loss of earnings damages to an injured claimant. Under the Courts Act 2003, what power does the court have in relation to how those damages are paid?

options.A: The court must always order a lump sum; periodical payments require the parties' consent.
options.B: The court may order periodical payments for future pecuniary loss (including future care costs and future loss of earnings) without the parties' consent if it considers it appropriate.
options.C: Periodical payments are only available where the defendant is the Motor Insurers' Bureau.
options.D: The court may recommend periodical payments but the claimant has an absolute right to insist on a lump sum.

Tort Law.explanation: Under the Courts Act 2003 s.2, the court has power to order periodical payments in respect of future pecuniary loss in personal injury cases, and this power can be exercised without the consent of either party. The court must consider whether a periodical payment order would be appropriate in the circumstances. This was a significant change from the previous position where consent was required. Options A and D are therefore incorrect. Option C is incorrect — the power is general and not limited to MIB cases.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Hassan Nazari engages an independent contractor to rewire his restaurant. The contractor is negligent and a customer, Priya Mehta, is injured by a faulty electrical installation. Nazari argues he is not liable because the work was carried out by a contractor. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Nazari has no liability; an occupier is never liable for the negligence of an independent contractor.
options.B: Nazari discharges his duty under OLA 1957 s.2(4)(b) if it was reasonable to entrust the work to a contractor, he took reasonable steps to satisfy himself the contractor was competent, and (where reasonable) inspected the completed work.
options.C: Nazari is strictly liable for all personal injury caused on his premises regardless of cause.
options.D: Nazari is liable only if the contractor was an employee for employment law purposes.

Tort Law.explanation: Under OLA 1957 s.2(4)(b), where damage is caused by a danger arising from work carried out by an independent contractor, the occupier discharges the common duty of care if: (i) it was reasonable to entrust the work to a contractor; (ii) the occupier took such steps as were reasonable to satisfy itself that the contractor was competent; and (iii) where it was reasonable to inspect, the occupier inspected the work. The occupier is not automatically absolved (A is wrong). Strict liability (C) does not apply to OLA 1957. The employment law status (D) is irrelevant — OLA 1957 applies to independent contractors engaged by occupiers.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering has operated a noisy forge in Wharton Magna for 30 years. Rachel Okonkwo recently bought a house next to the forge and now complains of the noise. Hollowell argues Okonkwo 'came to the nuisance'. Is this a defence?

options.A: Yes — a claimant who moves to an existing nuisance cannot complain about it.
options.B: No — coming to the nuisance is not a defence in English law (Miller v Jackson [1977]; Sturges v Bridgman [1879]).
options.C: Yes — provided Hollowell has operated at the same intensity for more than 20 years, giving him a prescriptive right.
options.D: No — but Okonkwo's damages will be reduced proportionately because she chose to move there.

Tort Law.explanation: Coming to the nuisance is not a recognised defence in English tort law. In Sturges v Bridgman [1879] the court held that a claimant who moves near an existing activity can still sue in private nuisance. Miller v Jackson [1977] reaffirmed this. A prescriptive right to commit a nuisance (C) requires 20 years of open, continuous noxious use without secrecy or permission — this is distinct from 'coming to the nuisance' and is not automatically acquired by longevity alone; the use must have been tortious from the start and the claimant's predecessor must have had cause of action. Option D is not a recognised approach — there is no automatic proportionate reduction based on knowledge of pre-existing conditions.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Ravi Sharma grows prize orchids in a specially humidified greenhouse. His neighbour, Pat Hollowell, operates spray painting equipment that causes chemical deposits to settle on the orchids. Ordinary plants would be unaffected. Sharma sues in private nuisance. Which principle is likely to limit Sharma's claim?

options.A: Ex turpi causa — Sharma's orchid growing is not a normal use of land.
options.B: The sensitive claimant rule — a defendant is not liable in nuisance if damage occurs only because of the abnormal sensitivity of the claimant's use; the test is the effect on the ordinary person using land in the ordinary way (Robinson v Kilvert [1889]).
options.C: Volenti non fit injuria — Sharma consented to the risk by planting sensitive orchids near an industrial area.
options.D: The Rylands v Fletcher strict liability rule displaces private nuisance here.

Tort Law.explanation: In Robinson v Kilvert [1889] a defendant landlord's activities raised the temperature in a basement, damaging the claimant's sensitive paper goods, but would not have damaged ordinary goods. The court held no nuisance because the damage only occurred due to the abnormal sensitivity of the claimant's use. If ordinary use would be unaffected, there is no nuisance. This 'sensitive claimant' rule limits Sharma's claim. Ex turpi causa (A) has nothing to do with sensitive claimants. Volenti (C) requires an informed consent to the specific risk — using land for orchids does not constitute consent to chemical contamination. Rylands v Fletcher (D) is a separate tort and does not displace nuisance.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A batch of electrical heating pads manufactured by a company causes burns to users. There is no design defect and the manufacturing process conformed to industry standards. The company argues there is no defect for CPA 1987 purposes. Which statement is correct?

options.A: The company is correct — there is no defect if the product conforms to industry standards.
options.B: Under CPA 1987 s.3, a product is defective if its safety is not such as persons generally are entitled to expect; the test is not conformity with industry standards.
options.C: The company is liable only if the user can prove the company was negligent.
options.D: There is no defect because the burns indicate misuse by the consumer.

Tort Law.explanation: Section 3(1) CPA 1987 defines a defect as existing when the safety of the product is not such as persons generally are entitled to expect. The standard is what the public is entitled to expect, taking into account the presentation, reasonably expected use, and the time of supply. Compliance with industry standards is a relevant factor but not conclusive — if the product causes unexpected harm, it may still be defective. Option A is therefore wrong. Option C incorrectly imports a negligence standard — CPA 1987 creates strict liability. Option D is speculative on the facts.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Wharton Magna Community School engages a self-employed swimming instructor to teach pupils. The instructor negligently fails to supervise a child adequately and the child is injured. The school argues it cannot be vicariously liable because the instructor is self-employed. Is the school correct?

options.A: Yes — vicariously liability can only arise in an employment relationship.
options.B: No — the school owes pupils a non-delegable duty of care; it cannot escape liability for the negligent performance of that duty even if it delegates it to an independent contractor (Woodland v Swimming Teachers Association [2013]).
options.C: No — the school is vicariously liable because the instructor was providing an integrated function of the school's business.
options.D: Yes — the parents must sue the instructor directly.

Tort Law.explanation: In Woodland v Swimming Teachers Association [2013] the Supreme Court confirmed that a school owes pupils a non-delegable duty to take reasonable care for their safety during core educational activities. This duty cannot be discharged by delegating it to an independent contractor — the school remains liable for the contractor's negligence. This is distinct from vicarious liability (which requires an employment or quasi-employment relationship). Option A is therefore too narrow. Option C (akin extended vicarious liability) is a separate route which might also be available but B is the more precise and authoritative answer on these facts. Option D is wrong.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.subtopic: Personal injury — primary and extended limitation periods
Tort Law.stem: Priya Mehta was injured in a road accident on 1 March 2021. She did not issue proceedings until 15 April 2024. The defendant argues the claim is time-barred. Under the Limitation Act 1980, is the defendant correct?

options.A: Yes — personal injury claims must be brought within two years of the accident.
options.B: Yes — personal injury claims must be brought within three years of the date of the accident; proceedings were issued more than three years after 1 March 2021.
options.C: No — the primary limitation period is three years from the date of knowledge, and the court has a residual discretion under s.33 to disapply the period.
options.D: No — personal injury claims have a six-year limitation period from the date of the tort.

Tort Law.explanation: Under Limitation Act 1980 s.11, personal injury claims must be brought within three years of: (i) the date on which the cause of action accrued (i.e., the date of the accident); or (ii) the date of knowledge (if later). The accident was on 1 March 2021; three years expires on 1 March 2024. Proceedings issued on 15 April 2024 are therefore prima facie time-barred. However, the court does have a residual discretion under s.33 to extend this period if it is equitable to do so — Option C is partially correct in identifying s.33 but incorrectly states the primary period runs from date of knowledge (it runs from the earlier of date of accrual or date of knowledge). Option A is wrong (two years is not the correct period). Option D applies to general contract/tort claims, not personal injury.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.subtopic: Novus actus interveniens — third party act
Tort Law.stem: Alek Kowalski negligently injures Donna Pearce in a road accident. Pearce is taken to hospital. A nurse, acting independently, administers the wrong medication due to an unrelated error, worsening Pearce's injuries. Kowalski argues the nurse's act breaks the chain of causation. Which statement best reflects the law?

options.A: The nurse's act is a novus actus interveniens breaking the chain of causation because it was an independent act of a third party.
options.B: A subsequent medical error is a foreseeable consequence of the original injury; it does not break the chain of causation unless it is so extraordinary and unforeseeable as to eclipse Kowalski's negligence (Hogan v Bentinck [1949]; Webb v Barclays Bank [2001]).
options.C: Kowalski is only liable for the original injuries, not any aggravation caused by the hospital.
options.D: The chain of causation is automatically broken by any independent act of a third party.

Tort Law.explanation: Medical treatment following an injury is a foreseeable consequence of the original negligence. A subsequent medical error will only break the chain of causation if it is so extraordinary and unforeseeable that it eclipses the defendant's original negligence. Ordinary medical errors in treating injuries caused by the defendant do not generally break the chain (Webb v Barclays Bank [2001]). Option A and D overstate the novus actus doctrine. Option C would unfairly limit the defendant's liability by excluding foreseeable consequences of the original tort.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Pat Hollowell stores a large quantity of industrial solvent on his land. A pipe fractures and solvent seeps underground, eventually contaminating Hassan Nazari's water well on adjacent land. Nazari claims under Rylands v Fletcher. Hollowell argues there has been no escape because the solvent stayed underground. Is Hollowell correct?

options.A: Yes — Rylands v Fletcher requires the substance to escape above ground and be visible.
options.B: No — escape for Rylands v Fletcher purposes means escape from the defendant's land to the claimant's land; underground seepage satisfies this requirement.
options.C: No — but Nazari must prove Hollowell was negligent in storing the solvent.
options.D: Yes — the Rylands v Fletcher rule has been absorbed into negligence and no longer applies as an independent cause of action.

Tort Law.explanation: Escape under Rylands v Fletcher means escape from the defendant's control and onto or affecting the claimant's land — it does not require the substance to escape above ground or to be visible. Underground contamination that reaches and damages the claimant's property satisfies the escape requirement. Option A imports an unjustified 'above ground' or 'visible' limitation. Option C incorrectly introduces a negligence requirement — Rylands is strict liability (subject to defences). Option D is incorrect — although the House of Lords in Cambridge Water v Eastern Counties Leather [1994] limited the rule, they reaffirmed it as an independent head of liability.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Frank Hollis sees on live television news footage a disaster unfolding at Wharton Magna sports ground. His son is among those injured. Hollis suffers recognised psychiatric illness. He sues the event organiser as a secondary victim. Which Alcock control mechanism is most likely to defeat his claim?

options.A: Hollis does not satisfy the proximity of relationship requirement because a parent-child relationship is not sufficiently close.
options.B: Hollis does not satisfy the proximity in time and space requirement: witnessing events on live television does not satisfy the requirement to be present at the scene or its immediate aftermath (Alcock v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire [1991]).
options.C: Hollis cannot claim because he suffered psychiatric harm and not physical injury.
options.D: Hollis cannot claim as a secondary victim because the disaster was not caused by a single identifiable defendant.

Tort Law.explanation: In Alcock v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire [1991] (Hillsborough litigation) the House of Lords held that a secondary victim must satisfy three requirements: (i) a close tie of love and affection with the primary victim; (ii) proximity in time and space — present at the accident or its immediate aftermath; and (iii) perception of the accident through their own unaided senses. Watching events on live television does not satisfy the direct perception requirement, even if the claimant knows a relative is involved. Lord Ackner confirmed this expressly in Alcock. Option A is wrong — parent-child presumptively satisfies the close tie requirement. Option C is wrong — recognised psychiatric illness suffices. Option D is not a recognised Alcock mechanism.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Victoria Ashworth attends a motor racing event at Wharton Magna Circuit. A car loses control in an unexpected manner due to a defective barrier installed by the organisers and debris strikes her. The organisers argue volenti non fit injuria because Ashworth knew racing was inherently dangerous. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Volenti is a complete defence because spectators at motor racing events consent to all inherent risks.
options.B: Volenti requires consent to the specific risk that materialised; Ashworth may have consented to the ordinary risks of motor racing but not to the additional risk created by the organisers' negligence in installing a defective barrier.
options.C: Volenti does not apply to spectators at sporting events; contributory negligence is the appropriate defence.
options.D: Ashworth's claim is defeated by ex turpi causa because attending a dangerous sport is an illegal activity.

Tort Law.explanation: Volenti requires that the claimant freely and voluntarily agreed to accept the specific legal risk, not merely that they were aware of general danger. A spectator at a motorsport event consents to the inherent risks of the sport (e.g., a car leaving the track in a normal racing incident) but does not consent to the additional risk created by the defendant's negligence (e.g., a defective barrier). The organisers cannot rely on volenti to cover their own negligence in maintaining safety infrastructure. Option A overstates the scope of consent. Option C is incorrect — volenti can apply to spectators, but on these facts it fails for the reason in B. Option D is plainly wrong.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.subtopic: Duty to report — money laundering and tort claims
Tort Law.stem: Diana Pelletier is a solicitor advising Northgate Couriers on a personal injury claim brought against the company. During the case, she discovers that Northgate has been engaging in systematic insurance fraud — submitting inflated personal injury claims against third parties. What is Pelletier's primary professional obligation?

options.A: Pelletier must continue to act for Northgate regardless, as her duty of confidentiality to her client is paramount.
options.B: Pelletier should make a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) to the National Crime Agency under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 and must not tip off Northgate; she should also consider whether she can continue to act.
options.C: Pelletier should inform the opposing claimants directly so they can take appropriate action.
options.D: Pelletier can continue to act provided she does not personally assist in the fraudulent activities.

Tort Law.explanation: Insurance fraud generates criminal proceeds within the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002. A solicitor who has knowledge or suspicion of money laundering must make a SAR to the NCA under POCA 2002 s.330 (or s.338 for a permitted disclosure prior to proceeding). The tipping-off provisions in POCA 2002 s.333A mean Pelletier must not inform Northgate of the report. SRA Code para 7.1 also requires solicitors not to assist in unlawful conduct. Option A is wrong — client confidentiality does not override statutory reporting obligations under POCA 2002. Option C would likely constitute tipping off if Northgate learned indirectly. Option D underestimates the statutory obligation.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.subtopic: Witness coaching and duty to the court
Tort Law.stem: Ravi Sharma is a solicitor representing Hassan Nazari in a nuisance claim. Before the trial, Nazari asks Sharma to go through the likely questions he will be asked and to suggest how he should phrase his answers to make them more persuasive. Sharma is unsure whether this crosses any professional boundaries. What should Sharma do?

options.A: Sharma can coach Nazari as fully as he wishes — a solicitor's duty is to advance his client's case as effectively as possible.
options.B: Sharma may properly prepare Nazari by explaining court procedure, discussing the issues, and reviewing documents; but Sharma must not coach Nazari to give specific answers or to present false or misleading evidence — this would breach the duty not to mislead the court (SRA Code paras 1.4 and 2.6).
options.C: Sharma should refuse to prepare Nazari at all to avoid any risk of witness coaching.
options.D: Sharma can suggest how to phrase answers provided the substance is not changed.

Tort Law.explanation: There is a clear distinction between legitimate witness preparation and impermissible witness coaching. Legitimate preparation includes explaining court procedure, discussing the evidence and issues in the case, and reviewing documents. It does not include suggesting specific answers, rehearsing testimony, or advising on how to present evidence in a more persuasive or misleading way. SRA Code para 1.4 requires solicitors to act with integrity, and para 2.6 prohibits making or using false or misleading statements. Option A is wrong — the duty to the court overrides the duty to advance the client's case when these conflict. Option C is excessively cautious. Option D is incorrect because suggesting phrasing amounts to coaching regardless of whether the substance is technically unchanged.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Diana Pelletier agrees to act for Priya Mehta on a personal injury claim under a conditional fee agreement (CFA). Pelletier does not explain the financial implications of the CFA or the potential deduction from damages for the success fee. Which SRA obligation does Pelletier most clearly breach?

options.A: The duty to act in clients' best interests (SRA Code para 1.2) and the obligation to give clients sufficient information about costs, including the effect of any CFA, to allow them to make informed decisions.
options.B: The duty not to mislead the court.
options.C: The duty to maintain client confidentiality.
options.D: The obligation to report material information to opposing solicitors.

Tort Law.explanation: SRA Code para 1.2 requires solicitors to act in clients' best interests. SRA Code para 8.6 requires solicitors to give clients the best information they reasonably can about the likely overall cost of their matter and how it will be affected by a CFA. Failure to explain a CFA's financial implications — including the success fee that will be deducted from the client's damages — is a breach of the costs information obligation and the duty to act in the client's best interests. The other options are not engaged by a failure to explain costs: B relates to conduct before the court, C relates to disclosure to third parties, and D is not a recognised standalone duty in this context.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Which of the following statements best describes the principle of separate legal personality established in Salomon v Salomon [1897]?

options.A: A company is a legal person distinct from its members, with its own legal rights and liabilities
options.B: A company is merely an aggregation of its members with no separate existence at law
options.C: A company's legal personality exists only insofar as it benefits the members
options.D: Members of a company are personally liable for all company debts regardless of incorporation

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A is correct: Salomon established that incorporation creates a separate legal entity. B is wrong because the company IS a distinct legal person—not merely an aggregation. C is wrong because legal personality exists independently, not conditionally on member benefit. D is wrong because incorporation provides limited liability; members are liable only to the extent of their shares.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Marcus Bridgewater is considering setting up a new trading venture with two business associates, Graham Grange and Pat Hollowell. They want to avoid creating a separate legal entity and prefer simple management. Which business structure is most appropriate?

options.A: Partnership, which is an unincorporated association with no separate legal personality
options.B: Limited Liability Partnership (LLP), which provides legal separation from personal assets
options.C: Sole trader, which requires Marcus Bridgewater to trade alone without partners
options.D: Private Company Limited by Shares, which requires formal incorporation

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A is correct: Partnership allows multiple parties without incorporation and is relatively simple to establish. B is wrong because LLP is a separate legal entity (incorporated). C is wrong because Marcus has two partners, so sole trader is not appropriate. D is wrong because it requires incorporation and registration.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: What is the primary purpose of the Memorandum of Association when incorporating a private company limited by shares?

options.A: To set out the company's objects, constitution, and shareholders' undertaking to be bound by the articles
options.B: To record the names and residential addresses of all members of the board of directors
options.C: To specify the detailed procedures for holding annual general meetings
options.D: To regulate the day-to-day management and internal governance of the company

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A is correct: The Memorandum (CA 2006) evidences the company's constitutional status and members' agreement. B is wrong; addresses are recorded elsewhere. C is wrong; AGM procedure is in the Articles. D is wrong because that is the Articles' function.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Under CA 2006 s.172, what is a director's primary duty regarding the company's affairs?

options.A: To promote the success of the company for the benefit of its members as a whole
options.B: To act solely in accordance with the wishes of the largest shareholder
options.C: To ensure all company decisions are approved by all shareholders before implementation
options.D: To act in a manner that maximizes shareholder value above all other considerations

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A is correct: CA 2006 s.172 requires directors to promote company success for member benefit. B is wrong because duty is to the company and all members, not one shareholder. C is wrong because directors have delegated authority; not all decisions require shareholder approval. D is wrong because it ignores other stakeholders and constraints.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Under CA 2006 s.175, if a director has a direct conflict of interest in a proposed transaction, what is the correct procedure?

options.A: The director must disclose the conflict; the company may authorize the conflict with appropriate director involvement
options.B: The director may proceed without disclosure if the conflict is minor
options.C: The conflict is automatically void and cannot be cured
options.D: The director must immediately resign from the board

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A is correct: CA 2006 s.175 requires disclosure, and conflicts can be authorized by uninterested directors or shareholders. B is wrong; disclosure is always required. C is wrong; authorization can validate conflicts. D is wrong; resignation is not mandatory.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: In a private company, what is the key advantage of using CA 2006 ss.288-300 written resolutions compared to holding an in-person general meeting?

options.A: Written resolutions are faster and more cost-effective, requiring no meeting to be convened
options.B: Written resolutions do not require shareholder agreement or majority support
options.C: Written resolutions allow a single shareholder to block any decision
options.D: Written resolutions have greater legal force and cannot be challenged

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A is correct: Written resolutions under CA 2006 ss.288-300 avoid the need for a meeting and are more efficient. B is wrong; agreement is still required. C is wrong; all shareholders voting together hold power. D is wrong; they have the same legal force but different procedures.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: What is the primary purpose of pre-emption rights inserted in a company's articles of association?

options.A: To give existing shareholders the first opportunity to purchase shares before external third parties
options.B: To allow shareholders to compel the company to buy back their shares at any time
options.C: To allow directors to prevent any share transfer for any reason
options.D: To ensure that only the board of directors may hold company shares

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A is correct: Pre-emption rights protect existing shareholders' interests and maintain control. B is wrong; share buyback is a separate matter. C is wrong; articles cannot give blanket veto power. D is wrong; multiple shareholders may hold shares.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Atlas Contracts Ltd has profits of £100,000 in its latest financial year but carries accumulated losses of £250,000 from prior years. Can it lawfully pay a dividend under CA 2006 s.830?

options.A: No, because CA 2006 s.830 restricts dividends to distributable profits, and accumulated losses exceed current profits
options.B: Yes, because Atlas Contracts has current year profits regardless of accumulated losses
options.C: No, unless Atlas Contracts obtains a court order to cancel the accumulated losses
options.D: Yes, but only if approved by a special resolution of shareholders

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A is correct: CA 2006 s.830 requires that profits available for distribution exceed accumulated losses. B is wrong; prior losses affect distributable profits. C is wrong; accumulated losses cannot be cancelled by court order. D is wrong; shareholder approval does not override the statutory test.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: What is the key distinction between a fixed charge and a floating charge over company assets?

options.A: A fixed charge secures assets that remain static, while a floating charge applies to current assets only
options.B: A fixed charge is a charge on specific identified assets with restricted dealing rights; a floating charge applies to assets subject to change
options.C: A fixed charge cannot be registered at Companies House, but a floating charge must be
options.D: A floating charge has priority over a fixed charge in an insolvency

Business Law & Practice.explanation: B is correct: Fixed charges restrict the chargor's dealing; floating charges allow unrestricted asset dealing until crystallization (Re Spectrum Plus [2005]). A is wrong because both types can secure current or fixed assets. C is wrong; both must be registered. D is wrong; fixed charges typically have priority.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Under IA 1986 s.214, the wrongful trading provision applies when a company director continues trading whilst knowing the company is insolvent. What is the consequence?

options.A: The company's creditors must pursue a separate action; there are no consequences for the director
options.B: The director may be required by the court to contribute to company assets and faces potential disqualification
options.C: The director is disqualified from holding office for a period determined by the court
options.D: The director is automatically imprisoned for a fixed term

Business Law & Practice.explanation: B is correct: IA 1986 s.214 allows courts to order contribution and disqualification. A is wrong; the liquidator can pursue the s.214 claim. C is wrong; contribution is also a remedy. D is wrong; wrongful trading is civil, not criminal.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Marcus Bridgewater incorporated a company, Bridgewater Consulting Ltd, to run his property consultancy. He personally guarantees all client contracts and uses his personal bank account for company payments. When a major client sues for breach, they seek to pierce the veil and hold Marcus personally liable. Which of the following best reflects the law?

options.A: The veil is automatically pierced when any director uses personal resources for company purposes
options.B: The veil cannot be pierced under Adams v Cape Industries [1990] unless the company is a mere façade or alter ego used to evade existing legal obligations
options.C: The veil can be pierced because Marcus personally guaranteed contracts
options.D: The veil can be pierced because Marcus used his personal bank account

Business Law & Practice.explanation: B is correct: Adams v Cape establishes a narrow test; veil-piercing is exceptional. Marcus Bridgewater’s personal guarantees and mixed finances alone do not pierce the veil. A is wrong; there is no automatic piercing. C is wrong; personal guarantees are legitimate security. D is wrong; mixed finances do not justify piercing.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Victoria Ashworth has been appointed a non-executive director of Nazari Properties Ltd. She is not an accountant or finance expert. During her first board meeting, she votes to approve a major acquisition without reviewing the financial documentation, on the basis that ‘the finance director must have checked it.’ The acquisition turns out to be severely overpriced. Can Victoria defend herself by relying on CA 2006 s.174?

options.A: Yes, because s.174 applies a subjective standard only
options.B: No, although CA 2006 s.174 permits some reliance, Victoria must exercise the care and skill expected of a director, which includes some review of material information
options.C: Yes, s.174 allows directors to rely entirely on other directors’ expertise without scrutiny
options.D: No, CA 2006 s.174 has been repealed and directors face strict liability

Business Law & Practice.explanation: B is correct: CA 2006 s.174 requires the care of a reasonably competent director; blind reliance on others without any review breaches this duty (Re City Equitable Fire Insurance [1925]). A is wrong; the standard includes competent director conduct. C is wrong; reliance must be reasonable. D is wrong; s.174 remains in force.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd (a private company) proposes to lend £50,000 to its managing director, Pat Hollowell, to help her purchase a house. Under CA 2006 s.197, what is the correct position?

options.A: The loan is permitted without restriction because Hollowell Engineering is a private company
options.B: The loan may be made only if the company obtains prior written shareholder approval and complies with s.197
options.C: The loan is automatically prohibited and void
options.D: The loan may proceed without approval because Pat Hollowell is a director-employee

Business Law & Practice.explanation: B is correct: CA 2006 s.197 requires written approval of members before directors’ loans are made (applies to private companies too). A is wrong; private company status does not exempt from s.197. C is wrong; loans are not automatically void if properly authorized. D is wrong; employment does not authorize loans without approval.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Leila Nazari holds 20% of shares in Nazari Properties Ltd. The board (dominated by Hassan Nazari who holds 70%) has voted to pay inflated management fees to a maintenance company connected to Hassan. Leila believes this is unfairly prejudicial to her as a shareholder. She seeks a remedy under CA 2006 s.994. Which of the following best reflects the law?

options.A: Leila’s claim will fail because she is not a member of the board
options.B: Leila can apply for relief if she can show the conduct of the company’s affairs is or has been unfairly prejudicial to her interests as a shareholder
options.C: Leila can challenge only if she proves Hassan breached a specific statutory duty
options.D: Leila’s remedy is limited to a proportionate share of company assets

Business Law & Practice.explanation: B is correct: CA 2006 s.994 allows members to seek relief for unfairly prejudicial conduct; inflated fees benefiting insiders can constitute such conduct. A is wrong; non-directors may bring s.994 claims. C is wrong; s.994 does not require proof of statutory breach. D is wrong; courts have broad remedial powers under s.996.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd has an authorized capital of £500,000. The articles authorize directors to allot up to £200,000 of new shares without further shareholder approval. The directors wish to allot £250,000 of new ordinary shares. What is the position under CA 2006 s.551?

options.A: The allotment is prohibited because it exceeds the authorized amount
options.B: The allotment requires shareholder approval via ordinary resolution because it exceeds the directors’ delegated authority
options.C: The allotment may proceed without approval because the authorized capital includes the new shares
options.D: The allotment is valid without approval but the company must increase authorized capital first

Business Law & Practice.explanation: B is correct: CA 2006 s.551 requires authority for share allotment; exceeding delegated authority requires fresh shareholder approval. A is wrong; authorized capital is separate from authority to allot. C is wrong; authorization limits apply. D is wrong; authorized capital amendment is separate from allotment authority.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd granted a floating charge to its bank over its stock and equipment. The charge documentation stated that the charge would ‘crystallize automatically upon the company missing a payment.’ When Hollowell missed a payment, did the charge crystallize automatically under the clause?

options.A: Yes, because the clause is clear and unambiguous
options.B: Yes, but only if the bank took steps to enforce the charge
options.C: No, crystallization requires either event of default (with notice) or specific action by the charge holder, not an automatic mechanical trigger
options.D: No, automatic crystallization clauses are void as they conflict with the nature of floating charges

Business Law & Practice.explanation: C is correct: Floating charges crystallize upon notice of enforcement or specific trigger (not automatic operation). Automatic clauses are ineffective because they undermine the floating charge nature. A is wrong; mere words do not override principles. B is wrong; enforcement action is not always required, but automatic operation is invalid. D is correct in principle but C is more precise.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Atlas Contracts Ltd created a fixed charge over its Unit 2 premises at Wharton Industrial Park in favour of its lender on 1 April. The company failed to register the charge at Companies House. On 1 May, the lender discovered the omission. Which of the following best reflects the position under CA 2006 s.860?

options.A: The charge is void against the liquidator and creditors because not registered within 21 days
options.B: The charge remains valid despite late registration because the lender had knowledge
options.C: Atlas Contracts may apply to court for late registration; if refused, the charge is void against the liquidator and creditors
options.D: The charge is automatically valid even without registration if it is fixed

Business Law & Practice.explanation: C is correct: CA 2006 s.860 provides that unregistered charges are void against liquidator/creditors; courts may allow late registration. A is wrong; registration can be obtained retrospectively. B is wrong; knowledge does not cure voidness. D is wrong; fixed charges must also be registered.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Atlas Contracts Ltd entered administration on 1 July. In April (three months before), the company paid £20,000 to Hollowell Engineering Ltd, a steel supplier, even though Atlas was already insolvent and struggling to pay other creditors. Under IA 1986 s.239, can the administrator recover this payment?

options.A: No, because the payment occurred more than 3 months before insolvency proceedings
options.B: No, because Hollowell Engineering is not an associate of Atlas Contracts
options.C: Yes, if Atlas Contracts was insolvent at the time and the payment was a preference that put Hollowell Engineering in a better position than in liquidation
options.D: Yes, automatically, without any need to prove insolvency or preference

Business Law & Practice.explanation: C is correct: IA 1986 s.239 allows recovery of preferences made within 6 months if the company was insolvent (or became so) and the recipient was preferred. A is wrong; the 6-month lookback applies. B is wrong; non-associates can also be subject to preference claims. D is wrong; insolvency and preference must be proven.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Atlas Contracts Ltd sold construction equipment worth approximately £80,000 to its director, Owen Davies, for £10,000 cash. This occurred 9 months before Atlas entered liquidation. Atlas was insolvent at that time. Under IA 1986 s.238, can the liquidator recover the equipment or its value?

options.A: No, because the sale occurred more than 6 months before liquidation
options.B: No, because Owen Davies is a director and entitled to preferential treatment
options.C: Yes, if Atlas Contracts received no reasonably equivalent value and was insolvent at the time or became insolvent due to the transaction
options.D: Yes, automatically, but Owen Davies may defend by proving he is an associate

Business Law & Practice.explanation: C is correct: IA 1986 s.238 (2-year lookback for associates such as directors) allows recovery of transactions at undervalue. A is wrong; 2 years apply to associates like Owen Davies as a director. B is wrong; directorship does not immunize from s.238. D is wrong; being an associate expands the claim, not a defence.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers engages a driver who sets their own hours, uses their own vehicle, and is free to work for other courier firms. The driver invoices Northgate Couriers monthly for services rendered and pays their own income tax and National Insurance. Under the Employment Rights Act 1996, what is the driver’s employment status?

options.A: The driver is an employee and entitled to all statutory employment rights
options.B: The driver is a worker but not an employee, with some statutory rights
options.C: The driver is self-employed (independent contractor) with limited statutory rights
options.D: The driver’s status cannot be determined without examining the written contract

Business Law & Practice.explanation: C is correct: The control test, mutual obligation, and absence of integration indicators point to self-employment for this Northgate Couriers driver. A is wrong; the indicators contradict employee status. B is wrong; the facts suggest independent contractor, not worker. D is wrong; the contractual label is not determinative; substance matters.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: A fabrication worker has been employed at Wharton Foods Ltd for 2 years. Without warning, the production manager dismissed him verbally after an argument about work methods. There was no investigation, no disciplinary hearing, and no chance to respond. The worker claims unfair dismissal under the Employment Rights Act 1996. Is his claim likely to succeed?

options.A: No, because the worker has worked for only 2 years and needs 5 years of service
options.B: No, because dismissal is an employer’s prerogative and can be done at will
options.C: Yes, because the procedure was manifestly unfair; even if there were a fair reason, the process was procedurally deficient
options.D: Yes, but only if the worker can prove the manager’s argument was unjustified

Business Law & Practice.explanation: C is correct: Unfair dismissal claims require both fair reason AND fair procedure; the absence of any hearing is grossly procedurally unfair. A is wrong; 2 years’ service is the minimum required, which is met here. B is wrong; unfair dismissal law applies to qualifying employees. D is wrong; procedural fairness is independent of whether the reason was justified.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Wharton Foods Ltd is closing its distribution depot due to falling orders. There are 15 employees at the depot. The company provides 4 weeks’ notice to all staff but does not consult employees about alternatives to redundancy or fair selection criteria. Under the Employment Rights Act 1996, what is the position?

options.A: The redundancies are fair if they are genuinely due to cessation of business activity
options.B: The redundancies are fair because proper notice was given
options.C: The redundancies are unfair because no consultation took place; proper consultation (or its futility) must be shown
options.D: The redundancies are unfair only if employees can prove alternatives to closure existed

Business Law & Practice.explanation: C is correct: Even if redundancy is genuine, unfair dismissal arises from failure to consult (unless consultation would be futile). A is wrong; genuine reason does not preclude unfairness if procedure fails. B is wrong; notice is separate from fair procedure. D is wrong; consultation is required regardless of whether alternatives existed.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.topic: Sale and Purchase of a Business
Business Law & Practice.stem: Bridgewater Developments Ltd is considering acquiring Harlow Builders Ltd, a local construction contractor. Harlow has ongoing client contracts, employment contracts, and environmental liabilities totalling approximately £2m. Marcus Bridgewater is concerned about assuming unknown liabilities. Would an asset purchase or share purchase better protect Bridgewater Developments?

options.A: A share purchase, because the purchasing company inherits the separate legal entity
options.B: Both are equivalent in terms of liability protection
options.C: An asset purchase, because Bridgewater Developments acquires only specified assets and does not assume liabilities unless explicitly agreed
options.D: Neither offers protection; all liabilities transfer automatically

Business Law & Practice.explanation: C is correct: Asset purchase allows selective acquisition; liabilities remain with Harlow Builders unless assumed by Bridgewater Developments. A is wrong; share purchase means acquiring the entire liability-bearing entity. B is wrong; they are fundamentally different. D is wrong; asset purchase avoids automatic liability transfer.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: The board of Hollowell Engineering Ltd is considering a £5m acquisition of a rival firm. Director Victoria Ashworth owns 2% of Hollowell’s shares personally. She also chairs a major customer of the target company and would benefit commercially if the acquisition does not proceed (avoiding competitive displacement). Victoria discloses both potential conflicts. Can she participate in the board decision under CA 2006 ss.172–175?

options.A: No, dual-hat roles always prevent director participation regardless of authorization
options.B: No, Victoria must abstain from voting because she has a material interest
options.C: Yes, Victoria’s minority shareholding is immaterial and cannot constitute a conflict
options.D: Yes, if uninterested directors and/or shareholders authorize the conflict, Victoria may participate and vote

Business Law & Practice.explanation: D is correct: CA 2006 allows conflict authorization by uninterested directors/shareholders after disclosure. A is wrong; authorization is the key, not automatic abstention. B is wrong; authorization can permit participation. C is wrong; even minority holdings can conflict if material.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Derivative Claims and Foss v Harbottle
Business Law & Practice.stem: Under CA 2006 Part 11, when may a shareholder bring a derivative claim on behalf of the company for a breach of directors' duty?

options.A: Never; Foss v Harbottle [1902] is absolute and bars all derivative actions
options.B: Only if the shareholder holds more than 50% of shares and can direct the company
options.C: Only with unanimous shareholder approval and court permission
options.D: If the statutory conditions are met, including that it is prima facie in the company's interests and the shareholder has not authorized or ratified the breach

Business Law & Practice.explanation: D is correct: CA 2006 Part 11 permits derivative claims under statutory conditions (prima facie case, member not authorized/ratified, in company interests). A is wrong; the statutory regime overrides Foss v Harbottle's blanket bar. B is wrong; share percentage is not determinative. C is wrong; statutory procedure applies, not blanket approval requirement.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Corporation Tax and Income Tax Planning
Business Law & Practice.stem: Wharton Foods Ltd has net profits of £150,000. If structured as a company, it pays corporation tax at 25%. The shareholder then receives a dividend of £112,500 (after-tax profit). The shareholder is a higher-rate taxpayer. What is the combined tax burden (corp tax + dividend tax), and does this compare favourably to operating as a sole trader?

options.A: Combined burden is 25% (only corp tax); better than sole trader at 40%
options.B: Combined burden is 50% (25% + 25%); worse than sole trader at 40%
options.C: Combined burden is 37.5% (25% corp + 12.5% dividend); worse than sole trader at 40%
options.D: Combined burden is 42.5%; approximately equal to sole trader and the choice is neutral on tax

Business Law & Practice.explanation: D is correct: Corp tax 25% + dividend tax on distributed profits yields approximately 42.5% combined; a sole trader at the higher rate pays 40–45%. The differential is marginal. A is wrong; ignores dividend tax entirely. B is wrong; there is no 25% dividend tax on all distributions. C is wrong; the dividend tax calculation is incorrect.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Bridgewater Developments Ltd wishes to acquire Harlow Builders Ltd via a leveraged buyout. Harlow Builders would be acquired by a newco financed by bank debt. The acquisition agreement proposes that Harlow Builders’ assets be mortgaged to secure the acquisition debt. Under CA 2006 s.678, is this permissible?

options.A: Yes, because Harlow Builders’ assets provide security for the loan
options.B: Yes, if the directors believe it is in Harlow Builders’ interests and obtain shareholder approval
options.C: No, unless Harlow Builders is a private company and the parties are all connected entities
options.D: No, because this constitutes financial assistance for the acquisition of Harlow Builders’ shares, which is prohibited

Business Law & Practice.explanation: D is correct: CA 2006 s.678 prohibits financial assistance (loans, guarantees, mortgages of assets) for the acquisition of the company’s own shares. A is wrong; security does not cure the prohibition. B is wrong; director belief and shareholder approval do not override s.678. C is wrong; the prohibition applies regardless of company type or connection.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Atlas Contracts Ltd entered insolvency with debts of £2m and no assets. During the 6 months before insolvency, Owen Davies and the board continued trading, incurring new debts of £800,000. The liquidator claims wrongful trading under IA 1986 s.214. Owen Davies argues they had ‘some prospect’ of recovery. Under IA 1986 s.214(2)(b), what is the correct legal test?

options.A: Directors are liable only if they acted dishonestly or with reckless indifference
options.B: Directors are liable if any loss occurred and they continued trading without creditor consent
options.C: Directors are liable if they failed to take every possible step to minimize losses
options.D: Directors are liable if, when insolvent, they knew or ought to have known there was no reasonable prospect of avoiding liquidation and took no steps to minimize creditor loss

Business Law & Practice.explanation: D is correct: IA 1986 s.214(2)(b) requires knowledge (actual or constructive) of insolvency with no reasonable prospect AND failure to minimize loss. A is wrong; honesty is irrelevant. B is wrong; the test requires insolvency awareness and failure to minimize loss. C is wrong; ‘every possible step’ is not the standard.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd outsources its IT function to a specialist IT company under a 3-year service contract. Under the TUPE Regulations 2006, do Hollowell’s existing IT employees automatically transfer to the IT company?

options.A: No, outsourcing contracts are never caught by TUPE; only asset transfers trigger TUPE
options.B: No, because the IT company is a separate company and employment contracts cannot transfer
options.C: Yes, all employees must transfer automatically; TUPE always applies to outsourcing
options.D: Yes, if there is a relevant transfer (change of service provider) of an organised grouping with an identifiable economic entity

Business Law & Practice.explanation: D is correct: TUPE Regulations 2006 may apply to service provision changes if there is a change in provider of a recognised service with economic continuity. A is wrong; TUPE applies beyond asset transfers. B is wrong; employment contracts can transfer by operation of law under TUPE. C is wrong; outsourcing is not automatically covered.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd’s bank holds a charge and imposes restrictive covenants on expenditure and dividends. The bank appoints a representative who attends board meetings, reviews financial information, and effectively directs major decisions, though Pat Hollowell and the formal board retain nominal authority. Is the bank representative likely to be classified as a shadow director under CA 2006 s.251, subject to directors’ duties?

options.A: No, because the bank representative is not formally appointed as a director
options.B: No, because a chargee’s representative cannot be a shadow director; they have distinct rights
options.C: Yes, but only if the person holds a formal qualification as a director
options.D: Yes, if the person is accustomed to act in accordance with whose instructions the directors are accustomed to act, the person is a shadow director regardless of formal title

Business Law & Practice.explanation: D is correct: CA 2006 s.251 defines shadow directors as those whose instructions directors habitually follow; formal title is irrelevant. The bank’s representative, by effectively directing major decisions at Hollowell Engineering, fits this definition. A is wrong; shadow director status is determined by substance, not formal appointment. B is wrong; chargeholder representatives can be shadow directors. C is wrong; qualifications are irrelevant to shadow director status.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Duty to act within powers — s.171 CA 2006
Business Law & Practice.stem: Victoria Ashworth is a director of Ashworth Developments Ltd. The company's articles restrict disposals of land to those approved by a shareholders' resolution. Ashworth sells a parcel of land without obtaining that resolution, believing the sale is commercially advantageous. Which CA 2006 duty has she most clearly breached?

options.A: Duty to promote the success of the company (s.172).
options.B: Duty to act within powers — a director must act in accordance with the company's constitution and only exercise powers for the purposes for which they are conferred (s.171).
options.C: Duty to avoid conflicts of interest (s.175).
options.D: Duty to exercise independent judgement (s.173).

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Section 171 CA 2006 requires a director to act in accordance with the company's constitution and to exercise powers only for the purposes for which they are conferred. Disposing of land in breach of an express articles restriction is a direct breach of s.171, regardless of whether the director thought it commercially advantageous.
