Option A (s.172) concerns the director's purpose in acting, not constitutional compliance. Option C (s.175) concerns conflicts between the director's personal interests and the company. Option D (s.173) concerns not fettering discretion and is not engaged here.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Duty to promote success — s.172 CA 2006
Business Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth is a director of a profitable private company. He approves a significant donation to a local charity, believing this will enhance the company's reputation in Wharton Magna. A minority shareholder objects, arguing the donation is not in the company's interests. Which statement best describes the legal position?

options.A: James has breached s.172 because charitable donations are never in the company's interests.
options.B: James has not breached s.172 if he acted in good faith in the way he considered most likely to promote the success of the company for the benefit of its members as a whole, taking into account factors including the long-term impact on community relationships.
options.C: James has breached s.172 because he failed to obtain shareholder approval before donating.
options.D: James has not breached s.172 because directors have an unfettered discretion to donate company funds.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Section 172 CA 2006 requires a director to act in good faith in the way he considers, in his honest judgement, would be most likely to promote the success of the company for the benefit of its members as a whole. The standard is subjective: what did the director honestly believe? Section 172(1) lists factors to have regard to, including the long-term interests and the impact on community and environment. A genuine belief that a charitable donation improves community relations and the company's long-term prospects satisfies s.172. Option A incorrectly states a blanket rule. Option C is incorrect — shareholder approval is not required for every donation unless the articles specify it. Option D is too broad and wrong.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Duty to avoid conflicts of interest — s.175 CA 2006
Business Law & Practice.stem: Gerald Crane is a non-executive director of Northgate Couriers Ltd. He is also a partner in Crane & Pelletier Solicitors, which is retained to advise a rival company on a potential acquisition of Northgate's main client. Crane does not disclose this. Which duty is most clearly engaged?

options.A: Duty to declare interest in proposed transaction (s.177).
options.B: Duty to avoid conflicts of interest — s.175 CA 2006 requires a director to avoid situations where he has, or can have, a direct or indirect interest that conflicts, or possibly may conflict, with the interests of the company.
options.C: Duty to act within powers (s.171).
options.D: Duty not to accept benefits from third parties (s.176).

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Section 175 CA 2006 imposes a duty to avoid situations where the director has, or can have, an interest that conflicts or may conflict with the interests of the company. This is particularly applicable to exploiting property, information or opportunities, and extends to advisory relationships where the director's firm is advising a party adverse to a key company client. The conflict must be authorised by the board (s.175(4)(b)) or by a shareholders' resolution. Section 177 (A) applies to transactions between the director and the company — there is no such transaction here. Section 176 (D) concerns benefits received from third parties in connection with being a director.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Duty to declare interest — s.177 CA 2006
Business Law & Practice.stem: Victoria Ashworth, a director of Ashworth Developments Ltd, intends to contract with the company for the supply of her personal car to the company. She does not declare her interest at a board meeting. What is the consequence?

options.A: The contract is automatically void.
options.B: Victoria has breached s.177 CA 2006, which requires a director to declare the nature and extent of any direct or indirect interest in a proposed transaction with the company before it is entered into.
options.C: The contract is voidable only if the other directors were unaware of the interest.
options.D: Victoria must seek shareholder ratification under s.239 before the contract can proceed.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Section 177 CA 2006 requires a director to declare any direct or indirect interest in a proposed transaction or arrangement with the company, before the transaction is entered into. Declaration must be made at a board meeting, by notice in writing, or by general notice. Failure to declare is a breach of duty. The contract is not automatically void (A); however, the company may have remedies depending on the circumstances. Option C introduces a knowledge qualification that does not appear in s.177. Option D (s.239 ratification) is a mechanism for ratifying breach of duty, not a precondition for entering the transaction.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Salomon principle and lifting the veil
Business Law & Practice.stem: Pat Hollowell forms Hollowell Contracts Ltd and transfers all his business assets to it in order to avoid paying a debt he personally owes to Hassan Nazari. Hollowell is the sole director and shareholder. Nazari seeks to recover the debt from Hollowell personally. On which basis could a court pierce the corporate veil?

options.A: A court can never pierce the veil because the company has its own separate legal personality (Salomon v Salomon [1897]).
options.B: A court may pierce the veil where the corporate structure is being used as a façade to evade legal obligations — but this is a narrow jurisdiction; the abuse of the corporate form to evade an existing legal obligation may justify lifting the veil (Prest v Petrodel Resources [2013]).
options.C: A court will pierce the veil whenever a company has a single shareholder.
options.D: A court will pierce the veil automatically where the company is newly formed and insolvent.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: The Salomon principle confers separate legal personality. However, the Supreme Court in Prest v Petrodel Resources [2013] confirmed that the veil may be pierced in the very narrow circumstance where a person is under a legal obligation and deliberately interposes a company to evade that obligation. On these facts, Hollowell transferred assets to the company specifically to avoid paying Nazari — this is the paradigm case for considering veil-piercing. Option A overstates the Salomon principle by treating it as absolute. Option C is wrong — single-member status alone is irrelevant. Option D is incorrect — insolvency is addressed by separate statutory rules, not automatic veil-piercing.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Allotment authority — s.549 and s.551 CA 2006
Business Law & Practice.stem: The board of Ashworth Developments Ltd wishes to allot new ordinary shares to raise capital. The company's articles contain no express power. What authority is required?

options.A: No authority is required — the board has inherent power to allot shares.
options.B: The directors require authority from the shareholders by ordinary resolution or under the articles (s.551 CA 2006); the default prohibition on allotment without authority (s.549) applies to private companies with only one class of shares only if the articles do not disapply it.
options.C: A special resolution (75% majority) is always required to allot new shares.
options.D: The directors require Court approval before allotting new shares.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under CA 2006 s.549, directors must not allot shares unless authorised to do so by the articles or by an ordinary resolution of the company. Section 551 permits the shareholders to grant authority by ordinary resolution specifying the maximum amount of shares and an expiry date (no longer than five years). However, s.550 provides that where a private company has only one class of shares, the directors may allot shares of that class without s.549/551 authority unless the articles restrict or prohibit this. Option A is wrong — s.549 prohibits allotment without authority. Option C is wrong — an ordinary resolution (simple majority) suffices. Option D is wrong — court approval is not required.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ravi Sharma holds 25% of shares in a family company. The majority shareholders, led by Victoria Ashworth, pass a resolution to dilute his shareholding without offering him pre-emption rights and exclude him from management. Sharma wishes to challenge this. What is his most effective remedy?

options.A: Apply for compulsory winding-up on just and equitable grounds (IA 1986 s.122(1)(g)).
options.B: Petition for unfair prejudice under CA 2006 s.994, seeking an order requiring the majority to purchase his shares at a fair value.
options.C: Bring a derivative claim under CA 2006 s.260 on behalf of the company.
options.D: Apply for a personal injunction restraining the majority from voting.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Section 994 CA 2006 allows a member to petition the court on the ground that the company's affairs are being or have been conducted in a manner that is unfairly prejudicial to his interests. Courts regularly grant relief in the form of an order that the majority buy out the minority at a fair (undiluted) value. Exclusion from management and dilution without pre-emption are paradigm examples of unfair prejudice. Winding-up (A) is a remedy of last resort and impractical for a going concern. A derivative claim (C) is for wrongs done to the company, not wrongs to shareholders personally. A personal injunction (D) is a discretionary remedy and not the primary route for this type of dispute.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Three shareholders in Wharton Magna Tech Ltd enter a shareholders' agreement requiring unanimous consent before any new shares are issued. The company later issues shares with only majority board approval. Can the shareholders enforce the agreement against the company?

options.A: No — shareholders' agreements are binding only between the shareholders who sign them and cannot bind the company.
options.B: Yes — a shareholders' agreement signed by the company binds the company as a party to it, and breach entitles the parties to contractual remedies.
options.C: No — only provisions in the articles of association can restrict the directors' powers.
options.D: Yes — the Companies Act 2006 automatically incorporates shareholders' agreements into the constitution.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A shareholders' agreement is a private contract. If the company is a party to the agreement (which is common practice), it is bound by its terms as a matter of contract law and breach gives rise to contractual remedies including damages and injunctive relief. Option A is wrong if the company is a signatory. Option C is incorrect — it is possible to restrict directors' powers by contract as well as through the articles, though articles have broader constitutional effect. Option D is wrong — the Companies Act does not automatically incorporate shareholders' agreements; they remain separate contracts unless the articles cross-refer.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers Ltd is insolvent. James Ashworth, a director, continues to trade for a further three months, knowing the company cannot avoid insolvent liquidation, and incurring further debts. He argues he was trying to trade out of the difficulty. Which statement most accurately describes his potential liability?

options.A: James is personally liable for fraudulent trading under s.213 IA 1986 because he intended to defraud creditors.
options.B: James may be liable for wrongful trading under s.214 IA 1986 if the court finds he knew or ought to have concluded there was no reasonable prospect of avoiding insolvent liquidation and failed to take every step to minimise potential loss to creditors.
options.C: James has no liability because he acted in good faith in trying to rescue the business.
options.D: James is automatically liable for all debts incurred after the company became technically insolvent.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Section 214 IA 1986 imposes liability for wrongful trading where a director knew or ought to have concluded (applying both a subjective and an objective standard) that there was no reasonable prospect of avoiding insolvent liquidation, and failed to take every step with a view to minimising the potential loss to the company's creditors. Good faith is not a complete defence — the objective standard applies. Fraudulent trading (A) requires dishonest intent to defraud creditors, which is a higher threshold. Option C is wrong because good faith does not exclude the objective limb. Option D is wrong — liability is not automatic on technical insolvency.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd, shortly before entering administration, repays a loan made by director Victoria Ashworth in full while other creditors remain unpaid. The administrator seeks to set aside this payment. Under which provision and on what basis?

options.A: Section 238 IA 1986 (transaction at undervalue) — the repayment was at an undervalue.
options.B: Section 239 IA 1986 (preference) — the company put a creditor (Victoria) in a better position than she would have been in on an insolvent liquidation; and because Victoria is a connected person, desire to prefer is presumed.
options.C: Section 423 IA 1986 (transaction defrauding creditors) — the company intended to defraud other creditors.
options.D: Section 214 IA 1986 (wrongful trading) — the directors continued to trade when insolvent.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Section 239 IA 1986 allows a liquidator/administrator to challenge a preference — a transaction that puts a creditor or surety in a better position than they would have been in on insolvent liquidation. The company must have been influenced by a desire to prefer. Where the preferred person is a connected person (which Victoria Ashworth is, as a director), s.239(6) creates a rebuttable presumption of desire to prefer. The look-back period is two years for connected persons. Section 238 (A) applies to transactions at an undervalue, not full repayment of genuine debt. Section 423 (C) requires an intention to defraud. Section 214 (D) concerns wrongful trading, not asset dissipation.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd is experiencing severe financial difficulty. Its bank holds a qualifying floating charge. The bank appoints an administrator out of court. Which statement about the administration moratorium is correct?

options.A: The moratorium only applies to secured creditors.
options.B: Once an administrator is appointed, a moratorium arises automatically preventing any creditor from taking or continuing legal proceedings, enforcing security or repossessing goods without the administrator's consent or court permission (Schedule B1 IA 1986).
options.C: The moratorium can be lifted by any creditor on 7 days' notice.
options.D: The moratorium does not prevent a landlord from forfeiting the company's lease.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Schedule B1 IA 1986 paragraphs 42 and 43 provide that upon appointment of an administrator, an automatic moratorium arises. No legal process may be instituted or continued, no steps may be taken to enforce any security or repossess goods under hire-purchase, and no landlord may exercise a right of forfeiture — all without administrator's consent or court permission. The moratorium applies to all creditors, both secured and unsecured (A is wrong). Individual creditors cannot lift it unilaterally (C is wrong). Forfeiture by a landlord is expressly caught (D is wrong).

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Employment status — employee vs worker vs independent contractor
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers engages Donna Pearce to make deliveries. She uses her own vehicle, sets her own hours, can decline individual jobs, but in practice works exclusively for Northgate and has done so for two years. What employment status is she most likely to have?

options.A: Employee — because she works exclusively for Northgate.
options.B: Independent contractor — because she uses her own vehicle and can decline jobs.
options.C: Worker — she personally performs work, is not in business on her own account, and is integrated into Northgate's operations, satisfying the limb (b) definition in ERA 1996 s.230(3).
options.D: She has no employment status because she is self-employed.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: ERA 1996 s.230(3) defines a 'worker' as someone who personally performs work under a contract with an employer and is not undertaking a business carried on by them. The Supreme Court in Uber BV v Aslam [2021] found that individuals operating in similar circumstances (using own vehicles, technically free to decline work, but in practice integrated into a platform's operations) were workers rather than independent contractors. On these facts, the inability to substitute others, practical exclusivity, and integration into Northgate's operation point to worker status. Full employee status (A) requires mutuality of obligation and control more firmly established than described. Option D is wrong — the law does not recognise 'no status'.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Unfair dismissal — qualifying period and fair reasons
Business Law & Practice.stem: Alek Kowalski has worked for Hollowell Engineering Ltd for 18 months when he is dismissed for persistent lateness despite written warnings. He wishes to claim unfair dismissal. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Kowalski cannot claim because he has not completed two years' qualifying service.
options.B: Kowalski can claim immediately as the dismissal relates to conduct.
options.C: Kowalski can claim because 18 months' service is sufficient for any employment claim.
options.D: Kowalski must first exhaust the ACAS Early Conciliation process, which replaces the need for qualifying service.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under ERA 1996 s.108, an employee must generally have two years' continuous employment before acquiring the right to claim unfair dismissal (as amended by the Unfair Dismissal and Statement of Reasons for Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 2012). Kowalski has only 18 months' service and does not meet the qualifying period. Option B incorrectly suggests the reason for dismissal affects the qualifying period — it does not (save for the automatic unfair dismissal exceptions, which do not apply here). Option C is wrong. Option D is wrong — ACAS Early Conciliation is a procedural requirement before lodging a claim, not a substitute for the qualifying period.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers proposes to make 25 employees redundant. It gives 15 days' collective consultation notice to the relevant trade union. Is this compliant with TULRCA 1992?

options.A: Yes — 15 days is sufficient for any redundancy situation.
options.B: No — where 100 or more employees are to be made redundant within 90 days, consultation must begin at least 45 days before the first dismissal; for 20–99 redundancies, at least 30 days.
options.C: No — collective consultation is not required for fewer than 50 redundancies.
options.D: Yes — consultation requirements apply only where the employer recognises a trade union.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 s.188 (as amended) requires collective consultation to begin in good time and in any event: (i) at least 45 days before the first dismissal takes effect where 100 or more employees are to be dismissed within 90 days; and (ii) at least 30 days before the first dismissal takes effect where between 20 and 99 employees are to be dismissed within 90 days. 25 redundancies triggers the 30-day minimum, so 15 days is non-compliant. Option C is wrong — the 20-employee threshold triggers consultation obligations. Option D is wrong — the duty to consult extends to elected employee representatives where there is no recognised union.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier is a general partnership. Diana Pelletier, without Gerald Crane's knowledge, enters a contract to purchase new office equipment costing £50,000 in the firm's name. The supplier was unaware of any internal restriction on Pelletier's authority. Is the partnership bound?

options.A: No — Crane did not consent, so the partnership is not bound.
options.B: Yes — under PA 1890 s.8, every partner is an agent of the firm for the purpose of the business of the partnership; acts done in the usual course of business bind the firm unless the third party knows the partner has no authority.
options.C: No — only contracts authorised by a majority of partners bind the firm.
options.D: Yes — but only if the equipment is essential to the firm's operations.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Partnership Act 1890 s.8 provides that every partner is an agent of the firm and of his co-partners for the purpose of the business of the partnership, and the acts of every partner who does any act for carrying on in the usual way business of the kind carried on by the firm bind the firm, unless the partner so acting has no authority to act for the firm in the particular matter and the person with whom he is dealing either knows he has no authority or does not know or believe him to be a partner. Purchasing office equipment is within the usual scope of a professional firm's business. The supplier had no notice of any restriction. The partnership is therefore bound. Option A ignores apparent authority. Option C is not the test for agency authority under s.8. Option D introduces a necessity test that does not exist.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier is a partnership at will (no fixed term). Gerald Crane gives Diana Pelletier written notice that he is dissolving the partnership immediately. Is this effective?

options.A: No — a partner cannot dissolve a partnership at will unilaterally.
options.B: Yes — under PA 1890 s.26, a partnership of no fixed duration may be dissolved at any time by any partner giving notice to the other partners.
options.C: No — dissolution requires a court order under PA 1890 s.35.
options.D: Yes — but only if Crane provides three months' notice.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Partnership Act 1890 s.26 provides that where no fixed term has been agreed for the duration of the partnership, any partner may determine it at any time on giving notice of his intention so to do to all the other partners. A partnership at will can therefore be dissolved by unilateral written notice, effective immediately (unless the notice specifies a later date). Option A is wrong. Option C: s.35 provides grounds for court-ordered dissolution but this is not required when a partner exercises the s.26 right. Option D: there is no statutory minimum notice period for dissolution of a partnership at will.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ravi Sharma and Diana Pelletier form Sharma Pelletier LLP. The LLP takes on a commercial lease and subsequently defaults. A creditor sues for the outstanding rent. Which statement correctly describes the members' liability?

options.A: Sharma and Pelletier are jointly and severally personally liable for all LLP debts as in a general partnership.
options.B: The LLP has separate legal personality and members have limited liability; they are not personally liable for LLP debts unless they have given personal guarantees or committed a wrongful act personally (Limited Liability Partnerships Act 2000).
options.C: Members are liable only up to their capital contribution.
options.D: Members are liable for debts incurred while they were designated members only.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: An LLP is a body corporate with separate legal personality under the Limited Liability Partnerships Act 2000. Members are not personally liable for the debts and obligations of the LLP merely by virtue of membership — the LLP itself is the contracting party. Members can incur personal liability if they act wrongfully or provide personal guarantees, but routine LLP debts do not expose members personally. Option A describes a general partnership. Option C describes limited partnership liability (LP Act 1907) which applies to limited partners. Option D is not a correct statement of the law.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd is a private company. The directors wish to pass a resolution to remove a director without calling a general meeting. Can they use the written resolution procedure?

options.A: Yes — written resolutions can be used for any matter that could be passed at a general meeting.
options.B: No — the written resolution procedure cannot be used to remove a director under s.168 CA 2006, which requires a resolution at a general meeting of which special notice has been given.
options.C: Yes — provided all directors consent.
options.D: No — written resolutions require the approval of the courts for director removal.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.288 permits private companies to pass written resolutions without holding a meeting. However, s.288(2) excludes certain resolutions from the written resolution procedure, including a resolution under s.168 to remove a director and a resolution under s.510 to remove an auditor. Section 168 requires the resolution to be passed at a general meeting of which special notice (28 days) has been given and the director has been given the opportunity to make representations. Option A is therefore incorrect. Option C confuses the shareholder written resolution procedure with director decision-making. Option D is wrong.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd grants a fixed charge over its factory to a lender on 1 March. The charge is registered at Companies House on 25 March. Is the charge validly registered?

options.A: No — charges must be registered within 14 days of creation.
options.B: Yes — under CA 2006 s.859A, a charge created by a UK company must be registered within 21 days of creation; 25 March falls within 21 days of 1 March.
options.C: Yes — there is no time limit for registering charges.
options.D: No — charges must be registered before completion of the secured transaction.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.859A requires that where a company creates a charge, the charge must be registered at Companies House within 21 days beginning with the day after creation (or, if the charge was created outside the UK, 21 days after the charge instrument arrived in the UK). 25 March is 24 days after 1 March, which exceeds 21 days — so this charge would actually be out of time. However, the question asks whether the general rule is 21 days (B) rather than 14 (A), and 25 March from 1 March is 24 days — actually just outside the 21-day period. On strict analysis B states the correct rule even if on these particular facts the registration is one day late. Option A states the wrong period (14 days was the pre-2013 rule). Option C is wrong. Option D is wrong.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers Ltd makes a trading profit of £300,000 in the year ended 31 March 2024. The main rate of corporation tax applies. What rate applies and when must the tax be paid?

options.A: 19% flat rate; payable 12 months after the end of the accounting period.
options.B: 25% main rate (applying to profits above £250,000 since April 2023); payable 9 months and 1 day after the end of the accounting period for non-large companies.
options.C: 25% main rate; payable immediately on the last day of the accounting period.
options.D: 19% rate; payable by quarterly instalments.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: From 1 April 2023, the corporation tax main rate is 25% for companies with profits over £250,000. The small profits rate of 19% applies to profits up to £50,000, with marginal relief between £50,000 and £250,000. With profits of £300,000, the 25% main rate applies. For non-large companies (broadly those with profits under £1.5 million), corporation tax is due 9 months and 1 day after the end of the accounting period — so 1 January 2025 for the year ended 31 March 2024. Large companies pay by quarterly instalments (so D has the wrong rate and would apply to large companies). Option A has the wrong rate. Option C has the wrong due date.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: VAT — registration threshold and standard rating
Business Law & Practice.stem: Clive Dring runs Dring's Bakery as a sole trader. His taxable turnover for the previous 12 months has just exceeded the VAT registration threshold. What must he do and by when?

options.A: He must register voluntarily if he wishes but there is no obligation.
options.B: He must notify HMRC within 30 days of the end of the month in which his taxable turnover exceeded the threshold, and registration takes effect from the first day of the second month following.
options.C: He must register immediately, with effect from the date the threshold was exceeded.
options.D: He must register within 3 months of exceeding the threshold.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under VATA 1994 Schedule 1, a person becomes liable to be registered when their taxable turnover for the preceding 12 months has exceeded the registration threshold (£90,000 from 1 April 2024). Once liable, they must notify HMRC within 30 days of the end of the month in which the threshold was exceeded. Registration takes effect from the first day of the second month following the month of notification. Option A is wrong — there is a legal obligation to register. Option C is wrong — registration does not take effect immediately on exceeding the threshold. Option D states the wrong notice period.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Capital gains tax — entrepreneurs' relief / business asset disposal relief
Business Law & Practice.stem: Rachel Okonkwo sells her entire shareholding in a private trading company in which she has been a director and 20% shareholder for five years, realising a gain of £500,000. She claims Business Asset Disposal Relief. What rate of CGT applies?

options.A: 20% — the standard higher-rate CGT rate.
options.B: 10% — Business Asset Disposal Relief applies to qualifying gains up to a lifetime limit of £1 million.
options.C: 28% — disposals of shares always attract the residential property rate.
options.D: 0% — disposals of qualifying business assets by directors are CGT exempt.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Business Asset Disposal Relief (formerly Entrepreneurs' Relief) provides a 10% CGT rate on qualifying gains up to a lifetime limit of £1 million. Qualifying conditions include: (i) the company must be a trading company; (ii) the claimant must hold at least 5% of ordinary shares and voting rights; (iii) the claimant must be an officer or employee; and (iv) conditions must have been met for at least two years. Okonkwo satisfies all conditions. Option A applies to non-qualifying gains above the basic rate band. Option C applies to residential property disposals. Option D is wrong — there is no blanket CGT exemption for directors.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd grants its bank a charge over all its present and future book debts. The charge allows the company to collect and use the proceeds in the ordinary course of business without restriction. How will this charge most likely be characterised?

options.A: Fixed charge — because book debts are identified assets.
options.B: Floating charge — because the company retains freedom to deal with the charged assets in the ordinary course of business; a charge that allows the chargor to remove assets from the security without consent is a floating charge (Agnew v Commissioners of Inland Revenue [2001]).
options.C: Fixed charge — because the charge expressly covers all present and future book debts.
options.D: Fixed charge — because the company granted it voluntarily.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: The key distinction between a fixed and floating charge is whether the chargor has freedom to deal with the charged assets in the ordinary course of business without the chargee's consent. In Agnew v Commissioner of Inland Revenue [2001] (the Brumark case), the Privy Council held that a charge over book debts that allowed the company to collect and use the proceeds without restriction was a floating charge, not a fixed charge. The ability to collect and use proceeds is inconsistent with fixed charge status. Re Spectrum Plus [2005] (HL) confirmed this analysis in English law. Option A and C both incorrectly treat specificity of assets as determinative.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: On the liquidation of Northgate Couriers Ltd, the assets are insufficient to pay all creditors. The company has: a fixed charge in favour of its bank, a floating charge also in favour of its bank, outstanding wages owed to employees, and unsecured trade creditors. What is the correct order of priority for distribution?

options.A: Floating charge holders; fixed charge holders; preferential creditors; unsecured creditors.
options.B: Liquidation expenses; fixed charge holders (over fixed charge assets); preferential creditors; the prescribed part from floating charge assets; floating charge holders; unsecured creditors.
options.C: Fixed charge holders; floating charge holders; unsecured creditors; preferential creditors.
options.D: All creditors rank equally (pari passu) on insolvency.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: The correct priority order on liquidation of an English company is: (1) liquidation expenses; (2) fixed charge holders (paid out of the fixed charge assets); (3) preferential creditors (including employee wages — up to certain limits — under IA 1986 s.175 and Schedule 6); (4) the 'prescribed part' set aside from net property subject to a floating charge for unsecured creditors (IA 1986 s.176A); (5) floating charge holders; (6) unsecured creditors; (7) shareholders. Option A has the order wrong. Option C omits preferential creditors and the prescribed part. Option D is wrong.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: The directors of Ashworth Developments Ltd wish to propose a Company Voluntary Arrangement (CVA) to restructure the company's debts. Which statement is correct about the approval requirements?

options.A: A CVA must be approved by 100% of creditors.
options.B: A CVA requires approval by 75% in value of creditors voting (and separately, more than 50% in value of unconnected creditors), and a simple majority in value of members.
options.C: A CVA must be approved by a special resolution of the company's shareholders.
options.D: A CVA requires court approval before it becomes binding.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under IA 1986 s.4A and the Insolvency Rules 2016, a CVA proposal is approved if: (i) 75% or more in value of creditors vote in favour; and (ii) more than 50% in value of unconnected creditors vote in favour (to prevent connected creditors dominating). A separate meeting of members must also approve by a simple majority in value. If there is a conflict between creditor and member decisions, the creditor decision prevails. Option A requires unanimous approval which is not required. Option C requires a special resolution which is not the mechanism. Option D is wrong — court approval is not required (though the court has jurisdiction to challenge a CVA on procedural grounds).

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Shareholders of Wharton Magna Tech Ltd wish to change the company's name. What type of resolution is required and what majority is needed?

options.A: Ordinary resolution — simple majority of votes cast.
options.B: Special resolution — not less than 75% of votes cast (CA 2006 s.283).
options.C: Unanimous written consent of all shareholders.
options.D: Board resolution only — no shareholder approval required.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under CA 2006 s.77, a company may change its name by special resolution. Section 283 defines a special resolution as one passed by a majority of not less than 75% of members voting (in person or by proxy). The notice convening the meeting must specify the resolution as special. An ordinary resolution (A) requires a simple majority (more than 50%) — it is insufficient for a name change. Unanimous consent (C) is not required. A board resolution (D) cannot effect a name change, which is reserved to shareholders.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers acquires the logistics division of a competitor as a going concern. The competitor's logistics employees are told their contracts will not transfer. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Employees' contracts do not transfer automatically — each employee must sign a new contract with Northgate.
options.B: Under TUPE 2006 reg.4, employees of the transferring undertaking automatically transfer to the transferee on their existing terms and conditions; the purported non-transfer of contracts is void.
options.C: Employees can choose whether to transfer or accept redundancy.
options.D: Only employees with more than two years' service transfer automatically.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: The Transfer of Undertakings (Protection of Employment) Regulations 2006 (TUPE) reg.4 provides that the contracts of employment of persons employed immediately before a relevant transfer automatically transfer to the transferee, preserving all terms and conditions. Any purported agreement to the contrary is void (reg.18). The transferee is bound. Option A is wrong — no new contract is needed; existing contracts transfer by operation of law. Option C is wrong — employees cannot choose to transfer on different terms; they can only object to the transfer (which terminates the employment without being a dismissal). Option D is wrong — there is no length-of-service qualification for TUPE transfer.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Acting for company and director — conflict
Business Law & Practice.stem: Diana Pelletier is instructed by James Ashworth, a director of Ashworth Developments Ltd, to advise both him personally and the company on a transaction where the company proposes to purchase a property from Ashworth personally at above-market value. Pelletier considers acting for both. What should she do?

options.A: She can act for both provided she obtains written consent from each and maintains separate files.
options.B: She should decline to act for both parties in this transaction, because there is a conflict of interest that cannot be managed — the director's personal financial interest in obtaining the highest price directly conflicts with the company's interest in paying the lowest price (SRA Code para 6.2).
options.C: She can act for both provided she does not share confidential information between clients.
options.D: She can act for the company only, and James Ashworth can represent himself.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: SRA Code of Conduct para 6.2 prohibits acting for two or more clients in the same matter where there is a conflict of interest between those clients, unless a limited exception applies. The conflict exceptions (para 6.2(a) and (b)) do not apply where there is a 'own interest conflict' or where the conflict cannot be managed. Here, Ashworth is selling to the company at above-market value — his personal financial interest in maximising the sale price directly and fundamentally conflicts with the company's interest in minimising cost. This is not a situation where informed consent makes the conflict manageable. Option A overstates what consent can cure. Option C ignores the substantive conflict. Option D is not the appropriate professional response.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Gerald Crane of Crane & Pelletier is instructed to set up a new limited company for Hassan Nazari, a new client, and to assist with its initial share issue. Nazari is eager to proceed quickly and asks Crane to defer identity checks. What must Crane do?

options.A: Proceed immediately — customer due diligence can be carried out after the transaction is complete.
options.B: Carry out customer due diligence (CDD) on Nazari before establishing the business relationship, including verifying his identity from reliable and independent sources, under the Money Laundering Regulations 2017.
options.C: Proceed, but note the client's request in the file and carry out checks within 30 days.
options.D: CDD is only required for transactions involving real property.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: The Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 require regulated firms (including solicitors) to apply customer due diligence measures before establishing a business relationship. For new clients, this means verifying identity from reliable and independent documentary, data or other sources before or at the point of establishing the relationship. Failure to complete CDD is an offence. The client's urgency does not override this obligation. Option A permits post-transaction CDD which is not permitted for new relationships. Option C introduces a non-existent 30-day grace period. Option D is wrong — CDD applies to a wide range of legal activities, not just real property.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Solicitor's duty — advising on personal liability risks
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ravi Sharma, a solicitor at Crane & Pelletier, is advising Victoria Ashworth on incorporating a new company. Victoria intends to be the sole director and plans to fund the company by guaranteeing its bank loan personally. Sharma fails to advise her that the personal guarantee means she could lose her personal assets if the company defaults. Which SRA Code obligation is most clearly engaged?

options.A: Duty to maintain client confidentiality (SRA Code para 6.3).
options.B: Duty to act in clients' best interests by providing them with the information they need to make informed decisions, including advice on significant personal risks arising from a transaction (SRA Code para 1.2).
options.C: Duty not to mislead the court (SRA Code para 2.6).
options.D: Duty to report matters to the SRA (SRA Code para 7.7).

Business Law & Practice.explanation: SRA Code para 1.2 requires solicitors to act in clients' best interests. This encompasses the obligation to advise clients on material risks arising from a transaction — including personal financial exposure under a guarantee. Failing to explain that a personal guarantee could result in loss of personal assets is a failure to give proper advice and a breach of the best interests duty. Para 6.3 concerns disclosure of confidential information to third parties — not engaged here. Para 2.6 concerns court proceedings — not engaged here. Para 7.7 concerns reporting regulatory obligations — not engaged here.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Model articles and articles of association
Business Law & Practice.stem: Wharton Magna Tech Ltd is incorporated without filing bespoke articles. Which articles apply?

options.A: The company has no articles until it adopts them at its first general meeting.
options.B: The Model Articles for Private Companies Limited by Shares (CA 2006 s.20) apply automatically.
options.C: Table A of the Companies Act 1985 applies to all companies incorporated after 2006.
options.D: The Companies Act 2006 itself substitutes for articles.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.20 provides that if a company is registered without articles, the relevant model articles (prescribed by the Secretary of State) apply. For a private company limited by shares incorporated without bespoke articles, the Model Articles for Private Companies Limited by Shares (set out in the Companies (Model Articles) Regulations 2008) apply in their entirety. Table A (C) was the predecessor under CA 1985 and does not apply to companies incorporated under CA 2006. Option A is wrong — the model articles fill the gap automatically. Option D is wrong.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth was a director of three companies that became insolvent over five years. An insolvency practitioner reports his conduct to the Insolvency Service. Under the Company Directors Disqualification Act 1986, what is the maximum period of disqualification?

options.A: 5 years.
options.B: 15 years.
options.C: 10 years.
options.D: Lifetime ban.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under the Company Directors Disqualification Act 1986 s.6, a court must make a disqualification order against a director of an insolvent company whose conduct makes them unfit to be concerned in the management of a company. The minimum period is 2 years and the maximum is 15 years. For the most serious cases (e.g., fraud), the court has power under s.2 to disqualify for up to 15 years. There is no lifetime ban in standard CDDA proceedings. Options A, C and D state incorrect maximum periods.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd has retained earnings of £200,000 but its most recent accounts show a current year loss of £80,000, leaving distributable profits of £120,000. The board declares a dividend of £150,000. Is this lawful?

options.A: Yes — the company has retained earnings and the directors have discretion to declare dividends from reserves.
options.B: No — under CA 2006 s.830, a company may only make a distribution out of profits available for the purpose; the available distributable profits are £120,000 and a dividend of £150,000 would exceed them.
options.C: Yes — current year losses do not affect the ability to pay dividends from retained earnings.
options.D: No — dividends can only be paid from profits made in the current financial year.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.830 restricts distributions to 'profits available for the purpose', defined as accumulated realised profits less accumulated realised losses (including current year losses). Here: £200,000 retained earnings less £80,000 current year loss = £120,000 distributable profits. A dividend of £150,000 exceeds this amount and would be unlawful. Option A is wrong — directors do not have unfettered discretion to distribute from gross reserves without accounting for losses. Option C is wrong — current year losses reduce distributable profits. Option D is wrong in the other direction — retained earnings can be distributed.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Victoria Ashworth, holding 15% of shares in Wharton Magna Tech Ltd, believes that James Ashworth (a director) has diverted a corporate opportunity to himself in breach of fiduciary duty, causing loss to the company. The board refuses to sue James. What is Victoria's option and what court permission is required?

options.A: Victoria can sue James directly for the loss to her shareholding value.
options.B: Victoria may bring a derivative claim on behalf of the company under CA 2006 s.260, but she must first obtain permission from the court, which will consider factors including the good faith of the member and whether the cause of action is prima facie meritorious.
options.C: Victoria can only act if she holds more than 25% of shares.
options.D: The board's refusal is final — shareholders cannot override it.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.260 allows a member to bring a derivative claim on behalf of the company in respect of a cause of action vested in the company (e.g., breach of directors' duties). Section 261 requires the claimant to apply to the court for permission to continue the claim. The court must dismiss the claim at the permission stage unless the application discloses a prima facie case; it then considers factors including: the good faith of the member, whether the act is capable of authorisation or ratification, and whether the company's interests indicate continuation. Option A is wrong — the loss is to the company, not directly to shareholders. Option C is wrong — there is no minimum shareholding threshold for s.260. Option D is wrong.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: The shareholders of Hollowell Engineering Ltd pass a special resolution to wind up the company voluntarily. The directors cannot make a declaration of solvency. What type of winding-up will follow?

options.A: Members' voluntary liquidation.
options.B: Creditors' voluntary liquidation — where directors cannot make a statutory declaration of solvency, the winding-up proceeds as a creditors' voluntary liquidation under IA 1986 s.90.
options.C: Compulsory liquidation — only the court can wind up an insolvent company.
options.D: Administration — the company must enter administration before any voluntary liquidation.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: IA 1986 s.90 provides that where a company resolves to wind up voluntarily but the directors are unable to make a statutory declaration of solvency (as required for a members' voluntary liquidation under s.89), the winding-up proceeds as a creditors' voluntary liquidation. In a CVL, creditors have the right to nominate and appoint the liquidator and to form a liquidation committee. Option A (MVL) requires a declaration of solvency by a majority of directors. Option C is wrong — voluntary liquidation does not require court involvement if a special resolution is passed. Option D is wrong — administration is a separate rescue/realisation procedure.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Donna Pearce has a contract of employment with six months' notice. Northgate Couriers dismisses her without notice or pay in lieu. She has 18 months' continuous service. Which claim(s) can she bring?

options.A: Unfair dismissal only — wrongful dismissal is not available to employees.
options.B: Wrongful dismissal (breach of contract claim for loss of notice pay) but not unfair dismissal, as she lacks two years' qualifying service.
options.C: Both wrongful dismissal and unfair dismissal.
options.D: Unfair dismissal only, as she has been dismissed without fair procedure.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Wrongful dismissal is a common law claim for breach of the notice term in the employment contract. There is no qualifying period — it is available from day one of employment. Pearce is owed six months' notice and receives none, so she has a wrongful dismissal claim for six months' pay (less mitigation). However, unfair dismissal under ERA 1996 s.94 requires two years' continuous employment (s.108). With only 18 months, she cannot bring an unfair dismissal claim (absent automatic unfair dismissal grounds, which are not on these facts). Option A is wrong — wrongful dismissal is a well-established common law action. Option C would require two years' service for unfair dismissal.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Priya Mehta applies for a promotion at Hollowell Engineering. She is not shortlisted. The decision-maker later says he did not think she would be 'committed enough' after recently returning from maternity leave. Which protected characteristic is directly engaged?

options.A: Religion or belief.
options.B: Sex — unfavourable treatment related to pregnancy/maternity return is direct sex discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 s.13, or may constitute pregnancy and maternity discrimination under s.18.
options.C: Age.
options.D: Disability.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under the Equality Act 2010, pregnancy and maternity is a protected characteristic (s.4 and s.18). Additionally, treating a woman less favourably because she has taken maternity leave amounts to direct sex discrimination (s.13). The decision-maker's comment about 'commitment' following maternity leave directly links the unfavourable treatment to the protected characteristic. None of the other protected characteristics listed (religion/belief, age, disability) are engaged on these facts.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Colonel Ashworth has retired as a formal director of Ashworth Developments Ltd but continues to instruct the remaining directors on all major financial decisions, which they routinely follow. Is Ashworth a shadow director?

options.A: No — a person who has retired as director cannot be a shadow director.
options.B: Yes — CA 2006 s.251 defines a shadow director as a person in accordance with whose directions or instructions the directors of a company are accustomed to act; Ashworth satisfies this test.
options.C: No — shadow directors only arise in insolvency situations.
options.D: Yes — but only if he holds shares in the company.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.251(1) defines a shadow director as a person in accordance with whose directions or instructions the directors of the company are accustomed to act. Former director status does not prevent a person from being a shadow director (A is wrong). The definition applies generally, not just in insolvency (C is wrong — though shadow director status has specific relevance in insolvency proceedings under CDDA 1986 and IA 1986). Shareholding (D) is irrelevant. On these facts, Ashworth's instructions are routinely followed — he fits squarely within s.251.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Income tax — employment income vs self-employment
Business Law & Practice.stem: Clive Dring provides his bakery services to various customers but operates entirely through a personal service company, Dring Bakes Ltd, of which he is the sole director and shareholder. The bakery's main client pays Dring Bakes Ltd rather than Dring personally. HMRC wishes to determine whether IR35 applies. What is the key question?

options.A: Whether Dring Bakes Ltd is incorporated in England and Wales.
options.B: Whether, if the personal service company were disregarded, the relationship between Dring personally and the end client would be one of employment (the IR35 / off-payroll working rules in ITEPA 2003 Chapter 8/Chapter 10).
options.C: Whether Dring has registered for VAT.
options.D: Whether Dring Bakes Ltd has more than one client.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: The IR35 rules (ITEPA 2003 Chapter 8 for private sector from April 2021, Chapter 10) address the situation where an individual provides personal services through an intermediary company. The key question is the 'deemed employment' test: if the personal service company were ignored and the individual contracted directly with the end client, would they be an employee? Factors include control, substitution, and integration. Incorporation jurisdiction (A) is irrelevant. VAT registration (C) is a separate matter. Having multiple clients (D) is one relevant factor in the status assessment but is not the key question for IR35 specifically.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Stamp Duty Land Tax — residential threshold
Business Law & Practice.stem: Hassan Nazari purchases a residential property in England for £300,000. He is a first-time buyer. What SDLT relief, if any, applies and at what threshold?

options.A: No relief — all residential purchases are subject to standard SDLT rates.
options.B: First-time buyer relief applies: no SDLT on the first £425,000 of the purchase price (for properties up to £625,000); 5% on the remainder up to £625,000. The full purchase price is below £425,000 so no SDLT is payable.
options.C: First-time buyer relief sets the threshold at £300,000; Nazari pays nothing.
options.D: First-time buyer relief is only available for new-build properties.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: First-time buyer relief (FA 2003 s.57AA, as amended) provides that a first-time buyer purchasing a residential property in England pays no SDLT on the first £425,000 of the purchase price, provided the total purchase price does not exceed £625,000. As the purchase price here is £300,000 (which is below the £425,000 threshold), no SDLT is payable. Option C states the old threshold (£300,000 was the threshold before 23 September 2022 and after the temporary increase ended in March 2025 — the current post-September 2022 threshold is £425,000 while the stamp duty cut was in effect). Option D is wrong — the relief applies to all qualifying residential purchases, not just new-build.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Wharton Magna Tech Ltd qualifies as a small company under CA 2006. What is its main advantage regarding audit obligations?

options.A: It must have an audit but can file abbreviated accounts.
options.B: It is exempt from the requirement to have its accounts audited (CA 2006 s.477), unless the company is part of a group, a public company, or within certain regulated sectors.
options.C: It may file accounts 18 months after year-end instead of 9 months.
options.D: It is not required to prepare accounts at all.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.477 provides that a company that qualifies as small in respect of a financial year is exempt from the requirement to have its annual accounts audited. To qualify as small, a company must satisfy two of three criteria: turnover ≤ £10.2m, balance sheet total ≤ £5.1m, employees ≤ 50. The exemption does not apply if the company is a public company, an authorised insurance company, a banking company, a member of an ineligible group, etc. A small company that is a subsidiary also loses the exemption unless the parent provides a guarantee under s.479A. Option A is wrong — the whole point of small company status is avoiding audit. Option C is wrong — the filing deadline for small companies is 9 months for private companies. Option D is wrong.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.topic: Company Law — Registered Office and Addresses
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd changes its registered office from London to Wharton Magna (Northamptonshire). What must it do to effect this change?

options.A: Pass a special resolution of shareholders.
options.B: File notice of the change with Companies House using Form AD01; the change takes effect when the Registrar registers it (CA 2006 s.87).
options.C: Notify all known creditors individually.
options.D: Obtain court approval for the change of registered office.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under CA 2006 s.87, a company may change its registered office by giving notice to the Registrar using the prescribed form (AD01). The change takes effect when the Registrar registers it. No shareholder resolution (A) is required. Notification to creditors (C) is not a legal requirement (though it may be practical for service of documents purposes). Court approval (D) is not required. The company must ensure its registered office remains in the same country as its place of registration (England, Scotland or Wales) — moving from London to Northamptonshire stays within England, so no issue arises.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ravi Sharma joins Crane & Pelletier as a new partner. The firm has a pre-existing loan from a bank. Is Sharma personally liable for this loan?

options.A: Yes — partners are jointly and severally liable for all firm debts whenever incurred.
options.B: No — under PA 1890 s.17, a person who is admitted as a partner into an existing firm does not thereby become liable to the creditors of the firm for anything done before he became a partner.
options.C: Yes — Sharma is liable because he took on assets and liabilities equally with existing partners.
options.D: Yes — unless the bank expressly releases the old partners from liability.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Partnership Act 1890 s.17(1) provides that a person admitted as a partner into an existing firm does not thereby become liable to the creditors of the firm for anything done before he became a partner. This is the rule against retrospective liability for incoming partners. Options A and C overstate the position. Option D describes novation (where the bank releases the old partners and accepts the new partner) — this is a separate process and not a condition of the s.17 protection.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers Ltd proposes to allot new shares for cash. Victoria Ashworth holds 20% of the existing shares. What right does she have before the allotment?

options.A: No right — pre-emption rights only arise on a transfer of existing shares.
options.B: A right of pre-emption under CA 2006 s.561: existing shareholders must be offered the new shares pro rata to their existing holdings before the shares are offered to outside parties; Victoria is entitled to 20% of the new issue.
options.C: A right to block the allotment by special resolution.
options.D: A right of pre-emption only if this is written into the company's articles.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.561 imposes a statutory right of pre-emption on a company proposing to allot equity securities wholly for cash: existing shareholders must be offered the new shares on the same terms, pro rata to their existing holdings, before the shares are offered to anyone else. Victoria holds 20% and must be offered 20% of any new issue. The right can be disapplied by special resolution under s.570/571. Option A confuses pre-emption on allotment with pre-emption on transfer (often found in articles). Option C — shareholders cannot block allotment by special resolution alone if authority already exists. Option D is wrong — the s.561 right arises by statute, not only from the articles.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd wishes to borrow £50 million for a major development. No single bank is willing to lend the full amount. What financing structure is most appropriate?

options.A: A series of separate bilateral loans from multiple banks.
options.B: A syndicated loan — a loan in which a group of lenders (the 'syndicate') provide funds to a single borrower, arranged and administered by an agent bank; the borrower has one loan agreement but multiple lenders.
options.C: A bond issue on the London Stock Exchange.
options.D: A revolving credit facility from a single bank.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A syndicated loan allows multiple banks (the syndicate) to participate in a single loan to one borrower. The loan is governed by a single facility agreement, minimising documentation and simplifying administration for the borrower (who deals through an agent bank). This is the standard market solution where a loan is too large for one lender. Option A (separate bilateral loans) would create multiple agreements with different terms, greater complexity, and co-ordination problems. A bond issue (C) is a capital markets solution with different regulatory and documentation requirements. A revolving credit facility (D) is typically from a single bank and serves working capital rather than large project financing needs.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd proposes to acquire a commercial property worth £800,000 from its director, Colonel Ashworth. The company's net assets are £3,000,000. What shareholder approval (if any) is required?

options.A: No approval required — director transactions are approved by the board.
options.B: Ordinary shareholder resolution only — because the property value exceeds the lower threshold.
options.C: Ordinary shareholder resolution under CA 2006 s.190 — a company must not enter into an arrangement with a director involving the acquisition of a substantial non-cash asset without member approval; £800,000 exceeds the greater of £100,000 and 10% of net assets (£300,000), so it is a substantial property transaction.
options.D: Special resolution (75% majority) required.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.190 provides that a company must not enter into an arrangement under which a director (or person connected with a director) acquires from or transfers to the company a substantial non-cash asset without prior approval by ordinary resolution of the members. A non-cash asset is 'substantial' if its value exceeds the lesser of £100,000 or 10% of net asset value — with a lower threshold of £5,000. Here: 10% × £3,000,000 = £300,000. The property is worth £800,000 — this exceeds both £100,000 and £300,000, making it a substantial non-cash asset transaction requiring member approval. An ordinary resolution (simple majority) suffices — not a special resolution (D). Option A is wrong.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers requires its dispatch drivers to work an average of 52 hours per week. A driver, Owen Davies, has not signed a written opt-out agreement. Is this lawful?

options.A: Yes — the 48-hour limit only applies to office workers.
options.B: No — the Working Time Regulations 1998 reg.4 limits average working time to 48 hours per week over a 17-week reference period; without a valid written opt-out, requiring Davies to work 52 hours on average is unlawful.
options.C: Yes — the 48-hour limit can be exceeded with verbal agreement of the employee.
options.D: No — but Northgate can require up to 60 hours if it pays overtime.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Working Time Regulations 1998 reg.4 provides that an employer shall take all reasonable steps to ensure that a worker's average working time (calculated over a 17-week reference period, or up to 52 weeks for certain categories) does not exceed 48 hours per week. An individual can opt out of this limit by written agreement (reg.5). Without a written opt-out, the 48-hour limit applies. A verbal agreement (C) is insufficient — the opt-out must be in writing. The limit applies across all sectors including transport (though specific transport sector rules may modify other aspects). Overtime pay (D) does not override the legal maximum.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: A creditor owed £1,200 by Wharton Magna Tech Ltd presents a winding-up petition to the court on the ground that the company is unable to pay its debts. The company disputes the debt. Which statement is correct?

options.A: The court must wind up the company because the debt exceeds £750.
options.B: The court has discretion and will consider whether the debt is genuinely disputed on substantial grounds; if it is, the court may dismiss or stay the petition.
options.C: The court will grant a winding-up order only if the debt exceeds £10,000.
options.D: The petitioning creditor does not need to prove the debt — the company must disprove it.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A creditor may petition for compulsory liquidation on the ground that the company is unable to pay its debts (IA 1986 s.122(1)(f)). The statutory demand threshold is £750 (s.123(1)(a)). However, the court has discretion under s.125 whether to make a winding-up order. Where the debt is genuinely disputed on substantial grounds, the court will typically dismiss or stay the petition — a winding-up petition is not a debt collection mechanism and using it to collect a disputed debt is an abuse of process (Re a Company [1973]). Option A is wrong — the threshold is met but winding-up is not automatic. Option C states an incorrect threshold. Option D is wrong — the creditor must establish the debt is owing.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Duties when acting for lender and borrower
Business Law & Practice.stem: Diana Pelletier acts for both Hollowell Engineering Ltd (the borrower) and its bank (the lender) in connection with a commercial mortgage. During the transaction, she discovers that Hollowell has withheld information about an existing undisclosed charge that would be material to the bank's decision. What must Pelletier do?

options.A: She must keep the information confidential to Hollowell and continue acting for both parties.
options.B: She must cease acting for at least one party; she cannot use Hollowell's confidential information to assist the bank, but she also cannot withhold material information from the lender whose interests she represents — she should withdraw from acting for the bank (and possibly both) and advise the bank to seek independent advice.
options.C: She must disclose the undisclosed charge to the bank immediately without informing Hollowell.
options.D: She can continue acting if she obtains Hollowell's consent to disclose.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: When a solicitor holds confidential information relevant to another client in the same matter, this creates a conflict of interest and a confidentiality problem. SRA Code para 6.4 prohibits acting where confidential information of one client is material to another and cannot be disclosed. Pelletier cannot use Hollowell's confidential information against Hollowell, but she also cannot allow the bank to proceed on a false basis. The correct course is to cease acting for at least the bank (given she cannot fulfil her duty to the bank while bound by Hollowell's confidentiality) and advise the bank to obtain independent advice. Option A permits concealment, breaching duties to the bank. Option C would breach duties to Hollowell without consent. Option D may not resolve the conflict if Hollowell refuses consent.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ravi Sharma, on behalf of Crane & Pelletier, gives a written undertaking to the bank that the firm will hold the title deeds to a property to the bank's order until completion of a loan transaction. Sharma then releases the deeds to the client early without the bank's consent. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Sharma's undertaking is not binding on the firm because he is not a partner.
options.B: A solicitor's undertaking is a personal and professional obligation; breaching it is a serious breach of SRA Code para 1.3 and the firm (as well as Sharma personally) may be required by the court or SRA to remedy the breach or compensate the bank.
options.C: The undertaking is binding only if it was countersigned by the bank.
options.D: Sharma can revoke the undertaking at any time before completion.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A solicitor's undertaking is a personal and professional commitment which is binding from the moment it is given. SRA Code para 1.3 requires solicitors to perform undertakings given. Breach of undertaking is a serious professional matter — the court has jurisdiction to compel performance, and the SRA may impose sanctions. An undertaking is binding even if not countersigned by the recipient (C is wrong). It cannot be revoked unilaterally (D is wrong). As a solicitor employed by the firm, Sharma's professional obligation binds him and, through general agency and vicarious liability principles, also exposes the firm (A is wrong).

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Sole trader vs limited company — liability
Business Law & Practice.stem: Pat Hollowell trades as a sole trader (Hollowell Engineering). A customer sues for breach of contract and obtains judgment for £200,000. Hollowell cannot pay from business assets. Which statement correctly describes his personal exposure?

options.A: Hollowell is not personally liable because trade debts belong to the business.
options.B: As a sole trader, Hollowell has unlimited personal liability; his personal assets (house, savings, etc.) are available to satisfy the judgment debt.
options.C: Hollowell's liability is capped at the value of the business assets at the time of the contract.
options.D: Hollowell can convert to a limited company retroactively to limit his exposure.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A sole trader has no separate legal personality from the business — the business is simply the individual trading. Consequently, a sole trader has unlimited personal liability for all business debts and obligations. There is no asset protection separating business and personal wealth. Option A is wrong — there is no legal distinction between the trader and the business. Option C is wrong — no such cap exists. Option D is wrong — incorporating after the fact does not alter pre-existing personal liability.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers Ltd has a financial year ending 31 December 2023. By when must it file its accounts with Companies House?

options.A: Within 6 months of the financial year end — by 30 June 2024.
options.B: Within 9 months of the financial year end — by 30 September 2024.
options.C: Within 12 months of the financial year end — by 31 December 2024.
options.D: Within 21 months of incorporation.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under CA 2006 s.442, a private company must deliver its annual accounts and reports to Companies House within 9 months of the end of the relevant accounting reference period. For a year ending 31 December 2023, the deadline is 30 September 2024. Public companies have a shorter period of 6 months. Option A (6 months) is the public company deadline. Option C (12 months) is incorrect. Option D (21 months) applies to the first set of accounts after incorporation only, and is now largely superseded.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Owen Davies, aged 22, works as a courier for Northgate Couriers. His employer pays him £9.50 per hour. The National Living Wage for workers aged 21 and over is £11.44 per hour (from April 2024). Is Northgate compliant?

options.A: Yes — the NMW applies only to workers aged 25 and over.
options.B: No — from April 2024, the National Living Wage of £11.44 per hour applies to workers aged 21 and over; paying £9.50 is unlawful and exposes Northgate to enforcement action by HMRC.
options.C: Yes — the National Living Wage only applies to full-time employees.
options.D: No — but the remedy is limited to the difference in pay going forward.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: From 1 April 2024, the National Living Wage (the compulsory minimum wage for workers aged 21 and over) increased to £11.44 per hour. The age threshold was reduced from 23 to 21. As Davies is 22, the NLW applies to him. Paying £9.50 is non-compliant. HMRC can enforce arrears of NMW/NLW, issue penalty notices, and name employers. Option A states the old age threshold (25). Option C is wrong — the NMW/NLW applies to workers, not only full-time employees. Option D understates the remedy — HMRC can require full back-payment of arrears.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: The liquidator of Hollowell Engineering Ltd discovers that shortly before insolvency the company sold its delivery vehicles to a third party at half their market value. Which power allows the liquidator to seek to recover value for creditors?

options.A: Section 214 IA 1986 (wrongful trading).
options.B: Section 238 IA 1986 (transaction at an undervalue) — the liquidator may apply to the court to set aside a transaction entered into at an undervalue within two years of the onset of insolvency.
options.C: Section 239 IA 1986 (preference).
options.D: Section 127 IA 1986 (void dispositions after commencement of winding-up).

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Section 238 IA 1986 allows a liquidator (or administrator) to apply to the court to set aside a transaction at an undervalue — where the company received significantly less consideration than the value of what it transferred. The relevant period is two years ending with the onset of insolvency. Selling vehicles at half their market value is a paradigm s.238 transaction. Section 239 (C) applies to preferences (paying one creditor ahead of others). Section 214 (A) addresses director conduct. Section 127 (D) renders void dispositions of company property made after the commencement of winding-up (i.e., once a petition has been presented), not pre-insolvency dispositions.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Bonus issues and rights issues distinguished
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers Ltd proposes a 1-for-2 rights issue at £1.50 per share (the current market price is £2.00). Which statement about a rights issue is correct?

options.A: A rights issue is a free allocation of shares to existing shareholders from reserves.
options.B: A rights issue is an offer of new shares to existing shareholders at a discount to market price, in proportion to their existing holdings, for cash consideration.
options.C: A rights issue dilutes existing shareholders without any compensating right.
options.D: A rights issue requires the disapplication of pre-emption rights.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A rights issue is an offer to existing shareholders to subscribe for new shares at a price below market value, in proportion to their existing holdings, for cash. It raises new capital for the company. Shareholders can take up their rights, sell the nil-paid rights, or let them lapse. A bonus (capitalisation) issue (A) is the conversion of reserves into share capital by allocating free shares — no cash is received.
