Option B is incorrect — the HRA does not automatically invalidate prerogative employment powers; they must be exercised compatibly with Convention rights. Option D is incorrect — statute is superior to prerogative in cases of conflict.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Rule of law and the principle of legality
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: What does the 'principle of legality' require in the interpretation of legislation affecting fundamental rights?

options.A: Courts will presume that Parliament did not intend to remove fundamental common law rights unless the statute expresses that intention clearly and explicitly
options.B: Courts must strike down any legislation that appears to remove fundamental rights
options.C: Courts must defer to Parliament's stated purpose, even if it removes fundamental rights
options.D: Fundamental rights can only be protected by the HRA 1998, not by common law interpretation

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option D is correct. The principle of legality (R v SSHD ex p Simms [1999], R v SSHD ex p Leech [1994]) holds that courts will not assume Parliament intended to remove fundamental rights unless clear words are used (FLK1-CAL-053). This is a tool of interpretation, not a power to strike down statutes. Option A is incorrect — courts cannot strike down primary legislation. Option B is incorrect — courts do protect rights through interpretation. Option C is incorrect — common law interpretation (the principle of legality) does protect fundamental rights, alongside the HRA 1998.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Legislative competence and the Sewel convention
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Scottish Parliament has exclusively devolved powers to legislate on education. The UK Parliament passes an Act that amends education law in Scotland without securing the consent of the Scottish Parliament (contrary to the Sewel convention). A Scottish education group seeks judicial review of the UK Act. Based on Miller [2017], what would be the court's likely response?

options.D: The court would recognise the Sewel convention as a constitutional principle but would not enforce it as law; the Act would remain valid, though the breach may be significant politically
options.A: The court would require the UK Parliament to obtain Scottish consent before the Act becomes law
options.B: The court would not hear the claim because devolution matters are outside the scope of JR
options.C: The court would quash the UK Act as unconstitutional because it breaches the Sewel convention

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option D is correct. R (Miller) v SSHD [2017] confirmed that the Sewel convention is recognized by the courts but is not legally enforceable (FLK1-CAL-049). A breach has political consequences, not legal ones. The Act would remain valid. Option A is incorrect — the court cannot impose a legal requirement to obtain consent; that is a political matter. Option B is incorrect — devolution matters are within the scope of JR, but the enforcement of conventions is not. Option C is incorrect — conventions are not enforceable in law.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Fettering of discretion and the balance of competing interests
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A local authority adopts a policy that states: 'Due to budget constraints, all applications for discretionary rate relief will be refused unless the applicant can demonstrate exceptional hardship.' A small business applies for relief but is refused summary consideration of its circumstances, simply because it did not demonstrate hardship at the threshold stage. The business argues the authority has fettered its discretion. Which statement best explains the legal position?

options.D: The authority has fettered its discretion by setting a threshold that prevents individual consideration; although the policy provides a general framework, each application must receive individual assessment, and summary refusal based on the threshold alone is unlawful
options.A: The policy is lawful because budget constraints always justify refusing discretionary benefits
options.B: The authority has a blanket right to refuse relief due to budget constraints and does not need to consider individual applications
options.C: The authority has not fettered discretion because it has acknowledged the possibility of exceptions ('unless exceptional hardship')

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option D is correct. A policy that sets a threshold but then refuses applications summary without individual consideration has fettered discretion (FLK1-CAL-037). Although policies are lawful and budgets are relevant, the authority must genuinely consider whether the circumstances of a particular applicant warrant an exception. Automatic refusal at the threshold is unlawful. Option A is incorrect — budget constraints are relevant but do not override the duty to consider individual cases. Option B is incorrect — the authority must exercise discretion individually, not mechanically apply budget constraints. Option C is incorrect — the presence of an exception clause does not cure the fettering if the authority never genuinely considers exceptions.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Councillor Stubbs, acting as chair of Wharton Magna Planning Committee, refuses Marcus Bridgewater's planning application on the ground that the development 'would not benefit local businesses'. This reason is not found in planning legislation. On what ground of judicial review is this decision most vulnerable?

options.A: Irrationality — no reasonable committee would reach this conclusion.
options.B: Illegality — the decision-maker has taken into account an irrelevant consideration and/or acted outside the legal powers conferred by the relevant legislation (Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for the Civil Service [1985]).
options.C: Procedural impropriety — the committee failed to follow its own procedure.
options.D: Proportionality — the refusal is disproportionate to the benefit obtained.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In CCSU v Minister for the Civil Service [1985] the House of Lords identified three grounds of judicial review: illegality, irrationality (Wednesbury unreasonableness), and procedural impropriety. Illegality encompasses acting outside the scope of legal powers (ultra vires), including taking into account irrelevant considerations or ignoring relevant ones. 'Benefit to local businesses' is not a material planning consideration under the Town and Country Planning Act — it is legally irrelevant and its use renders the decision unlawful on grounds of illegality. Irrationality (A) concerns extreme unreasonableness, not the use of wrong criteria. Procedural impropriety (C) concerns process failures. Proportionality (D) is recognised in HRA/EU contexts but is less straightforwardly applicable here.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Which statement correctly describes the Wednesbury unreasonableness ground of judicial review?

options.A: A decision is unlawful if the court disagrees with the outcome.
options.B: A decision is unlawful if it is so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could ever have come to it (Associated Provincial Picture Houses Ltd v Wednesbury Corporation [1948]); the court does not substitute its own view for that of the decision-maker.
options.C: A decision is unlawful if it lacks proportionality.
options.D: A decision is unlawful if the decision-maker failed to give reasons.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Wednesbury unreasonableness (Associated Provincial Picture Houses v Wednesbury [1948]) is a high threshold: the decision must be so unreasonable that no reasonable authority, properly directing itself on the relevant law and facts, could have reached it. It is not enough for the court to disagree — there must be an extreme or outrageous unreasonableness. This test is deferential to public authorities. Courts have applied a more intensive review in cases involving fundamental rights (R v Ministry of Defence ex parte Smith [1996]). Proportionality (C) is a related but distinct ground. Failure to give reasons (D) is a procedural impropriety point.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Marcus Bridgewater applies for a licence from Wharton Magna Council. The council refuses the application without giving him any opportunity to make representations. Which ground of judicial review is engaged?

options.A: Illegality — the council acted outside its powers.
options.B: Procedural impropriety — including breach of natural justice (the right to a fair hearing / audi alteram partem); Bridgewater had a legitimate expectation of being heard before an adverse decision was made.
options.C: Irrationality — no reasonable authority would refuse without hearing the applicant.
options.D: Proportionality — the refusal without hearing is disproportionate.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Procedural impropriety encompasses two main categories: (i) failure to comply with statutory procedural requirements; and (ii) breach of the rules of natural justice (including the right to a fair hearing — audi alteram partem — and the rule against bias — nemo iudex in causa sua). Where a decision significantly affects an individual's rights or legitimate expectations, natural justice requires that person to be given notice and an opportunity to make representations before the decision is taken (Ridge v Baldwin [1964]). Bridgewater had no opportunity to be heard — this is a procedural impropriety. The other grounds do not directly address the procedural defect.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Marcus Bridgewater wishes to seek judicial review of a planning decision made four months ago. He delayed because he was trying to resolve the matter informally. What time limit applies?

options.A: Six years — the standard limitation period applies to judicial review.
options.B: An application for judicial review must be made promptly and in any event within three months of the date when the grounds for the claim first arose (CPR r.54.5); planning cases have a shorter period of six weeks; the court may extend time but delay without good reason will be refused.
options.C: There is no fixed time limit — the court has complete discretion.
options.D: One year — the same as for personal injury claims.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: CPR r.54.5 requires a claim for judicial review to be filed promptly and in any event within three months of the date when the grounds for the claim first arose. For planning cases under the Town and Country Planning Act 1990, the statutory period is six weeks. The court may extend time where there is good reason to do so, but a four-month delay to explore informal resolution may or may not be accepted depending on the circumstances. The overriding requirement is promptness even within the three-month window. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Marcus Bridgewater wins his judicial review of a planning refusal. The court wishes to quash the unlawful decision. What is the modern name for the remedy formerly called certiorari?

options.A: Mandatory order.
options.B: Quashing order — formerly certiorari; it sets aside (quashes) the unlawful decision.
options.C: Prohibiting order.
options.D: Declaration.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Civil Procedure (Amendment No.4) Rules 2000 renamed the prerogative remedies: (i) certiorari → quashing order (quashes an unlawful decision); (ii) mandamus → mandatory order (compels a public body to perform a public duty); (iii) prohibition → prohibiting order (prevents a public body from acting unlawfully in the future). A declaration (D) is an equitable remedy stating the legal position but not itself quashing the decision. The court grants remedies at its discretion even after a successful judicial review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Wharton Magna Council passes a byelaw prohibiting any public protest within 200 metres of the Town Hall. Marcus Bridgewater challenges this as a breach of Article 10 ECHR. The Council argues public order justifies the restriction. Which analysis framework applies?

options.A: Article 10 is absolute — any restriction is automatically unlawful.
options.B: Article 10 is a qualified right: a restriction is permissible if it is (i) prescribed by law; (ii) pursues a legitimate aim listed in Article 10(2) (including public safety and prevention of disorder); and (iii) is necessary in a democratic society — i.e., proportionate to the legitimate aim (Handyside v UK (1976)).
options.C: The restriction is lawful provided a majority of councillors voted for it.
options.D: Article 10 only protects political speech by elected representatives.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 10 ECHR protects freedom of expression but is a qualified right — restrictions can be justified under Article 10(2). The three-part test is: (i) the restriction must be prescribed by law (clear and accessible legal basis); (ii) it must pursue a legitimate aim from the exhaustive list in Article 10(2); (iii) it must be necessary in a democratic society — pressing social need, proportionate, and with reasons that are relevant and sufficient (Handyside v UK). A blanket 200m exclusion zone may fail the proportionality test. Options A and D misstate the nature and scope of Article 10. Option C is a pure democratic legitimacy argument that does not satisfy the Article 10(2) analysis.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The government compulsorily acquires Ashworth Hall without paying compensation. Colonel Ashworth challenges this under the ECHR. Which provision protects his property rights?

options.A: Article 8 — right to respect for home.
options.B: Article 1 of Protocol 1 — every natural or legal person is entitled to the peaceful enjoyment of possessions; deprivation of property is only lawful in the public interest, subject to conditions provided for by law, and subject to compensation.
options.C: Article 6 — right to a fair trial.
options.D: Article 14 — prohibition of discrimination.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 1 of Protocol 1 (A1P1) to the ECHR protects the peaceful enjoyment of possessions. Under A1P1, the state may deprive a person of their possessions only: (i) in the public interest; (ii) subject to conditions provided for by law; and (iii) subject to general principles of international law, which require payment of compensation. The absence of compensation in a compulsory purchase would render the acquisition unlawful under A1P1 (James v UK [1986]). Article 8 (A) may also be engaged regarding the home, but A1P1 is the primary property protection. Articles 6 and 14 are engaged in different contexts.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Victoria Ashworth's law tutor argues that the European Communities Act 1972 was a 'constitutional statute' that could not be implicitly repealed by later inconsistent legislation. What is the legal basis for this argument?

options.A: The Diceyan principle of parliamentary sovereignty — later Acts always override earlier Acts.
options.B: The principle established in Thoburn v Sunderland CC [2002] that certain Acts of constitutional importance (including the ECA 1972, MCA 1997, HRA 1998) are 'constitutional statutes' that can only be expressly repealed, not implicitly repealed by inconsistent later legislation.
options.C: EU law requires member states to entrench EU-implementing legislation.
options.D: The common law prohibits the repeal of any legislation more than 25 years old.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In Thoburn v Sunderland CC [2002] Laws LJ argued for a category of 'constitutional statutes' which, by virtue of their fundamental constitutional importance, cannot be implicitly repealed by later inconsistent ordinary legislation — only express repeal would suffice. He identified the ECA 1972, the MCA 1997, and the HRA 1998 as examples. This modifies the traditional Diceyan principle (A) that later legislation automatically prevails over earlier inconsistent Acts. The ECA 1972 has since been repealed expressly by the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, which itself was a constitutional statute. Option C is incorrect as a basis. Option D has no legal basis.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The government publishes a policy document promising that existing licence holders will be given 12 months' notice before any change in licensing conditions. Wharton Magna Builders Ltd holds such a licence. The government then changes the conditions with 2 weeks' notice. Which public law principle is engaged?

options.A: Parliamentary sovereignty — the government can always change policy.
options.B: Legitimate expectation — a clear and unambiguous representation by a public body to an identifiable person or group gives rise to a procedural legitimate expectation that they will be given notice or consulted before the expected benefit is removed (R v North and East Devon HA ex parte Coughlan [2001]).
options.C: Rule of law — changes to legal rules must be published.
options.D: Proportionality — only major changes require notice.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The doctrine of legitimate expectation protects individuals who have reasonably relied on representations made by public bodies. In R v North and East Devon HA ex parte Coughlan [2001] the Court of Appeal recognised that a clear and unambiguous promise or representation by a public authority gives rise to a legitimate expectation — either procedural (the right to be heard before the benefit is withdrawn) or substantive (the right to the promised benefit itself). A published policy of 12 months' notice creates a clear expectation that licence holders will be consulted or given that notice before a change. Ignoring this expectation without good public interest justification is unlawful. Option A overstates the scope of parliamentary/executive power to override legitimate expectations.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statute provides that the Minister's decision 'shall not be questioned in any court'. Marcus Bridgewater wishes to challenge the decision by judicial review. Can he do so?

options.A: No — Parliament's express instruction that decisions cannot be reviewed must be followed.
options.B: Yes — courts have historically construed ouster clauses narrowly and refused to allow them to exclude judicial review of jurisdictional errors; the courts protect the rule of law by ensuring the executive remains subject to legal scrutiny (Anisminic Ltd v Foreign Compensation Commission [1969]).
options.C: No — ouster clauses are always effective.
options.D: Yes — but only if the minister acted with bad faith.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In Anisminic Ltd v Foreign Compensation Commission [1969] the House of Lords held that a statute providing that decisions 'shall not be questioned in any court' did not protect a decision that was made in error of law — because a decision tainted by an error of law was not a 'determination' within the meaning of the statute at all. Courts have consistently read ouster clauses narrowly to preserve judicial review jurisdiction, on the basis that Parliament cannot have intended to oust review of unlawful conduct. The Supreme Court has more recently examined the tension between parliamentary sovereignty and the rule of law in Privacy International [2019]. Option A gives ouster clauses more effect than courts allow. Option C is wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Frank Hollis is detained by police under terrorism legislation for 48 hours without being brought before a court. Which Article 5 guarantee is directly engaged?

options.A: Article 5(1) — the right to liberty; detention must be within one of the exhaustive permitted grounds in Article 5(1)(a)-(f).
options.B: Article 5(3) — a person arrested on reasonable suspicion must be brought promptly before a judge; prolonged pre-charge detention without judicial oversight may violate this guarantee.
options.C: Article 5(4) — right to speedily challenge the lawfulness of detention before a court.
options.D: All of the above — all three sub-articles are simultaneously engaged by 48-hour detention without judicial oversight.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 5 ECHR contains multiple inter-related guarantees: (1) Article 5(1) protects the fundamental right to liberty — any deprivation must fall within one of the exhaustive permitted categories; (2) Article 5(3) requires that a person arrested under 5(1)(c) (for criminal offences) be brought promptly before a judge; (3) Article 5(4) gives any detained person the right to challenge the lawfulness of detention speedily before a court (habeas corpus equivalent). Forty-eight hours without any judicial oversight potentially engages all three. Option D is the most complete answer.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A national environmental NGO wishes to challenge a government decision to grant planning permission for a new coal mine in Wharton Magna. The NGO has no property interest near the mine but campaigns on environmental issues. Does it have standing to bring judicial review?

options.A: No — only persons with a direct property interest have standing in planning cases.
options.B: Yes — under CPR r.54.4, the claimant must have a 'sufficient interest' in the matter to which the application relates (Senior Courts Act 1981 s.31(3)); courts have recognised that pressure groups and NGOs with relevant expertise and concern for the subject matter may have sufficient interest (R v Foreign Secretary ex parte World Development Movement [1995]).
options.C: No — judicial review is only available to individuals, not organisations.
options.D: Yes — any citizen can challenge any government decision by judicial review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Senior Courts Act 1981 s.31(3) requires the claimant to have a 'sufficient interest'. In R v Foreign Secretary ex parte World Development Movement [1995] the Divisional Court held that an NGO had sufficient interest to challenge a government decision on overseas aid, despite having no direct financial or property interest. Factors included the importance of vindicating the rule of law and the absence of anyone with a greater personal interest who would challenge the decision. Environmental NGOs with relevant expertise and a demonstrable concern for the subject matter regularly satisfy the sufficient interest test in planning and environmental cases. Option A is too restrictive. Option C is wrong. Option D overstates standing.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Prime Minister exercises prerogative power to deploy armed forces overseas. Marcus Bridgewater seeks judicial review of this decision. What is the court likely to decide?

options.A: The court will quash the decision as unlawful.
options.B: The deployment of armed forces is a non-justiciable prerogative power; courts will decline to review decisions in this area on the basis that it involves questions of national security and foreign policy that are not susceptible to judicial review (R v Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs ex parte Everett [1989]; CCSU v Minister for the Civil Service [1985]).
options.C: The court will review the decision but apply a lower standard of scrutiny.
options.D: The Prime Minister's prerogative powers are immune from any legal challenge whatsoever.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In CCSU v Minister for the Civil Service [1985] the House of Lords confirmed that while prerogative powers are in principle reviewable, certain prerogative decisions are non-justiciable — they are not amenable to judicial review because they involve high policy matters (national security, foreign affairs, deployment of armed forces, treaty-making) that courts are institutionally ill-equipped to assess. The deployment of armed forces falls into this category. Courts are not immune from ever reviewing any prerogative exercise (D overstates), but will decline where the subject matter is non-justiciable. Option A overstates judicial willingness to intervene. Option C understates the non-justiciability principle.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: In R (Miller) v Prime Minister [2019] (Miller No.2), the Supreme Court ruled that the Prime Minister's advice to prorogue Parliament for five weeks was unlawful. On what basis?

options.A: The prorogation breached the European Communities Act 1972.
options.B: The prorogation was unlawful because it had the effect of frustrating Parliament's ability to carry out its constitutional functions without reasonable justification — the prerogative to prorogue cannot be used in a way that defeats the constitutional principles of Parliamentary sovereignty and the accountability of the executive to Parliament.
options.C: The Queen acted unlawfully by proroguing Parliament.
options.D: Prorogation is always unlawful if it lasts more than four weeks.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In R (Miller) v Prime Minister [2019] the Supreme Court held unanimously that the Prime Minister's advice to prorogue Parliament for five weeks (in a period of acute constitutional importance around Brexit) was unlawful. The Court reasoned that the prerogative to advise prorogation cannot be used in a way that frustrates Parliament from exercising its fundamental constitutional functions (scrutiny of the executive and legislation). There was no reasonable justification for the extended prorogation. The decision was justiciable — courts can review exercises of prerogative power for constitutional lawfulness. Option A is wrong. Option C is wrong — the Queen acted on advice. Option D invents a fixed time limit.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The UK Parliament wishes to legislate on a matter that falls within the legislative competence of the Scottish Parliament. What constitutional convention applies?

options.A: The UK Parliament cannot legislate on devolved matters under any circumstances.
options.B: The Sewel Convention — by convention, the UK Parliament will not normally legislate on devolved matters without the consent of the devolved legislature (expressed through a legislative consent motion); this convention was given statutory recognition in the Scotland Act 2016 s.2 but remains a convention, not a legally enforceable rule.
options.C: The Scottish Parliament must hold a referendum before consenting.
options.D: The UK Parliament must obtain court approval before legislating on devolved matters.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Sewel Convention provides that the UK Parliament will not normally legislate with regard to devolved matters without the consent of the relevant devolved legislature. It is expressed through Legislative Consent Motions (LCMs). Scotland Act 2016 s.2 gave the convention statutory recognition ('it is recognised that the Parliament of the United Kingdom will not normally legislate with regard to devolved matters without the consent of the Scottish Parliament'), but the Supreme Court in R (Miller) v Secretary of State [2017] (Miller No.1) confirmed that it remains a political convention, not a legally enforceable obligation — the UK Parliament retains sovereignty to legislate even without consent. Option A overstates the constraint on the UK Parliament. Options C and D are wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The government introduces a restriction on the right to assembly that interferes with Article 11 ECHR. In assessing the lawfulness of the restriction under the HRA 1998, what test does the court apply?

options.A: Wednesbury unreasonableness only.
options.B: A structured proportionality analysis: (i) is the aim legitimate? (ii) is the restriction rationally connected to the aim? (iii) could a less restrictive measure achieve the aim? (iv) does the restriction strike a fair balance between the rights of the individual and the interests of the community? (De Freitas v Permanent Secretary [1999]; Bank Mellat v Her Majesty's Treasury [2013]).
options.C: The court defers entirely to the government's judgment on national security grounds.
options.D: The restriction is automatically unlawful if it infringes an Article 11 right.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In HRA 1998 cases involving qualified Convention rights, English courts apply a structured proportionality analysis. In Bank Mellat v Her Majesty's Treasury (No.2) [2013] the Supreme Court set out a four-stage test: (i) whether the objective is sufficiently important to justify limiting the right; (ii) whether the measure is rationally connected to the objective; (iii) whether a less intrusive measure could achieve the same objective; and (iv) whether, balancing the severity of the rights restriction against the importance of the objective, a fair balance has been struck. This is more intensive than Wednesbury (A). Blanket deference (C) is inappropriate for human rights interferences. Automatic invalidity (D) is wrong — Article 11 is a qualified right with permitted restrictions.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Marcus Bridgewater wishes to challenge a planning inspector's decision by judicial review. An alternative statutory appeal exists under the Town and Country Planning Act 1990 s.288 to the High Court. What is the court likely to do with his judicial review application?

options.A: Hear the judicial review regardless of the alternative remedy.
options.B: Likely refuse permission for judicial review on the basis that the claimant has an adequate alternative remedy available — courts generally require claimants to exhaust available alternative remedies before granting judicial review.
options.C: Grant judicial review automatically because planning decisions affect property rights.
options.D: Transfer the judicial review application to the planning inspector.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: A fundamental principle governing the grant of permission for judicial review is that the claimant must have no adequate alternative remedy (or must have exhausted available alternatives). Where Parliament has provided a specific statutory right of appeal (such as s.288 TCPA 1990), courts will ordinarily refuse judicial review and require the claimant to use the statutory route. This prevents judicial review from becoming a general appeal mechanism. Exceptions exist (e.g., the statutory route is inadequate or the point is one of pure law) but they are narrow. Options A, C and D ignore the alternative remedy principle.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Wharton Magna Council provides a benefit to married couples but not to unmarried couples in otherwise identical situations. A cohabiting couple challenges this under Article 14 ECHR. What must they show?

options.A: Article 14 is a free-standing right to equality that applies in all circumstances.
options.B: Article 14 prohibits discrimination in the enjoyment of the rights and freedoms set out in the Convention; the couple must show: (i) they fall within the ambit of another Convention right (e.g., Article 8 — family life); (ii) they have been treated differently from others in an analogous situation; (iii) there is no objective justification for the difference in treatment.
options.C: The couple must show the council intended to discriminate against them.
options.D: Article 14 can only be invoked by persons in protected classes defined by UK statute.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 14 ECHR prohibits discrimination in the enjoyment of Convention rights and freedoms — it is not a free-standing right to equality (A). To invoke Article 14, the claimant must bring the complaint within the ambit of another Convention right. Once within the ambit, the court considers whether: (i) the claimant is treated differently from others in an analogous situation; (ii) there is an objective and reasonable justification for the different treatment (proportionality). Marital status is a ground analogous to those listed in Article 14. Unlike UK equality law, Article 14 does not require proof of intent to discriminate (C). The grounds listed in Article 14 are not exhaustive — 'other status' can include marital/relationship status (D is wrong).

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A Minister purports to use a wide Henry VIII clause in a statute to repeal primary legislation without Parliamentary approval. Lord Judge (former Lord Chief Justice) criticises this. On what constitutional principle is this criticism based?

options.A: Separation of church and state.
options.B: The rule of law and Parliamentary sovereignty — Henry VIII clauses allow the executive to amend or repeal primary legislation by secondary legislation, bypassing the normal legislative process; this concentrates power in the executive and undermines parliamentary scrutiny and the constitutional principle that primary legislation should only be changed by Parliament.
options.C: The royal prerogative.
options.D: EU law supremacy.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Henry VIII clauses (so named after the Statute of Proclamations 1539, which gave Henry VIII power to legislate by proclamation) are provisions in primary legislation that grant ministers power to amend or repeal Acts of Parliament by statutory instrument. Critics argue they breach the constitutional principles of parliamentary sovereignty (primary law should be made by Parliament) and the rule of law (citizens should be governed by democratically enacted rules subject to proper scrutiny). The Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee and senior judges including Lord Judge have expressed serious concerns. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Councillor Stubbs's planning committee refuses Marcus Bridgewater's application but gives no written reasons. Is there a general duty to give reasons for administrative decisions?

options.A: Yes — there is always a general common law duty to give reasons.
options.B: No — there is no general common law duty to give reasons, but a duty may arise in specific contexts: where statute requires it; where the decision affects a fundamental right; where there is a legitimate expectation of reasons; or where fairness demands it (R v Higher Education Funding Council ex parte Institute of Dental Surgery [1994]).
options.C: No — reasons are never required for planning decisions.
options.D: Yes — all public decisions must be accompanied by written reasons under the HRA 1998.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: English common law does not impose a general duty on all public bodies to give reasons for all decisions. However, the courts have recognised that in certain circumstances a duty to give reasons does arise: where statute requires it (e.g., the Town and Country Planning (Development Management Procedure) Order 2012 requires reasons for planning refusals, making this a statutory duty in planning); where the decision affects a person's livelihood or fundamental rights; where there is a legitimate expectation of reasons; and in some cases of important administrative decisions (Stefan v GMC [1999]). Option A overstates the common law position. Option C is wrong — planning refusals specifically require reasons by statute. Option D is wrong — HRA 1998 does not create a blanket reasons obligation.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A prisoner dies in Wharton Magna prison in suspicious circumstances. The state's investigation is conducted internally without independent scrutiny. Which Article 2 obligation is engaged beyond the negative duty not to kill?

options.A: Article 2 has no positive obligations — only the negative duty not to kill applies.
options.B: Article 2 imposes a positive procedural obligation to conduct an effective official investigation into deaths where the state may be implicated; the investigation must be independent, adequate, and capable of leading to the identification and punishment of those responsible (Jordan v UK (2001)).
options.C: Article 3 applies instead of Article 2 where the death occurred in custody.
options.D: The state's internal investigation is always sufficient to discharge Article 2 obligations.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 2 ECHR imposes both substantive and procedural obligations. The substantive obligation includes the negative duty not to take life unlawfully and positive duties to take preventive operational measures where there is a real and immediate risk to life (Osman v UK [1998]). The procedural obligation requires an effective official investigation into deaths where agents of the state are involved or where the state may bear responsibility — the investigation must be independent, adequate, reasonably prompt, and open to public scrutiny (Jordan v UK [2001]). An internal investigation without independence would breach this procedural obligation. Option A is wrong. Option C is wrong — Article 2 applies to deaths, Article 3 to treatment. Option D is wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A senior civil servant at the Department for Transport makes a catastrophic error in a major procurement process, costing taxpayers hundreds of millions of pounds. The Minister was unaware. Which constitutional convention is most directly engaged?

options.A: Parliamentary sovereignty — Parliament can investigate the matter.
options.B: Individual ministerial responsibility — by convention, ministers are accountable to Parliament for the actions of their departments, including errors by civil servants acting in their name; the extent of personal responsibility depends on whether the minister knew of or directed the error.
options.C: The Salisbury Convention — the House of Lords should not block government business.
options.D: Collective Cabinet responsibility — all ministers must collectively support the decision.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The convention of individual ministerial responsibility requires ministers to be accountable to Parliament for the actions of their departments. However, the extent of personal accountability has evolved: following the Crichel Down Affair (1954), ministers would resign for serious errors even without personal involvement; modern practice is more nuanced — ministers are responsible for explaining errors and providing remedies, but personal resignation may only be expected where the minister was personally involved or where confidence in their leadership has been destroyed. The error by a civil servant engages this convention most directly. The Salisbury Convention (C) concerns Lords-Commons relations. Collective responsibility (D) concerns Cabinet unity.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A judicial review claim by Marcus Bridgewater challenges a planning permission granted to Ashworth Developments Ltd. The developer has not been named as a defendant. What procedural step should be taken?

options.A: The developer has no right to participate in proceedings affecting its permission.
options.B: Ashworth Developments Ltd should be named as an interested party under CPR r.54.6; interested parties are directly affected by the outcome of the claim and are entitled to file an acknowledgment of service and to participate in the hearing.
options.C: The developer must issue a separate action to protect its interests.
options.D: Only government departments can be defendants in judicial review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: CPR r.54.6 requires the claim form in judicial review proceedings to identify any person the claimant considers to be an interested party (a person directly affected by the claim). The permission holder (here Ashworth Developments Ltd) is directly affected by any order quashing the planning permission and must be named. Interested parties may file an acknowledgment of service and participate in the hearing. Option A is wrong — the developer has significant rights at stake and a right to participate. Option C is wrong — there is no need for separate proceedings. Option D is wrong — defendants are public bodies but interested parties can be private persons or companies.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Can a private individual sue another private individual directly under the HRA 1998 for breach of a Convention right?

options.A: Yes — the HRA 1998 creates direct rights between all persons.
options.B: No — the HRA 1998 s.6 makes it unlawful for a public authority to act in breach of Convention rights; private individuals cannot directly sue each other under the HRA 1998, though Convention rights may have indirect horizontal effect through courts developing the common law compatibly with Convention rights.
options.C: Yes — but only for Article 8 (privacy) claims.
options.D: No — only the ECtHR can enforce Convention rights against private parties.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: HRA 1998 s.6 creates a duty only on 'public authorities' not to act incompatibly with Convention rights. A private person cannot sue another private person directly under the HRA 1998 (no 'direct horizontal effect'). However, courts are themselves public authorities and must act compatibly with Convention rights (s.6(3)(a)); this means courts must develop the common law and interpret existing law in a Convention-compatible way even in private litigation (indirect horizontal effect) — as seen in privacy and defamation cases (Campbell v MGN [2004]). Option A overstates the effect. Option C is wrong. Option D is wrong — the ECtHR only has jurisdiction against state parties, not private individuals.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Injunctive relief — interim relief against the Crown
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: In judicial review proceedings against a government minister, a claimant urgently seeks an interim injunction restraining the minister from implementing a decision pending the outcome of the case. In light of M v Home Office [1993], is an interim injunction available against the Crown?

options.A: No — the Crown has absolute immunity from injunctions.
options.B: Yes — following M v Home Office [1993] HL, interim injunctions are available against government ministers acting in their official capacity; the Crown is not immune from injunctive relief in public law proceedings.
options.C: Yes — but only for declarations, not injunctions.
options.D: No — only the European Court can grant injunctions against the Crown.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In M v Home Office [1993] the House of Lords held that courts have jurisdiction to grant injunctions against ministers acting in their official capacity (as distinct from the Crown itself, to which the Crown Proceedings Act 1947 s.21 still limits injunctions). The case also confirmed that ministers can be found in contempt of court for breaching injunctions — maintaining judicial oversight of executive action. Option A incorrectly asserts absolute immunity. Option C is wrong — injunctions, not just declarations, are available. Option D is wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A local council adopts a rigid policy that all planning applications in a conservation area will automatically be refused, with no exceptions permitted. Marcus Bridgewater applies and is automatically refused. Is the policy lawful?

options.A: Yes — a council may set firm policies to ensure consistency.
options.B: No — a public authority that has a statutory discretion must genuinely exercise it in each case; adopting a rigid policy that fetters the discretion by allowing no exceptions is unlawful (British Oxygen Co v Board of Trade [1971]).
options.C: Yes — provided the policy was adopted by a full council vote.
options.D: No — only central government can create planning policies.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In British Oxygen Co v Board of Trade [1971] the House of Lords held that a public authority may formulate a policy for the exercise of its statutory discretion, but must not so slavishly follow that policy as to refuse to listen to applicants with exceptional cases. A body with a discretion must genuinely consider each case on its merits — a rigid 'no exceptions' policy fetters the discretion and makes the refusal unlawful without proper consideration. The council may have a general presumption against development in conservation areas, but must consider individual applications. Options A and C permit an unlawful rigidity. Option D is wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Following the repeal of the Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011 by the Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022, how is Parliament now dissolved?

options.A: Parliament dissolves automatically at the end of a five-year fixed term.
options.B: Parliament is dissolved by prerogative power — the monarch, on the advice of the Prime Minister, dissolves Parliament; the FTPA 2011's fixed-term and early-election mechanisms have been repealed and the prerogative restored.
options.C: Parliament can only be dissolved by a two-thirds vote of the House of Commons.
options.D: Parliament can only be dissolved by Act of Parliament.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011 introduced fixed five-year terms and a mechanism for early elections requiring a two-thirds majority. The Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022 repealed the FTPA 2011 and restored the monarch's prerogative power to dissolve Parliament on the advice of the Prime Minister. The Act also expressly provides that the decision to dissolve and the calling of a new Parliament are non-justiciable. Options A and C describe the now-repealed FTPA regime. Option D is wrong — no Act of Parliament is needed.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Costs in judicial review — protective costs orders
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: An environmental NGO brings a judicial review challenging a government environmental decision. The NGO is concerned about costs exposure if it loses. What costs protection mechanism may be available?

options.A: No costs protection is available in judicial review.
options.B: A Protective Costs Order (PCO) / Cost Capping Order under CPR r.46.16-17 — in public interest litigation, a court may cap the claimant's liability for the defendant's costs if the case raises an issue of genuine public importance and it would not be reasonable to expect the claimant to proceed without costs protection (Corner House Research [2005]).
options.C: Legal aid is automatically available to NGOs bringing environmental claims.
options.D: The claimant always pays the defendant's costs in judicial review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In R (Corner House Research) v Secretary of State for Trade and Industry [2005] the Court of Appeal set out the criteria for Protective Costs Orders (now Cost Capping Orders under CPR r.46.16-17) in public interest litigation: (i) the case raises issues of genuine public importance; (ii) the public interest requires the case to be resolved; (iii) the claimant has no private interest in the outcome; (iv) the claimant would not proceed without costs protection; (v) it is fair and just in all the circumstances. In Aarhus Convention cases (environmental judicial review), CPR r.45.41-44 provides specific fixed costs protection for qualifying claimants. Option A is wrong. Option C is wrong. Option D is wrong — the normal rule of costs following the event applies, subject to PCO/CCO.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A court finds that a public authority has violated Marcus Bridgewater's Convention rights. In deciding whether to award damages under HRA 1998 s.8, what principle does the court apply?

options.A: Damages are automatically awarded for every Convention breach.
options.B: The court may award damages only if it is necessary to afford just satisfaction under s.8(3), having regard to the principles applied by the ECtHR; damages are not automatically awarded — a declaration or other relief may be sufficient.
options.C: HRA damages are assessed on the same basis as common law tort damages.
options.D: Only the ECtHR can award damages for Convention breaches.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: HRA 1998 s.8 provides that where a court finds a public authority has acted unlawfully (in breach of s.6), it may grant such relief or remedy as it considers just and appropriate, including damages. However, s.8(3) restricts the power to award damages to cases where it is necessary to afford just satisfaction. The court is guided by the principles of the ECtHR, which often awards modest 'just satisfaction' rather than full compensatory damages. A declaration that rights have been violated may be sufficient in some cases. HRA damages are not assessed the same as common law tort damages (C). Option D is wrong — domestic courts have this power under s.8.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Advising on judicial review — prospects assessment
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Gerald Crane is instructed to bring a judicial review on behalf of Marcus Bridgewater. Crane privately thinks the claim has very limited prospects of obtaining permission. He does not share this view with Bridgewater. Which SRA Code obligation is most directly engaged?

options.A: The duty to act in clients' best interests includes providing an honest assessment of the merits and prospects of litigation, even if this advice is unwelcome; withholding this assessment breaches para 1.2 and para 1.4 (SRA Code).
options.B: No obligation — the client gives instructions and the solicitor follows them.
options.C: The duty of confidentiality prevents Crane from sharing his views.
options.D: The duty to the court requires Crane to disclose his doubts to the Administrative Court.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: SRA Code para 1.2 requires solicitors to act in clients' best interests, which includes giving honest advice about the merits and risks of litigation — even when this is unwelcome. Para 1.4 requires acting with integrity. Withholding an honest assessment of poor prospects deprives the client of information essential for an informed decision about whether to pursue costly proceedings. SRA Code para 3.4 also requires solicitors not to suppress their professional judgment. The duty of confidentiality (C) concerns disclosure to third parties, not honesty with one's own client. Disclosure to the court (D) is not required — though a solicitor should not make representations about merits they do not believe.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Marcus Bridgewater challenges a Home Office decision refusing him indefinite leave to remain on grounds it interferes with his Article 8 ECHR rights. The Upper Tribunal applies proportionality review. Which statement best describes the proportionality analysis under Bank Mellat v HM Treasury (No 2) [2013]?

options.A: The court asks only whether the decision is manifestly disproportionate, deferring entirely to ministerial judgment on necessity.
options.B: The court applies a structured four-stage test: legitimate aim, rational connection, no less restrictive alternative, and fair balance between individual and community interests.
options.C: Proportionality under the HRA is identical to Wednesbury unreasonableness and adds no additional scrutiny.
options.D: The court may only apply proportionality when the claimant relies on an absolute Convention right such as Article 3.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Bank Mellat (No 2) [2013] UKSC 39 confirmed a structured four-stage proportionality analysis: (i) sufficiently important objective; (ii) rational connection between measure and objective; (iii) no less restrictive alternative; (iv) fair balance. This is more intensive than Wednesbury and applies across qualified rights. Option A conflates proportionality with an older margin of appreciation approach; C incorrectly equates the tests; D wrongly limits proportionality to absolute rights.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Article 9 ECHR — freedom of thought, conscience and religion
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Donna Pearce, a care home employee, is dismissed after refusing to work Sunday shifts on religious grounds. She argues her Article 9 ECHR rights were violated. The employer is a private company. Which statement correctly identifies the legal position?

options.A: Article 9 ECHR binds private employers directly under the HRA 1998, so the dismissal is automatically unlawful.
options.B: The HRA does not bind private employers directly, but courts as public authorities must develop common law compatibly with Convention rights, and employment tribunals must interpret the Equality Act 2010 compatibly with Article 9.
options.C: Article 9 is an absolute right and cannot be interfered with by any employer, public or private.
options.D: Article 9 protection is limited to attendance at religious services and does not extend to refusal to work on a particular day.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The HRA 1998 s.6 binds public authorities, not private employers directly. However, tribunals as public authorities (s.6) must act compatibly with Convention rights, and s.3 requires legislation (including EA 2010) to be read compatibly. Article 9(2) allows interference for legitimate aims proportionately pursued — it is a qualified, not absolute, right. Courts develop the common law consistently with Convention rights (Campbell v MGN). Options A and C overstate horizontal direct effect; D wrongly narrows the scope of Article 9.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Article 11 ECHR — freedom of assembly and association
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Councillor Stubbs issues a dispersal order under the Police, Crime, Sentencing and Courts Act 2022 preventing a protest group organised by Rachel Okonkwo from assembling in the town square. Rachel seeks judicial review arguing a breach of Article 11. Which ground is most likely to succeed?

options.A: The order is unlawful because Article 11 confers an absolute right to peaceful assembly that cannot be restricted.
options.B: The order may be unlawful if it was not prescribed by law, did not pursue a legitimate aim, or was not necessary in a democratic society — the court applying proportionality review.
options.C: Dispersal orders under statute can never be challenged in judicial review as Parliament has authorised them.
options.D: Article 11 does not apply to outdoor protests; its protection is limited to indoor meetings.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 11(2) permits interference with assembly and association rights where it is: (i) prescribed by law; (ii) pursues a legitimate aim (public safety, prevention of disorder, etc.); (iii) necessary in a democratic society (proportionate). Courts apply structured proportionality review under the HRA. The power must be exercised compatibly; a blanket ban without particularised justification may be disproportionate. A is wrong — Article 11 is qualified; C is wrong — statutory powers must be exercised compatibly with HRA; D has no basis in law.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Judicial deference and the separation of powers
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: In a judicial review of a government decision to deploy troops abroad, the court is asked to assess whether the operation is consistent with international law. Which principle best explains why the court is likely to decline to adjudicate the substantive military question?

options.A: The court lacks jurisdiction over any matter involving the executive branch.
options.B: Decisions involving high policy, foreign affairs and national security are non-justiciable or attract a wide margin of appreciation, and courts will not second-guess complex military and political judgments.
options.C: The principle of parliamentary sovereignty means courts must follow executive decisions on military matters.
options.D: Judicial review is only available for domestic decisions; international matters are entirely excluded from the supervisory jurisdiction.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Courts recognise areas of non-justiciability — matters so inherently political or requiring specialist expertise (military strategy, foreign policy) that judicial intervention is inappropriate (Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for the Civil Service [1985]). This is distinct from an absolute bar on jurisdiction; courts retain supervisory jurisdiction but apply a wide margin of appreciation in high-policy areas. A is too absolute — courts have jurisdiction but exercise restraint; C misstates parliamentary sovereignty; D is incorrect — domestic courts can consider international law issues.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A declaration of incompatibility has been made in respect of a provision of the Immigration Act. The relevant minister wishes to amend the legislation quickly without a full parliamentary bill. Which mechanism enables this?

options.A: The minister may unilaterally repeal the incompatible provision by executive order without parliamentary involvement.
options.B: The minister may use a remedial order under s.10 HRA 1998 to amend primary legislation by statutory instrument, subject to parliamentary scrutiny under Schedule 2.
options.C: No amendment is possible without a full Act of Parliament — delegated legislation cannot amend primary legislation.
options.D: The minister must apply to the Supreme Court for an order directing Parliament to legislate.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Section 10 HRA 1998 provides a 'fast-track' procedure: where a declaration of incompatibility has been made (or a judgment of the ECtHR requires it), a minister may make a remedial order by statutory instrument to amend the incompatible legislation. Schedule 2 HRA sets out parliamentary scrutiny requirements (usually super-affirmative procedure). This is a Henry VIII power specifically created by the HRA. A is wrong — parliamentary involvement is required; C is incorrect — s.10 expressly permits SI amendment of primary legislation; D does not reflect the legal position.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Senedd passes legislation regulating land transactions in Wales. Westminster subsequently passes an Act of Parliament covering the same subject matter without consulting Cardiff. Which statement best describes the constitutional position?

options.A: The Westminster Act is invalid because devolution permanently transferred land law competence to the Senedd.
options.B: Westminster retains parliamentary sovereignty and may legislate for Wales on any matter, but the Sewel Convention requires it to seek the Senedd's legislative consent for devolved matters.
options.C: The Senedd has exclusive competence over land law in Wales and Westminster legislation automatically ceases to apply.
options.D: Devolved legislation always takes precedence over Westminster Acts in Wales by virtue of the Wales Act 2017.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Parliamentary sovereignty means Westminster retains legislative competence over all matters including Wales (Wales Act 2017 s.2 restates supremacy). The Sewel Convention (now reflected in Wales Act 2017 s.107(6)) requires Westminster to seek legislative consent from the Senedd before legislating in devolved areas. However, in Miller [2017] the Supreme Court confirmed the convention is not legally enforceable — Parliament may legislate without consent. The Senedd's competence is conferred by statute and subject to reserved matters. A and C are wrong; D misstates the hierarchy.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Northern Ireland — Belfast Agreement and the Assembly
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A Northern Ireland Assembly Act purports to legislate on a 'excepted' matter under Schedule 2 to the Northern Ireland Act 1998. The Attorney General refers the question to the Supreme Court. What is the likely outcome?

options.A: The Act is valid because the Northern Ireland Assembly has unfettered legislative competence.
options.B: The Act is outside the Assembly's legislative competence and the court will make a declaration accordingly, with the provision having no effect.
options.C: The Supreme Court has no jurisdiction to review Northern Ireland Assembly legislation.
options.D: Excepted matters may be legislated on by the Assembly with the Secretary of State's written consent.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Northern Ireland Act 1998 s.6 provides that an Assembly Act is not law to the extent it is outside legislative competence. Schedule 2 lists 'excepted matters' (reserved exclusively to Westminster, e.g., the Crown, international relations, defence). The Supreme Court has jurisdiction to determine legislative competence under s.11 NIA 1998. A provision on an excepted matter has no effect. Option D describes the position for 'reserved matters' (Schedule 3), not excepted matters. Options A and C are incorrect.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: FOIA 2000 — qualified exemptions and public interest test
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Ravi Sharma makes a FOIA request to Wharton Borough Council for internal communications relating to a planning decision affecting Ashworth Developments Ltd. The council refuses under s.36 FOIA (prejudice to effective conduct of public affairs). What must the council demonstrate?

options.A: Disclosure would absolutely harm the conduct of public affairs — no balancing exercise is required.
options.B: The qualified person has given a reasonable opinion that disclosure would or would be likely to inhibit free and frank discussion, and the public interest in maintaining the exemption outweighs the public interest in disclosure.
options.C: The information is entirely exempt from disclosure because it relates to a local authority decision.
options.D: The council need only show the information is commercially sensitive to rely on s.36.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Section 36 FOIA is a qualified exemption applicable to public authorities (other than government departments for s.36(2)(c)). The 'qualified person' (usually the Speaker/minister/monitoring officer) must give a reasonable opinion that one of the specified prejudices would occur. Being qualified, the exemption is subject to the public interest test in s.2 FOIA — the public interest in maintaining the exemption must outweigh the public interest in disclosure. A wrongly treats it as absolute; C and D misstate the basis for the exemption.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Natural justice — rule against bias (nemo iudex)
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A planning inspector conducting an inquiry into an application by Ashworth Developments Ltd is discovered to hold shares in a rival development company. Diana Pelletier, objecting to the development, applies for judicial review. Which test determines whether the inspector is disqualified?

options.A: Whether the inspector actually allowed the shareholding to influence the decision (subjective bias).
options.B: Whether the inspector's interest is so substantial that it automatically disqualifies them regardless of any appearance (automatic disqualification for pecuniary interest).
options.C: Whether a fair-minded and informed observer would conclude there is a real possibility that the decision-maker was biased (the Porter v Magill objective test).
options.D: Whether the claimant can prove on the balance of probabilities that the inspector consciously preferred the rival company's interests.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Porter v Magill [2001] UKHL 67 established that the test for apparent bias is whether a fair-minded and informed observer, having considered the facts, would conclude there is a real possibility that the tribunal was biased. A financial interest in a rival (rather than the applicant) would be analysed under this objective test rather than automatic disqualification (which applies to direct financial interests in the outcome — Dimes v Grand Junction Canal). Options A and D require proof of actual bias; B applies to direct financial interests in the proceedings, not a rival company.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Secretary of State for Health publishes a policy document stating that NHS patients who have been waiting more than 18 weeks will receive priority treatment. Hassan Nazari has waited 22 weeks and is told the policy no longer applies following budget cuts. He seeks judicial review. Which statement best reflects the law on substantive legitimate expectation?

options.A: Substantive legitimate expectations are not recognised in English administrative law; only procedural expectations can be enforced.
options.B: A clear and unambiguous promise or established practice may give rise to a substantive legitimate expectation; the authority may only depart from it if there is an overriding public interest justification proportionate to the impact on the individual.
options.C: The Secretary of State may freely depart from any published policy without any obligation to Hassan because policy documents are not legally binding.
options.D: Once a legitimate expectation arises, the authority is absolutely bound by the original promise and cannot depart from it under any circumstances.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: English courts recognise substantive legitimate expectations following R v North and East Devon HA ex p Coughlan [2001]. Where there is a clear, unambiguous representation to an individual or group, the court may hold the authority to its promise unless: (i) it is unlawful to do so; or (ii) there is a sufficiently overriding public interest justification, proportionately pursued. The court must balance the public interest against the impact on the individual. A is wrong — substantive expectations are recognised; C overstates the freedom to depart; D is too absolute.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statutory tribunal interprets its enabling Act as excluding a class of applicants from its jurisdiction. The applicant argues this interpretation is wrong in law. Following Anisminic Ltd v Foreign Compensation Commission [1969] and Cart v Upper Tribunal [2011], which proposition correctly states the supervisory jurisdiction of the High Court?

options.A: The High Court cannot review errors of law made within the tribunal's jurisdiction — only 'excess of jurisdiction' is reviewable.
options.B: Following Anisminic, all errors of law by inferior tribunals are jurisdictional; the High Court retains supervisory jurisdiction over all errors of law, subject to Cart limitations on upper tribunal review.
options.C: Only the Supreme Court can correct errors of law made by specialist tribunals.
options.D: Judicial review of tribunal decisions on legal interpretation is barred by the principle of statutory finality.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Anisminic [1969] AC 147 collapsed the distinction between jurisdictional and non-jurisdictional errors: any error of law by an inferior body is a jurisdictional error subject to judicial review. Cart v Upper Tribunal [2011] UKSC 28 (subsequently refined by R (Cart) considerations and the Judicial Review and Courts Act 2022) addressed the Upper Tribunal specifically. The general principle remains that superior courts supervise inferior tribunals for legal error. A reflects the pre-Anisminic position; C is wrong; D is incorrect — finality clauses are limited by Anisminic.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Priya Mehta obtains a declaration from the Administrative Court that the Secretary of State's policy on welfare benefits is unlawful. The Secretary of State maintains the policy in place. What is the legal effect?

options.A: The declaration is automatically enforceable and the Secretary of State may be imprisoned for contempt if the policy continues.
options.B: A declaration is a discretionary remedy that pronounces the legal position but is not directly coercive; in practice, public authorities comply, but non-compliance must be enforced through further proceedings such as mandamus or contempt (if an injunction is also granted).
options.C: Declarations are only advisory opinions and create no legal obligation whatsoever.
options.D: The effect of a declaration is to immediately and automatically void the impugned policy without further order.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: A declaration is a discretionary remedy that authoritatively states the law but does not directly compel action (R v Secretary of State for Employment ex p Equal Opportunities Commission [1995]). Public authorities conventionally comply. Enforcement of compliance requires additional orders — a mandatory order (mandamus) or an injunction — and contempt proceedings may follow breach of a court order, not the declaration itself. M v Home Office [1994] confirmed even the Crown can be found in contempt for breach of an injunction. Option C understates the legal significance; D is incorrect; A overstates the direct enforceability of a bare declaration.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statutory power to grant emergency licences is vested in the Secretary of State for Business. A senior civil servant in the department exercises the power without the Secretary of State's specific awareness of this particular decision. Is this lawful?

options.A: No — all statutory powers vested in a minister must be personally exercised by that minister and cannot be delegated.
options.B: Yes — under the Carltona principle, civil servants exercising statutory powers vested in a minister act as the minister's alter ego; the minister remains constitutionally responsible.
options.C: Yes, but only if the civil servant holds a grade above Deputy Secretary.
options.D: Yes, but only if Parliament has expressly authorised delegation in the enabling Act.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Carltona principle (Carltona Ltd v Commissioners of Works [1943]) holds that powers conferred on a minister may be exercised by officials of appropriate seniority within the department without express statutory authorisation to delegate. The minister remains constitutionally and politically responsible for the exercise of the power. The principle reflects the constitutional reality of modern government. Express statutory authorisation is not required (distinguishing Carltona delegation from delegation to outside bodies). A is wrong; C and D add requirements not found in the Carltona principle.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Crown immunity and public interest immunity
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: In judicial review proceedings brought by Gerald Crane, the government claims public interest immunity (PII) over certain documents on the ground of national security. The court must decide whether to uphold the claim. Which statement best describes the court's role?

options.A: The court must accept any ministerial certificate claiming PII without further inquiry.
options.B: The court must balance the public interest in non-disclosure against the public interest in the administration of justice, and may inspect the documents privately (in camera) to make that assessment.
options.C: PII claims automatically prevail over disclosure obligations in all cases involving national security.
options.D: PII is an absolute right of the executive that cannot be challenged in judicial proceedings.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Following Conway v Rimmer [1968] AC 910 and R v Chief Constable of West Midlands Police ex p Wiley [1995], courts do not simply accept ministerial PII certificates. They conduct a balancing exercise: the public interest in non-disclosure (e.g. national security, candour) weighed against the public interest in the administration of justice. The court may inspect documents privately. The Conway v Rimmer decision overruled the House of Lords' earlier deference in Conway v Rimmer and confirmed judicial oversight. Options A, C and D reflect the pre-1968 position, which is no longer law.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Alek Kowalski, a local resident living 3 km from a proposed industrial facility, applies for judicial review of the planning permission granted to Hollowell Engineering by Wharton Borough Council. Has he sufficient standing?

options.A: Only immediately adjacent neighbours have standing to challenge planning decisions in judicial review.
options.B: Alek must demonstrate he is directly employed in the local area to satisfy the sufficient interest test.
options.C: Under s.31(3) Senior Courts Act 1981, the court grants permission if the claimant has sufficient interest in the matter; proximity, local connection and potential impact on amenity may collectively satisfy this, even at 3 km.
options.D: Standing in planning judicial review is governed by the Town and Country Planning Act 1990 and requires Alek to have made representations at the planning inquiry.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Section 31(3) Senior Courts Act 1981 requires 'sufficient interest'. Courts have interpreted this broadly (R v IRC ex p National Federation of Self-Employed [1982]). In planning cases, local residents who may be affected by environmental or amenity impacts typically have sufficient interest even without living immediately adjacent. Prior participation in the planning process is relevant but not always required. A and B apply unduly narrow criteria; D incorrectly substitutes a statutory test from TCPA for the JR standing test.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Challenge to delegated legislation — ultra vires
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Minister for Housing makes regulations under the Housing Act 2020 s.45, which provides power to make regulations 'for the purposes of this Act'. The regulations introduce a new criminal offence not contemplated by the 2020 Act. Rachel Okonkwo is prosecuted under the regulation. Which ground of challenge is strongest?

options.A: The regulation is ultra vires because it goes beyond the scope of the enabling power — creating criminal offences requires express Parliamentary authority.
options.B: The regulation is valid because a minister always has implied power to create criminal offences when exercising delegated legislation powers.
options.C: The challenge must be brought within three months of the regulation being made, otherwise Rachel cannot rely on its invalidity at her prosecution.
options.D: Criminal courts cannot consider the vires of delegated legislation; only the Administrative Court can do so.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Delegated legislation must remain within the scope of the enabling power (the ultra vires doctrine). Courts will not readily imply power to create criminal offences — clear and express statutory language is required for penal provisions (R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Fire Brigades Union [1995]; principle of legality). A general purpose clause does not suffice.
