options.B: The magistrates follow the allocation procedure: consider whether the case is suitable for summary trial, applying the Sentencing Council allocation guideline; if they accept jurisdiction, the defendant is put to election; if the defendant elects Crown Court trial or the magistrates decline jurisdiction, the case is sent to the Crown Court.
options.C: The defendant has an automatic right to Crown Court trial for any either-way offence without magistrates' consideration of allocation.
options.D: The value of goods stolen automatically determines the court of trial.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: For either-way offences, the Criminal Justice Act 2003 (as amended by the Magistrates' Courts Act 1980 and Criminal Procedure Rules) requires: (i) hearing representations on allocation; (ii) magistrates considering allocation having regard to sentencing powers and the Allocation Guideline; (iii) if they accept jurisdiction, the defendant is given the election to choose Crown Court or summary trial; (iv) if magistrates decline jurisdiction or defendant elects Crown Court, the case is sent to the Crown Court. Value is a factor in the guideline but does not mechanically determine venue. A and D are incorrect; C overstates the defendant's right.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: At Frank Hollis's trial for handling stolen goods, the prosecution seeks to adduce a written statement from a witness who has since died. On what basis may this hearsay statement be admitted?

options.A: Hearsay is inadmissible in all circumstances in criminal proceedings.
options.B: Under s.116 CJA 2003, a hearsay statement may be admitted where the maker is unavailable (including death), provided the conditions are satisfied; additionally, the court has a residual inclusionary discretion under s.114(1)(d).
options.C: The statement is automatically admissible as documentary hearsay without any gateway.
options.D: The death of the witness means the defendant loses their right to a fair trial and the prosecution must stay proceedings.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 116 CJA 2003 provides a gateway for hearsay where the witness is unavailable due to specified reasons, including death (s.116(2)(a)). The statement must otherwise comply with s.116 conditions (the witness would have been competent, the statement was made by a person, etc.). The court also has a residual discretion under s.114(1)(d) to admit hearsay in the interests of justice. The court retains the s.126 discretion to exclude where admission would be unfair. Option A is wrong — hearsay is admissible through CJA 2003 gateways; C is wrong — gateways must be met; D is wrong.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis has previous convictions for handling stolen goods. The prosecution applies to adduce this bad character evidence at his current trial for handling. Through which gateway under s.101 CJA 2003 is this most likely to be admitted?

options.A: Gateway (a) — agreed by all parties.
options.B: Gateway (d) — the evidence is relevant to an important matter in issue between the defendant and the prosecution (specifically, propensity to commit offences of the kind charged).
options.C: Gateway (f) — it is evidence to correct a false impression given by the defendant.
options.D: Gateway (g) — the defendant has made an attack on another person's character.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 101(1)(d) CJA 2003 allows admission of bad character evidence relevant to an important matter in issue between defendant and prosecution. Section 103(1)(a) provides that 'an important matter in issue' includes propensity to commit offences of the kind charged. Previous convictions for the same type of offence (handling) are classically admitted through gateway (d) to establish propensity. The court retains s.101(3) discretion to exclude if admitting would make the proceedings unfair. Gateway (a) requires agreement; (f) and (g) apply in different circumstances.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth takes a laptop computer belonging to Hollowell Engineering from a store cupboard, believing the company owes him money and he is entitled to keep it until paid. Has James committed theft?

options.A: Yes — any unauthorised taking of property belonging to another is theft.
options.B: No — James has a genuine belief in a legal entitlement to the property under s.2(1)(a) Theft Act 1968, which negates dishonesty; provided the jury accepts this belief is genuine (not necessarily reasonable), the dishonesty element is not satisfied.
options.C: No — the actus reus of theft requires permanent deprivation, and James intends to return the laptop once paid.
options.D: Yes — a belief in a right to property can never negate dishonesty in theft.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Dishonesty under the Theft Act 1968 includes s.2(1)(a): a person is not dishonest if they appropriate property believing they have the legal right to deprive the other of it. This is a subjective test — the belief need not be reasonable, merely genuine. If the jury accept James genuinely (even if mistakenly) believed he was entitled to the laptop, dishonesty is negated. Option C is incorrect — theft does not require permanent deprivation to be proved separately (s.6 deals with intention to permanently deprive); s.6 is broadly interpreted. Option A ignores the dishonesty element; D is wrong.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis submits invoices to Ashworth Developments Ltd for building work he knows was never carried out. He intends to make a gain. Which offence best describes his conduct?

options.A: Obtaining property by deception under s.15 Theft Act 1968.
options.B: Fraud by false representation under s.2 Fraud Act 2006 — making a false representation dishonestly, knowing it is or might be untrue, intending to make a gain or cause a loss.
options.C: Theft under s.1 Theft Act 1968.
options.D: Handling stolen goods under s.22 Theft Act 1968.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 2 Fraud Act 2006 creates the offence of fraud by false representation: the defendant (i) makes a false representation; (ii) dishonestly; (iii) knowing the representation is or might be untrue or misleading; (iv) with intent to make a gain or cause a loss. Submitting invoices for non-existent work is a false representation about the existence of the debt. The offence is complete on making the representation — it is inchoate; no gain needs to result. Option A refers to an abolished offence; C and D do not fit the conduct.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Assault occasioning actual bodily harm — s.47 OAPA 1861
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: During a confrontation at a Wharton Magna building site, James Ashworth punches Alek Kowalski, causing bruising and a cut above his eye requiring two stitches. James is charged with assault occasioning actual bodily harm (ABH) under s.47 OAPA 1861. What must the prosecution prove as to James's mental state?

options.A: James must have intended to cause actual bodily harm.
options.B: James must have intended or been reckless as to causing the assault or battery (the underlying contact) — it is not necessary to prove he intended or foresaw that ABH would result.
options.C: James must have been reckless as to causing grievous bodily harm.
options.D: Strict liability applies to s.47 — no mental element need be proved.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: R v Roberts (1971) and confirmed in R v Savage; R v Parmenter [1992] AC 699: for s.47 ABH, the prosecution need only prove the mental element for the underlying assault or battery (intention or recklessness as to unlawful force/apprehension of force). It is not necessary to prove the defendant intended or foresaw the actual bodily harm — the ABH is the consequence that 'occasions' from the assault. Option A imposes a higher mens rea than the law requires; C refers to GBH; D is wrong — s.47 requires mens rea for the assault.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Grievous bodily harm — s.18 and s.20 OAPA 1861
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth, during the same incident, produces a wrench and strikes Alek Kowalski on the arm, fracturing it. What distinguishes liability under s.18 from s.20 OAPA 1861?

options.A: The distinction lies only in the severity of injury — fractures are always s.20, not s.18.
options.B: Section 18 requires proof of specific intent — the defendant must intend to cause GBH or resist/prevent lawful arrest. Section 20 requires only that the defendant intends or is subjectively reckless as to causing some harm (not necessarily GBH).
options.C: Section 18 is a summary offence; s.20 is triable on indictment only.
options.D: Section 20 requires direct physical contact; s.18 can be committed indirectly.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The critical distinction between ss.18 and 20 OAPA 1861 lies in mens rea. Section 18 (wounding/GBH with intent) requires the defendant to have specifically intended to cause GBH or to resist/prevent lawful arrest — this is a specific intent crime. Section 20 (malicious wounding/GBH) requires only Cunningham recklessness — the defendant must have foreseen that their act might cause some (not necessarily grievous) bodily harm (R v Mowatt [1968]). Both are indictable; the injury type (GBH) may be the same — the distinction is the higher mens rea for s.18.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis enters Ashworth Developments Ltd's site office as a trespasser intending to steal. He finds nothing of value and leaves. Which offence, if any, has he committed?

options.A: No offence — theft requires actual appropriation, and Frank took nothing.
options.B: Burglary under s.9(1)(a) Theft Act 1968 — entering a building as a trespasser with intent to commit one of the specified offences (theft, GBH, unlawful damage), which is complete on entry with intent.
options.C: Burglary under s.9(1)(b) only — this requires him to have actually committed theft once inside.
options.D: Attempted theft only.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 9(1)(a) Theft Act 1968 creates burglary by entering a building (or part of a building) as a trespasser with intent to: steal, inflict GBH, or do unlawful damage. The offence is complete on entry with the requisite intent — no further acts are required. Frank entered as a trespasser (without authority or beyond any permission given) intending to steal. The fact he found nothing does not negate the s.9(1)(a) offence. Section 9(1)(b) is the alternative form requiring commission of specified offences once inside. A and D are incorrect.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Self-defence — s.76 Criminal Justice and Immigration Act 2008
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth is attacked by Frank Hollis. In response, James punches Frank with considerable force, causing fractures. James claims self-defence. Which statement correctly reflects the law?

options.A: A defendant may only use force in self-defence proportionate to the actual threat as assessed objectively at the time.
options.B: Under s.76 CJIA 2008, the force used must not be disproportionate in the circumstances as the defendant believed them to be (subjectively); the defendant is judged on the facts as they genuinely believed, and some latitude is given for decisions made in the heat of the moment.
options.C: Self-defence is only available where the defendant attempts to retreat before using force.
options.D: The defendant must establish self-defence on the balance of probabilities.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 76 CJIA 2008 codifies the common law. The test is: (i) was the force used necessary in the circumstances the defendant genuinely believed to exist? (s.76(3)-(4) — subjective belief, even if mistaken and not reasonably so); (ii) was the force not disproportionate in those circumstances? The standard is whether a person of good character in the same circumstances might have done the same — the jury should not judge too precisely, recognising decisions made in the heat of the moment (Palmer v R [1971]). There is no duty to retreat (though failure to retreat is a factor). The prosecution bears the burden of disproving self-defence. A applies a purely objective test; C imposes a retreat requirement; D reverses the burden.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth, after drinking heavily at a work event, smashes equipment at Hollowell Engineering, causing £12,000 of damage. He claims he did not form any intention due to his intoxicated state. He is charged with criminal damage under s.1(1) Criminal Damage Act 1971. Is his intoxication defence likely to succeed?

options.A: Yes — voluntary intoxication is a complete defence to all criminal charges.
options.B: No — criminal damage under s.1(1) is a basic intent offence; voluntary intoxication cannot negate basic intent (recklessness), following DPP v Majewski [1977].
options.C: Yes — intoxication always negates mens rea regardless of the type of offence.
options.D: No — intoxication is never relevant to criminal proceedings.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: DPP v Majewski [1977] AC 443: voluntary intoxication is a defence to specific intent crimes (where the intent cannot be formed) but not to basic intent crimes. Criminal damage under s.1(1) CDA 1971 is a basic intent offence — recklessness suffices. Because James voluntarily got intoxicated, the recklessness in getting into a state where he might cause damage is treated as satisfying the mens rea for basic intent offences. His intoxication cannot negate that recklessness. Option A is wrong — voluntary intoxication is not a complete defence; C is wrong; D is wrong — it is relevant in limited circumstances.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis drives a getaway car for a robbery. He claims he did so only because members of a criminal gang threatened to kill his family if he refused. Which statement correctly states the law on duress by threats?

options.A: Duress is available for all offences including murder, provided the threat is sufficiently serious.
options.B: Duress is available for offences other than murder, attempted murder, and certain treason offences. The defendant must have reasonably believed there was a threat of death or serious injury, it was directed at the defendant (or close family), there was no evasion reasonably available, and a sober person of reasonable firmness sharing the defendant's characteristics would have responded in the same way.
options.C: Duress is a complete defence to all offences where the defendant subjectively believed the threat, regardless of how a reasonable person would have responded.
options.D: Duress applies only where the threat comes from the co-defendant in the same criminal enterprise.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: R v Hasan [2005] UKHL 22 restated the elements of duress by threats: (i) a reasonable belief in a threat of death or serious physical injury; (ii) the threat must be directed at the defendant or a person for whom they are responsible; (iii) the threat must be immediate and operative; (iv) the sober person of reasonable firmness sharing the defendant's characteristics would have responded as the defendant did; (v) the defendant must not have voluntarily exposed themselves to the risk of compulsion. Duress is not available for murder (R v Howe [1987]) or attempted murder (R v Gotts [1992]). Option C removes the objective element; A is wrong; D limits the source of duress incorrectly.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis pleads guilty to fraud at the Crown Court. The judge considers the appropriate sentence. Which statement correctly identifies the statutory purposes of sentencing under the Sentencing Act 2020?

options.A: The only purpose of sentencing is punishment; rehabilitation is dealt with separately by the probation service.
options.B: Under s.57 Sentencing Act 2020, the purposes of sentencing for adults include: punishment of offenders, reduction of crime (including by deterrence), reform and rehabilitation, protection of the public, and making reparation to victims.
options.C: Sentencing purposes vary by court — magistrates must prioritise rehabilitation; Crown Court must prioritise punishment.
options.D: The sentencing judge must select a single sentencing purpose and apply it exclusively.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 57 Sentencing Act 2020 (re-enacting CJA 2003 s.142) provides that any court dealing with an offender must have regard to the following purposes of sentencing: (a) punishment; (b) reduction of crime (including deterrence); (c) reform and rehabilitation; (d) protection of the public; (e) reparation to victims. The judge balances these purposes, guided by sentencing guidelines. There is no hierarchy or exclusive purpose. Options A, C and D are all incorrect.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth enters a guilty plea at the Plea and Trial Preparation Hearing (PTPH) in the Crown Court. Under the Sentencing Council's guideline on reduction for guilty pleas, what is the maximum reduction in sentence he can expect?

options.A: One-third reduction if the plea is entered at the PTPH.
options.B: One-quarter reduction regardless of when the plea is entered.
options.C: One-half reduction for a plea at any stage before trial.
options.D: No reduction — guilty pleas are simply mitigating factors at large.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The Sentencing Council's Reduction in Sentence for a Guilty Plea guideline (2017, updated) provides that the maximum reduction is one-third where the guilty plea is entered at the first stage of proceedings (magistrates' or PTPH in Crown Court). The reduction reduces to one-quarter at the trial date and one-tenth if entered on the day of trial. The PTPH is the equivalent of the first stage for Crown Court purposes. Option B is wrong on the percentage and the fixed nature; C overstates; D is wrong — there is a structured discount scheme.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis is convicted of fraud and the Crown Court judge concludes that the custody threshold is passed and the appropriate custodial term is 15 months. The judge is considering whether to suspend the sentence. Which conditions must be met?

options.A: A custodial sentence of any length may be suspended provided the judge considers it appropriate.
options.B: Under s.277 Sentencing Act 2020, the court may suspend a custodial sentence of two years or less if it is of the opinion that immediate custody is not required and the suspension is justified; the court must impose requirements as part of a Suspended Sentence Order.
options.C: Only sentences of 12 months or less may be suspended.
options.D: Suspended sentences are only available in the magistrates' court.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Under s.277 Sentencing Act 2020 (formerly CJA 2003 s.189), a court may suspend a custodial sentence of 2 years or less (in the Crown Court; 6 months or less in the magistrates' court, subject to amendments). The court must be of the opinion that immediate custody is not required (the custody threshold having been crossed). A Suspended Sentence Order must include at least one requirement (community requirement). The offender is subject to the requirement(s) during the operational period; breach may activate the custodial sentence. Option A removes the 2-year cap; C states an outdated 12-month limit; D is wrong.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth pleads guilty to theft. The prosecution asserts the offence involved sophisticated planning; James denies this. The difference in factual basis materially affects sentence. What is the correct procedure?

options.A: The judge must accept the defendant's factual basis for sentence as they have pleaded guilty.
options.B: A Newton hearing takes place — the judge hears evidence on the disputed factual basis and makes findings of fact. The judge sentences on those findings; the defendant retains their guilty plea discount.
options.C: The prosecution's version of events always prevails on sentencing where the defendant has entered a guilty plea.
options.D: The judge adjourns for a pre-sentence report and sentences on probation's assessment of the offence.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Where there is a material dispute about the factual basis of a guilty plea that affects the appropriate sentence, a Newton hearing is held (R v Newton (1982)). The judge hears evidence and makes findings on the balance of probabilities. The defendant retains the guilty plea discount (though this may be reduced if the Newton hearing finds against them). The prosecution does not automatically prevail; the judge determines the facts. A is wrong; C is wrong; D is the wrong procedure for this situation.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Drug offences — Misuse of Drugs Act 1971
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: PC Okoro stops and searches Marcus Bridgewater and finds a quantity of cocaine in his pocket. Marcus admits the cocaine is his and says he intended it for personal use. Which offence is he most likely charged with?

options.A: Possession with intent to supply under s.5(3) MDA 1971, as the amount alone justifies the inference.
options.B: Simple possession under s.5(2) MDA 1971, as there is no evidence of supply and the defendant admits personal use.
options.C: Importation under s.3 MDA 1971.
options.D: Producing a controlled drug under s.4(2) MDA 1971.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 5(2) MDA 1971 creates the offence of unlawful possession of a controlled drug. Where the defendant admits personal use and there is no evidence of supply (quantity, packaging, paraphernalia, cash, phones suggesting dealing), a charge of simple possession under s.5(2) is appropriate. The CPS charging standard considers quantity, context and evidence before charging s.5(3) PWITS. A overreads the evidence; C and D do not reflect the facts.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Money laundering — Proceeds of Crime Act 2002
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis receives payment for construction work into his business account. DCI Thorn believes the payments are the proceeds of fraud committed by James Ashworth. Frank claims he did not know or suspect this. Which element of the principal money laundering offence under s.327 POCA 2002 is likely to be most contested?

options.A: Whether Frank 'converted' criminal property — this is a strict liability element.
options.B: Whether Frank knew or suspected that the property was criminal property — the mens rea requirement under s.340 POCA 2002.
options.C: Whether James's fraud is a predicate offence capable of generating 'criminal property'.
options.D: Whether the SRA Code requires Frank to report the transaction.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 327 POCA 2002 prohibits concealing, disguising, converting, transferring or removing criminal property. Section 340 defines criminal property as property constituting a benefit from criminal conduct where the alleged offender knows or suspects this. The critical contested element on these facts is Frank's knowledge or suspicion — did he know or suspect the payments derived from fraud? This is a subjective test. Option A incorrectly applies strict liability; C is less contested (James's fraud generates criminal property); D relates to separate regulatory obligations, not criminal liability.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth is charged with fraud. His solicitor is aware that the police have unused material including a witness statement supporting James's version of events. The prosecution has not disclosed it. What is the prosecution's disclosure obligation?

options.A: The prosecution has no obligation to disclose unused material — only evidence it intends to use at trial must be provided.
options.B: Under the Criminal Procedure and Investigations Act 1996, the prosecution must disclose initial unused material that might reasonably be considered capable of undermining the prosecution case or assisting the accused's case (s.3 CPIA primary disclosure); after the defence statement, further disclosure of material satisfying the same test follows.
options.C: Disclosure is entirely at the discretion of the investigating officer.
options.D: The prosecution must disclose all unused material regardless of whether it assists the defence.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The CPIA 1996 s.3 requires the prosecution to disclose (or give notice that there is nothing to disclose) unused material that might reasonably be considered capable of undermining the prosecution case or of assisting the case for the accused. After the accused serves a defence statement, s.7A requires further disclosure of material satisfying the same test. The AG's Guidelines and the Code of Practice elaborate the disclosure obligation. A is wrong — unused material may include exculpatory evidence; C is wrong; D overstates — not all unused material need be disclosed, only that which meets the test.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth is on trial for fraud. He decides not to give evidence. The judge directs the jury. Which statement is correct regarding the defendant's failure to give evidence?

options.A: The jury must be directed that they may draw no inference whatsoever from his failure to testify.
options.B: Under s.35 CJPOA 1994, the judge must warn the jury that they may draw such inferences as appear proper from the defendant's failure to testify, provided the defendant was made aware that this inference may be drawn; however, the inference alone cannot prove guilt.
options.C: The defendant's failure to testify automatically leads to a directed acquittal.
options.D: Failure to testify is treated as an admission of guilt.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 35 CJPOA 1994: where a defendant does not give evidence, the court or jury may draw such inferences as appear proper. The defendant must first be warned (by the judge under s.35(2)) that the stage has been reached at which they may give evidence and that inferences may be drawn from failure to do so. The inference is a permissible inference, not a mandatory one, and cannot alone prove guilt (s.38(3) CJPOA). Option A reflects the pre-1994 position; C and D are wrong.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Appeals from Crown Court — Court of Appeal
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth is convicted of fraud after a Crown Court trial. He seeks to appeal his conviction. Which ground of appeal is most likely to succeed?

options.A: That the jury returned an unpopular verdict which the public disagrees with.
options.B: That the conviction is unsafe — e.g. there was a material irregularity in the trial, fresh evidence emerges, or the judge misdirected the jury on an important point of law.
options.C: That James did not personally like the judge.
options.D: That the prosecution used witnesses James found credible but did not believe.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 2(1) Criminal Appeal Act 1968: the Court of Appeal shall allow an appeal against conviction if it thinks the conviction is 'unsafe'. Grounds include legal misdirection, material irregularity, fresh evidence (under s.23) that may have affected the outcome, or failure to disclose. The Court of Appeal is not a rehearing of the facts. Options A, C and D do not identify legally recognised grounds. 'Unsafe' is assessed objectively by the Court; the jury's assessment of credibility is generally final.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis and James Ashworth agree to defraud Hollowell Engineering by submitting false invoices. Neither has yet taken any further step. Are they guilty of conspiracy?

options.A: No — conspiracy requires an overt act in furtherance of the agreement.
options.B: Yes — under s.1 Criminal Law Act 1977, conspiracy is complete when two or more persons agree to pursue a course of conduct that, if carried out in accordance with their intentions, will necessarily amount to or involve the commission of a criminal offence.
options.C: No — fraud is not an offence capable of being the subject of a statutory conspiracy.
options.D: Yes, but only if one of them is the ringleader with a previous criminal record.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 1 Criminal Law Act 1977 creates statutory conspiracy: it is complete on agreement, without any further act. The conspiracy is to pursue a course of conduct that will necessarily amount to an offence (here, fraud under the Fraud Act 2006). No overt act is required (unlike US federal conspiracy law). Both parties must intend the agreement to be carried out. Option A incorrectly imports an overt act requirement; C is wrong — fraud is a valid subject matter for conspiracy; D adds a condition not found in s.1.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Sexual offences — consent and s.1 SOA 2003
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: For an offence of rape under s.1 Sexual Offences Act 2003, which statement best describes the element of consent?

options.A: Consent means active verbal agreement; silence implies consent.
options.B: Under s.74 SOA 2003, a person consents if they agree by choice and have the freedom and capacity to make that choice. The defendant commits rape if they did not reasonably believe the complainant consented — assessed in light of all the circumstances including steps taken to ascertain consent.
options.C: Consent is a strict liability element; the defendant's belief in consent is irrelevant.
options.D: Consent can be inferred from the parties' prior sexual relationship.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 74 SOA 2003 defines consent as agreement by choice where the person has the freedom and capacity to make that choice. Section 1(1)(c) provides that the defendant must not reasonably believe the complainant consents — this is an objective test applied in light of all circumstances, including any steps taken (s.1(2)). The evidential (ss.75-76) and conclusive (s.76) presumptions assist. Option A is wrong — active verbal agreement is not required but silence does not imply consent; C is wrong — reasonable belief is the defendant's mens rea; D is wrong — previous relationships do not imply consent.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Defence solicitor — duties to court and client in criminal proceedings
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A solicitor represents James Ashworth at Wharton Crown Court. During a conference, James tells the solicitor that he intends to give false evidence that he was in another country at the time of the alleged fraud. James insists the solicitor continues to represent him. What must the solicitor do?

options.A: Continue to represent James as instructed, putting his fabricated alibi to the jury as directed.
options.B: Advise James strongly not to give false evidence; if James insists, the solicitor must withdraw from the case. The solicitor cannot put forward a case they know to be false (SRA Code para 1.4, 2.4 and the duty not to mislead the court).
options.C: Report James to the police for proposed perjury.
options.D: Continue to represent James but refuse only to question the prosecution witnesses on the false alibi.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: SRA Code of Conduct 2019 paragraph 1.4 prohibits a solicitor from knowingly misleading the court; paragraph 2.4 requires the solicitor not to tamper with evidence or assist a client to do so. A solicitor who knows the client intends to commit perjury cannot put forward the false evidence. The solicitor must first advise the client not to proceed; if the client insists, the solicitor must withdraw. The solicitor cannot simply pick and choose which elements of the false case to advance (D is wrong). Option C — reporting to police — is not required (confidentiality may apply and perjury has not yet been committed), and is not the primary professional response. Option A involves the solicitor in misleading the court.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Dr. Felix Unwin, a GP in Wharton Magna, fails to diagnose a patient's sepsis following a series of clinical errors. The patient dies. The CPS considers charging Dr Unwin with gross negligence manslaughter. Which elements must the prosecution prove?

options.A: That Dr Unwin owed a duty of care, breached it, and the breach caused death — identical to the civil negligence test.
options.B: That Dr Unwin owed a duty of care; breached that duty; the breach caused the death; and the breach was so grossly negligent that it should be judged criminal, assessed by whether the conduct fell so far below the standard of a reasonably competent doctor that it deserves to be categorised as criminal (R v Adomako [1994]).
options.C: That Dr Unwin intended to harm the patient or was reckless as to death.
options.D: That Dr Unwin's conduct amounted to unlawful act manslaughter.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: R v Adomako [1994] AC 212 (HL) established the gross negligence manslaughter test: (i) duty of care (often straightforward in doctor-patient); (ii) breach of that duty; (iii) breach caused death; (iv) the breach was so grossly negligent that, having regard to the risk of death involved, the jury consider it criminal. The final limb requires the jury to evaluate whether the conduct was truly exceptional rather than merely negligent. Intention or Cunningham recklessness are not required (distinguishing from unlawful act manslaughter). Civil negligence (A) does not require the criminal threshold; C and D misidentify the type of manslaughter.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis punches Clive Dring once in the face in a short altercation. Clive, unknown to Frank, has a pre-existing heart condition; the punch triggers a fatal cardiac arrest. Frank is charged with unlawful act manslaughter. Which statement best describes the applicable law?

options.A: Frank can only be convicted if he foresaw or intended Clive's death.
options.B: Under the unlawful act manslaughter principle (Church [1966]; Newbury and Jones [1977]), Frank is guilty if he committed an intentional unlawful act (the punch — assault/battery) which was objectively dangerous (sober reasonable person would recognise risk of some harm), which caused death — regardless of Clive's pre-existing condition (thin skull rule).
options.C: Frank is not liable because Clive's pre-existing condition was an unforeseeable intervening act breaking the chain of causation.
options.D: Frank must have intended to commit the battery and known of Clive's vulnerability.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: R v Church [1966] and R v Newbury and Jones [1977] establish unlawful act manslaughter: (i) an intentional unlawful act (here, battery); (ii) objectively dangerous — sober reasonable person would recognise the act creates risk of some physical harm to another (not necessarily serious); (iii) the act caused death. The defendant need not foresee any harm; the victim's vulnerability (Blaue v R [1975] — thin skull/egg-shell skull) means causation is not broken by the pre-existing condition. Option A requires foresight, which is not required; C incorrectly applies intervening act analysis; D adds a knowledge requirement not in the law.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis grabs Priya Mehta's handbag from her shoulder, pushing her to the ground in the process. He runs off with the bag. Is this robbery or theft?

options.A: Theft only — the force was incidental and not used to overcome resistance.
options.B: Robbery under s.8 Theft Act 1968 — theft with force or the threat of force, on the person, immediately before or at the time of stealing, in order to steal. Pushing her to the ground is force used immediately before or at the time of stealing.
options.C: Robbery requires the use of a weapon; a push is insufficient.
options.D: This is assault with intent to steal, not robbery.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 8 Theft Act 1968: a person is guilty of robbery if they steal and immediately before or at the time of doing so, and in order to do so, they use force on any person or puts or seeks to put any person in fear of being then and there subjected to force. Physical force of any degree suffices (R v Dawson and James [1976] — nudging the victim was sufficient). Pushing Priya constitutes force used at the time of stealing in order to steal. No weapon is required. Option A narrowly mischaracterises the s.8 test; C adds a weapon requirement; D is not a recognised offence category.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Marcus Bridgewater spray-paints graffiti on Wharton Borough Council's wall. The graffiti cannot be removed by washing and requires repainting at a cost of £900. Marcus argues the wall has not been 'damaged'. Is his argument correct?

options.A: Yes — paint can be washed off; the structure of the wall is undamaged.
options.B: No — damage under s.1 CDA 1971 is interpreted broadly; any impairment of value or utility, or any change that requires time and money to remedy, can constitute damage. The need for repainting at cost to the council satisfies the damage element (Morphitis v Salmon [1990]; Roe v Kingerlee [1986]).
options.C: Yes — the cost must exceed £5,000 for s.1 criminal damage to apply.
options.D: No — but the offence requires Marcus to have intended the damage to be permanent.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Roe v Kingerlee [1986] held that mud smeared on a police cell wall constituting damage even though it could be washed off — damage is to be construed widely. Morphitis v Salmon [1990] clarified that damage includes impairment of value or usefulness. Graffiti requiring repainting at cost clearly constitutes damage. There is no minimum financial threshold under s.1(1) (though value is relevant to triability). Permanence is not required — any impairment suffices. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Handling stolen goods — s.22 Theft Act 1968
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis receives a stolen laptop from James Ashworth, knowing it to be stolen, and stores it at his warehouse. Which element of handling stolen goods under s.22 Theft Act 1968 must the prosecution prove as to knowledge?

options.A: The prosecution must prove Frank was certain the laptop was stolen.
options.B: The prosecution must prove Frank knew or believed the goods to be stolen — suspicion alone is insufficient, but knowledge or belief (including wilful blindness) suffices.
options.C: Handling is a strict liability offence — knowledge is irrelevant.
options.D: The prosecution must prove Frank knew the identity of the original thief.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 22 Theft Act 1968 requires that the defendant handles stolen goods 'knowing or believing them to be stolen'. The House of Lords in R v Hall (1985) confirmed that 'belief' requires the defendant to be virtually certain (not merely suspicious) that the goods are stolen, but 'wilful blindness' — deliberately shutting one's mind to the obvious — can equate to knowledge or belief. Suspicion alone is insufficient. Certainty is not the test (A is too high); C is wrong — mens rea is required; D adds an unnecessary element.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Diminished responsibility — s.2 Homicide Act 1957 (as amended)
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth kills Frank Hollis. He raises diminished responsibility as a partial defence to murder. Which elements must he establish under s.2 Homicide Act 1957 as amended by the Coroners and Justice Act 2009?

options.A: That he suffered from any mental disorder recognised by psychiatry and this caused him to act as he did.
options.B: That he suffered from an abnormality of mental functioning arising from a recognised medical condition; that this substantially impaired his ability to understand the nature of his conduct, form a rational judgment, or exercise self-control; and that the abnormality provides an explanation for the killing.
options.C: That he did not know the nature or quality of his act, or did not know it was wrong — the M'Naghten test.
options.D: That he acted under an irresistible impulse caused by a recognised medical condition.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 2 Homicide Act 1957 as amended by the Coroners and Justice Act 2009 requires: (i) an abnormality of mental functioning; (ii) arising from a recognised medical condition; (iii) which substantially impaired the defendant's ability to understand the nature of their conduct, form a rational judgment, or exercise self-control; (iv) the abnormality provides an explanation for the act (i.e. is a significant cause). The defence is for the defendant to prove on the balance of probabilities. If successful, reduces murder to manslaughter. Option A is too broad; C describes the insanity defence; D was part of the old law but the irresistible impulse test was abolished by the 2009 Act amendments.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Loss of control — s.54 Coroners and Justice Act 2009
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Victoria Ashworth kills her husband James Ashworth after a long history of domestic abuse. She raises loss of control as a partial defence. Which conditions must be satisfied under ss.54-55 Coroners and Justice Act 2009?

options.A: Victoria must have been provoked by the deceased's immediate act at the moment of killing.
options.B: Victoria must have lost self-control at the time of the killing; the loss of control must have been attributable to a qualifying trigger (fear of serious violence from the deceased, or circumstances of an extremely grave character giving Victoria a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged); and a person of the defendant's sex and age with a normal degree of tolerance and self-restraint in those circumstances might have reacted similarly.
options.C: The defence is unavailable where the killing was not spontaneous — a planned or considered response to abuse cannot found loss of control.
options.D: The defence requires medical evidence of a recognised mental disorder.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Sections 54-55 CJA 2009 replaced provocation. The requirements are: (i) the defendant lost self-control; (ii) attributable to a qualifying trigger under s.55 — fear of serious violence from V (s.55(3)) or circumstances of an extremely grave character giving the defendant a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged (s.55(4)); (iii) a person of D's sex and age with normal tolerance/self-restraint in the circumstances might have acted similarly. Unlike the old provocation, a time delay does not automatically bar the defence (s.54(2)). Option A imports the old 'immediate act' requirement; C incorrectly reinstates immediacy as a bar; D conflates with diminished responsibility.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Stalking — s.4A Protection from Harassment Act 1997
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Marcus Bridgewater repeatedly follows Diana Pelletier, sends her dozens of unwanted messages each day, monitors her social media, and waits outside her home. Diana is in a state of fear. Which offence best describes this conduct?

options.A: Common assault under s.39 Criminal Justice Act 1988.
options.B: Stalking involving fear of violence or serious alarm/distress under s.4A Protection from Harassment Act 1997, which includes a course of conduct amounting to stalking that causes the victim to fear violence will be used, or causes serious alarm or distress with a substantial adverse effect on daily activities.
options.C: Harassment under s.2 Protection from Harassment Act 1997 only — stalking is not a distinct offence.
options.D: Breach of a community protection notice.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 4A PHA 1997 (inserted by Protection of Freedoms Act 2012) creates the aggravated stalking offence where a course of conduct amounts to stalking (the behaviours listed in s.2A(3) include following, contacting, monitoring online activity, watching/spying) and either causes the victim to fear violence will be used or causes serious alarm/distress with substantial adverse effect on their usual activities. Marcus's conduct falls squarely within s.4A. Option C is wrong — stalking is a distinct offence; A requires apprehension of immediate unlawful force; D is a civil mechanism.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Marcus Bridgewater is convicted of criminal damage (community/low culpability). The Crown Court imposes a community order. Which statement is correct about the requirements that may be attached?

options.A: A community order must always include unpaid work.
options.B: A community order may include one or more requirements from the menu in the Sentencing Act 2020, including unpaid work, rehabilitation activity, programme, prohibited activity, curfew, exclusion, residence, mental health treatment, drug rehabilitation, alcohol treatment, attendance centre, and foreign travel prohibition requirements.
options.C: Community orders are only available in magistrates' courts.
options.D: A community order automatically includes a 12-month supervision period with no other requirements permitted.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The Sentencing Act 2020 (formerly CJA 2003 ss.177-180) provides that a community order must include one or more of the listed requirements. The requirements menu is extensive (ss.200-223 SA 2020) and includes unpaid work (40-300 hours), rehabilitation activity, curfew, exclusion, programme, prohibited activity, etc. The requirements must be appropriate given the seriousness of the offence and the defendant's circumstances. Crown Courts and magistrates' courts can both impose community orders. Option A overstates; C is wrong; D is wrong.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis is convicted of fraud by false representation. The Crown applies for a confiscation order under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002. The court finds that Frank has benefited from criminal conduct and his available assets are less than his benefit figure. What amount is confiscated?

options.A: The full benefit figure, even if Frank does not have sufficient assets to satisfy it.
options.B: The available amount — the lesser of the benefit figure and the defendant's realisable assets at the time of making the order.
options.C: Only assets directly traceable to the specific fraud charged.
options.D: A nominal amount, with the court prioritising rehabilitation.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: POCA 2002 s.7 provides that the confiscation order is the lesser of: (i) the defendant's benefit figure; or (ii) the 'available amount' — the value of the defendant's realisable assets. Where the available amount is less than the benefit, the order is for the available amount. If the defendant later acquires assets, the prosecution may apply to increase the order (s.22). Option A would mean ordering confiscation of assets the defendant does not have; C introduces a direct tracing requirement not in POCA; D is wrong — confiscation is not limited by rehabilitation considerations.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth sends a letter to Gerald Crane demanding £50,000 or he will disclose to the press that Crane & Pelletier has been falsely inflating client bills. If the disclosure is true, can James still be guilty of blackmail?

options.A: No — blackmail requires the threat to disclose something false or illegal.
options.B: Yes — under s.21 Theft Act 1968, a person is guilty of blackmail if they make an unwarranted demand with menaces with a view to gain. The demand is unwarranted unless the person believes (i) they have reasonable grounds to make it and (ii) the use of menaces is a proper means of reinforcing the demand. Even a truthful disclosure threat can constitute menaces; the propriety of using threats to extort money is the key issue.
options.C: No — disclosure of a true fact cannot constitute a menace under s.21.
options.D: Yes, but only if James can show he is owed the £50,000 by Crane & Pelletier.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 21 Theft Act 1968: blackmail is a demand with menaces which is unwarranted. The demand is warranted only if the defendant believes they have reasonable grounds and that the use of menaces is a proper means of reinforcing it. Even a threat to disclose true information can constitute menaces (Thorne v Motor Trade Association [1937]). The threat to expose true facts does not make the demand 'warranted' — demanding money in exchange for silence, even about true facts, is not a proper means of reinforcing any claim. Option A and C incorrectly limit menaces to threats about false facts; D focuses on a separate issue (entitlement) that may affect the 'warranted' analysis but does not determine the outcome on these facts.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: DCI Thorn wishes to hold a video identification procedure (VIPER) in which a witness will attempt to identify Frank Hollis from a line of images. Frank refuses to participate. What are the consequences of his refusal under Code D?

options.A: The police cannot proceed with any identification procedure if the suspect refuses.
options.B: Under Code D paragraph 3.21, the police may proceed with an identification procedure using a covert image of Frank or a still from CCTV; the jury may be told of his refusal and invited to draw such inferences as appear proper.
options.C: Refusal to participate in a VIPER automatically leads to acquittal as the prosecution cannot establish identity.
options.D: The police must obtain a court order compelling Frank to participate.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: PACE Code D provides that where a suspect refuses to participate in a video identification procedure, the police may proceed using available images (covert photograph, CCTV still) and show the witness an identification procedure constructed from those images. The jury may be informed of the refusal and invited to draw adverse inferences. Option A would allow suspects to defeat identification evidence by simply refusing; C is wrong — the case can proceed on other evidence; D is not the procedure under PACE.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth plans to forge a company cheque to defraud Ashworth Developments Ltd. He has printed a forged cheque and is walking to the bank to present it when PC Okoro arrests him. Has James committed an attempt to commit fraud?

options.A: No — James has not yet taken any action at the bank; preparation is not sufficient.
options.B: Yes — under s.1 Criminal Attempts Act 1981, a person is guilty of attempt if they do an act which is more than merely preparatory to the commission of the offence, with intent to commit it. Printing the forged cheque and setting out for the bank to present it is arguably more than merely preparatory.
options.C: Yes — any intent to commit fraud, however early the stage, is sufficient for attempt.
options.D: No — attempt to commit fraud by false representation is not a recognised offence.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 1 Criminal Attempts Act 1981: the defendant must (i) intend to commit the full offence; (ii) do an act more than merely preparatory to it. The 'more than merely preparatory' test is a question of fact for the jury. R v Geddes [1996] and R v Jones [1990] illustrate the boundary. Printing the forged instrument and physically travelling to the bank to present it takes the conduct beyond mere preparation — it is an act in the execution of the offence. Option A overly restricts attempt to acts at the very point of commission; C extends attempt too broadly; D is wrong — fraud by false representation can be attempted.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Public order — s.4 and s.5 Public Order Act 1986
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: During a heated confrontation at a Wharton Magna market, Marcus Bridgewater shouts threatening words at Alek Kowalski, causing Alek to fear immediate unlawful violence. Which offence does this most precisely describe?

options.A: Section 5 Public Order Act 1986 — using threatening or abusive words within the hearing of a person likely to be caused harassment, alarm or distress.
options.B: Section 4 Public Order Act 1986 — using threatening, abusive or insulting words towards another with intent to cause them to believe immediate unlawful violence will be used, or to provoke immediate unlawful violence.
options.C: Section 4A Public Order Act 1986 — causing intentional harassment, alarm or distress.
options.D: Common assault under s.39 CJA 1988.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 4 POA 1986 requires: (i) use of threatening, abusive or insulting words/behaviour (or distribution of written material); (ii) either with intent to cause the other to believe that immediate unlawful violence will be used against them or another, or to provoke immediate unlawful violence. On these facts, Alek fears immediate unlawful violence — this fits s.4. Section 5 (A) covers less serious conduct not causing fear of violence; s.4A (C) requires intentional harassment/alarm/distress; D requires actual apprehension of immediate physical contact.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis accesses Crane & Pelletier's computer system without authorisation to obtain client fee information. Which offence does this engage under the Computer Misuse Act 1990?

options.A: No offence under the CMA 1990 — accessing information held on another's computer is only unlawful if data is deleted.
options.B: Section 1 CMA 1990 — unauthorised access to computer material: the defendant knowingly causes a computer to perform a function to secure access to a program or data, without authorisation and knowing they are not authorised.
options.C: Section 3 CMA 1990 — unauthorised modification of computer material.
options.D: The Computer Misuse Act 1990 does not apply to accessing a firm's computer network.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 1 CMA 1990 creates the basic offence of unauthorised access: (i) the defendant causes a computer to perform a function; (ii) to secure access to any program or data; (iii) access is unauthorised; (iv) the defendant knows at the time they are not authorised. Frank accessing Crane & Pelletier's system without permission satisfies all elements. No modification or deletion is needed. Option A is wrong; C applies to unauthorised modification; D is wrong — the CMA applies to computer networks.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Sending to Crown Court — s.51 Crime and Disorder Act 1998
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth is charged with fraud on indictment (indictable-only offence). He appears at Wharton Magistrates' Court. What must the magistrates do?

options.A: The magistrates must determine allocation and, if appropriate, try the case summarily.
options.B: Under s.51 Crime and Disorder Act 1998, the magistrates' court must send the case immediately to the Crown Court for trial — there is no allocation hearing for indictable-only offences.
options.C: The magistrates must hear a committal proceeding and the defendant may make a submission of no case to answer.
options.D: The magistrates must accept jurisdiction and sentence if the defendant pleads guilty.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 51 CDA 1998 (as amended) requires that where an adult is charged with an indictable-only offence, the magistrates' court must send them to the Crown Court immediately for trial (and any related either-way or summary offences). There is no allocation hearing or committal proceedings for indictable-only offences. Committal proceedings were abolished for adults by the Crime and Disorder Act 1998. Option A describes the allocation procedure for either-way offences; C describes the abolished committal procedure; D is wrong — magistrates have no jurisdiction to sentence for indictable-only offences.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth's trial for fraud has been delayed for four years due to prosecution failures. He applies for a stay of proceedings as an abuse of process. On what basis may the court grant a stay?

options.A: The court may stay proceedings only where the prosecution has acted in bad faith.
options.B: The court may stay proceedings as an abuse of process where: (i) it would be impossible to give the defendant a fair trial (e.g. due to irretrievable prejudice from delay); or (ii) it would be contrary to the public interest in the integrity of the criminal justice system to try the defendant in the particular circumstances (Beckford [1996]; Attorney General's Reference No 1 of 1990 [1992]).
options.C: A stay of proceedings is only available at the Court of Appeal.
options.D: The four-year delay automatically entitles the defendant to a stay.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The court's power to stay proceedings as an abuse of process derives from its inherent jurisdiction (R v Horseferry Road Magistrates' Court ex p Bennett [1994]). The two established categories are: (i) where a fair trial is impossible — e.g. irretrievable prejudice to the defendant from delay; or (ii) where it would be an affront to public conscience to try the defendant — e.g. entrapment or executive wrongdoing. Delay alone (without prejudice to a fair trial) does not automatically justify a stay (Attorney General's Reference No 1 of 1990 [1992]). Options A and D mistate the test; C is wrong on the forum.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Evidence against Frank Hollis was obtained during a search that DCI Thorn accepts was conducted without the required inspector's authorisation under s.18 PACE. Frank's solicitor applies to exclude the evidence under s.78 PACE 1984. What must the court consider?

options.A: Evidence obtained in breach of PACE is automatically excluded.
options.B: Under s.78 PACE 1984, the court may exclude prosecution evidence if, having regard to all the circumstances including the circumstances in which the evidence was obtained, its admission would have such an adverse effect on the fairness of the proceedings that the court ought not to admit it. The court exercises discretion.
options.C: Section 78 only applies to confession evidence.
options.D: The court must exclude evidence only if the breach was intentional.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 78 PACE 1984 gives the court a discretionary power to exclude prosecution evidence where its admission would have such an adverse effect on the fairness of the proceedings that it ought not to be admitted. The court considers: whether there has been a breach of PACE or Codes of Practice, the seriousness of the breach, whether it was deliberate or negligent, and the overall impact on fairness. Automatic exclusion does not follow from a breach (R v Khan [1997]). Option A imposes automatic exclusion; C limits s.78 to confessions (s.76 covers confessions; s.78 is broader); D requires intentionality, which is not the test.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: PC Okoro takes a victim personal statement from Priya Mehta following the robbery by Frank Hollis. What is the status of a victim personal statement in sentencing?

options.A: Victim personal statements are binding on the sentencing judge — the victim's recommended sentence must be followed.
options.B: A victim personal statement allows the victim to explain in their own words the impact of the offence on them. The sentencing court takes it into account as part of the circumstances of the offence; however, the judge is not bound by any sentencing recommendation in it.
options.C: Victim personal statements are inadmissible at sentencing — only prosecution counsel may address the court on impact.
options.D: A victim personal statement may only be presented if the defendant consents.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Victim personal statements are part of the Victims' Code and criminal procedure (Criminal Practice Direction V). They allow the victim to describe the impact of the offence. The sentencing judge considers the statement as part of the overall picture but is not bound by any sentencing recommendation contained in it — sentencing remains the exclusive function of the court. They may be read aloud or presented in writing. Option A wrongly binds the judge; C is wrong — victim personal statements are expressly used at sentencing; D requires no consent from the defendant.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A Crown Prosecution Service advocate is prosecuting Frank Hollis for fraud. During the trial, new evidence emerges strongly suggesting Frank is innocent. What is the advocate's primary obligation?

options.A: The advocate must continue the prosecution as instructed regardless of the new evidence.
options.B: A prosecution advocate's primary duty is to the court and to justice, not to secure a conviction. On receiving material evidence that the defendant may be innocent, the advocate must refer the matter to the CPS who may discontinue proceedings; the advocate cannot suppress the evidence.
options.C: The advocate must immediately disclose the evidence to the defence and then make a closing speech as normal.
options.D: The advocate must seek the judge's direction in private before deciding what to do.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The prosecution advocate owes a primary duty to the court and to justice (R v Banks [1916]; Bar Standards Board Code rC5; SRA Code for solicitors). The role is to present the case fairly, not to obtain a conviction at all costs. Where new evidence significantly undermines the prosecution case or suggests the defendant's innocence, the advocate must bring this to the CPS's attention and, if necessary, notify the court. Suppression would be professionally improper and potentially a contempt. Option A ignores the duty to the court; C is partial; D is not the appropriate response.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth, acting as an agent for Ashworth Developments Ltd, receives a payment from a subcontractor to ensure they are awarded contracts. Which offence under the Bribery Act 2010 is most relevant to the company?

options.A: The company is automatically immune because James acted in his personal capacity.
options.B: Section 7 Bribery Act 2010 — failure of a commercial organisation to prevent bribery. The company commits this offence if a person associated with it bribes another intending to obtain or retain business for the company, unless the company can prove it had adequate procedures to prevent bribery.
options.C: Only James can be prosecuted — companies have no criminal liability under the Bribery Act 2010.
options.D: Section 2 Bribery Act 2010 — offences relating to being bribed, applicable to companies.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 7 Bribery Act 2010 creates a strict liability corporate offence: where a person associated with a commercial organisation bribes another person intending to obtain or retain business or a business advantage for the organisation, the organisation commits an offence. The only defence is that the organisation had adequate procedures in place to prevent bribery (s.7(2)). James as an associated person (agent) bribing the subcontractor for the company's benefit engages s.7. Section 2 applies to the passive bribery offence (being bribed) for natural persons; C is wrong — corporate liability is the point of s.7; A is wrong.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth is charged with a complex fraud involving multiple defendants and extensive documentary evidence. The Crown Court judge orders a preparatory hearing under the Criminal Procedure and Investigations Act 1996 Part III. What is the primary purpose of such a hearing?

options.A: To enter a guilty plea in advance of the trial date.
options.B: To identify the key issues in the case in advance, to assist the jury's understanding of complex issues, and to facilitate the management of the trial — the judge may give binding rulings on questions of law.
options.C: To replace the PTPH and bail hearings in all fraud cases.
options.D: To allow the prosecution to test the defence's evidence before trial.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: A preparatory hearing under CPIA 1996 Part III (or the Criminal Justice Act 1987 for serious fraud) is used in complex, lengthy or serious fraud cases. Its purposes (s.29 CPIA 1996) include: identifying issues likely to be material; assisting the jury's comprehension of those issues; expediting the proceedings; facilitating the management of the trial. The judge may give binding rulings on questions of law. These are the same in principle as preparatory hearings under s.8 CJA 1987. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis is sentenced for three separate offences of fraud, handling and blackmail arising from different incidents. The judge imposes consecutive sentences for each. What principle must the judge apply when imposing multiple consecutive sentences?

options.A: The sentences must be run consecutively in full — the total is irrelevant.
options.B: The totality principle requires the judge to stand back and ensure the overall sentence is just and proportionate to the overall offending — if the total is disproportionate, individual sentences should be adjusted so the total reflects the overall criminality.
options.C: Multiple sentences must always run concurrently to avoid double punishment.
options.D: Consecutive sentences require the approval of the Lord Chief Justice.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The Sentencing Council's Totality Guideline (2012) requires a sentencing court imposing multiple sentences (consecutive or concurrent) to review the overall total to ensure it is just and proportionate to the overall offending. If the arithmetic total of consecutive sentences is disproportionate, the judge adjusts individual sentences. The guideline also addresses when sentences should be concurrent (same/related incident) versus consecutive (separate incidents). Option A ignores the totality principle; C incorrectly mandates concurrent sentences; D has no legal basis.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: After being charged with fraud, James Ashworth contacts prosecution witnesses and persuades them to change their evidence. Which offence does this describe?

options.A: Contempt of court only.
options.B: Perverting the course of justice — the common law offence of doing an act (or a series of acts) that has a tendency to pervert and is intended to pervert the course of public justice.
options.C: Perjury under s.1 Perjury Act 1911.
options.D: Witness intimidation under s.51 Criminal Justice and Public Order Act 1994 only.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Perverting the course of justice is a common law offence: the defendant does an act or series of acts which tends to pervert the course of justice, with intent to do so. Interfering with prosecution witnesses by persuading them to change evidence clearly falls within this offence. It may overlap with witness intimidation under s.51 CJPOA 1994 and potentially contempt, but the most precise charge for deliberately corrupting witness evidence is perverting the course of justice. Perjury (C) would apply to the witness making false statements on oath, not to James persuading them.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Priya Mehta is a witness in the robbery trial of Frank Hollis. She is frightened of giving evidence in open court in Frank's presence. Which measure can the court apply under the Youth Justice and Criminal Evidence Act 1999?

options.A: The court cannot protect adult witnesses in criminal trials.
options.B: Under the YJCEA 1999, the court may grant special measures for eligible vulnerable or intimidated witnesses, including: screens to shield the witness from the defendant, evidence by live link, evidence given in private, removal of wigs and gowns, and video-recorded evidence-in-chief.
options.C: Special measures are only available for witnesses under 18.
options.D: Priya must apply to the High Court for special measures.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The YJCEA 1999 ss.23-30 provide a menu of special measures for eligible witnesses — either 'vulnerable' (under 18, or affected by mental/physical disorder) or 'intimidated' (quality of evidence likely to be diminished by fear or distress about testifying — which includes fear of the defendant). Priya qualifies as an intimidated witness. Available measures include live link, screens, evidence in private, video-recorded examination-in-chief, and others. The application is made to the trial court; no High Court application is needed. Options A and C are wrong.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis makes a confession at Wharton Police Station after DC Griffiths tells him that his friend James will be charged and his family will lose their home unless Frank confesses quickly. Frank later argues the confession should be excluded. On what ground under s.76 PACE 1984 would his argument be strongest?

options.A: The confession should be excluded under s.76(2)(a) because it was obtained by oppression.
options.B: The confession should be excluded under s.76(2)(b) because it was obtained in consequence of things said or done which were likely, in the consequences existing at the time, to render any confession unreliable.
options.C: The confession is admissible because Frank made it voluntarily.
options.D: The confession can only be challenged after conviction on appeal.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 76(2) PACE 1984 requires exclusion of a confession where it was obtained: (a) by oppression (torture, inhuman treatment, or the exercise of power in a way that saps free will — s.76(8)); or (b) in consequence of anything said or done which was likely in the circumstances to render unreliable any confession. DC Griffiths's false threat about James being charged and the family's home is not oppression in the s.76(8) sense (no physical mistreatment or improper pressure at that level), but it is 'something said' likely to render the confession unreliable — Frank confessed to protect others, not because the confession is true. Option A (oppression) applies to more egregious conduct; C ignores the s.76 grounds; D is wrong — challenges to confessions are made at the voir dire before or during trial.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: The Crown Prosecution Service applies to the Crown Court for a restraint order against James Ashworth under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 to prevent him dealing with his assets before a confiscation order is made. When may a restraint order be made?

options.A: Only after a confiscation order has been made.
options.B: A restraint order may be made under POCA 2002 s.41 at any point if a criminal investigation has been started, a prosecution has been commenced, or an application for a confiscation order has been made — provided there is reasonable cause to believe the defendant has benefited from criminal conduct.
options.C: Only after conviction but before sentencing.
options.D: Restraint orders are made by the High Court, not the Crown Court.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: POCA 2002 s.40 provides that a restraint order may be made where a criminal investigation has been started (s.40(1)(a)), a prosecution has been commenced (s.40(1)(b)), or a confiscation order application has been made (s.40(1)(c)). The court must be satisfied there is reasonable cause to believe the defendant has benefited from criminal conduct. Restraint orders are available pre-charge, pre-trial and post-conviction. The Crown Court has jurisdiction (s.58 POCA). Options A, C and D are wrong.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Frank Hollis installs malware on Crane & Pelletier's server that deletes client files without authorisation. Which provision of the Computer Misuse Act 1990 is engaged?

options.A: Section 1 CMA 1990 — unauthorised access.
options.B: Section 3 CMA 1990 — unauthorised acts with intent to impair the operation of a computer or programs or data, or to prevent/hinder access to programs or data.
options.C: Section 3ZA CMA 1990 — unauthorised acts causing or creating risk of serious damage.
options.D: Section 2 CMA 1990 — unauthorised access with intent to commit a further offence.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Section 3 CMA 1990 covers unauthorised modification of computer material: any act that causes an unauthorised modification of computer contents with intent to impair operation of any computer, to prevent access, or to impair data or programs. Deleting files is an unauthorised modification of data. Section 1 applies to mere access; s.2 applies to access with intent to commit a further offence; s.3ZA (added by Serious Crime Act 2015) applies to acts causing or creating risk of serious damage to essential infrastructure — not the primary charge for this conduct.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Ancillary orders — Sexual Harm Prevention Order
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Marcus Bridgewater is convicted of a sexual offence and the prosecution applies for a Sexual Harm Prevention Order (SHPO). Under what legislation and on what basis may an SHPO be made?

options.A: Under the Sexual Offences Act 2003 — an SHPO may be imposed on conviction of a listed offence where necessary to protect the public from sexual harm.
options.B: Under the Protection of Freedoms Act 2012 — the court may impose an SHPO as a licensing condition.
options.C: Under the Criminal Justice Act 2003 — an SHPO is a sentence in its own right.
options.D: An SHPO requires a separate civil application by the chief constable.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: Sexual Harm Prevention Orders are made under the Sexual Offences Act 2003 ss.103A-103K (as amended by the Anti-social Behaviour, Crime and Policing Act 2014). On conviction of a listed sexual offence, the sentencing court (or later, a magistrates' court on complaint) may make an SHPO if it is necessary to protect the public from sexual harm by the defendant. The order imposes prohibitions on the defendant and has a minimum duration of 5 years. Options B, C and D misidentify the statutory basis.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law and Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.subtopic: Defence solicitor — disclosure of client information
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: During a conference, James Ashworth tells his solicitor that he has hidden a large sum of the alleged proceeds of fraud in an overseas account. The solicitor believes this may constitute money laundering. What are the solicitor's obligations?

options.A: The solicitor must immediately disclose all information to the National Crime Agency.
options.B: The solicitor must consider whether the information is subject to legal professional privilege (LPP). If it is (communications for the purpose of giving legal advice in current criminal proceedings are privileged), the solicitor cannot disclose without consent. If an LPP exception applies (information given to facilitate a crime/fraud — the crime/fraud exception), the solicitor should make a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) to the NCA under POCA 2002 s.330, but must not 'tip off' the client (s.333A POCA).
options.C: The solicitor must disclose the information to the prosecution as part of their duty to the court.
options.D: The solicitor has no money laundering obligations when acting in criminal proceedings.

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The position involves the intersection of LPP, the regulated sector money laundering obligations and the POCA 2002 s.330 failure to disclose offence (regulated sector). Communications made in the context of giving legal advice in criminal defence proceedings are generally privileged (Three Rivers DC v Bank of England [2004]). The crime/fraud exception (Cox and Railton [1884]) removes LPP where the communication was made to facilitate a crime. If the information falls outside LPP (e.g. because disclosing a pre-existing asset position rather than seeking advice), the solicitor in the regulated sector must submit a SAR and cannot tip off the client (s.333A POCA). Option A imposes an unqualified obligation; C misapplies the duty to the court; D is wrong — solicitors in the regulated sector have AML obligations.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under LP(MP)A 1989 s.2, what is the essential requirement for a valid contract for sale of land?

options.A: The contract must be in writing and signed by both parties, incorporating all material terms.
options.B: The contract must be in writing and signed by the seller only.
options.C: The contract must be in writing but need not identify all material terms if agreed orally.
options.D: The contract must be signed by both parties but can be in electronic format only.
