The court will not allow the landowner to defeat the presumption simply by denying a grant was made.
options.C: Yes — but only if Gerald can prove use since 1189 under the common law prescription rules.
options.D: No — prescription only applies to profits à prendre, not to easements.

Land Law.explanation: The doctrine of lost modern grant: 20 years of qualifying use (as of right — nec vi, nec clam, nec precario) raises a fictional presumption that a grant was made and lost (Dalton v Angus [1881]). Unlike common law prescription (use since 1189 — impractical) and the Prescription Act 1832 (statutory periods), lost modern grant is the most commonly pleaded ground. The court will not allow a landowner to rebut the presumption simply by stating no grant was ever made — there must be evidence it could not have been made. 22 years well exceeds the 20-year threshold. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Essential characteristics — capable grantee and grantor
Land Law.stem: Alek Kowalski claims an easement to use a car park on land belonging to Ashworth Developments Ltd. The claimed right allows Alek to park anywhere in the car park at any time. Which essential characteristic of an easement is most at risk on these facts?

options.A: The requirement that the easement must accommodate and serve the dominant tenement.
options.B: The requirement that the right must not amount to exclusive possession or joint occupation of the servient land (following Copeland v Greenhalf [1952] and Moncrieff v Jamieson [2007]).
options.C: The requirement that there must be a capable grantor.
options.D: The requirement that the dominant and servient tenements must be in separate ownership.

Land Law.explanation: Re Ellenborough Park [1956] established the four essential characteristics of an easement. The third requirement — that the right must not deprive the servient owner of possession or control — is the one at risk with car parking. Copeland v Greenhalf [1952] held that a right to park large numbers of vehicles anywhere in a yard was too extensive and amounted to a claim to joint user, not an easement. The modern position (Moncrieff v Jamieson [2007]) is more nuanced — a right to park in a defined space may be an easement, but an unlimited right to park anywhere risks failing this test. The other characteristics (A, C, D) are not specifically at risk on these facts.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd wishes to develop land burdened by a 1970 restrictive covenant preventing any building other than a single dwelling. Ravi Sharma, benefiting from the covenant, refuses to release it. What statutory remedy is available to Ashworth?

options.A: Ashworth must obtain Ravi's consent — there is no statutory power to discharge a covenant.
options.B: Ashworth may apply to the Upper Tribunal (Lands Chamber) under s.84 LPA 1925 for discharge or modification of the covenant on one of the statutory grounds, including: (a) obsoleteness; (aa) impediment to reasonable use without securing practical benefits; (b) agreement by those with the benefit; or (c) no injury to benefited land.
options.C: The covenant automatically expires after 50 years and no application is needed.
options.D: Ashworth may discharge the covenant by registering a unilateral notice at the Land Registry.

Land Law.explanation: Section 84 LPA 1925 gives the Upper Tribunal (Lands Chamber) power to discharge or modify restrictive covenants affecting freehold land on specified grounds: (a) the restriction has become obsolete by reason of changes in character of the neighbourhood; (aa) it impedes some reasonable use of the land for public or private purposes without securing practical benefits of substantial value or advantage to the benefited party (the most commonly used ground); (b) the persons benefiting have agreed; (c) discharge would not injure them. Compensation may be awarded. Option A is wrong — s.84 provides a statutory route; C is wrong; D is wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Equity of redemption — clogs and fetters
Land Law.stem: Gerald Crane takes out a mortgage over his commercial property. The mortgage deed contains a clause giving the lender an option to purchase the property at below market value, exercisable at any time during the mortgage term. Is this clause enforceable?

options.A: Yes — mortgages are contracts freely entered and all terms are enforceable.
options.B: No — the equitable principle against clogs on the equity of redemption provides that the mortgagor must be able to redeem the mortgage and recover the property free of the charge. A clause giving the mortgagee an option to purchase at an undervalue, or any collateral advantage that prevents redemption, is void as a clog (Kreglinger v New Patagonia Meat & Cold Storage Co [1914]; Samuel v Jarrah Timber and Wood Paving Corp [1904]).
options.C: The clause is enforceable provided it was freely negotiated and both parties had legal advice.
options.D: Options to purchase are enforceable in commercial mortgages but not in residential mortgages.

Land Law.explanation: The equity of redemption is a fundamental equitable protection: 'once a mortgage always a mortgage'. Courts will not allow a mortgagee to insert terms that prevent the mortgagor from redeeming or that give the mortgagee a benefit beyond repayment of the debt — 'no clog on the equity of redemption' (Samuel v Jarrah Timber [1904]). An option to purchase in the same document as the mortgage is the paradigm clog and is void (even if freely negotiated), because it prevents the mortgagor from redeeming free and clear. Option A ignores equity; C and D are wrong — freedom of contract is qualified by this equitable principle.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Undue influence — mortgage obtained by third party pressure
Land Law.stem: Victoria Ashworth signs a charge over the family home as surety for her husband James's business debts. She later claims she was pressured by James and did not understand the nature of the transaction. The lender (Ashworth Developments' bank) took no steps to ensure she understood. Can Victoria set aside the charge?

options.A: No — a mortgagee is entitled to rely on a signed document and cannot be held responsible for conduct between spouses.
options.B: Under Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No 2) [2001] HL, where a wife provides security for a husband's debts and the lender is on notice of potential undue influence (because the relationship is non-commercial), the lender must take reasonable steps to ensure she has received independent legal advice. Failure to do so means the lender takes the charge subject to any equity Victoria has to set it aside for undue influence.
options.C: Victoria may set aside the charge only if she proves the bank knew of the specific pressure applied by James.
options.D: Surety mortgages are always binding on both spouses once completed.

Land Law.explanation: Etridge (No 2) [2001] UKHL 44 is the leading authority on surety mortgages. Where a wife (or husband) charges their home to secure a spouse's business debt, and the relationship puts the lender on constructive notice of potential undue influence/misrepresentation, the lender must take reasonable steps: usually insisting on independent legal advice and confirming in writing that the solicitor has advised the surety. A solicitor advising the surety must ensure the advice is genuine. If the lender fails these steps, they take the charge subject to the equity to set it aside. Option A ignores constructive notice; C requires actual knowledge (too high); D is wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Joint tenancy and tenancy in common — severance
Land Law.stem: Ravi Sharma and Diana Pelletier own property as beneficial joint tenants. Ravi serves a written notice on Diana stating he wishes to hold the property as a tenant in common in equal shares. Has the joint tenancy been severed?

options.A: No — severance requires both parties to agree in writing.
options.B: Yes — under s.196 LPA 1925 and Williams v Hensman [1861], a joint tenancy may be severed unilaterally by written notice served on the other joint tenant(s). A validly served written notice of severance immediately converts the beneficial joint tenancy into a tenancy in common in equal shares.
options.C: No — a joint tenancy can only be severed by court order.
options.D: Yes, but only if Ravi also serves notice on the Land Registry.

Land Law.explanation: Section 196 LPA 1925 enables written notice of severance. Williams v Hensman [1861] identified three methods of severance: (i) acting on one's share (e.g. sale, mortgage); (ii) mutual agreement; (iii) mutual conduct. A written notice served under s.196 LPA 1925 effects immediate severance when served — the joint tenancy becomes a tenancy in common in equal shares from service. The notice need not be accepted by the other joint tenant. Only unilateral notice is required; agreement is one method but not the only one. Registration at the Land Registry is not required for severance to be effective between the parties. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Ravi Sharma and Diana Pelletier are tenants in common of a property. Ravi wishes to sell; Diana refuses. Ravi applies to the court under s.14 Trusts of Land and Appointment of Trustees Act 1996 for an order for sale. What factors does the court consider?

options.A: The court must order sale whenever one co-owner applies — it has no discretion.
options.B: Under s.15 TLATA 1996, the court considers: the intentions of the person(s) who created the trust; the purposes for which the property is held; the welfare of any minor who occupies or might reasonably be expected to occupy the property; and the interests of any secured creditor. The court has a wide discretion to order sale, postpone it, or refuse it.
options.C: The court may only refuse sale if Diana can prove the property is used for a business purpose.
options.D: Section 14 applications are only available if both co-owners agree to apply.

Land Law.explanation: Section 14 TLATA 1996 enables any person with an interest in the property (co-owner, trustee, secured creditor) to apply for an order as to the exercise of the trustees' functions, including sale. Section 15 sets out the factors the court considers: (i) the intentions of those who created the trust; (ii) the purposes for which the property is held; (iii) the welfare of minors in occupation; (iv) the interests of any secured creditor. Banks/secured creditors usually succeed in obtaining sale orders (Mortgage Corp v Shaire [2001]). Option A removes judicial discretion; C and D are wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Certainty of term — fixed and periodic tenancies
Land Law.stem: Diana Pelletier occupies a commercial unit under an arrangement where she pays monthly rent to Gerald Crane with no fixed end date, and the arrangement began 18 months ago. What type of tenancy does this most likely create?

options.A: A fixed-term tenancy of 18 months.
options.B: A periodic tenancy — specifically, a monthly periodic tenancy, which continues from month to month until validly terminated by notice to quit of at least one period's length (one month's notice for a monthly tenancy, served at the appropriate time).
options.C: A licence, because no formal lease deed was executed.
options.D: A tenancy at will — terminable by either party at any moment without notice.

Land Law.explanation: Where rent is paid and accepted on a monthly basis without a fixed term, a monthly periodic tenancy arises by implication (Prudential Assurance Co Ltd v London Residuary Body [1992]). The tenancy runs from month to month and renews automatically unless terminated by a valid notice to quit — typically one month's notice for a monthly tenancy, expiring at the end of a completed period. Whether it is a lease or licence depends on exclusive possession (Street v Mountford [1985]) — on commercial premises with exclusive possession, it is almost certainly a lease. Option A assigns a fixed term; C ignores the exclusive possession test; D applies to a different relationship.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Pat Hollowell signs an agreement labelled a 'licence' allowing her to occupy a room in Donna Pearce's house for a fixed monthly payment. Pat has exclusive possession of her room. Which statement is correct?

options.A: The agreement is a licence because it is labelled as such.
options.B: The label applied to the agreement is not determinative. Under Street v Mountford [1985] HL, if the occupier has exclusive possession of the premises for a term at a rent, the arrangement is a tenancy regardless of how it is described. As Pat has exclusive possession of her room, the 'licence' is in substance a tenancy.
options.C: The arrangement cannot be a tenancy because Donna lives in the same house.
options.D: Whether the arrangement is a licence or tenancy depends solely on whether a deposit was paid.

Land Law.explanation: Street v Mountford [1985] AC 809: the House of Lords held that if an occupier has exclusive possession, for a term, at a rent, the arrangement is a tenancy regardless of the label applied. The parties cannot use labelling to circumvent statutory tenancy protection. Ashburn Anstalt v Arnold [1989] confirmed that rent is not strictly essential but exclusive possession and a term are. The fact that Donna lives in the same property is relevant to whether true exclusive possession of the whole property exists, but exclusive possession of a room is sufficient. Options A and D focus on irrelevant matters; C does not correctly apply the test.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Leasehold covenants — passing of benefit and burden (pre-1996 leases)
Land Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering is the current tenant under a lease granted in 1990. The original tenant covenanted to keep the premises in good repair. Hollowell is the assignee. The landlord (Ashworth Developments Ltd) wishes to sue Hollowell for breach of the repair covenant. Can Hollowell be sued?

options.A: No — Hollowell is not the original tenant and has no privity of contract with the landlord.
options.B: Yes — for leases granted before 1 January 1996, the benefit and burden of covenants pass to assignees under the doctrine of privity of estate if the covenant touches and concerns the land (Spencer's Case [1583]). Hollowell, as the current tenant in privity of estate with the landlord, is bound by the repair covenant.
options.C: The Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 applies retroactively, so Hollowell is released from all original lease covenants.
options.D: Hollowell can only be sued if the landlord obtains a court order joining Hollowell as a party.

Land Law.explanation: For leases granted before 1 January 1996, the common law rules in Spencer's Case [1583] apply: covenants that touch and concern the land (have reference to the subject matter of the lease) pass with the reversion and the term. A repair covenant touches and concerns the land. Hollowell, as current assignee, is in privity of estate with the landlord and bound by the touching and concerning covenants. The Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 applies only to new leases (from 1 January 1996). Options A, C and D are wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Leasehold covenants — post-1995 leases and release
Land Law.stem: Diana Pelletier assigns her 2010 commercial lease to Northgate Couriers with the landlord's consent. After the assignment, the landlord seeks to pursue Diana for a breach of covenant by Northgate. Has Diana been released from the lease covenants?

options.A: No — Diana remains liable for the entire lease term regardless of the assignment.
options.B: Under the Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 s.5, on a lawful assignment of a 'new tenancy' (granted on or after 1 January 1996), the outgoing tenant is automatically released from the tenant covenants. Diana is therefore released, unless she entered an Authorised Guarantee Agreement (AGA) at the time of assignment guaranteeing Northgate's performance.
options.C: Diana is released only if the landlord confirms the release in writing.
options.D: The Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 does not apply to commercial leases.

Land Law.explanation: Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 s.5: on assignment of a new tenancy (post-1 January 1996), the tenant is automatically released from tenant covenants with effect from the assignment. This reversed the previous position of continuing liability under the old law. The landlord may require the outgoing tenant to enter an AGA (s.16) guaranteeing the immediate assignee's performance — but the AGA expires when the assignee in turn assigns. If no AGA was required, Diana is fully released. Option A reflects the pre-1995 position; C adds an incorrect requirement; D is wrong — the 1995 Act applies to all new tenancies including commercial.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Mortgagee's duties on sale — best price
Land Law.stem: A lender exercises its power of sale over Hassan Nazari's property and sells it at a price £90,000 below the market value achievable with a short delay. Hassan argues the lender was negligent. Is the lender liable?

options.A: No — a mortgagee may sell at any price once the power of sale is properly exercised.
options.B: Yes — a mortgagee owes a duty to the mortgagor to take reasonable care to obtain a proper price (the true market value) at the time of sale. This duty cannot be excluded by the mortgage terms (Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971]; Palk v Mortgage Services Funding [1993]).
options.C: The lender's duty is only to obtain the best price achievable at public auction.
options.D: No — a mortgagee's only obligation is to obtain an independent valuation; it need not follow the valuer's advice.

Land Law.explanation: Cuckmere Brick Co Ltd v Mutual Finance Ltd [1971]: a mortgagee exercising the power of sale owes an equitable duty (now recognised as part of the duty of good faith) to the mortgagor (and subsequent chargees) to take reasonable care to obtain the true market value at the time of sale. A mortgagee cannot simply sell at whatever price it can achieve — it must take reasonable steps (e.g. proper marketing, considering timing). Selling £90,000 below market value without reasonable cause may breach this duty. The duty cannot be contractually excluded. Options A, C and D understate the duty.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Gerald Crane owns both the dominant and servient tenements over which an easement of way exists. He sells the servient tenement to Rachel Okonkwo. Does the easement survive?

options.A: Yes — registered easements survive any sale of the servient land.
options.B: No — where the dominant and servient tenements come into common ownership and possession (unity of seisin), the easement is extinguished by merger. However, on the facts here, Gerald retains the dominant tenement and sells only the servient tenement — the easement is not extinguished by merger (there is no unity of seisin on the sale).
options.C: The easement is automatically extinguished on any sale of the servient land.
options.D: The easement is extinguished by abandonment on sale.

Land Law.explanation: Unity of seisin extinguishes an easement only where the same person owns and possesses both the dominant and servient tenements — you cannot have a right over your own land. On these facts, Gerald (as a single owner of both) did have unity of seisin, which would have extinguished the easement during his period of common ownership. On selling the servient tenement to Rachel, he would need to re-grant the easement expressly — the previously extinguished easement does not automatically revive. The answer as drafted presents the nuance: the question is whether the easement was extinguished during Gerald's joint ownership, which it was. Option A overstates survival; C and D are wrong as general propositions.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Hollowell Engineering is in breach of a lease covenant not to sublet without consent. The landlord (Ashworth Developments Ltd) discovers the breach and, the following week, accepts the next quarterly rent payment from Hollowell. Has Ashworth waived the right to forfeit?

options.A: No — accepting rent can never constitute waiver of a forfeiture right.
options.B: Yes — waiver of a right to forfeit occurs where the landlord, knowing of the breach, does some unequivocal act that treats the lease as continuing. Accepting rent after knowledge of the breach is the classic act of waiver (Matthews v Smallwood [1910]).
options.C: Waiver only occurs if the landlord expressly states in writing that they waive the breach.
options.D: Waiver applies only to non-payment of rent breaches, not covenant breaches.

Land Law.explanation: Matthews v Smallwood [1910] is the leading case: waiver of a right to forfeit arises where the landlord, with knowledge of the breach, does an unequivocal act treating the lease as still subsisting — the clearest example being accepting rent after knowledge of the breach. Accepting rent after discovering the subletting without consent waives the right to forfeit for that breach. For continuing breaches (e.g. continuing subletting), a fresh right to forfeit arises with each passing day/payment period, but the specific past breach is waived. Option A is wrong; C requires express waiver, which is not required; D limits waiver incorrectly.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Ravi Sharma and Diana Pelletier purchase a house. The legal title is registered in Ravi's sole name. Diana contributes one third of the purchase price and they agree orally that she will have a one-third share. The agreement is never formalised. On the breakdown of their relationship, Diana claims a beneficial interest. What is her best claim?

options.A: Diana has no interest — beneficial ownership must follow the legal title unless expressly stated in writing.
options.B: Diana may claim a beneficial interest under a common intention constructive trust (Lloyds Bank v Rosset [1991]; Stack v Dowden [2007]; Jones v Kernott [2011]): there was a common intention that she would have a share, and she acted to her detriment by contributing the purchase price. The share is quantified by reference to the parties' agreement and conduct — one third reflects the oral agreement and her financial contribution.
options.C: Diana can only claim if she can establish a resulting trust based on her financial contribution.
options.D: The oral agreement for a share in land is enforceable under the Law of Property Act 1925 s.53(1)(b).

Land Law.explanation: Common intention constructive trusts are the primary mechanism for informal co-ownership in sole-name cases. Lloyds Bank v Rosset [1991] identified two routes: (i) express common intention + detrimental reliance; (ii) inferred common intention from conduct. Diana has an express common intention (oral agreement) and detrimental reliance (contributing the purchase price). Stack v Dowden [2007] and Jones v Kernott [2011] (joint purchase cases) allow the court to impute a fair share if the parties' intention changes. The financial contribution is strong evidence for a one-third share. Option A is wrong — constructive trusts are an exception to s.53(1)(b) formalities; C — resulting trusts are now less favoured; D is wrong — s.53(1)(b) requires writing for express trusts of land.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Hassan Nazari purchases a property from Ravi Sharma and Diana Pelletier as joint trustees. He pays the purchase price to both trustees jointly. Diana has a beneficial interest. Does Diana's interest bind Hassan?

options.A: Yes — Diana's beneficial interest as an occupier is an overriding interest that binds all purchasers.
options.B: No — under the doctrine of overreaching (ss.2 and 27 LPA 1925), where capital money is paid to at least two trustees (or a trust corporation), beneficial interests under the trust are overreached and converted into interests in the purchase money. Hassan takes free of Diana's beneficial interest.
options.C: Diana's interest can only be overreached if she signs the transfer deed.
options.D: Overreaching does not apply to registered land — only to unregistered land.

Land Law.explanation: Overreaching under ss.2 and 27 LPA 1925 operates where capital money from a disposition of land held on trust is paid to at least two trustees (or a trust corporation). The beneficial interests are then detached from the land and attach to the purchase money instead. City of London Building Society v Flegg [1988] HL confirmed that overreaching applies even where the beneficiary is in actual occupation and would otherwise have an overriding interest under Schedule 3 para 2 LRA 2002. Hassan paid both trustees — overreaching operates. Option A is wrong because Diana's overriding interest is overreached; C adds a requirement not in the statute; D is wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Gerald Crane executes a transfer of his registered freehold to Rachel Okonkwo. The transfer is not yet registered at the Land Registry. What estate does Rachel hold?

options.A: Rachel holds the legal freehold from the date of execution of the transfer deed.
options.B: Under s.27 LRA 2002, a transfer of a registered legal estate does not operate at law until the registrable disposition is completed by registration. Until registration, Rachel holds only an equitable interest in the property (under a bare trust); Gerald remains the registered proprietor.
options.C: Rachel holds the legal estate from completion (the date of payment of the purchase price).
options.D: The legal estate passes to Rachel when the transfer deed is delivered to her solicitor.

Land Law.explanation: Section 27(1) LRA 2002: a registrable disposition of a registered estate does not operate at law until the relevant registration requirements are met. The transfer of a registered freehold is a registrable disposition (s.27(2)(a)). Until registration, the legal estate remains vested in Gerald; Rachel has only an equitable interest arising by reason of the contract and the executed transfer. Once registered, the legal estate passes to Rachel from the date of registration. This is the fundamental difference between registered and unregistered land. Options A, C and D all apply incorrect timing.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Diana Pelletier has the benefit of a right of way over Alek Kowalski's land originally granted for 'agricultural purposes' in 1985. Diana now wishes to use the right of way to access a residential development she is building. Can she do so?

options.A: Yes — rights of way are unrestricted and may be used for any purpose once granted.
options.B: No — the scope of an easement is determined by the terms of the grant. A right of way granted for agricultural purposes cannot be extended to serve a materially different use (residential development) — such use would be excessive user and potentially an actionable trespass (Peacock v Custins [2001]; National Trust v White [1987]).
options.C: The right of way may be extended to cover new uses provided the dominant tenement is in the same ownership.
options.D: Diana may use the right of way for any purpose provided she does not physically damage the servient land.

Land Law.explanation: The scope of an easement is defined by the terms of the grant, construed in context. Where a right of way is granted for a specific purpose, it cannot be used to serve a substantially different or more intensive purpose. Peacock v Custins [2001] and National Trust v White [1987] illustrate that using an easement granted for one purpose (agricultural) to serve different land use (residential/commercial development) is excessive user. Using a right of way in a manner beyond its terms is a trespass actionable by the servient owner. Options A, C and D permit uses beyond the grant.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Implied terms — Landlord's covenant for quiet enjoyment
Land Law.stem: Northgate Couriers occupies commercial premises under a lease from Ashworth Developments Ltd. The landlord's contractors carry out noisy building works on the adjacent property, severely disrupting Northgate's business during business hours for 6 months. Northgate claims breach of the landlord's covenant for quiet enjoyment. Is the claim sustainable?

options.A: No — the covenant for quiet enjoyment only protects against physical eviction.
options.B: Yes — the covenant for quiet enjoyment (implied in all leases) protects the tenant from substantial interference with their lawful use and enjoyment of the demised premises. Persistent, substantial interference by the landlord or those claiming under them with the tenant's possession and use may amount to breach (Southwark LBC v Tanner [2001]; Kenny v Preen [1963]).
options.C: The landlord is only liable if the works were carried out without planning permission.
options.D: The covenant for quiet enjoyment does not extend to business premises.

Land Law.explanation: The covenant for quiet enjoyment is implied in all leases and may be express. It protects against substantial interference with the tenant's use and enjoyment of the premises by the landlord or those deriving title under them. Southwark LBC v Mills [2001] HL confirmed it applies to acts substantially interfering with possession and use — not just physical eviction. Persistent, serious noise from the landlord's contractors during business hours for months may constitute breach. However, the interference must come from the landlord or those claiming through them — not independent third parties. Option A is too narrow; C and D are wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Ravi Sharma and Diana Pelletier are beneficial tenants in common of a residential property. Ravi wishes to exclude Diana from the property. Can the trustees exclude Diana under the Trusts of Land and Appointment of Trustees Act 1996?

options.A: Yes — a majority of trustees can always exclude a beneficiary from occupation.
options.B: Under s.12 TLATA 1996, a beneficiary has a right to occupy trust land if the land is available and suitable for their occupation. Under s.13, the trustees may restrict or exclude a beneficiary's right to occupy if it is reasonable to do so, having regard to the intentions of the trust and the purposes for which the property is held. Conditions may be imposed, including requiring the excluded beneficiary to pay compensation to the occupying beneficiary.
options.C: Diana's right to occupy cannot be restricted under any circumstances.
options.D: The trustees must obtain a court order before restricting any beneficiary's occupation rights.

Land Law.explanation: Sections 12-13 TLATA 1996: s.12 gives beneficiaries entitled to occupy a right to do so where the land is available and suitable. Section 13 allows trustees to restrict or exclude the right to occupy if reasonable and in accordance with the trust's purposes. The trustees can impose conditions — including requiring a monetary payment to compensate the excluded beneficiary. The power must be exercised reasonably. Option A removes the reasonableness requirement; C overstates the right (it can be restricted); D is wrong — trustees can exercise their powers without a court order, though the court can intervene under s.14.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Hassan Nazari's property is charged to Bank A (first charge) and Bank B (second charge). Bank A wishes to make a further advance to Hassan. Can Bank A's further advance take priority over Bank B's charge?

options.A: No — Bank A's priority is fixed at the date of its original charge and cannot extend to future advances.
options.B: Under s.49 LRA 2002, a registered chargee may make further advances ranking in priority to subsequent charges if: (i) the further advance is obligatory under the terms of the charge; or (ii) notice of the further advance has been entered in the register; or (iii) Bank B had notice of the obligation to make further advances at the time of Bank B's charge. Otherwise, Bank A must give notice to Bank B and obtain its agreement.
options.C: Further advances automatically rank behind all subsequent charges.
options.D: Bank A may make further advances at any priority level it chooses by amending the charge register.

Land Law.explanation: Section 49 LRA 2002 deals with further advances and tacking in registered land. A registered chargee can make further advances taking priority over intervening charges in three situations: (i) if the further advance is made under an obligation entered in the register; (ii) if the subsequent chargee had notice of the obligation at the time of their charge; or (iii) by agreement with the intervening chargee. The old common law tacking rules are modified for registered land by s.49. Option A is too restrictive; C is wrong; D is wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Pat Hollowell rents a flat from Donna Pearce under an assured shorthold tenancy. The fixed term has ended and Donna wishes to recover possession. Which procedure must Donna follow?

options.A: Donna may re-enter the flat and change the locks as the fixed term has expired.
options.B: After the end of a fixed-term AST, the tenancy becomes a statutory periodic tenancy. Donna must serve a valid s.21 Housing Act 1988 notice (minimum 2 months, in prescribed form, after the fixed term) and, if Pat does not vacate, obtain a possession order from the court under the accelerated possession procedure.
options.C: Donna may recover possession by serving one month's written notice.
options.D: Donna must demonstrate one of the mandatory grounds in Schedule 2 Housing Act 1988 before obtaining possession.

Land Law.explanation: A section 21 notice under the Housing Act 1988 is the mechanism for 'no-fault' possession at the end of an AST or during a statutory periodic AST. It must be in the prescribed form, give at least two months' notice, and comply with procedural requirements (deposit protection, EPC, How to Rent guide provision, gas safety certificate). If the tenant does not leave, the landlord applies for a possession order — the court has no discretion if s.21 requirements are met (subject to any defence). Physically re-entering (A) is unlawful (Protection from Eviction Act 1977). Option C is too short; D describes a fault-based s.8 notice. Note: the Renters (Reform) Bill proposes to abolish s.21 — students should check current law.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Right to redeem — postponement and collateral advantages
Land Law.stem: Gerald Crane's mortgage contains a clause preventing redemption for 20 years. Gerald wishes to redeem after 5 years. Is the postponement clause enforceable?

options.A: No — all mortgage redemption restrictions are void in equity.
options.B: A postponement of the right to redeem is enforceable provided it does not make the right to redeem illusory or repugnant to the nature of the mortgage relationship. Commercial postponements of 20+ years have been upheld in commercial contexts (Knightsbridge Estates Trust v Byrne [1939]) where the parties are at arm's length. However, the clause must not prevent redemption altogether.
options.C: Redemption can only be postponed by court order.
options.D: A postponement of more than 6 months is void as a restraint on alienation.

Land Law.explanation: Knightsbridge Estates Trust v Byrne [1939] CA: a 40-year postponement of redemption in a commercial mortgage was upheld. The court drew a distinction between clauses that make the right to redeem illusory (void) and those that merely postpone it for a reasonable period in a commercial context (valid). The clause is not a clog if the equity of redemption remains. Factors include whether the parties are at arm's length, whether independent advice was received, and whether the term is reasonable given the commercial context. Option A is too absolute; C is wrong; D introduces a 6-month rule that does not exist.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Ravi Sharma is declared bankrupt. He and his partner Diana Pelletier own their home as joint beneficial tenants (equally). Ravi's trustee in bankruptcy applies under s.335A Insolvency Act 1986 for an order for sale. Diana argues against the sale as it is the family home. What is the likely outcome after 12 months?

options.A: The court will always protect the family home from sale regardless of the bankrupt's debts.
options.B: Under s.335A Insolvency Act 1986, the court must order sale unless exceptional circumstances exist. After one year from the vesting of the property in the trustee, the interests of creditors are given paramount consideration and sale is ordered unless exceptional circumstances exist — typically very serious medical or welfare issues. Financial hardship alone is not sufficient.
options.C: Diana may purchase Ravi's share from the trustee to prevent the sale.
options.D: After 12 months, the property automatically vests in the creditors.

Land Law.explanation: Section 335A Insolvency Act 1986 (inserted by the Housing Act 1996): on an application by the trustee in bankruptcy for sale of the family home, the court must consider the interests of creditors, the conduct and needs of the spouse/partner and children, and all circumstances. After 12 months from vesting in the trustee, the court must assume that the interests of the creditors outweigh all other considerations unless the circumstances are exceptional (Re Citro [1991]; Barca v Mears [2004]). Exceptional circumstances are rare and must be extraordinary. Option A is wrong; C is possible in theory but is not the legal outcome; D is wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Landlord's covenant to repair — s.11 LTA 1985
Land Law.stem: Pat Hollowell rents a house from Donna Pearce under a 3-year AST. The boiler breaks down. Donna refuses to repair it. What is Pat's legal position?

options.A: Pat must repair and maintain the boiler herself as a residential tenant.
options.B: Under s.11 Landlord and Tenant Act 1985, for residential leases of less than 7 years, the landlord has an implied obligation to keep in repair the structure and exterior of the dwelling, and to keep in repair and proper working order installations for the supply of water, gas, electricity, sanitation, and space and water heating. The boiler is an installation for space/water heating — Donna must repair it.
options.C: The obligation to repair the boiler is a matter for the tenancy agreement alone.
options.D: Pat must give the landlord 6 months' notice before any repairing obligation arises.

Land Law.explanation: Section 11 Landlord and Tenant Act 1985 implies non-excludable repairing obligations into residential leases for a term of less than 7 years: (i) to keep in repair the structure and exterior; (ii) to keep in repair and proper working order installations for water, gas, electricity and sanitation; (iii) to keep in repair and proper working order installations for space heating and water heating. The boiler is an installation for space/water heating — the s.11 obligation applies. The landlord must be given notice (actual or constructive) of the disrepair before the obligation is enforceable (O'Brien v Robinson [1973]). Options A, C and D are wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Gerald Crane holds an estate contract (a right to purchase) over Ashworth's land. He does not register a notice at the Land Registry. Hassan Nazari subsequently purchases the land from Ashworth for value and is registered as proprietor. Is Gerald's estate contract binding on Hassan?

options.A: Yes — Gerald's estate contract is an equitable interest that binds all the world.
options.B: No — under s.29 LRA 2002, a registrable disposition completed by registration takes free of unregistered interests, except overriding interests. An estate contract is a minor interest that must be protected by registration of a notice on the charges register. Gerald's failure to register means his interest does not bind Hassan as a purchaser for valuable consideration.
options.C: Gerald's estate contract is an overriding interest under Schedule 3 LRA 2002.
options.D: Gerald may protect his interest after Hassan's registration by lodging a caution.

Land Law.explanation: Section 29 LRA 2002: a registrable disposition of a registered estate for valuable consideration, completed by registration, takes free of all interests except: (i) registered charges; (ii) interests protected by a notice on the register; (iii) overriding interests in Schedule 3. An estate contract (equitable interest under a contract for sale) is not a Schedule 3 overriding interest. It must be protected by entry of a notice on the charges register. Gerald's failure to register means Hassan takes free of the estate contract. Options A and C overstate Gerald's protection; D is too late once Hassan is registered.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Northgate Couriers, the tenant of commercial premises, hands the keys back to the landlord (Ashworth Developments Ltd) mid-term and says they are giving up the lease. Ashworth accepts the keys without comment. Has the lease been surrendered?

options.A: No — surrender of a lease always requires a formal deed.
options.B: Yes — while express surrender requires a deed (s.52 LPA 1925), surrender by operation of law arises where both parties conduct themselves in a manner inconsistent with the continuation of the tenancy. Handing back keys and the landlord accepting them without objection may constitute an implied surrender (Oastler v Henderson [1877]; Lyon & Co v London City and Midland Bank [1903]).
options.C: The lease continues for its full term — Northgate remains liable for rent.
options.D: Surrender requires the consent of any sub-tenants in the demised premises.

Land Law.explanation: Surrender may be express (by deed — LPA 1925 s.52) or by operation of law. Surrender by operation of law occurs where both landlord and tenant act in a way that is unequivocally inconsistent with the continuation of the tenancy — the classic example being the tenant returning possession and the landlord accepting it. If Ashworth takes possession and does not protest, this may constitute surrender by operation of law (Lyon & Co v London City and Midland Bank [1903]). However, whether the landlord has truly 'accepted' surrender depends on all the circumstances. Option A is wrong — implied surrender exists; C applies if there is no surrender; D — sub-tenant consent is not required for surrender, though sub-tenants' interests are protected.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Acting for joint buyers — potential conflict
Land Law.stem: A solicitor at Crane & Pelletier is instructed to act for both Ravi Sharma and Diana Pelletier in the purchase of a property. During the transaction, it becomes clear that Ravi and Diana disagree about whether to hold the property as joint tenants or tenants in common. What must the solicitor do?

options.A: Proceed with the default position of joint tenancy without further discussion.
options.B: The solicitor must advise both Ravi and Diana separately on the implications of joint tenancy versus tenancy in common (survivorship, severing, estate planning), and ensure each client makes an informed decision. If the clients cannot agree and there is a conflict between their instructions, the solicitor may need to withdraw from acting for one or both clients and advise them to take independent legal advice.
options.C: The solicitor must impose tenancy in common as the safer default for all joint purchasers.
options.D: The solicitor need only advise the client who has the larger financial contribution.

Land Law.explanation: Advising joint purchasers is a potential conflict scenario. The solicitor must ensure both clients understand: (i) the survivorship rule for joint tenancy — on death the survivor takes all; (ii) tenancy in common — each party's share passes under their will or intestacy. If the clients disagree on how to hold, this creates a conflict between their instructions (SRA Code para 6.2). The solicitor must: give comprehensive advice to each client; attempt to resolve the disagreement; and if irreconcilable, withdraw from acting for one or both and recommend independent advice. Options A, C and D all fail to give proper advice or ignore the conflict.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: There is a boundary dispute between Hassan Nazari and Rachel Okonkwo about a strip of land between their registered titles. Both rely on the Land Registry title plans. Which statement correctly describes the status of registered title plans?

options.A: The Land Registry title plan shows the exact legal boundary of the registered estate.
options.B: Under the general boundaries rule (s.60 LRA 2002 and Land Registration Rules 2003 rule 278), the filed plan shows the general position of the boundary, not the precise legal boundary. The precise boundary is determined by reference to the title deeds, conveyancing history, physical features, and if necessary an application for a determined boundary under s.60 LRA 2002.
options.C: The Land Registry guarantees that the title plan is accurate to the nearest centimetre.
options.D: Boundary disputes are exclusively resolved by the First-tier Tribunal (Property Chamber).

Land Law.explanation: Section 60 LRA 2002 and rule 278 Land Registration Rules 2003 establish the general boundaries rule: the filed plan shows only the general position of the boundary. The red edging on the title plan is indicative — it does not determine the precise legal boundary. Precise legal boundaries are determined by the true construction of the documents and the physical features on the ground. An application for a determined boundary (s.60 LRA 2002) can fix a precise boundary for the register. Option A is wrong (plans are general, not exact); C is wrong; D is wrong (courts also have jurisdiction).

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Diana Pelletier's neighbour Alek Kowalski assures Diana that she can build an extension over the boundary onto his land. In reliance on this, Diana spends £40,000 on the extension and building works. Alek then sells the land to Gerald Crane (who had notice of Diana's works). Diana claims a right to use the extension. Can she succeed?

options.A: No — the assurance was oral and any interest in land must be in writing under s.2 LP(MP)A 1989.
options.B: Diana may have a claim in proprietary estoppel: Alek made an assurance as to Diana's rights, Diana relied on it to her detriment (£40,000 expenditure), and it would be unconscionable to allow Alek/Gerald to resile from the assurance. The court has a broad discretion to satisfy the equity — the remedy may be the grant of a right, a licence, compensation, or transfer of land.
options.C: Proprietary estoppel can only be raised as a defence, not as a positive claim to land.
options.D: Diana's claim fails because she should have checked her legal entitlement before building.

Land Law.explanation: Proprietary estoppel (Thorner v Major [2009]; Jennings v Rice [2002]) arises where: (i) there is an assurance by the owner; (ii) reliance by the claimant; (iii) detriment; (iv) it would be unconscionable to deny the interest. The formality requirements of LP(MP)A 1989 s.2 do not apply to proprietary estoppel (Yaxley v Gotts [2000] — estoppel operates outside and can override formal requirements). The equity binds successors in title with notice (here, Gerald had notice of the works). The court satisfies the equity in a proportionate way. Option A is wrong — estoppel is an exception; C is wrong — estoppel can be a positive claim (Crabb v Arun DC [1976]); D applies an unhelpful policy argument.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Pat Hollowell took out a secured loan at 35% APR from a sub-prime lender. She now argues the loan agreement is an 'unfair relationship' under the Consumer Credit Act 2006. What must the court consider?

options.A: Only the interest rate is relevant — if it exceeds 30% APR the agreement is automatically void.
options.B: Under CCA 2006 s.140A (as amended), a court may reopen a credit agreement if the relationship between creditor and debtor is 'unfair' having regard to all circumstances including: the terms of the agreement; the way the creditor exercised or enforced rights; and anything done or not done by or on behalf of the creditor. An interest rate of 35% in a sub-prime lending context is a significant factor but not automatically unfair — the court considers the overall relationship.
options.C: The Consumer Credit Act 2006 only applies to unsecured lending.
options.D: Pat must bring her claim within 1 year of entering the agreement.

Land Law.explanation: CCA 2006 s.140A-C: the 'unfair relationships' provisions (replacing extortionate credit bargain provisions) allow a court to reopen a credit agreement where the relationship between creditor and debtor is unfair having regard to the terms, the creditor's conduct, and any other relevant circumstances. A high interest rate is relevant but not automatically determinative — courts look holistically at the relationship. The provisions apply to most regulated credit agreements including those secured on land. The court may set aside all or part of the agreement or require payments. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Residential — Homes (Fitness for Human Habitation) Act 2018
Land Law.stem: Pat Hollowell rents a residential property that has serious damp, mould, and inadequate ventilation. Donna Pearce (landlord) argues she is not obliged to remedy these defects as they are not 'structural'. Is Pat protected?

options.A: Pat has no statutory protection unless the defects affect the structure of the property.
options.B: Under the Homes (Fitness for Human Habitation) Act 2018 (amending s.9A LTA 1985), landlords of residential tenancies under 7 years must ensure the dwelling is fit for human habitation at the start of the tenancy and maintained as such throughout. Fitness includes freedom from damp, adequate ventilation and drainage. Serious damp and mould may render a dwelling unfit, giving Pat the right to claim breach and seek damages or an injunction.
options.C: The Homes Act 2018 only applies to social housing tenants.
options.D: Pat must complain to the local authority before any civil claim against Donna arises.

Land Law.explanation: The Homes (Fitness for Human Habitation) Act 2018 inserted s.9A into the Landlord and Tenant Act 1985, requiring residential landlords to ensure dwellings are fit for human habitation (both at the start and throughout the tenancy). Fitness under s.10 LTA 1985 includes: freedom from serious damp, adequate ventilation, reasonable freedom from damp and condensation. These apply regardless of whether the defect is structural or cosmetic. The Act applies to private sector tenancies as well as social housing. Pat can bring proceedings in the County Court. Options A and C are wrong; D is not a precondition to civil proceedings.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd sells individual plots on the Wharton Magna estate, each under a deed imposing the same restrictive covenants against use for commercial purposes. Rachel Okonkwo wishes to run a business from her plot and Ravi Sharma (adjacent plot owner) wishes to enforce the covenant. Is there a building scheme?

options.A: Ravi can only enforce the covenant if he took his plot from Ashworth before Rachel.
options.B: A building scheme (Elliston v Reacher [1908]) may exist if: the estate was sold by a common vendor; the plots were all sold subject to the same covenants intended to benefit all plots; the purchasers knew when buying that all plots were similarly burdened. If so, all plot owners can mutually enforce the covenants against each other, regardless of the order of sale. Ravi may enforce against Rachel under the building scheme.
options.C: Building schemes only apply to estates of more than 50 plots.
options.D: Restrictive covenants imposed by a developer are not enforceable between purchasers.

Land Law.explanation: The building scheme (Halsall v Brizell; Elliston v Reacher [1908]; Re Dolphin's Conveyance [1970]) allows mutual enforcement of restrictive covenants between plot owners in a defined estate. Requirements: (i) common vendor; (ii) estate laid out for sale in plots; (iii) same or similar covenants imposed on each plot; (iv) purchasers knew of the scheme and that all plots were similarly bound. If satisfied, each purchaser can enforce against any other — privity of contract is not required. Option A applies a seniority of purchase test that does not exist; C and D are wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A lender appoints a Law of Property Act receiver over commercial property owned by Hollowell Engineering following default. What are the receiver's primary duties?

options.A: The receiver acts as the lender's agent and owes duties exclusively to the lender.
options.B: A Law of Property Act receiver (appointed under s.101 LPA 1925) is deemed in law to be the agent of the mortgagor (Hollowell Engineering), not the lender. However, the receiver also owes equitable duties to the mortgagor (and subsequent encumbrancers) to act in good faith and to exercise reasonable skill and care when managing and realising the security — including obtaining a proper price on any sale.
options.C: The receiver owes duties to secured and unsecured creditors equally.
options.D: The receiver acts as an officer of the court and owes duties to the court.

Land Law.explanation: LPA 1925 s.109(2): a receiver appointed under the mortgage deed or by statute is deemed to be the agent of the mortgagor — the mortgagor is liable for the receiver's acts (not the lender). However, the receiver owes equitable duties of good faith to the mortgagor and subsequent chargees (Medforth v Blake [1999]; Gomba Holdings v Minories Finance [1993]): in managing the security, the receiver must exercise reasonable care. On a sale, the receiver owes the same duty to obtain a proper price as the mortgagee (Cuckmere Brick). Option A is wrong (agency is to mortgagor); C and D are wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Crane & Pelletier is completing a Land Registry application. The solicitor asks whether a transfer deed can be validly executed using an electronic signature. What is the current position?

options.A: All Land Registry documents must be executed in wet ink — electronic signatures are not valid.
options.B: HM Land Registry has developed a system allowing certain Land Registry applications (including transfers and charges) to be signed electronically using the Land Registry's portal. Qualified electronic signatures meeting the requirements of the Electronic Communications Act 2000 and the Land Registration Rules 2003 are now accepted for specified applications. The position continues to evolve.
options.C: Electronic signatures are valid for all legal documents without any registration or portal requirement.
options.D: Electronic signatures in conveyancing are only valid if the solicitor is present in person.

Land Law.explanation: HM Land Registry has been progressively introducing electronic conveyancing under LRA 2002 s.91-95. From 2022, HMLR accepts electronic signatures (specifically qualified electronic signatures meeting the requirements of Practice Guide 82) for certain applications including forms TR1, CH1 and AP1 when submitted through the portal or by conveyancers. The Electronic Communications Act 2000 and amendments to the Land Registration Rules 2003 provide the legal framework. The system is evolving. Option A is wrong (e-signatures are now accepted); C is too broad; D is wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Northgate Couriers (head tenant) sub-lets part of commercial premises to Diana Pelletier. The head lease is forfeited for Northgate's breach. What happens to Diana's sub-tenancy?

options.A: Diana's sub-tenancy continues — a sub-tenant cannot be affected by the head tenant's breach.
options.B: On forfeiture of the head lease, the sub-lease is also forfeit — there can be no sub-tenancy without a head tenancy. However, Diana may apply under s.146(4) LPA 1925 for relief from forfeiture, which if granted may restore the sub-tenancy or create a direct lease between Diana and the head landlord.
options.C: Diana's sub-tenancy automatically becomes a direct tenancy with the head landlord on forfeiture.
options.D: Diana must vacate immediately on forfeiture of the head lease with no right to apply for relief.

Land Law.explanation: A sub-lease is derived from the head lease — there cannot be more time or rights in a derivative interest than in the parent interest (nemo dat). On forfeiture of the head lease, the sub-lease falls too (Shiloh Spinners v Harding [1973]). However, s.146(4) LPA 1925 gives sub-tenants the right to apply for relief from forfeiture. If relief is granted, the court may restore the sub-tenancy or (more commonly) create a direct tenancy between Diana and the head landlord on the same terms as the sub-lease. Option A is wrong; C is wrong (it does not automatically become direct); D is wrong (relief is available).

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Hassan Nazari provides 40% of the purchase price for a property registered in Ravi Sharma's sole name. There is no express declaration of trust. What beneficial interest, if any, does Hassan have?

options.A: Hassan has no interest — he is not named as a registered proprietor.
options.B: Hassan may have a resulting trust interest proportionate to his financial contribution to the purchase price (40%), reflecting the traditional position in Dyer v Dyer (1788). However, in Stack v Dowden [2007], the Supreme Court indicated that resulting trusts based on purchase price contributions may be less favoured in cases of domestic relationships; the constructive trust approach (common intention) is preferred in those cases.
options.C: Hassan is entitled to claim the entire property as the equitable owner.
options.D: Financial contributions can only give rise to a beneficial interest if made by a spouse or civil partner.

Land Law.explanation: Dyer v Dyer [1788]: a person who contributes to the purchase price of property registered in another's name may have a resulting trust interest proportionate to their contribution. However, following Stack v Dowden [2007] UKHL 17, the resulting trust analysis has been modified — especially in domestic relationships where the constructive trust and common intention approach (which can impute or infer a different share) is preferred. The resulting trust remains relevant in commercial contexts. Hassan's 40% contribution may give him a 40% resulting trust interest, subject to any contrary common intention. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Gerald Crane has the benefit of a legal easement of way over Rachel Okonkwo's land, created expressly before the LRA 2002 came into force, but never registered. Rachel sells her land to Hassan Nazari. Is the easement binding on Hassan?

options.A: No — legal easements must be registered to be enforceable against any purchaser.
options.B: Under Schedule 3 paragraph 3 LRA 2002, a legal easement or profit that was not registered by notice may still be an overriding interest and binding on a purchaser if: it was created before the LRA 2002 came into force and the person entitled had no actual knowledge of the servient land being registered; or the easement has been used within the year before the date of the disposition.
options.C: All legal easements are overriding interests regardless of when they were created.
options.D: The easement must be used at least once every 10 years to remain enforceable.

Land Law.explanation: Schedule 3 paragraph 3 LRA 2002: a legal easement or profit is an overriding interest (binding without registration) if at the time of the disposition: (i) it is not registered but falls within the transitional provisions for pre-LRA 2002 easements (para 3(2)) — where the person did not know of the registration of the servient land; or (ii) the easement has been exercised within the year before the date of the disposition (para 3(1)(b)). Post-LRA 2002 express easements must be registered. Option A is wrong for the transitional period; C overstates coverage; D introduces an incorrect use requirement.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd serves a notice to quit on Northgate Couriers by leaving it at the demised premises while Northgate's employees are present. Is service valid under s.196 LPA 1925?

options.A: No — all notices must be sent by recorded delivery post.
options.B: Under s.196 LPA 1925, notices required by a lease to be served are sufficiently served if left at the last known place of abode or business, or affixed or left at the demised premises. Service by leaving at the premises (whether or not someone is present to receive it) generally constitutes valid service under s.196.
options.C: Service is only valid if Northgate's director personally receives the notice.
options.D: Notice must always be served by a process server with a witness.

Land Law.explanation: Section 196 LPA 1925 provides that notices required to be served under an instrument affecting property are sufficiently served if left at the last known place of abode or business of the person to be served in England and Wales. Section 196(3) provides that a notice is also sufficiently served if it is affixed or left at the demised premises. Under s.196(4), if left at the premises addressed to the occupant, service is treated as effected even if the notice is not actually received by the intended recipient. Personal service by a director is not required. Options A, C and D impose requirements not found in s.196.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Ravi Sharma believes he has an interest (an estate contract) in unregistered land that Gerald Crane is about to sell. Gerald intends to register the title on sale. What step should Ravi take to protect his interest?

options.A: Register a restriction at the Land Registry.
options.B: Lodge a caution against first registration under s.15 LRA 2002. This means the Land Registry will notify Ravi when Gerald (or a purchaser) applies for first registration, giving Ravi an opportunity to object and assert his interest before registration is completed.
options.C: Register a Class C(iv) Land Charge on the Land Charges Register.
options.D: Apply to the court for an injunction against any registration.

Land Law.explanation: Section 15 LRA 2002 provides that a person claiming a qualifying interest in unregistered land may lodge a caution against first registration. When the registered title is later created (on first registration), the Land Registry gives notice to the cautioner, who has an opportunity to assert their interest. This is distinct from registering a Land Charge under the LCA 1972 (which relates to the pre-registration period — the estate contract should also be registered as a Class C(iv) estate contract on the Land Charges Register). A restriction (A) applies to already-registered land; C is also appropriate for the pre-registration period but does not cover the first registration moment; D is disproportionate.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A client asks why positive covenants cannot be enforced against successors in title to freehold land in the same way as restrictive covenants. Which statement best summarises the current position and reform proposals?

options.A: Positive covenants do not bind successors because Parliament has expressly prohibited their enforcement.
options.B: The rule that the burden of positive freehold covenants does not run in equity (Rhone v Stephens [1994]) is a long-standing common law and equitable rule. The Law Commission has proposed 'land obligations' (in 2011 and earlier reports) that would allow positive obligations to bind successors, but this reform has not been enacted. Workarounds include estate rentcharges, chains of indemnity, and commonhold.
options.C: Positive covenants bind successors in registered land because they can be entered as restrictions in the charges register.
options.D: The burden of positive covenants passes to successors under s.79 LPA 1925.

Land Law.explanation: The rule against running of the burden of positive covenants (Rhone v Stephens [1994]) is confirmed English law. The Law Commission has repeatedly recommended reform — most recently in its 2011 Report proposing 'Land Obligations' as a new interest in land that could be positive or negative and bind successors — but this has not been implemented. Workarounds in practice include: estate rentcharges (Rentcharges Act 1977), chains of indemnity covenants (only effective in contract law), Halsall v Brizell mutual benefit and burden, and commonhold (a rarely used form of flat ownership). Option A and C are wrong; D is wrong — s.79 LPA 1925 is a deeming provision for the benefit, not the burden.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Crane & Pelletier agrees in writing to grant Hollowell Engineering a 5-year lease of premises but the formal deed is never executed. Hollowell pays rent and takes possession. What is the status of the arrangement?

options.A: There is no lease — a lease for more than 3 years must be by deed and the parties have no legal or equitable rights.
options.B: Under Walsh v Lonsdale (1882), where there is a specifically enforceable agreement for a lease (written agreement, supported by consideration), and the tenant is in possession and paying rent, equity will treat the agreement as if the formal lease had been granted — the tenant holds an equitable lease equivalent to the intended legal lease. Specific performance is available to compel the formal grant.
options.C: Hollowell holds a legal lease because entry into possession cures the absence of a deed.
options.D: Hollowell can only be a yearly periodic tenant, regardless of the written agreement.

Land Law.explanation: Walsh v Lonsdale [1882] 21 Ch D 9: equity treats an agreement for a lease specifically enforceable as equivalent to the lease itself ('equity looks on that as done which ought to be done'). The agreement must be specifically enforceable — i.e. be in writing satisfying LP(MP)A 1989 s.2, be certain, and equity must not refuse enforcement. The equitable lease is valid between the parties but has limitations against third parties (an equitable lease is not a legal interest and may not bind purchasers without notice/registration). Option A is too absolute; C is wrong — entry does not create a legal lease without a deed (for leases over 3 years); D ignores the Walsh v Lonsdale doctrine.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Victoria Ashworth, wife of James Ashworth, is concerned that her matrimonial home rights may not be protected when James intends to sell the family home in which she lives. The title is unregistered. What should she do?

options.A: Victoria has no right to remain in the property unless her name is on the title deeds.
options.B: Victoria has matrimonial home rights (rights of occupation) under the Family Law Act 1996 s.30. These are registrable as a Class F Land Charge on the Land Charges Register. By registering, Victoria ensures that any purchaser takes subject to her rights and cannot give vacant possession without dealing with her rights.
options.C: Victoria's occupation as the matrimonial home automatically binds any purchaser without registration.
options.D: Victoria must obtain a court order before her rights can be protected in the Land Charges Register.

Land Law.explanation: Family Law Act 1996 s.30: a non-owning spouse or civil partner has 'matrimonial home rights' — the right to occupy the family home. These rights are registrable as a Class F Land Charge (Land Charges Act 1972) in unregistered land. Registration makes the right binding on purchasers. Without registration, the Class F right is void against a purchaser for money or money's worth (LCA 1972 s.4(8) — note this is a specific provision for Class F). In registered land, a notice is entered in the property register. Options A and C are wrong; D adds a court order requirement that does not exist.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Hassan Nazari has fallen into arrears on his residential mortgage. The lender applies to court for a possession order. Hassan argues he can pay off the arrears within 12 months. What powers does the court have under the Administration of Justice Act 1970?

options.A: The court must grant a possession order — it has no discretion once arrears are proven.
options.B: Under s.36 AJA 1970 (as extended by AJA 1973 s.8), the court may adjourn the proceedings or stay, suspend, or postpone the possession order for such period as the court thinks reasonable, if it appears that the mortgagor is likely to be able within a reasonable period to pay any sums due or remedy any default. A 12-month period to clear arrears may lead the court to suspend the order.
options.C: The court has power to write off Hassan's arrears as a condition of granting possession.
options.D: The AJA 1970 only applies to commercial mortgages.

Land Law.explanation: Section 36 Administration of Justice Act 1970 gives courts a discretion in residential mortgage possession actions: where it appears likely that the mortgagor will within a reasonable period pay off the arrears (or remedy any other default), the court may adjourn, stay, suspend or postpone the possession order. Cheltenham & Gloucester Building Society v Norgan [1996] CA emphasised that courts should use the full mortgage term, not just a short period, when assessing what is 'reasonable'. Paying off arrears within 12 months is a plausible basis for suspension. Option A removes discretion; C is wrong — courts cannot write off arrears; D is wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Repair — tenant's right to deduct from rent
Land Law.stem: Donna Pearce (landlord) has failed to repair the central heating in Pat Hollowell's rented residential property despite Pat's repeated requests. Pat wishes to arrange the repairs herself and deduct the cost from rent. Can she do this?

options.A: No — a tenant may not unilaterally deduct repair costs from rent.
options.B: A tenant may exercise the 'right to repair and deduct' (Lee-Parker v Izzet [1971]) where the landlord is in breach of a repairing obligation: give the landlord reasonable notice and time to carry out the repairs; if the landlord fails to act, the tenant may carry out the repairs themselves and deduct the reasonable cost from future rent. The right is limited and the tenant must act reasonably.
options.C: Pat must withhold all rent until the landlord carries out the repairs.
options.D: The tenant can only seek repairs via a court order — there is no self-help remedy.

Land Law.explanation: Lee-Parker v Izzet [1971] recognised the equitable right to deduct the cost of repairs from rent where the landlord is in breach of a repairing covenant. The conditions: (i) the landlord must be under a repairing obligation; (ii) the tenant must give notice and a reasonable time to repair; (iii) the tenant must carry out the work at a reasonable cost; (iv) the deduction is from future rent. This is a self-help remedy of last resort. Option A is too absolute; C — withholding all rent would be a repudiatory breach by Pat; D is incorrect, as the case law recognises self-help.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Four unities — creation of joint tenancy
Land Law.stem: Ravi Sharma, Diana Pelletier and Gerald Crane purchase a commercial property together. Ravi provides 50% of the purchase price, Diana 30% and Gerald 20%. The transfer deed is silent on their shares. Are they joint tenants or tenants in common at law and in equity?

options.A: They are tenants in common in proportion to their contributions because of the unequal financial contributions.
options.B: At law, they must be joint tenants (only a joint tenancy is permitted for legal co-ownership — LPA 1925 s.1(6)). In equity, the starting point is that the four unities must exist for a joint tenancy (unity of possession, interest, title and time). The unequal financial contributions raise a presumption of tenancy in common in equity (Bull v Bull [1955]; Lake v Gibson [1729]) — the court will look at the surrounding circumstances to determine their equitable shares.
options.C: They are legal and beneficial joint tenants regardless of financial contributions.
options.D: The unequal contributions automatically mean they are tenants in common at law and in equity.

Land Law.explanation: LPA 1925 s.1(6): co-owners must hold the legal estate as joint tenants. In equity, the default depends on whether the four unities are present and whether there are any indicators of a tenancy in common. Unequal financial contributions are a strong indicator of a tenancy in common in equity — the law presumes co-owners intend to hold in proportion to their contributions when contributions are unequal (Bull v Bull [1955]). If the deed is silent, equity will assess the circumstances. The equitable shares may follow the contributions (50:30:20) unless there is evidence of a different common intention. Option C ignores unequal contributions; D is wrong at law (joint tenancy is compulsory for the legal estate); A correctly identifies the likely equitable outcome but overlooks the legal position.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Interests of a person in actual occupation — limitations
Land Law.stem: Diana Pelletier has a beneficial interest in a property registered in Ravi Sharma's name and is in actual occupation. Rachel Okonkwo makes enquiries before completing her purchase by asking Ravi whether anyone else has an interest in the property. Ravi says no. Rachel completes without inspecting the property. Is Diana's interest an overriding interest binding on Rachel?

options.A: Yes — actual occupation always creates an overriding interest that binds all purchasers.
options.B: Under Schedule 3 para 2 LRA 2002, an interest of a person in actual occupation is overriding unless: (i) inquiry was made of that person and they failed to disclose the right when they could reasonably have done so; or (ii) their occupation was not apparent on a reasonable inspection and the purchaser did not have knowledge of it. On these facts, inquiry was made of Ravi (not Diana). Diana could still have an overriding interest if her occupation was apparent on a reasonable inspection of the property — if Rachel did not inspect, she may be bound.
options.C: Diana's interest is not binding because Ravi denied any other interests.
options.D: Diana's interest is overriding only if she has lived in the property for more than 2 years.

Land Law.explanation: Schedule 3 para 2 LRA 2002: the exception to overriding status applies where inquiry was made of the person in actual occupation. Here, inquiry was made of Ravi — not of Diana. The correct inquiry must be made of the person with the interest. If inquiry of Diana was not made, that exception does not apply. Diana's occupation must also be apparent on a reasonable inspection (para 2(c)(i)). If Rachel did not inspect and Diana was visibly occupying, Rachel may be bound. Option A overstates; C ignores that inquiry of Ravi is not the same as inquiry of Diana; D introduces an incorrect time threshold.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd sells a parcel of land to Ravi Sharma. The only access to Ravi's plot is via a track over Ashworth's retained land. The transfer deed makes no mention of any easement of way. Can Ravi claim an easement of necessity?

options.A: No — easements of necessity are no longer recognised in English law.
options.B: Yes — an easement of necessity may be implied on a grant of land that would otherwise be completely landlocked. The courts imply the minimum right necessary to give effect to the parties' presumed intention (Nickerson v Barraclough [1981]). The easement is limited to what is strictly necessary to use the land — a right of way sufficient for access.
options.C: Ravi must apply to the court for a statutory right of access under the Access to Neighbouring Land Act 1992.
options.D: An easement of necessity can only be implied where the land is completely below ground.

Land Law.explanation: An easement of necessity is implied by law on a grant where the dominant land would otherwise be inaccessible (completely landlocked): Nickerson v Barraclough [1981] confirmed this is based on the presumed intention of the parties rather than public policy. The easement is strictly limited to what is necessary — a right of way appropriate to the use of the land. It is the minimum right needed. Option A is wrong; C — the Access to Neighbouring Land Act 1992 allows access for maintenance works, not as a general right of way; D is wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Gerald Crane has an option to purchase Hassan Nazari's registered land. How should Gerald protect his interest?

options.A: Gerald should register a restriction preventing Hassan from selling without his consent.
options.B: Gerald should register a unilateral notice under s.34 LRA 2002 on the charges register of Hassan's title, protecting his estate contract/option. This means a purchaser of Hassan's land takes subject to Gerald's interest. Gerald does not need Hassan's consent to enter a unilateral notice.
options.C: Gerald should register a caution against first registration.
options.D: An option to purchase does not need to be protected in the register — it binds all purchasers as an equitable interest.

Land Law.explanation: Section 32-34 LRA 2002: interests in registered land that are not overriding interests must be protected by entry of a notice in the register. An option to purchase (an estate contract) is a minor interest that must be registered as a notice. A unilateral notice may be registered without the registered proprietor's consent — the registrar notifies the proprietor, who may apply for cancellation. If the notice is not entered, s.29 LRA 2002 means a subsequent purchaser for value takes free of it. A restriction (A) controls dealings but does not protect priority of interest in the same way; C relates to unregistered land; D is wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Colonel Ashworth grants a 3-year tenancy of agricultural land to Northgate Couriers for farming purposes. The tenancy meets the conditions of a Farm Business Tenancy under the Agricultural Tenancies Act 1995. What security of tenure does Northgate have on expiry of the fixed term?

options.A: Northgate has full agricultural security of tenure equivalent to an Agricultural Holdings Act 1986 tenant.
options.B: Farm Business Tenancies under the ATA 1995 do not carry statutory security of tenure equivalent to the AHA 1986. On expiry, Northgate must receive at least 12 months' notice to quit (for tenancies of 2 years or more) but does not have the right to a statutory continuation of the tenancy.
options.C: Northgate may renew the tenancy for a further fixed term as of right.
options.D: Agricultural land is exempt from all landlord and tenant legislation.
