Land Law.explanation: The Agricultural Tenancies Act 1995 governs Farm Business Tenancies (FBTs) granted from 1 September 1995. FBTs do not have the extensive statutory security of tenure provided by the Agricultural Holdings Act 1986 (which applies to older tenancies). For FBTs of 2 years or more, the ATA 1995 requires a minimum of 12 months' written notice to terminate (s.6 ATA 1995). There is no right to continuation or renewal under the 1995 Act. Option A applies AHA 1986 protection incorrectly; C introduces a right of renewal not in the ATA 1995; D is wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A trust of land has two trustees, Ravi Sharma and Diana Pelletier. Ravi dies. Under what powers may a new trustee be appointed?

options.A: A new trustee can only be appointed by a court order.
options.B: Under s.36 Trustee Act 1925, a new trustee may be appointed by the surviving trustee (Diana), by the persons nominated in the trust instrument to appoint new trustees, or (if no one else is able and willing) by the personal representatives of the last surviving trustee. The beneficiaries have a right under s.19 TLATA 1996 to direct the appointment of a new trustee if all are of full age and capacity and collectively entitled.
options.C: On the death of a trustee, the surviving trustee must immediately transfer the trust property to court.
options.D: A new trustee is automatically appointed by the Land Registry on notification of the death.

Land Law.explanation: Section 36 Trustee Act 1925: where a trustee is dead, a new trustee may be appointed by: (i) the person nominated in the trust deed for appointing trustees; or (ii) the surviving or continuing trustees or trustee; or (iii) the personal representatives of the last surviving trustee. Section 19 TLATA 1996 also gives qualifying beneficiaries the right to give a direction for appointment of trustees. In practice, Diana as the surviving trustee has the power to appoint a replacement under s.36. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A court makes a charging order absolute in favour of a judgment creditor against Hassan Nazari's registered freehold property. What step is needed to protect the charge, and what is the effect?

options.A: Charging orders do not need to be registered — they bind all purchasers automatically once made.
options.B: A charging order must be registered as a notice or registered charge at the Land Registry to take priority over subsequent purchasers. Until registered, a purchaser for value of the registered estate may take free of the charging order under s.29 LRA 2002.
options.C: A charging order only binds Hassan personally and cannot be enforced against any subsequent owner.
options.D: Registration of a charging order automatically triggers a forced sale.

Land Law.explanation: A charging order (Charging Orders Act 1979) gives the judgment creditor a charge over the debtor's land, equivalent to a mortgage. To be binding on subsequent purchasers under LRA 2002, it must be protected by registration — typically as a notice in the charges register (or it may be registered as a registered charge). Under s.29 LRA 2002, a purchaser for value takes free of unregistered interests. Registration does not automatically force a sale; the creditor must apply for an order for sale separately. Option A is wrong; C is wrong (it can bind successors if registered); D is wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Diana Pelletier agrees orally to let a room to Pat Hollowell at £600 per month for 6 months, with Pat taking immediate possession. Is a valid lease created?

options.A: No — all leases must be by deed regardless of their length.
options.B: Yes — under s.54(2) LPA 1925, a lease for a term not exceeding 3 years may be created orally (or in writing without a deed) provided: it takes effect in possession immediately; it is at the best rent reasonably obtainable without taking a fine; and the term does not exceed 3 years. A 6-month oral lease satisfies all conditions.
options.C: The oral agreement creates only a licence, not a lease.
options.D: Only leases of land (not rooms) can be created without a deed under s.54(2).

Land Law.explanation: Section 54(2) LPA 1925 provides an exception to the requirement for a deed (s.52 LPA 1925) for leases not exceeding 3 years: such leases may be made orally or in writing, provided (i) they take effect in possession at law immediately; (ii) at the best rent reasonably obtainable; (iii) without payment of a premium (fine). A 6-month residential tenancy at market rent taking immediate effect satisfies all conditions. It is a legal lease. Option A states the general rule incorrectly; C — if there is exclusive possession, a lease arises (Street v Mountford); D is wrong — s.54(2) applies to rooms and flats.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: The Land Registry register shows Hassan Nazari as registered proprietor of a property. He is not physically present at the property (which is rented to a tenant). Is he a 'proprietor in possession' for the purposes of Schedule 4 LRA 2002 (rectification)?

options.A: No — only a registered proprietor who physically occupies the property in person is in possession.
options.B: Yes — under s.131 LRA 2002, 'in possession' includes receipt of rents and profits. As Hassan is receiving rent from the tenant, he is a registered proprietor in possession for the purposes of the rectification threshold in Schedule 4.
options.C: Hassan is not in possession because he has granted a lease — the tenant is in possession.
options.D: A registered proprietor is only 'in possession' if they live at the property as their only home.

Land Law.explanation: Section 131 LRA 2002 defines 'in possession': a person is in possession of land if they are in receipt of rents and profits, or would be in receipt but for some obstruction or other default. Hassan receives rent from the tenant — he is accordingly a registered proprietor in possession under s.131. This is significant for Schedule 4 rectification: rectification against a proprietor in possession requires the higher threshold (caused the mistake or unjust not to rectify). Option A and D are too narrow; C is wrong — the landlord receiving rent is 'in possession'.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Equity of redemption — clogs in commercial context
Land Law.stem: Gerald Crane's commercial mortgage with Wharton Bank contains a clause requiring him to sell all his timber to the bank at below-market prices for 5 years after repayment of the mortgage. Gerald repays the mortgage. Is the clause enforceable?

options.A: No — any collateral advantage after redemption is automatically void as a clog.
options.B: Post-redemption collateral advantages may be enforceable if they are not unconscionable, oppressive or in the nature of a clog on the equity of redemption. Where the mortgagor has repaid and the collateral advantage operates independently as a free-standing commercial arrangement, it may be enforceable (Kreglinger v New Patagonia Meat and Cold Storage Co [1914]). Courts distinguish between clogs during the mortgage and post-redemption arrangements that were part of a wider commercial deal.
options.C: Collateral advantages are always enforceable in commercial mortgages.
options.D: Collateral advantages must be registered as a land charge to be enforceable after redemption.

Land Law.explanation: Kreglinger v New Patagonia Meat and Cold Storage Co [1914] HL: the House of Lords held that a collateral advantage (option to purchase skins) that continued after redemption and was part of a wider commercial arrangement was not a clog on the equity of redemption and was enforceable. The court distinguished between (i) clogs that prevent free redemption (void) and (ii) independent collateral agreements that happen to accompany a mortgage but do not prevent redemption and were freely bargained for on a commercial basis (potentially valid). Option A applies an absolute rule; C is too permissive; D is wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Landlord's right to enter — s.1 Landlord and Tenant Act 1985
Land Law.stem: Donna Pearce (landlord) wishes to enter Pat Hollowell's rented residential property without notice to inspect for repairs. Does she have an absolute right to enter?

options.A: Yes — a landlord always has an absolute right to enter their property at any time.
options.B: No — while s.11 LTA 1985 implies a right for the landlord (or persons authorised by the landlord) to enter the premises to view the condition and state of repair, the tenant must be given at least 24 hours' written notice before entry. Entering without notice may amount to a breach of the covenant for quiet enjoyment and potentially harassment under the Protection from Eviction Act 1977.
options.C: Donna may only enter with the court's permission.
options.D: Donna has no right of entry at all during a fixed-term AST.

Land Law.explanation: Section 11(6) LTA 1985 implies a right for the landlord (or agent) to enter residential premises at reasonable times of day to view the condition of the property, provided at least 24 hours' written notice has been given. Entering without notice, or harassing the tenant by repeated unannounced entries, could amount to breach of the covenant for quiet enjoyment or an offence under the Protection from Eviction Act 1977. Option A is wrong (notice is required); C requires a court order (not generally needed for inspection); D is wrong — the right of inspection exists throughout.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: On completion of the sale of Hassan Nazari's property, his lender discharges the registered charge. What form is used to discharge a registered charge in England and Wales?

options.A: Form TR1 is used to discharge a registered charge.
options.B: Form DS1 (Discharge of Registered Charge) is the Land Registry's prescribed form for acknowledging that a registered charge has been repaid and requesting its removal from the register. In some cases, lenders use an electronic discharge (Form END) through the Land Registry's portal.
options.C: The registered charge is discharged automatically when the mortgage account is closed.
options.D: A court order is required to discharge any registered charge.

Land Law.explanation: Form DS1 is the prescribed form for the discharge of a registered charge. The lender (chargee) executes the DS1 confirming the debt has been repaid, and the solicitor submits it (with Form AP1) to the Land Registry to remove the charge from the register. Many institutional lenders now use the Electronic Discharge (ED) system through the HMLR portal, which updates the register electronically. TR1 is a transfer form; automatic discharge does not occur on mortgage repayment — a formal DS1 or ED is required. Court orders are not needed. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Undertaking to discharge mortgage — solicitor's personal liability
Land Law.stem: At completion of a sale by Gerald Crane, the solicitor at Crane & Pelletier gives an undertaking to the buyer's solicitor to discharge the registered charge on the property using the net proceeds of sale. The firm subsequently goes into administration and the partners misappropriate the proceeds. The mortgage is not discharged. What is the solicitor's personal position?

options.A: The solicitor is not personally liable — the undertaking was given on behalf of the firm, not personally.
options.B: A solicitor's undertaking is personally binding on the individual solicitor who gives it. The firm's insolvency does not extinguish the personal obligation. The solicitor may face: enforcement of the undertaking by court order; professional conduct proceedings (breach of an undertaking is one of the most serious professional failures); SRA disciplinary action; and personal liability for losses flowing from the breach.
options.C: The solicitor's liability is limited to the amount of the firm's professional indemnity insurance.
options.D: As the breach was caused by the partners, only they are liable — not the solicitor who gave the undertaking.

Land Law.explanation: A solicitor's undertaking is a personal professional obligation — not merely the obligation of the firm. Re a Solicitor [1966] and subsequent authorities confirm that courts can enforce solicitors' undertakings personally. The firm's insolvency or the conduct of other partners does not extinguish the solicitor's personal duty to honour the undertaking. Breach may result in: court enforcement (the court has summary jurisdiction); SRA investigation and disciplinary proceedings; personal professional liability. The Solicitors' Indemnity Fund/successor insurer may meet claims but the solicitor's personal conduct obligation remains. Options A, C and D understate the personal nature of the obligation.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Under s.9 of the Wills Act 1837, a valid will must satisfy which of the following requirements?

options.A: The will must be in writing, signed by the testator or at their direction, and witnessed by two persons who must be present together throughout the execution.
options.B: The will must be in writing, signed by the testator only (never by direction), and witnessed by at least one person in the testator's presence.
options.C: The will must be in writing, signed by the testator, and witnessed by two persons who must each sign in the presence of the testator but need not be present together.
options.D: The will must be in writing and signed by the testator, but no witnesses are required if the testator is over 70 years old.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 9 of the Wills Act 1837 requires: (1) the will to be in writing; (2) signed by the testator (or at their direction, with their knowledge); (3) the signature must be made or acknowledged in the presence of two witnesses; and (4) each witness must attest and sign in the presence of the testator. Crucially, both witnesses must be present simultaneously when the testator acknowledges the will and when each signs. This is a stringent requirement — 'simultaneous presence' has been strictly interpreted by the courts. Failure to comply renders the will invalid unless the court exercises dispensing power under s.21 of the Administration of Justice Act 1982.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator signs a will with a mark (X) instead of their full signature. Two witnesses are present simultaneously and sign afterwards. Is the will valid?

options.A: No, because the mark is not a formal signature and does not satisfy s.9 of the Wills Act 1837.
options.B: No, because s.9 requires a signature in the testator's usual handwriting.
options.C: Yes, provided the mark was made with the intention of authenticating the will and the court is satisfied it was the act of the testator.
options.D: Yes, but only if the testator declared to the witnesses that the mark was their signature.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A mark (such as an X, thumbprint, or initials) can constitute a valid signature for the purposes of s.9 of the Wills Act 1837 provided: (1) it was made by the testator with the intention of authenticating the will; and (2) the court is satisfied that the mark was indeed the deliberate act of the testator — see Re Davey [1900]. The signature does not have to be in any particular form, nor need it be in the testator's usual handwriting. A mark, if authenticated as the testator's own act done with testamentary intent, suffices. The formal requirements are functional, not prescriptive.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Which of the following correctly identifies the test for testamentary capacity established in Banks v Goodfellow (1870)?

options.A: The testator must understand the nature of making a will, the extent of their property, and the claims of those who might benefit, and must not suffer from any mental disorder.
options.B: The testator must be free from any medical condition, able to list all assets, and able to recall the names of all blood relatives.
options.C: The testator must have capacity for 28 days before and after the will's execution, and must be certified by a medical practitioner.
options.D: The testator need only understand the nature of a will and the extent of their property; understanding family claims is optional.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Banks v Goodfellow (1870) established the foundational test for testamentary capacity, which requires the testator to: (1) understand the nature of making a will and its legal effects; (2) understand the extent of the property being disposed of; (3) understand the claims of those who might reasonably expect to benefit; and (4) not be suffering from any disorder of the mind that 'poisons the affections, perverts the sense of right, or prevents the exercise of natural faculties' in disposing of property. All four limbs must be satisfied at the moment of execution. This is a test of functional understanding, not perfection — the testator need not have perfect memory or intelligence, only a reasonable grasp of these essential matters.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator with advancing dementia gave clear, detailed instructions for her will when mentally competent. By the time of execution, she had declined significantly but still understood that she was signing a will prepared in accordance with instructions she had previously given. Is the will valid under the principle in Parker v Felgate (1883)?

options.A: No, because Parker v Felgate requires full capacity at execution, which she did not have.
options.B: Yes, but only if a medical practitioner confirmed her capacity at the time of giving instructions.
options.C: No, because testamentary capacity must be assessed at the time of signature only; prior capacity is irrelevant.
options.D: Yes, provided the will was actually prepared in accordance with the instructions given when she had capacity and she understood at execution that it reflected her earlier wishes.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Parker v Felgate (1883) allows a will to be valid where: (1) the testator had capacity when giving instructions to the solicitor; (2) the will was prepared in accordance with those instructions; and (3) at execution, the testator understood that they were signing a will prepared pursuant to instructions given when they had capacity — even if they could no longer fully recall those details. This is a practical exception to the strict rule in Banks v Goodfellow. The testator in this scenario satisfies all three conditions: she had capacity when instructing, the will reflects those instructions, and she retained awareness at execution that the will embodied her earlier wishes. The will is therefore valid.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator signs her will in the presence of two witnesses. Witness A sees the testator sign but leaves before Witness B signs. Witness B never sees the testator sign, only hears from Witness A that she has done so. Both witnesses then sign. Is this valid under s.9 of the Wills Act 1837?

options.A: Yes, because both witnesses have signed and the testator was present when the first witness signed.
options.B: No, because both witnesses must be present simultaneously when the testator acknowledges or signs the will and when each witness signs.
options.C: Yes, provided Witness B heard the testator acknowledge the signature, even though they did not see it.
options.D: No, because Witness A left before the attestation was complete, breaking the chain of evidence.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 9 of the Wills Act 1837 requires that the testator's signature be 'made or acknowledged in the presence of two witnesses present at the same time' and that each witness must attest and sign 'in the presence of the testator'. This is interpreted to require that both witnesses be present simultaneously at both the testator's signing/acknowledgment and at each witness's signing. The principle in Cavendish (1889) and later cases confirms that simultaneous presence is essential — not sequential presence. In this scenario, Witness B was not present when the testator signed, and Witness A left before Witness B signed. Neither witness was present with the other at the critical moment. The will therefore fails to comply with s.9 and is invalid, unless the court exercises its dispensing power under s.21 of the Administration of Justice Act 1982.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator's daughter is one of two witnesses to the will. The testator intends his daughter to inherit a substantial legacy under the will. What is the legal consequence under s.15 of the Wills Act 1837?

options.A: The will is invalid in its entirety because a beneficiary cannot be a witness.
options.B: The will is valid, but the gift to the daughter is void and falls back into the estate.
options.C: The will is valid, and the gift to the daughter is valid because she was a proper witness.
options.D: The gift is void only if the daughter's signature was obtained improperly.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 15 of the Wills Act 1837 provides that a gift made to a witness (or to the spouse of a witness) is void, but the will itself remains valid. This rule applies even if the witness was unaware they were a beneficiary at the time of witnessing. The policy is to prevent the appearance of undue influence and to ensure independent attestation. However, the invalidity applies only to the gift to the beneficiary-witness — the rest of the will remains valid and is given effect. In this scenario, the will is valid, but the legacy to the testator's daughter is void and will fall back into the residuary estate or intestacy as appropriate.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator executes a valid will in 2020. In 2022, the testator marries. The testator makes no new will and does not execute any new codicil. What is the status of the 2020 will?

options.A: The will remains valid because subsequent marriage does not affect wills executed before the marriage.
options.B: The will is revoked by the marriage unless it was expressed to be made 'in contemplation of marriage'.
options.C: The will is revoked by the marriage, but the testator may apply to court to restore it.
options.D: The will is suspended until the testator makes a new will after marriage.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 18 of the Wills Act 1837 provides that subsequent marriage revokes a prior will unless the will was expressed to be made in contemplation of marriage. To avoid revocation, the testator would need to execute a new will after marriage or execute a codicil. In this scenario, the marriage in 2022 revokes the 2020 will.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator executes Will A in 2018. In 2020, the testator burns Will A with the intention of revoking it, and then executes Will B. In 2022, the testator is dissatisfied with Will B. Can Will A be revived?

options.A: No, because Will A was destroyed with the intent to revoke and cannot be revived.
options.B: Yes, Will A can be revived if the testator re-executes it or executes a codicil referring to it with intent to revive.
options.C: Yes, if the testator can show they did not intend to revoke Will A permanently.
options.D: No, because once destroyed, a will is permanently revoked under s.20 of the Wills Act 1837.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 22 of the Wills Act 1837 governs revival. A will that has been revoked can be revived in two ways: (1) by re-execution with all the formalities of s.9; or (2) by a codicil executed with s.9 formalities that expressly declares the testator's intent to revive the earlier will. Mere destruction of a subsequent will (Will B) does not automatically revive the earlier will (Will A) — there must be a positive act of revival. In this scenario, the testator burned Will A deliberately with intent to revoke; section 20 provides that destruction of a will with intent to revoke is effective. However, Will A is not permanently lost — it can be revived if the testator re-executes it (perhaps with the help of a copy) or executes a new codicil showing clear intent to revive it. Mere dissatisfaction with Will B does not achieve revival.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator's will provides: 'I leave my house to my son, and my residuary estate to my daughter.' The son predeceases the testator, leaving two children. What happens to the house?

options.A: The house lapses and falls into residue; the daughter takes the house as part of the residuary estate.
options.B: The house reverts to the testator's estate and is distributed under the intestacy rules.
options.C: The house passes to the son's two children by virtue of s.33 of the Wills Act 1837.
options.D: The house is held in trust for the son's widow.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Under s.33 of the Wills Act 1837, if a beneficiary under a will predeceases the testator, the gift normally lapses (fails). However, s.33 provides an important exception: if the beneficiary was the testator's child or remoter issue (descendant), and the deceased beneficiary left issue (children, grandchildren, etc.) who survive the testator, then the gift passes to the deceased beneficiary's issue in equal shares, as if the beneficiary had survived. This rule applies to both specific gifts (like the house) and residuary gifts. In this scenario, the son predeceased the testator but left children (the testator's grandchildren). Section 33 applies, and the house passes to those grandchildren in equal shares. The daughter does not inherit the house.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator's will bequeaths 'my shareholding in ABC Ltd' to the testator's nephew. At the time the will was made, the testator held 100 ordinary shares in ABC Ltd. Before the testator's death, ABC Ltd is acquired by another company and the testator's shares are converted into shares in the acquiring company. What happens to the bequest?

options.A: The bequest is adeemed (fails) because the original property no longer exists in the form specified.
options.B: The bequest is satisfied by the shares in the acquiring company, because they are the natural substitute for the original shares.
options.C: The bequest fails, and the nephew receives nothing, because specific gifts cannot be modified after the will is made.
options.D: The nephew receives the value of the original shares in cash from the estate.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Ademption is the common law doctrine that if a specific bequest (a gift of a particular item) is no longer owned by the testator in the form described at death, the gift fails. However, the courts have developed an exception where there has been a 'continuity of identity' — if the property has been converted into a substitute of substantially the same nature in the ordinary course of events, the gift is not adeemed. The classic example is shares that are converted through a merger or reorganisation. In this scenario, the testator's shares were converted into shares in the acquiring company — this is a continuity of identity as regards the testator's investment. The nephew takes the shares in the acquiring company. If, by contrast, the testator had sold the shares and the proceeds had been used for something else, the gift would be adeemed.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person dies intestate, leaving a spouse and two adult children. Under the Administration of Estates Act 1925 (as amended by the Intestates' Estates Act 1952 and subsequent amendments), what is the spouse's entitlement?

options.A: The spouse takes the entire estate absolutely.
options.B: The spouse takes personal chattels only; the children take the rest of the estate in equal shares.
options.C: The spouse takes half the estate; the children take the other half in equal shares.
options.D: The spouse takes personal chattels, plus a statutory legacy of approximately £322,000 (as at Feb 2020, subject to indexation), plus half of any residue after payment of debts and expenses; the children take the other half of the residue.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Under s.46 of the Administration of Estates Act 1925 (as amended), where the deceased leaves a spouse and issue (children), the spouse takes: (1) the personal chattels (defined as tangible movable property excluding money and securities) absolutely; (2) a statutory legacy (a fixed sum, currently £322,000 as at February 2020, subject to indexation); and (3) a life interest in half of the residue. However, the 'life interest' has been abolished by modern amendments; instead, the spouse takes an absolute interest in that half of the residue. The children take the other half of the residue on statutory trusts (meaning they take in equal shares, subject to the rights of remoter issue if a child predeceases). If the estate is insufficient to pay the statutory legacy in full, the spouse takes what is available. This rule applies to both spouses and civil partners, and it reflects a balance between protecting the surviving spouse and preserving rights for the deceased's children.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person dies intestate leaving no spouse or children, but leaving a surviving parent (P) and a surviving sibling (S) with two children. Under the Administration of Estates Act 1925, what is the order of entitlement?

options.A: The estate is divided equally among P, S, and S's children.
options.B: P and S take equal shares; S's children take nothing.
options.C: S and S's children take equal shares, per stirpes; P takes nothing.
options.D: P takes the entire estate; S and S's children take nothing.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 46 of the Administration of Estates Act 1925 sets out a fixed order of priority for intestate succession. The order is: spouse (if any); issue (children, grandchildren, etc. on statutory trusts); parents (equally, or wholly to one if only one survives); siblings and their issue (per stirpes); grandparents; uncles and aunts and their issue; and remote relatives. In this scenario, the deceased left no spouse or issue, so the estate passes to the parents. A surviving parent (P) takes the entire estate. The sibling (S) and their children are further down the priority list and take nothing if a parent survives. This rule reflects the principle that the intestacy rules prioritise lineal descendants (issue) and then lineal ancestors (parents), before moving sideways to collaterals (siblings).

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person dies intestate leaving a spouse and three children: A (deceased, but who left two children), B (survived by one child), and C (no children). How does the intestacy law distribute the children's share of the residue under the statutory trusts?

options.A: Equal division into three shares: one for A's children (shared between them), one for B's child, one for C.
options.B: Equal division into five shares: one each for A's two children, one each for B's child and C, and one reserve share.
options.C: Equal division into two shares: one for the children of the deceased's children, one for C.
options.D: Equal division into three shares (A, B, C), regardless of their children's status.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The statutory trusts for issue under s.46 of the Administration of Estates Act 1925 operate 'per stirpes', meaning 'by the stocks' or 'by branches'. The estate is divided into shares based on the number of the deceased's direct children (the 'stirpes'), not based on the total number of grandchildren. Each child's share is then divided among their own surviving issue. In this scenario, the deceased had three children; therefore, the children's portion of the residue is divided into three equal shares — one for A (which A's two children divide equally between them), one for B (which B's one child takes entirely), and one for C (who takes the whole share). This prevents the estates of deceased children from being diluted if they had multiple offspring.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Who is NOT eligible to apply under the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975 for a court order for financial provision from an estate?

options.A: A person who was married to the deceased at the date of death.
options.B: An adult child of the deceased who is in full-time employment and has a good salary.
options.C: A person who cohabited with the deceased as spouse or civil partner for at least two years immediately before death.
options.D: A person who was the deceased's legal dependent (relying on the deceased for financial support) at death.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 1 of the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975 defines 'any person' who may apply for an order. The statutory applicants are: (1) the spouse or civil partner of the deceased; (2) a former spouse or civil partner (if not remarried); (3) a cohabitant (two years immediately before death); (4) a child of the deceased; (5) a child of the family (i.e. someone the deceased treated as a child of the family); and (6) any other person who immediately before death was maintained wholly or partly by the deceased. An adult child with good employment income and financial independence is not eligible — the applicant must be a child (in the family law sense) and typically needs to be a minor or dependent. The Act protects dependants and children, not all children as of right. Note that eligibility differs from the strength of claim; an adult child with independent means simply does not fall within the Act's categories.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.subtopic: Reasonable financial provision — standard and factors
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A surviving spouse applies under the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975 for a court order for financial provision. What standard does the court apply, and what are some of the key matters the court must consider?

options.A: The court must provide the spouse with a fixed percentage of the estate (typically 50%). It considers only the spouse's age and current income.
options.B: The court must provide the spouse with 'maintenance only' — that is, enough to cover essential living expenses, not more.
options.C: The court must provide 'reasonable financial provision' as defined in s.2 of the Act, which for a spouse means such provision as would be reasonable in all the circumstances. The court must consider the spouse's financial needs, the estate's size, the deceased's obligations, the spouse's standard of living during marriage, and the deceased's wishes.
options.D: The court must apply the intestacy rules strictly; the spouse cannot claim more than they would have received under intestacy.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 2 of the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975 establishes different standards depending on the applicant's status. For a spouse or civil partner, the standard is 'reasonable financial provision in all the circumstances of the case'. This is much wider than 'maintenance only' (which applies to other applicants). The court must consider the matters listed in s.3, including: (1) financial resources and needs of applicants and beneficiaries; (2) the obligations and responsibilities the deceased had; (3) the standard of living during the marriage; (4) the age of the applicant and their earning capacity; (5) the physical and mental ability to work; (6) the manner in which the property was disposed of; and (7) the deceased's reasons for not making provision. The spouse's claim is not limited by what they would have received under intestacy; the Act is intended to provide greater flexibility than fixed intestacy rules.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A 25-year-old adult child of the deceased applies under the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975. The deceased's will left the entire estate to an unrelated charity. The child was not dependent on the deceased and has a good job. What standard applies, and is the application likely to succeed?

options.A: The spouse standard applies (reasonable financial provision). The child is likely to succeed because they are a child of the deceased.
options.B: The child must show they were dependent on the deceased or were a child of the family. If neither is true, they have no claim.
options.C: The maintenance standard applies: provision only for 'maintenance' (i.e. income to meet reasonable requirements). Even if eligible, the child is unlikely to succeed given their independent financial position.
options.D: The child must prove the deceased acted unreasonably by not providing for them. The burden is on the child to show the will is unjust.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: For most applicants other than spouses, the standard under s.2 of the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975 is 'such financial provision as it would be reasonable for the deceased to make for [the applicant's] maintenance'. 'Maintenance' does not mean the child's full financial needs or standard of living, but rather income sufficient to meet their reasonable requirements — typically housing, food, and essential expenses. The 'reasonable requirement' test is much lower than the spouse standard. In this scenario, the child is a direct child and is therefore eligible to apply. However, they must satisfy the court that they need maintenance (i.e. have a shortfall between their needs and available resources). An adult child with a good job and financial independence is unlikely to meet that threshold. The fact that the deceased left the estate to charity is not, by itself, evidence of unreasonableness or grounds for overriding the will — the court only intervenes if the deceased failed to make 'reasonable' provision for maintenance.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: What is the primary duty of a personal representative (executor or administrator) when administering an estate?

options.A: To collect in the estate's assets, pay debts and liabilities, and distribute the residue in accordance with the will (or intestacy rules) with due diligence and honesty.
options.B: To distribute the estate as quickly as possible to minimise costs.
options.C: To invest the estate's funds to generate income for beneficiaries.
options.D: To defend all claims against the estate, even if they have merit.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The primary duties of a personal representative are: (1) to collect in the estate's assets and realise them where necessary; (2) to pay the deceased's debts, liabilities, and the costs of administration; (3) to account for income and capital; (4) to distribute the residue among the beneficiaries (or to the intestate succession) in accordance with the will or the intestacy rules; and (5) to do all of this with due diligence, honesty, and care. Personal representatives act as trustees and owe fiduciary duties to the beneficiaries. The executor's year (roughly 12 months from death) is the period within which these duties should be substantially completed, though reasonable delays are permitted. A personal representative should not delay distribution indefinitely, but equally should not rush to distribute if doing so would jeopardise the proper settlement of claims.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.subtopic: Payment of debts — order of application
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: An estate is insolvent (liabilities exceed assets). The deceased's debts consist of: (1) funeral expenses, (2) a mortgage on the house (secured debt), (3) unsecured personal loans, and (4) income tax owed. Under the rules for payment of debts in an insolvent estate, what is the correct order of priority?

options.A: All debts are paid equally (pro rata) from the available funds.
options.B: Funeral expenses first, then income tax, then the secured mortgage, then unsecured loans.
options.C: Funeral expenses and costs of administration first, then secured debts (mortgages), then unsecured debts and taxes in equal proportion.
options.D: Secured debts (mortgages) are paid from the asset they secure; costs of administration and funeral expenses are paid first from general assets; unsecured debts are paid pro rata from what remains.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The order of payment of debts in an insolvent estate under s.34 of the Administration of Estates Act 1925 and general equity principles is: (1) costs of administration and the funeral expenses (from the general estate); (2) secured debts are paid from the secured asset (e.g. the mortgage is paid from the proceeds of sale of the mortgaged property); (3) all other debts (unsecured debts, taxes, etc.) are paid pro rata from the remaining funds, meaning each creditor receives a proportional share if there is insufficient money to pay them in full. The personal representative must not distribute assets to beneficiaries until debts are cleared or adequately provided for. Secured creditors have priority over their security (the mortgaged property), but their claims do not take priority over the costs of administration.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A personal representative has completed the administration of the estate and is ready to transfer a house to one of the beneficiaries. What document should the personal representative execute to transfer the legal title to the house?

options.A: A deed of gift, because the beneficiary is receiving the property free of charge.
options.B: A transfer deed, just like an ordinary sale between two parties.
options.C: A will codicil, amending the will to reflect the transfer.
options.D: An assent, which transfers the legal estate in the house from the personal representative to the beneficiary.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: An assent is a written document by which a personal representative transfers the legal estate in property from the deceased's estate to a beneficiary. The assent must be in writing and signed by the personal representative; it does not require formality beyond a signature, though it should identify the property, the beneficiary, and the estate. An assent can be given orally for personalty (personal property), but for realty (real property such as houses) it must be in writing. Once an assent has been given, the personal representative drops out of the title and the beneficiary acquires the legal estate. The assent is not a sale or a gift; it is simply a transfer of the property held in the estate to the beneficiary. An assent is not a transfer deed (which implies a sale); it is a unique document specific to estate administration.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person dies leaving a net estate of £500,000 (after debts). The estate includes their principal private residence worth £250,000, which is left to their adult daughter. What is the Inheritance Tax treatment?

options.A: The entire estate is charged at 40% IHT because it exceeds the nil rate band.
options.B: The nil rate band of £325,000 applies, plus the residential nil rate band (RNRB) of £175,000 (total £500,000). No IHT is payable.
options.C: Only the excess over £325,000 is charged at 40% IHT (i.e. £175,000 × 40% = £70,000), because the RNRB cannot be used here.
options.D: The house is exempt from IHT because it is being left to a direct descendant.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The nil rate band (NRB) under the Inheritance Tax Act 1984 is frozen at £325,000. The residential nil rate band (RNRB), introduced by the Finance Act 2015, provides an additional exemption of up to £175,000 where the deceased's home is left to a direct descendant (a 'qualifying beneficiary'), such as an adult child or grandchild. The RNRB is available in addition to the standard NRB. In this scenario, the deceased's estate of £500,000 falls within the combined NRB and RNRB allowances (£325,000 + £175,000 = £500,000). Therefore, no IHT is payable. Note that the RNRB is not available if the home is left to a non-qualifying beneficiary (e.g. a spouse or a trust), and it is subject to various conditions and phasing rules. However, the question states the home is left to the daughter, who qualifies.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person makes a gift of £10,000 to their spouse during life. After the testator's death, the testator's estate is £300,000. What is the Inheritance Tax treatment of these gifts and the estate?

options.A: The gift to the spouse is a potentially exempt transfer (PET) and is charged at 40% if the testator dies within 7 years.
options.B: The gift to the spouse is taxable at 20% (half rate) because it is to a spouse.
options.C: The gift to the spouse is exempt from Inheritance Tax because spouses are exempt (provided they are UK domiciled), so the gift is not charged and does not affect the nil rate band.
options.D: The gift to the spouse uses the annual exemption of £3,000.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Transfers between spouses and civil partners are exempt from Inheritance Tax under the IHTA 1984, provided both parties are UK domiciled (or the transferee is UK domiciled). This exemption applies to both lifetime gifts and gifts on death. There is no limit on the amount of the exemption — a spouse can inherit the entire estate without any IHT. The annual exemption of £3,000 and small gifts exemption of £250 per person apply to other gifts, not to gifts between spouses. In this scenario, the gift of £10,000 to the spouse is entirely exempt, and the spouse receives it free of IHT. The spouse's entitlement to the £10,000 does not count toward the testator's nil rate band, and the testator's nil rate band of £325,000 remains available for gifts to non-exempt beneficiaries.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person makes a gift of £50,000 to a discretionary trust during their lifetime. Four years later, the testator dies. What is the Inheritance Tax treatment of this gift?

options.A: The gift is a potentially exempt transfer (PET) that becomes exempt because the testator survived more than 3 years.
options.B: The gift is a chargeable lifetime transfer (CLT) that is subject to IHT at 20% during the testator's lifetime and again at 40% on the testator's death (subject to taper relief).
options.C: The gift is a CLT and is charged at 20% lifetime rate. On death, the 20% already paid is deducted, and only the difference up to 40% is charged.
options.D: The gift is exempt from IHT because it is to a trust, which is treated as a separate entity.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A chargeable lifetime transfer (CLT) is a transfer that is immediately subject to IHT (unlike a PET, which is potentially exempt and only charged if the transferor dies within 7 years). Gifts to trusts (other than bare trusts and trusts for a bereaved minor) are CLTs. In this scenario, the gift of £50,000 to a discretionary trust is a CLT. When made, it is subject to IHT at 20% (less any available nil rate band), so £30,000 would be exempt (nil rate band) and £20,000 would be charged at 20% (i.e. £4,000). When the testator dies (4 years later), the transfer is reconsidered at the full rate of 40%, and taper relief is applied. Because the testator survived 4 years (which falls between 3 and 4 years), taper relief reduces the rate from 40% to 35%. The lifetime tax of £4,000 is credited against the death tax, and the beneficiary (or trust) is liable for the additional tax due.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person owns 100% of the shares in a private limited company (unquoted shares) worth £400,000. The shareholder dies, leaving the shares to their child. What is the Inheritance Tax treatment of the shares?

options.A: The shares are valued at £400,000 and are charged to IHT at 40% (less the nil rate band).
options.B: The shares are subject to a partial relief of 50% under BPR, so £200,000 is exempt and £200,000 is charged.
options.C: The shares qualify for agricultural property relief (APR) only if they relate to farming.
options.D: The shares qualify for business property relief (BPR) at 100%, so they are exempt from IHT.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Business property relief (BPR) under the IHTA 1984 provides relief from Inheritance Tax on the transfer of certain business property. For unquoted shares (shares in private companies not listed on any stock exchange), BPR applies at a rate of 100%, meaning the entire value of the shares is exempt from IHT. BPR is subject to conditions: (1) the property must have been owned for at least 2 years before death; (2) the business must not be excepted (e.g. not mainly dealing in land, shares, or insurance); and (3) the property must be a 'relevant business property' (which includes unquoted shares). In this scenario, the deceased owned 100% of the shares in a private company — assuming the company is a genuine operating business (not a pure investment vehicle) and the shares were held for the required period, BPR at 100% applies. The shares are therefore exempt, and no IHT is charged on their transfer.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person owns a working farm (freehold) worth £600,000 and leaves it to their child. The person also leaves cash of £200,000 to a charity. Assuming agricultural property relief (APR) at 100% applies to the farm, what is the Inheritance Tax due?

options.A: IHT is due on the cash gift but not the farm, because APR provides 100% relief.
options.B: IHT is due on £200,000 (the excess over the nil rate band of £325,000), calculated at 40%.
options.C: IHT is due on £475,000 (£600,000 farm + £200,000 cash - £325,000 nil rate band), calculated at 40%.
options.D: No IHT is due, because APR at 100% exempts the farm and the charity gift is exempt.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Agricultural property relief (APR) under the IHTA 1984 provides relief from Inheritance Tax on the transfer of agricultural property (land and buildings used for agriculture, including working farms). APR is available at 100% (full relief) or 50% depending on the nature of the occupation and the interest held. Assuming APR at 100% applies, the farm is entirely exempt from IHT. A gift to a charity is also exempt from Inheritance Tax under s.58 of the IHTA 1984. In this scenario, the farm of £600,000 is exempt under APR, and the cash gift of £200,000 to charity is also exempt. The total estate of £800,000 is covered by exemptions, so no IHT is due. (Note: the nil rate band is not used here because there are sufficient exemptions to cover the entire estate.)

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person made a chargeable lifetime transfer (CLT) of £100,000 to a trust, using up their nil rate band. Four years later, the person dies. At death, the cumulative transfers in the 7 years before death total £350,000. What is the Inheritance Tax charge on death?

options.A: No IHT is due on death because taper relief applies after 4 years.
options.B: IHT is charged at 40% on the full £100,000 CLT, with no relief.
options.C: IHT is charged at 40% on the amount of the CLT that exceeds the nil rate band, with taper relief reducing the rate.
options.D: IHT is charged on the cumulative total of £350,000, with taper relief applied if the person survived more than 3 years.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Taper relief under the IHTA 1984 reduces the effective rate of Inheritance Tax on chargeable lifetime transfers (CLTs) and potentially exempt transfers (PETs) that become taxable on death, depending on how long the transferor survived. The relief applies on a sliding scale: if the transferor dies between 3 and 4 years after the transfer, the rate is reduced to 35% (a 25% reduction from the full 40% rate); between 4 and 5 years, it reduces to 30%; between 5 and 6 years, 25%; between 6 and 7 years, 20%. In this scenario, the CLT was made 4 years before death. The CLT of £100,000 was made when the nil rate band was available; therefore, £100,000 is exempt. On the death 4 years later, that CLT is reconsidered at the death rate with taper relief. The taper applies to any IHT charged on that CLT (which, in this case, is nil because the nil rate band sheltered it). However, taper relief also applies to subsequent PETs and CLTs made in the 7-year window; those would be recharged at the tapered rate.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person owns shares purchased for £50,000 which, at death, are worth £150,000. The shares are inherited by a beneficiary. What is the beneficiary's base cost for capital gains tax purposes?

options.A: £50,000 (the original purchase price, carried forward from the deceased).
options.B: £150,000 (the value at death — the 'uplift').
options.D: £0, because gifts inherit property free of capital gains tax.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 62 of the Taxation of Chargeable Gains Act 1992 provides that a beneficiary who inherits an asset acquires that asset at its market value at the date of death. This is called the 'uplift' (or 'step up') in base cost. The uplift means that there is no capital gains tax charge on the increase in value during the deceased's lifetime (the gain from £50,000 to £150,000). The beneficiary's base cost for future capital gains tax purposes is £150,000 (the value at death), so any gain is calculated from that point. This rule is one of the key tax benefits of holding assets until death — the unrealised gains during the deceased's lifetime escape capital gains tax entirely. If the beneficiary later sells the shares for £200,000, the capital gain is only £50,000 (£200,000 - £150,000), not £150,000.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator's estate includes a house that was the testator's principal private residence, purchased for £100,000 and worth £400,000 at death. The house is sold by the personal representative for £410,000 shortly after death. What is the capital gains tax position?

options.A: The estate pays capital gains tax on the entire gain of £310,000 (£410,000 - £100,000).
options.B: The estate pays capital gains tax at a lower rate because the assets are held in the estate.
options.C: No capital gains tax is due because the house was a principal private residence and there is relief for such property.
options.D: The estate pays capital gains tax on only the gain after death (£10,000), because the uplift removes the gain during life (£300,000), and principal private residence relief may also apply.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 62 of the TCGA 1992 provides that the beneficiary (or the estate, if sold before distribution) acquires the asset at its market value at death — in this case, £400,000. Therefore, any gain accruing after death (from £400,000 to £410,000 = £10,000) is a post-death gain and is potentially subject to capital gains tax. However, principal private residence relief under s.223 of the TCGA 1992 may apply if the house was the testator's only or main residence. The relief exempts gains on the disposal of a principal private residence. The critical point is that the uplift to £400,000 at death extinguishes the pre-death gain entirely; tax is only due on the post-death increase. Furthermore, if principal private residence relief applies, even the post-death gain may be exempt or partially exempt. The uplift is one of the key advantages of holding property until death.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator's will provides that the residuary estate is to be divided equally among 'my children'. At the time the will was made, the testator had two children, A and B. Subsequently, the testator had a third child, C. The testator dies, leaving A, B, and C surviving. How is the residue distributed?

options.A: The residue is divided equally among A and B only (one-half each), because the class was fixed when the will was made.
options.B: The residue is divided equally among A, B, and C (one-third each), because C is a member of the class 'my children' at the testator's death.
options.C: The residue is divided equally among A and B, with C having a claim only if A or B die before the testator.
options.D: The distribution depends on the names of the children; if C's name is not in the will, C has no claim.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A class gift (a gift to a group defined by a relationship, such as 'my children' or 'my grandchildren') is not fixed at the time the will is made. Instead, the class remains open until the moment of distribution (usually the testator's death, or sometimes the date of distribution if the gift is to a future class). All persons who fall within the description of the class at that moment take a share. In this scenario, the testator made a will when he had two children (A and B), but later had a third child (C). At the testator's death, the class 'my children' includes A, B, and C. All three are members of the class and take equal shares (one-third each). If the testator had intended to exclude afterborn children, he would have said 'my children now living' or 'my children born on or before [date]'. The class gift expands to include C.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator, aged 85 and in fragile health, is visited regularly by a distant relative, X. X takes control of the testator's affairs, prevents others from visiting, and persuades the testator to make a new will leaving the bulk of the estate to X. The testator is of sound mind. Is this will valid?

options.A: Yes, because the testator has testamentary capacity and executed the will properly.
options.B: No, because the testator's age and health suggest a presumption of undue influence.
options.C: Possibly no — the circumstances raise a presumption of undue influence that X must rebut by proving the will was freely made, and this may be difficult if X had opportunity and motive.
options.D: The will is valid only if X was not present at the will's execution.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Undue influence is a vitiating factor that can render a will invalid. The test is whether the testator's will was overborne by the wrongdoer's influence to the point that the will expresses the wrongdoer's wishes, not the testator's own. Proof of undue influence is difficult; it requires evidence that the testator lacked freedom of will. However, the courts have developed a principle of presumption: where there is evidence of (1) opportunity to influence (e.g. access and control); (2) motive (e.g. a benefit under the will); and (3) a resulting will that is rational on its face but suspicious in light of the relationship, then a presumption of undue influence arises. The burden then shifts to the person propounding the will (typically the beneficiary) to prove the testator's freedom of will by satisfactory evidence (e.g. evidence that the testator's solicitor took independent instructions, or evidence of the testator's own expressed wishes). In this scenario, X had opportunity, clear motive (inheriting the bulk), isolated the testator, and benefited substantially — a presumption of undue influence would likely arise. X would need to rebut this by proving the testator's freedom, which may be difficult.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator's will provides for specific gifts of £10,000 to each of three named beneficiaries, but is silent on the residue (the bulk of the estate after the specific gifts). The specific gifts total £30,000, and the total estate is £200,000. What happens to the remaining £170,000?

options.A: The remaining £170,000 is held on trust for the named beneficiaries in proportion to their gifts.
options.B: The remaining £170,000 is forfeited to the Crown as bona vacantia (ownerless property).
options.C: The remaining £170,000 falls into intestacy and is distributed under the intestacy rules (e.g. to the testator's spouse, children, or relatives as appropriate).
options.D: The remaining £170,000 goes to the testator's personal representative as a fee for administering the estate.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A partial intestacy occurs when a will disposes of only part of the testator's property, leaving the rest without any provision. The part that is not provided for by the will falls into intestacy and is distributed in accordance with the Administration of Estates Act 1925. In this scenario, the will provides for specific gifts totalling £30,000 but makes no provision for the residue (£170,000). This £170,000 is undisposed of and falls into partial intestacy. It will be distributed to the testator's spouse (if any), children, parents, siblings, or other relatives in accordance with the statutory order of succession, regardless of the provisions made by the will for the specific gifts. If there are no relatives, the property passes to the Crown. A partial intestacy is common where a testator makes specific legacies but forgets to name a residuary beneficiary or the residuary clause fails for some reason.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth dies leaving a valid will that gives his house to his daughter Victoria Ashworth but says nothing about his bank accounts and investments. He has a surviving spouse (Lady Ashworth) and two children. What happens to the assets not covered by the will?

options.A: The assets not covered pass entirely to the children as the will did not mention them.
options.B: A partial intestacy arises in relation to assets not disposed of by the will. These assets are distributed under the Administration of Estates Act 1925 intestacy rules: Lady Ashworth takes the statutory legacy (£322,000 as of 2023) plus half the remainder absolutely; the rest passes to the children in equal shares (held on a statutory trust for children under 18).
options.C: Assets not covered by the will pass to the Crown as bona vacantia.
options.D: The partial intestacy assets are distributed equally between all beneficiaries named in the will.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Where a will does not dispose of all the deceased's estate, a partial intestacy arises. The undisposed-of property passes under the intestacy rules (AEA 1925 ss.46-49). Where there is a surviving spouse/civil partner and issue: the spouse takes all personal chattels, the statutory legacy (£322,000 post-February 2020 — check current figure), and one-half of the remainder absolutely; the other half passes to the issue on a statutory trust. The house passes under the will; the residuary (bank accounts etc.) passes on partial intestacy. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth dies intestate leaving an estate valued at £280,000. He is survived by his wife Lady Ashworth and two adult children. What does Lady Ashworth receive under the intestacy rules?

options.A: One third of the estate — divided equally with each child.
options.B: Lady Ashworth takes the entire estate because its value (£280,000) is less than the statutory legacy (£322,000). The children receive nothing on this intestacy.
options.C: Lady Ashworth takes half the estate; the children split the other half.
options.D: Lady Ashworth takes only the personal chattels; the estate is then divided equally among all.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Under AEA 1925 s.46 (as amended by the Inheritance and Trustees' Powers Act 2014 and subsequent orders): where there is a surviving spouse/civil partner and issue, the spouse takes (i) all personal chattels absolutely; (ii) a statutory legacy (currently £322,000); (iii) one half of the remainder. Where the entire estate is worth less than the statutory legacy (here £280,000 < £322,000), the spouse takes the whole estate and the children receive nothing. The spouse effectively absorbs all assets. Option A applies an equal division; C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's wife died in 2015, having made no gifts and leaving her entire estate to Colonel Ashworth. She used none of her nil rate band. When Colonel Ashworth dies in 2026, what nil rate band is available?

options.A: Only the standard nil rate band of £325,000 for Colonel Ashworth alone.
options.B: Both Colonel Ashworth's own nil rate band (£325,000) and the unused proportion of his wife's nil rate band may be transferred — giving a combined nil rate band of £650,000, under the transferable nil rate band rules (IHTA 1984 s.8A-C, introduced by FA 2008).
options.C: The transferable NRB does not apply where the first spouse's estate was left to the surviving spouse.
options.D: The transferable NRB is only available if both spouses made wills.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IHTA 1984 s.8A (as inserted by Finance Act 2008): where a spouse or civil partner dies without using all of their nil rate band (NRB), the unused proportion may be transferred to the survivor's estate for use on the survivor's death. The wife used 0% of her NRB (she left everything to Colonel Ashworth — qualifying as an exempt transfer between spouses — so 100% of her NRB is unused). On Colonel Ashworth's death, 100% of the wife's NRB is added to his own NRB: £325,000 + £325,000 = £650,000. Options A, C and D misstate the transferable NRB rules.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth owns his main residence, Ashworth Hall. He leaves it to his two children (Victoria and James) under his will. The residence is valued at £500,000. He has no other assets. Is the Residence Nil Rate Band available?

options.A: No — the RNRB is only available where the property is left to a surviving spouse.
options.B: Yes — the Residence Nil Rate Band (RNRB) under IHTA 1984 s.8H is available where the deceased's main residence is inherited by lineal descendants (children, grandchildren, step-children, adopted children). Victoria and James are lineal descendants. The RNRB (currently £175,000 per individual) may reduce the IHT payable on the estate including the residence.
options.C: The RNRB is not available where the property is shared between more than one descendant.
options.D: The RNRB applies only to estates below the standard nil rate band.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IHTA 1984 s.8H-8M (Finance (No 2) Act 2015): the RNRB is an additional nil rate band for the family home. Conditions: (i) the deceased has a qualifying residential interest (QRI — a residence they have occupied); (ii) the QRI is closely inherited — i.e. it passes to lineal descendants (children, grandchildren, step-children, adopted children, foster children etc.); (iii) the estate value does not exceed the taper threshold (£2m). The RNRB is currently £175,000 per individual. Victoria and James are lineal descendants — the RNRB is available. Option A limits it to spouses; C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.subtopic: IHT — chargeable lifetime transfers and PETs
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth makes a gift of £200,000 to a discretionary trust during his lifetime and dies 4 years later. What is the IHT position on his death?

options.A: The gift into the discretionary trust is a Potentially Exempt Transfer (PET) and becomes fully exempt after 3 years.
options.B: A transfer into a discretionary trust is a Chargeable Lifetime Transfer (CLT), not a PET. If Colonel Ashworth dies within 7 years, the CLT is brought into account for IHT. Additional death tax may be charged on the CLT (less any taper relief for transfers between 3 and 7 years before death). Taper relief at 4 years: 40% reduction in the death rate — so effective rate is 24% (40% × 60%).
options.C: The discretionary trust gift is fully exempt from IHT as long as the trustees are resident in the UK.
options.D: There is no IHT on lifetime gifts to trusts — IHT is only charged on death.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Gifts to discretionary trusts are Chargeable Lifetime Transfers (CLTs) under IHTA 1984 — IHT is charged at lifetime rates (20%) on the excess above the NRB at the time of the CLT. If the donor dies within 7 years, a further death rate calculation is performed on CLTs in the 7 years before death. Taper relief (IHTA 1984 s.7(4)) reduces the death charge for gifts made between 3 and 7 years before death: 40% off at years 3-4; 60% off at years 4-5; 80% off at 5-6; no charge after 7 years. At 4 years, taper relief of 40% applies — so the death charge is 60% of 40% = 24%. PETs (A) apply only to outright gifts to individuals or to trusts for disabled persons. Options C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.subtopic: Annual exemption and small gifts exemption
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Each year for the last 5 years, Colonel Ashworth has given £3,000 to his son James and £250 to each of his 10 grandchildren. He dies this year. Are these gifts exempt from IHT?

options.A: The gifts are all PETs — they will be exempt if Colonel Ashworth survives 7 years from each gift.
options.B: The annual exemption (£3,000 per annum) covers the gift to James each year. The small gifts exemption (£250 per person per annum) covers each grandchild's gift. Both exemptions apply regardless of survival — no 7-year rule is needed for exempt transfers.
options.C: All gifts made within 7 years of death are always fully taxable.
options.D: The annual exemption is shared across all recipients — Colonel Ashworth can only give a total of £3,000 per year across all recipients.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IHTA 1984 s.19 (annual exemption): each transferor may give up to £3,000 per tax year free of IHT (unused allowance may be carried forward one year). The £3,000 gift to James uses the annual exemption. IHTA 1984 s.20 (small gifts): outright gifts of £250 or less to any number of individuals per tax year are fully exempt — but the small gifts exemption cannot be combined with the annual exemption for the same recipient. Each grandchild receives £250 — covered by the small gifts exemption. These are exempt transfers, not PETs, so no 7-year survival period is needed. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Victoria Ashworth is the sole executor of Colonel Ashworth's estate. Priya Mehta is a specific legatee entitled to Colonel Ashworth's vintage car collection. Victoria proposes to sell the cars to pay estate debts. Can she do so?

options.A: No — the executor must preserve all specific legacies and pay debts from the residue only.
options.B: Yes — under the Administration of Estates Act 1925 and the statutory order of payment of debts, assets must be applied in a statutory order (Schedule 1 Part II AEA 1925). A personal representative has power to sell assets to pay debts. Specific legacies may be adeemed or sold to pay debts if other assets are insufficient. Victoria must follow the statutory order before resorting to specific legacies.
options.C: Victoria must obtain Priya Mehta's consent before selling any specific legacy.
options.D: Specific legacies are always fully protected from sale for debt payment.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Administration of Estates Act 1925 Schedule 1 Part II sets out the order in which assets are applied to pay debts and funeral expenses: (i) undisposed property on intestacy; (ii) residuary estate; (iii) property charged with payment of debts; (iv) demonstrative legacies; (v) general legacies; (vi) specific legacies; (vii) statutory trust on death. Specific legacies (category vi) are near the bottom — they may be sold to pay debts only after earlier categories are exhausted. Victoria must follow this order; consent from Priya is not required, but Victoria must only resort to specific legacies after exhausting prior categories. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Victoria Ashworth is named as executor in Colonel Ashworth's will. She wishes to obtain a grant of probate. Which statement correctly describes the process?

options.A: Victoria automatically has authority to deal with the estate on the testator's death — no grant is needed.
options.B: Victoria must apply to the Probate Registry for a grant of probate. She must submit: the original will, a HMRC account (if IHT is payable), and swear/affirm a statement of truth in the executor's oath. The Probate Registry will issue the grant, which is the document establishing her authority to deal with the estate.
options.C: Probate is only required if the estate is worth more than £1m.
options.D: The solicitor obtains probate on Victoria's behalf and then has authority to administer the estate independently.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Probate confirms the executor's authority to administer the estate (Non-Contentious Probate Rules 1987; the Non-Contentious Probate (Amendment) Rules 2020 introduce HMCTS online probate applications). Although executors derive their authority from the will (not the grant), third parties (banks, asset holders) require the grant before releasing assets. Victoria must apply to HMCTS Probate Service, lodging the original will, any IHT account (form IHT400 or IHT205/400), and the executor's declaration. The grant then proves her authority. Option A is partially correct (executors derive authority from the will) but third parties require the grant; C imposes a false threshold; D mischaracterises the solicitor's role.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.topic: Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Lady Ashworth is left only £50,000 under Colonel Ashworth's will out of an estate of £2m. She believes this is inadequate provision. What is the standard of provision the court applies to her claim under the IPFDA 1975?

options.A: The court assesses whether provision is 'reasonable' using the same standard applied to all claimants.
options.B: Under IPFDA 1975 s.1(2)(a), for a surviving spouse or civil partner, the standard of provision is 'such financial provision as it would be reasonable in all the circumstances of the case for a husband or wife to receive, whether or not that provision is required for maintenance' — this is the 'Duxbury' standard, higher than the maintenance standard applied to other claimants.
options.C: Lady Ashworth must prove she has no other means of support before making a claim.
options.D: The standard for a surviving spouse is absolute equality — Lady Ashworth is entitled to half the estate regardless.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IPFDA 1975 s.1(2)(a): for a surviving spouse/civil partner, the standard is what would be 'reasonable in all the circumstances' for a spouse to receive — a higher standard than the mere 'maintenance' standard applicable to adult children and others. The court considers all the s.3 factors including the surviving spouse's financial position, duration of the marriage, standard of living, contributions, etc. It is not a right to half the estate (D), but the standard is more generous than maintenance. Option A applies the wrong standard; C adds a means test not in the Act.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.topic: Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: James Ashworth is an adult child of Colonel Ashworth. He was estranged from his father for 20 years and received nothing under the will. James claims under IPFDA 1975. What standard applies and what are the likely factors?

options.A: Adult children automatically receive a share of the estate regardless of the testator's wishes.
options.B: Under IPFDA 1975 s.1(1)(c) and s.1(2)(b), an adult child must show the will fails to make reasonable provision for their maintenance. The court applies the general s.3 factors including: the claimant's financial resources/needs; the size and nature of the estate; any obligations/responsibilities of the deceased; the relationship between deceased and claimant; estrangement is a relevant factor weighing against James's claim. Adult children face a higher hurdle than surviving spouses.
options.C: Estrangement from the deceased is an absolute bar to a claim under IPFDA 1975.
options.D: James may claim as of right to one third of the estate as a child.
