Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IPFDA 1975 s.1(1)(c): children of the deceased may claim. The standard is 'reasonable financial provision for maintenance' (s.1(2)(b) — lower than the spouse standard). Section 3 factors include: financial needs/resources of applicant and beneficiaries; obligations of deceased to applicant; size of estate; physical/mental disability; conduct; and the relationship. Estrangement is a significant factor — Re Hancock [1998] CA noted that adult children receive little sympathy from courts where there is no obvious need and there was a breakdown in the relationship. It is not an absolute bar (C) but it is highly relevant. Options A and D remove the court's discretion; C is too absolute.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth executes a new will aged 86 shortly after being hospitalised with a serious infection causing temporary confusion. His solicitor visits him in hospital. The will significantly benefits his carer Pat Hollowell over his children. His children challenge the will on grounds of lack of testamentary capacity. What must the court assess?

options.A: The court assesses only whether Colonel Ashworth could physically sign the will.
options.B: Under Banks v Goodfellow (1870), testamentary capacity requires the testator to: (i) understand the nature of making a will and its effects; (ii) understand the extent of the property they are disposing of; (iii) understand the claims of those who might expect to benefit; and (iv) not suffer from a disorder of the mind that poisons the affections, perverts the sense of right, or prevents the exercise of natural faculties in disposing of property. The court assesses capacity at the date of execution.
options.C: Mental capacity is assessed at the date the testator first gave instructions, not the date of execution.
options.D: A solicitor's presence at execution is conclusive proof of capacity.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Banks v Goodfellow [1870] LR 5 QB 549 remains the leading case on testamentary capacity. The four elements must all be satisfied at the time of execution (Re Key [2010] EWHC 408 — confirmed capacity must exist at execution). Temporary confusion during hospitalisation does not automatically negate capacity — the question is whether the individual had a 'lucid interval' at the time of signing. The solicitor's attendance is strong evidence of capacity but not conclusive. The 'golden rule' (Re Simpson [1977]; Kenward v Adams [1975] — solicitor should obtain a medical assessment for elderly/ill testators) is good practice. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's children argue that Pat Hollowell exerted undue influence to secure a large bequest under the will. What must they prove?

options.A: They need only prove that Pat and Colonel Ashworth had a close relationship — this raises a presumption of undue influence.
options.B: In testamentary undue influence, there is no presumption — the burden of proof is on those alleging it. They must prove actual undue influence: that the will is the product of Pat's coercion or pressure that overpowered the testator's freedom of will and caused him to make dispositions he would not otherwise have made (Hall v Hall [1868]; Wingrove v Wingrove [1885]).
options.C: Undue influence in wills is presumed whenever the testator was elderly and the beneficiary was a carer.
options.D: Proof that the testator lacked capacity at the time of the gift is sufficient to establish undue influence.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Testamentary undue influence differs from inter vivos undue influence (where a presumption may arise in certain relationships). In wills, there is no presumption of undue influence — it must be proved affirmatively: the claimants must show Pat actually coerced Colonel Ashworth so as to overpower his free will (Hall v Hall [1868]). Merely persuading or influencing is not enough — the pressure must amount to coercion that leaves the testator unable to make a free decision (Wingrove v Wingrove [1885]). Option A applies an incorrect presumption; C applies a factual generalisation incorrectly; D confuses capacity with undue influence (separate vitiating factors).

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.subtopic: Lapse — gift to a predeceasing beneficiary
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's will leaves £50,000 to 'my friend Clive Dring'. Clive Dring dies before Colonel Ashworth. What is the fate of the £50,000 gift?

options.A: The gift passes to Clive Dring's estate.
options.B: The gift lapses — a gift to a named beneficiary who predeceases the testator fails unless the will contains an alternative provision, or the gift falls within s.33 Wills Act 1837 (which saves gifts to issue of the testator who predecease). As Clive Dring is a friend (not issue), the lapse rule applies and the £50,000 falls into residue.
options.C: The gift is held on a resulting trust for the testator's estate.
options.D: The gift automatically passes to Clive Dring's spouse under the doctrine of survivorship.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The lapse rule: if a beneficiary predeceases the testator, the gift lapses and falls into residue (or intestacy if there is no residue clause). Section 33 Wills Act 1837 saves gifts to the testator's issue who predecease — the gift passes to the issue's own children if they die before the testator. Section 33 does not apply to gifts to friends, siblings or others (unless they are issue of the testator). As Clive is a friend, s.33 is irrelevant and the gift lapses. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's will leaves 'my Wharton Magna farmland' to his son James. Before his death, Colonel Ashworth sells the farmland. What happens to James's legacy?

options.A: James receives the proceeds of sale held in Colonel Ashworth's bank account, as they are traceable to the farmland.
options.B: The legacy is adeemed — the subject matter of the specific legacy no longer belongs to the testator at death, so the gift fails. James receives nothing (unless the will contains an alternative gift).
options.C: The legacy is converted to a general legacy of equivalent value.
options.D: Ademption only applies to chattels, not to real property.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Ademption applies where the subject matter of a specific legacy no longer belongs to the testator at the date of death: Re Slater [1907]. If Colonel Ashworth sells the farmland before he dies, the specific bequest of 'my Wharton Magna farmland' is adeemed — there is nothing answering that description in his estate at death. The legacy fails entirely. The proceeds of sale are not substituted (option A — there is no tracing in this context). Ademption applies to all specific legacies whether real or personal property. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Victoria Ashworth, as executor, receives demands from specific legatees to distribute the estate within 3 months of probate. She is still dealing with complex IHT issues. Can the legatees compel immediate distribution?

options.A: Yes — the executor must distribute within 3 months of probate or face a breach of duty claim.
options.B: No — under the Administration of Estates Act 1925 s.44 and the principle of the 'executor's year', the personal representative is not obliged to distribute within 12 months of death. Victoria has a year from the date of death to administer the estate. Legatees cannot demand payment within that year.
options.C: The executor must distribute all legacies within 6 months of the date of probate.
options.D: The executor's year does not apply to specific legacies — only residue.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: AEA 1925 s.44: if administration is not complete within one year of death, a beneficiary or creditor may require the personal representative to complete administration. The executor's year (12 months from death) gives the PR reasonable time to administer the estate, pay debts, deal with IHT and other obligations before distribution. Beneficiaries cannot compel immediate payment within the year. Interest may run on legacies after the executor's year if not paid. Options A, C and D incorrectly shorten the period or limit its scope.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth owns 100% of the shares in Ashworth Developments Ltd (a qualifying business property). He dies. His estate claims business property relief (BPR) on the shares. What conditions must be satisfied for 100% BPR?

options.A: BPR at 100% is available on all shares in any company owned by the deceased.
options.B: Under IHTA 1984 ss.103-114, BPR at 100% is available on: unquoted shares in a qualifying company (not mainly investment holding or dealing in land/investments). The deceased must have owned the property for at least 2 years before death. Ashworth Developments Ltd must be a qualifying business (trading, not mainly investment). If satisfied, 100% BPR reduces the IHT value of the shares to nil.
options.C: BPR is only available for listed (Stock Exchange-quoted) companies.
options.D: BPR requires the deceased to have worked full-time in the business for 5 years before death.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IHTA 1984 s.104: business property relief at 100% is available for: (i) a business or interest in a business; (ii) unquoted securities (including AIM-listed) that gave the transferor control of the company; (iii) unquoted shares. The relief at 50% applies to quoted controlling holdings and assets used in a business. Conditions: (i) qualifying business (not mainly investment/dealing); (ii) minimum ownership period of 2 years (s.106). A development company is a trading company (not merely investment) — BPR at 100% should be available if conditions met. Options A, C and D misstate the conditions.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth owns farmland in Wharton Magna that he has let to a tenant farmer for 10 years. Does agricultural property relief (APR) apply on his death?

options.A: No — APR only applies where the deceased personally farmed the land.
options.B: Yes — under IHTA 1984 ss.115-124B, APR at 50% or 100% is available on agricultural property that has been owned by the deceased for 7 years (if let to a qualifying tenant) or 2 years (if occupied by the owner for agricultural purposes). Property let for 10 years qualifies for APR (at 50% or 100% depending on the tenancy terms). Since the Inheritance Tax Act 1984 amendments (Finance Act 1995), land let since 1 September 1995 on qualifying tenancies qualifies for 100% APR.
options.C: APR is only available where the land has been let for less than 2 years.
options.D: APR and BPR cannot both apply to the same estate.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IHTA 1984 ss.115-124B: APR applies to agricultural property (land occupied for agricultural purposes). Where the deceased owned the land and it was let, the ownership period must be at least 7 years (s.117). For land let on a tenancy commencing on or after 1 September 1995 (Agricultural Tenancies Act 1995 farm business tenancies), 100% APR is available (Finance Act 1995). For older (AHA 1986) protected tenancies, 50% APR applies. The 10-year let satisfies the 7-year ownership requirement. Option A requires personal farming (incorrect for let land — the 7-year rule applies); C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth dies intestate. No executor was appointed. Who has priority to apply for letters of administration?

options.A: The oldest child of the deceased always has priority.
options.B: Under the Non-Contentious Probate Rules 1987 rule 22, priority to apply for a grant of letters of administration (intestacy) runs: (1) surviving spouse/civil partner; (2) children; (3) children of predeceased children; (4) parents; (5) brothers and sisters; (6) half-blood relatives; (7) grandparents; (8) uncles and aunts; (9) creditors. The surviving spouse/civil partner has first priority.
options.C: The court appoints an official receiver to administer all intestate estates.
options.D: The solicitor who knew the deceased applies for administration.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Non-Contentious Probate Rules 1987 r.22 sets out the priority of persons entitled to a grant of letters of administration on intestacy in England and Wales. The surviving spouse or civil partner takes first priority, followed by children, then more distant relatives. If no entitled person applies, creditors and ultimately the Crown (bona vacantia) may administer. Option A is wrong — it is not the oldest child but the surviving spouse who has priority; C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.subtopic: Revocation by marriage — s.18 Wills Act 1837
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Victoria Ashworth made a will in 2018. She marries Marcus Bridgewater in 2023. She dies in 2026 without making a new will. Is her 2018 will valid?

options.A: Yes — a will remains valid regardless of any subsequent marriage.
options.B: No — under s.18 Wills Act 1837, a will is revoked by the testator's subsequent marriage unless the will was made in contemplation of that particular marriage (a will expressed to be made in contemplation of marriage to a named person is not revoked by that marriage — s.18(3) as substituted). Victoria's 2018 will was not made in contemplation of her 2023 marriage to Marcus — it is revoked and she is treated as dying intestate.
options.C: The will is partially revoked — only gifts to her future husband are revoked.
options.D: Revocation by marriage only applies if the will was made more than 10 years before the marriage.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 18 Wills Act 1837 (as substituted by the Administration of Justice Act 1982 s.18): marriage revokes a will unless the will was made in contemplation of the particular marriage. The exception (s.18(3)) applies only where it appears from the will that at the time it was made the testator was expecting to marry a particular person and intended that the will should not be revoked by the marriage. Victoria's 2018 will predates Marcus by years and has no such contemplation clause. It is fully revoked. Victoria dies intestate. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth makes a will in 2020 leaving his estate to his children in equal shares. In 2024, he executes a codicil leaving an additional legacy of £30,000 to Donna Pearce. Which provisions survive?

options.A: The codicil revokes the 2020 will entirely.
options.B: The codicil confirms and supplements the 2020 will. Codicils revoke only the provisions of the existing will they are inconsistent with. The 2024 codicil adds a new gift of £30,000 to Donna Pearce; the existing gift to the children in equal shares (the residue) remains unchanged.
options.C: The children's entitlement is reduced by £30,000 each following the codicil.
options.D: A codicil must expressly revoke the earlier will for both documents to take effect.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A codicil is a testamentary document that supplements or amends an existing will. It does not automatically revoke the will. Only provisions of the existing will that are inconsistent with the codicil are revoked (implied revocation). An additional legacy in a codicil adds to the estate's dispositions; the will remains operative for all other provisions. The codicil confirms the will (re-publishing it). The children's residuary entitlement remains; Donna receives her £30,000 as a specific or demonstrative legacy. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Victoria Ashworth, as executor of Colonel Ashworth's estate, wishes to transfer Ashworth Hall (registered title) to herself as a beneficiary under the will. What document is used and what must be done?

options.A: Victoria uses a TR1 transfer form, the same as any other sale.
options.B: Victoria must execute a formal assent in favour of herself (the beneficiary) under AEA 1925 s.36. For registered land, the assent must be in Form AS1 (or AS3 for a charge). The assent must be registered at the Land Registry to transfer the legal estate to Victoria in her personal (non-executor) capacity.
options.C: No formal document is required — the property automatically passes to Victoria as sole executor on the death.
options.D: Victoria must obtain the consent of all other beneficiaries before making an assent in her favour.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Administration of Estates Act 1925 s.36: a personal representative may assent to the vesting of any property in any person (including themselves as beneficiary). For registered land, HM Land Registry Form AS1 is used for an assent (or AS3 for assenting a charge). The assent must be in writing and signed by the personal representative. It must be registered at the Land Registry under LRA 2002 to transfer the legal estate. TR1 is used for sales and other transfers, not personal representative assents. An executor taking property in their own favour may need to act carefully to avoid conflict of interest — the assent is still valid legally. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.subtopic: IHT — interaction with CGT on death
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Victoria Ashworth inherits shares in Ashworth Developments Ltd from Colonel Ashworth's estate. The shares were worth £100,000 when Colonel Ashworth bought them and £400,000 at his death. Victoria later sells them for £500,000. What are the IHT and CGT implications?

options.A: Victoria pays CGT on the full gain of £400,000 from the original cost.
options.B: On death, there is no CGT charge (TCGA 1992 s.62 — death is not a disposal for CGT). Victoria inherits the shares at their probate value (£400,000) — this is her base cost (the 'uplift' on death). She pays CGT only on any gain above £400,000 — so on the sale at £500,000, her gain is £100,000. IHT was charged on the estate at death (on the £400,000 value); there is no double tax relief for CGT.
options.C: Victoria receives the shares free of any future CGT liability because IHT was paid on them.
options.D: Victoria's base cost for CGT is Colonel Ashworth's original cost (£100,000).

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: TCGA 1992 s.62: on a person's death, the assets are treated as acquired by the personal representatives or legatees at market value at the date of death (the 'uplift' or 'step-up'). Death is not a disposal for CGT — no CGT is charged at that point. Victoria's base cost is the probate value (£400,000). On a sale at £500,000, her chargeable gain is £100,000 (£500,000 - £400,000). IHT is charged on the estate separately and there is no general relief for double taxation between IHT and CGT. Option A uses the wrong base; C is wrong (IHT payment does not exempt from CGT); D uses the original cost rather than the uplift.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth leaves 10% of his estate to the Wharton Magna Animal Trust (a registered UK charity). What is the IHT treatment of this gift and what is the effect on the IHT rate?

options.A: The charitable gift is treated as a potentially exempt transfer and is fully taxable.
options.B: The gift to a registered UK charity is an exempt transfer under IHTA 1984 s.23 and is free of IHT. Additionally, under the Inheritance Tax (Increased Nil Rate Band) (Limiting the Charge on the Settled Property) Order 2012, if the deceased leaves at least 10% of the net estate (above the NRB) to charity, the IHT rate on the chargeable estate is reduced from 40% to 36%.
options.C: Gifts to charities are exempt only up to £10,000.
options.D: The charitable gift reduces the available nil rate band.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IHTA 1984 s.23: transfers to qualifying UK charities are exempt from IHT. In addition, the Finance Act 2012 (IHTA 1984 s.7(2A) and Schedule 1A) introduced the reduced 36% IHT rate where 10% or more of the net estate (after NRB deductions) is left to charity. This incentivises charitable giving as the total IHT cost to the estate may be reduced even though more is given away. Option A is wrong (charitable gifts are exempt); C imposes a false cap; D is wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Victoria Ashworth, as executor, distributes the entire estate to residuary beneficiaries without first ascertaining all debts. An unsatisfied creditor (Northgate Couriers) presents a bill for £15,000. Is Victoria personally liable?

options.A: No — Victoria is protected by the grant of probate and cannot be personally liable for estate debts.
options.B: Yes — a devastavit occurs where a personal representative misapplies estate assets, including distributing to beneficiaries before all debts are ascertained and paid. Victoria may be personally liable to Northgate Couriers for the £15,000 if estate assets are no longer available. She should have placed statutory advertisements for creditors (s.27 Trustee Act 1925) before distributing.
options.C: The beneficiaries are personally liable to refund the debt, not Victoria.
options.D: Victoria is only liable if she knew about the debt at the time of distribution.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A PR who distributes assets before paying all debts commits a devastavit — a misapplication of estate assets. This renders the PR personally liable to unpaid creditors to the extent of assets misapplied. Section 27 Trustee Act 1925 provides a defence if the PR has placed statutory advertisements for creditors and claimants (in the London Gazette and a local newspaper) and waited two months before distribution — providing a safe harbour. Without s.27 protection, Victoria is personally liable to Northgate Couriers. Beneficiaries may also be required to contribute under the law on contribution, but Victoria's primary personal liability arises from the devastavit. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth owns immovable property in France and movables in England. He is domiciled in England. His will was validly executed in England. Which law governs the succession to each type of property?

options.A: English law governs all of Colonel Ashworth's estate as he is domiciled in England.
options.B: Under private international law: immovable property (land) is governed by the law of the situs — the French property is governed by French succession law. Movable property (assets in England) is governed by the law of the domicile — English law governs the English movables. The English will must comply with both regimes for the foreign property to pass under it.
options.C: EU Succession Regulation 650/2012 applies and English law governs the entire estate.
options.D: The French property passes under English law because the will was made in England.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Private international law rules: (i) succession to immovable property (real property) is governed by the lex situs — the law of where the property is situated. The French land is governed by French succession law regardless of the testator's domicile; (ii) succession to movables is governed by the law of the deceased's domicile at death — English law for English movables. Note: EU Succession Regulation 650/2012 (Brussels IV) allows EU residents to choose the law of their nationality — but the UK did not adopt Brussels IV before Brexit (and it does not apply post-Brexit). Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Victoria Ashworth, as executor, cannot locate a specific beneficiary (Hassan Nazari) who is entitled to a share of Colonel Ashworth's estate. The estate is ready for distribution. What can Victoria do to protect herself?

options.A: Victoria should retain the full share indefinitely until Hassan is found.
options.B: Victoria may apply to the court for a Benjamin order (Re Benjamin [1902]) — an order authorising distribution of the estate on the assumption that Hassan has predeceased Colonel Ashworth (or died without issue). If Hassan later appears, his remedy is against the beneficiaries who received his share, not against Victoria personally.
options.C: Victoria must apply for letters of administration to deal with Hassan's missing share.
options.D: Victoria can distribute without any protection — she has no duty to locate missing beneficiaries.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Re Benjamin [1902] 1 Ch 723: where a beneficiary cannot be found and the court is satisfied there is a substantial probability the person has died (or that their entitlement has failed), the court may make a Benjamin order authorising the PR to distribute on that basis. The PR is then protected — any subsequent claim by the missing beneficiary is against the recipients, not the PR personally. Alternatively, the PR may take out insurance against the risk of the missing beneficiary appearing. An application to court for the order gives the cleanest protection. Options A, C and D are inadequate or wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth purports to give Ashworth Hall to his son James in 2020 but continues to live there rent-free until his death in 2026. Is the gift effective for IHT?

options.A: Yes — the gift is effective from the date of the transfer in 2020 and falls outside the estate.
options.B: No — under IHTA 1984 s.102 and Schedule 20 Finance Act 1986 (gifts with reservation of benefit), where a donor makes a gift but retains a benefit from the property, the property remains within the donor's estate for IHT on death. The gift with reservation rules treat the property as still belonging to Colonel Ashworth at death — it is included in his estate at its death value.
options.C: The property is excluded from IHT because Colonel Ashworth's occupation was in the family home.
options.D: The gift is effective, but HMRC may only reclaim IHT if the reservation was worth more than £10,000 per year.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Finance Act 1986 s.102 and Schedule 20: the gifts with reservation (GWR) rules prevent IHT avoidance by giving away property while continuing to benefit from it. Where the donor retains a benefit from the gifted property (here, rent-free occupation), the gift is treated as a GWR. The property remains in the donor's estate for IHT at death value (if the benefit was not given up more than 7 years before death — s.102(4) provides that if the donor ceases to retain the benefit and survives 7 years, the property falls out of the estate). Colonel Ashworth retained occupation until death — the property is included at death. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's will was prepared by his solicitor. Colonel Ashworth, who had poor eyesight, did not read the will before signing. The will contains an unexpected clause benefiting the solicitor. What legal principle is engaged?

options.A: The will is automatically invalid if it was prepared by a professional.
options.B: Where the testator has not read the will, the propounder must show that the testator knew and approved the contents at the time of execution (Gill v Woodall [2010]; Fuller v Strum [2002]). Where a solicitor benefits under a will they prepared, the court requires strong evidence of knowledge and approval — the circumstances excite suspicion and the court requires the suspicion to be dispelled.
options.C: Knowledge and approval is presumed from valid execution — no further evidence is needed.
options.D: A beneficiary who prepared the will automatically takes a resulting trust of the benefit.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The doctrine of knowledge and approval requires that a testator must know and approve the contents of the will at the time of execution. Where suspicious circumstances exist (e.g. the propounder of the will benefits from it, or prepared it), the court requires positive proof that the testator knew and approved the contents (Barry v Butlin [1838]; Fuller v Strum [2002]; Gill v Woodall [2010] CA). A solicitor benefiting under a will they prepared is a classic suspicious circumstance — the SRA Code also addresses this (gifts from clients). The suspicion must be 'removed from the court's mind'. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.subtopic: Tracing — proprietary remedy in estate administration
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Victoria Ashworth (executor) mistakenly pays a legacy of £20,000 to the wrong person (Clive Dring instead of the intended Gerald Crane). Clive has spent the money. What remedies does Gerald have?

options.A: Gerald can only sue Victoria personally for breach of duty — no proprietary remedy exists.
options.B: Gerald may have a personal claim against Victoria for breach of duty as executor (devastavit) and may have a personal claim against Clive as a recipient of trust money paid under a mistake (unjust enrichment). If the money can be traced in Clive's hands, a proprietary remedy (tracing) may be available, but on these facts the money has been dissipated — making a personal claim against Clive the more practical route.
options.C: Gerald can only recover from the estate by applying to court for an order for re-administration.
options.D: As Clive received the money as a gift, he has a complete defence to any claim.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Where a PR pays a legacy to the wrong person, the rightful beneficiary has: (i) a personal claim against the PR for breach of duty (devastavit); (ii) potentially a proprietary claim (tracing) against the recipient if the money is identifiable; (iii) a personal claim in unjust enrichment (money had and received) against the recipient, subject to the change of position defence. Where the money has been spent, a proprietary remedy is unavailable (no identifiable fund) but the personal unjust enrichment claim remains unless Clive has changed his position in good faith (Lipkin Gorman v Karpnale [1991]). Options A and C are incomplete; D is wrong — change of position, not gift status, is the defence.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.subtopic: Solicitor's duty — capacity and undue influence in will preparation
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A solicitor at Crane & Pelletier is instructed to prepare a will for Colonel Ashworth (aged 91), who wishes to leave his entire estate to his nurse, Pat Hollowell, cutting out his family completely. The solicitor is concerned about Colonel Ashworth's capacity and the influence of the nurse. What should the solicitor do?

options.A: Draft the will as instructed — the client is an adult and professional autonomy requires compliance.
options.B: The solicitor should: see Colonel Ashworth alone (without Pat Hollowell); assess testamentary capacity under Banks v Goodfellow; follow the 'golden rule' (Kenward v Adams [1975]) by seeking a medical opinion on capacity; ensure the instructions are freely given and not the product of undue influence; and consider whether to decline to act if concerns cannot be resolved. The solicitor must not act for Colonel Ashworth in circumstances where capacity or free will cannot be established.
options.C: Report Pat Hollowell to the police for elder abuse before preparing the will.
options.D: The solicitor must draft the will but add a disclaimer noting concerns.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The 'golden rule' in Kenward v Adams [1975] TLR: where a testator is elderly or suffers from illness, the solicitor should obtain a medical practitioner's assessment of capacity and have the medical practitioner witness the will and confirm capacity. SRA Code (Principles 2, 4, 7): the solicitor must act in the client's best interests, with honesty and integrity. Seeing the client alone is essential to assess for undue influence. If capacity or free instructions cannot be established, the solicitor must decline to act — preparing a will in such circumstances could facilitate a future challenge. Option A ignores professional duty; C is premature; D is inadequate.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth died intestate leaving an estate of £800,000. His daughter Victoria wishes to redirect her statutory legacy to her son James. Which instrument should be used and what is the time limit?

options.A: A deed of variation executed within 2 years of death, signed by Victoria alone
options.B: A deed of disclaimer executed within 2 years of death, signed by Victoria alone
options.C: A deed of variation executed within 2 years of death, signed by Victoria and James as beneficiary taking the redirected share
options.D: A deed of variation executed within 6 months of death, signed by Victoria alone

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Under IHTA 1984 s.142, a variation of a deceased's estate is treated as if made by the deceased for IHT purposes if: (1) made within 2 years of death; (2) in writing; (3) signed by the person whose entitlement is varied. Victoria as the person redirecting her share need only sign — James as recipient does not need to sign unless he is also varying his own entitlement. Option C incorrectly requires James's signature. Option D has the wrong time limit.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Lady Ashworth died leaving her shares in Ashworth Developments Ltd to Victoria. Victoria executes a deed of variation redirecting the shares to James within 2 years of death. The shares have risen in value since death. What is the CGT position?

options.A: Victoria makes a CGT disposal at market value; James acquires at that value
options.B: The variation is treated as if James had inherited directly from Lady Ashworth; no CGT disposal by Victoria; James acquires at probate value
options.C: Victoria makes no CGT disposal; James acquires at market value at date of variation
options.D: The variation triggers CGT only if the gain exceeds the annual exempt amount

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Where TCGA 1992 s.62(6) election is made (included in the deed of variation), the variation is treated as if the deceased had made it — so no CGT disposal occurs and James is treated as having inherited directly from Lady Ashworth at probate value (the TCGA 1992 s.62 uplift value). Without the election, Victoria would make a disposal at market value. The s.142 IHT election and s.62(6) CGT election are separate; the deed should include both where advantageous.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: James Ashworth inherits £50,000 from Colonel Ashworth's estate but is insolvent. He disclaims the legacy. What is the legal effect?

options.A: The disclaimer is void as it constitutes a transaction at an undervalue
options.B: The disclaimer is effective; the legacy falls into residue and James's creditors cannot claim it
options.C: The disclaimer is effective but James's trustee in bankruptcy can set it aside under s.339 Insolvency Act 1986
options.D: The legacy vests automatically in James's trustee in bankruptcy and cannot be disclaimed

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A disclaimer is legally effective to prevent the legacy vesting in James. However, under IA 1986 s.339, if James's trustee in bankruptcy can show the disclaimer was made within 5 years before bankruptcy and was a transaction at an undervalue (receiving nothing in return), the court may set it aside. The disclaimer does not automatically prevent the trustee from clawing it back. Option D is incorrect — property can be disclaimed before it vests.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Pat Hollowell and Gerald Crane cohabited for 8 years without marrying. Gerald dies intestate. Pat is not a qualifying cohabitant under any current legislation. What is Pat's best avenue for financial provision?

options.A: Pat takes as next of kin under the Administration of Estates Act 1925
options.B: Pat can apply under the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975 as a person who lived in the same household as the deceased as husband or wife for at least 2 years immediately before death
options.C: Pat has no claim as cohabitants have no intestacy rights or statutory claim
options.D: Pat can claim a resulting trust over Gerald's property based on contributions

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Cohabitants have no automatic rights on intestacy under the AEA 1925. However, IPFDA 1975 s.1(1)(ba) allows a person who lived in the same household as the deceased as husband or wife (or civil partner) for the whole of the period of 2 years ending immediately before the death to apply for reasonable financial provision. The standard for such a claimant is the 'surviving spouse' standard (reasonable financial provision, not just maintenance). Pat qualifies having cohabited for 8 years.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Donna Pearce was financially maintained by her employer Frank Hollis before his death. Frank dies intestate leaving everything to his adult son. Donna seeks provision under IPFDA 1975. Which standard applies to Donna's claim?

options.A: The surviving spouse standard — reasonable financial provision
options.B: The maintenance standard — provision for maintenance only
options.C: The adult child standard — assessed on contribution to the deceased's care
options.D: No standard applies; Donna cannot claim as she was not a family member

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Under IPFDA 1975 s.1(1)(e), a person maintained by the deceased immediately before death may apply. The standard for all claimants other than a surviving spouse or civil partner is the 'maintenance standard' — such financial provision as would be reasonable in all the circumstances for the applicant's maintenance. The surviving spouse standard (s.1(2)(a)) applies only to spouses and civil partners. Donna as a maintained dependant can apply but only for maintenance.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Victoria Ashworth makes a successful claim under IPFDA 1975 against Colonel Ashworth's estate. The net estate is insufficient. The court wishes to consider Colonel Ashworth's severable share of a joint tenancy property. What doctrine applies?

options.A: The court can sever the joint tenancy retrospectively under IPFDA 1975
options.B: Under IPFDA 1975 s.9, the court can treat the deceased's severable share of joint property as part of the net estate if the other joint tenant is a party to the proceedings
options.C: Joint tenancy property cannot be included in IPFDA 1975 claims as it passes by survivorship outside the estate
options.D: The court can include joint property only if the deceased severed the tenancy before death

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IPFDA 1975 s.9 gives the court power to treat the deceased's severable share of jointly held property as part of the net estate. The court must order the severance and the other joint tenant must be joined as a party to the proceedings. This prevents a deceased from defeating IPFDA claims by holding assets as joint tenant. The power is discretionary and the other joint tenant's interests are considered.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Hassan Nazari transfers his business to his son for full consideration, then separately lends his son money interest-free to enable the purchase. HMRC argues these are associated operations. Under which provision might HMRC treat the transactions as a single transfer of value?

options.A: IHTA 1984 s.268 — associated operations
options.B: IHTA 1984 s.103 — limitation on business relief
options.C: FA 1986 s.102 — gifts with reservation
options.D: IHTA 1984 s.10 — disposition not intended to confer gratuitous benefit

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IHTA 1984 s.268 defines 'associated operations' as two or more operations affecting the same property or one of which is effected with reference to the other. HMRC can link separately occurring transactions to treat them as a single transfer of value, preventing artificial fragmentation of transfers. The interest-free loan enabling the purchase could be associated with the transfer. S.10 (option D) actually provides a defence — a disposition at full commercial consideration is not a transfer of value.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth and Lady Ashworth made wills in identical terms leaving everything to each other and then to Victoria, with an agreement not to revoke. Colonel Ashworth dies. Lady Ashworth subsequently makes a new will leaving everything to James. What is the legal position?

options.A: Lady Ashworth's new will is valid and takes effect as she had capacity
options.B: Lady Ashworth can revoke as the doctrine of mutual wills does not bind survivors
options.C: A constructive trust arises on Colonel Ashworth's death binding Lady Ashworth's estate to the agreed terms; the new will is valid but equity will enforce the mutual wills agreement
options.D: The new will is void for breach of contract

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The mutual wills doctrine (from Dufour v Perreau, confirmed in Re Goodchild) holds that when testators make wills pursuant to a binding agreement not to revoke, on the first death a constructive trust arises over the survivor's estate binding it to the agreed terms. Lady Ashworth's new will is technically valid (she has testamentary capacity and the revocation power is not removed), but equity will enforce the constructive trust — Victoria can claim the estate as beneficial owner. The agreement must be contractually binding (not merely a moral arrangement).

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's will leaves £100,000 to 'my executor Gerald Crane upon trusts already communicated to him'. The communication occurred after the will was executed. Gerald claims he holds absolutely. Is the half-secret trust valid?

options.A: Yes — communication after execution is sufficient for a half-secret trust
options.B: No — for a half-secret trust, communication must occur before or at the time of execution of the will
options.C: Yes — secret trust rules permit communication at any time before the testator's death
options.D: No — a half-secret trust requires attestation by the secret beneficiary

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: For a half-secret trust (where the will reveals the existence of a trust but not the terms), communication of the terms to the trustee must occur before or at the time of execution of the will: Re Keen [1937]. If communication occurs after execution, the half-secret trust fails. This differs from fully secret trusts, where communication may occur at any time before death. Gerald therefore holds on resulting trust for Colonel Ashworth's estate, not absolutely.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's will states: 'I give my personal chattels in accordance with the memorandum I have prepared.' The memorandum was written after the will was executed and signed. Can the memorandum be incorporated by reference?

options.A: Yes — any document referred to in a will is incorporated automatically
options.B: No — for incorporation by reference, the document must be in existence at the date the will is executed
options.C: Yes — provided the memorandum is signed by the testator
options.D: No — personal chattels cannot be distributed by memorandum

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The doctrine of incorporation by reference (confirmed in University College of North Wales v Taylor [1908]) requires that the document to be incorporated: (1) is in existence at the date of execution of the will; (2) is clearly identified in the will; and (3) is referred to as already existing. Since the memorandum was written after execution, it cannot be incorporated. The chattels would fall into residue or pass on intestacy unless a codicil re-executes the will after the memorandum was written.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's will was prepared by Crane & Pelletier. Due to a drafting error, it omits a legacy of £20,000 to Hassan Nazari that Colonel Ashworth had instructed. Hassan applies to rectify the will. Under which provision and within what time limit?

options.A: Administration of Justice Act 1982 s.20 — application within 6 months of the grant
options.B: Administration of Justice Act 1982 s.20 — application within 12 months of the grant
options.C: Wills Act 1837 s.9 — application within 2 years of death
options.D: Senior Courts Act 1981 s.49 — no time limit

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: AJA 1982 s.20 allows the court to rectify a will which fails to carry out the testator's intentions in consequence of a clerical error or a failure to understand the testator's instructions. The application must be made within 6 months of the date on which representation is first taken out (i.e. the grant of probate or administration), though the court has discretion to extend. As the omission arose from Crane & Pelletier's drafting error failing to follow instructions, s.20 applies.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's will leaves 'all my horses, vehicles, boats and other personal chattels'. A dispute arises whether his £500,000 share portfolio is included. What principle of construction applies?

options.A: The ejusdem generis rule — 'other personal chattels' is limited to items of the same kind as the listed items
options.B: The portfolio is included as a personal chattel under AJA 1982 s.55(1)(x)
options.C: The contra proferentem rule applies to include all assets in the gift
options.D: The portfolio passes as a specific legacy separately from personal chattels

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The ejusdem generis rule provides that where general words follow a list of specific items, the general words are confined to items of the same class or genus as those specifically listed. Horses, vehicles, and boats are tangible physical items of personal use — the general words 'other personal chattels' would be confined to similar tangible items. A share portfolio is a chose in action, not of the same genus. Note also that under AJA 1982 s.55(1)(x) (the statutory definition for intestacy), money and investments are excluded from personal chattels — applying by analogy here.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's will gives £300,000 to 'such of my grandchildren as attain 25'. At the date of his death, he has two grandchildren aged 30 and 28, and Victoria is pregnant. When does the class close?

options.A: The class closes at death — only grandchildren alive at death can benefit
options.B: The class closes when the first member becomes entitled — i.e. immediately as both grandchildren over 25 are entitled at death
options.C: The class remains open until Victoria's child (if born) attains 25
options.D: The class closes when all grandchildren alive at death have either attained 25 or died

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Under the rule in Andrews v Partington (1791), a class gift closes to new members when the first member becomes entitled to claim their share. Where the condition is attaining a specified age, and at the testator's death there are already members who have satisfied the condition (both grandchildren are over 25), the class closes at death. Victoria's unborn child is therefore excluded. This rule of construction prevents the class from remaining open indefinitely and allows earlier distribution.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's will leaves his residuary estate equally between his children Victoria and James. Before his death, Colonel Ashworth gave James £100,000 inter vivos with a direction to bring it into hotchpot. The residue is £500,000. How is the estate distributed?

options.A: Victoria receives £250,000; James receives £250,000
options.B: Victoria receives £300,000; James receives £200,000
options.C: Victoria receives £300,000; James receives £200,000 (but notionally James's total = £300,000)
options.D: James receives nothing as he has already received his share

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Hotchpot requires a beneficiary to bring inter vivos gifts into account when calculating their share. The notional estate is £500,000 (residue) + £100,000 (James's gift) = £600,000. Each child's share = £300,000. James has already received £100,000, so he receives £200,000 from residue. Victoria receives £300,000. James's total = £300,000 (same as Victoria). The hotchpot clause ensures equality between the children. Option A ignores hotchpot; Option D incorrectly disinherits James entirely.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Victoria Ashworth is the sole personal representative of Colonel Ashworth's estate. She wishes to appropriate the family home in satisfaction of her own legacy. What is required?

options.A: Victoria can appropriate to herself without consent as sole PR and sole beneficiary
options.B: Victoria requires the consent of the other beneficiaries or the court as she cannot consent to her own appropriation as PR
options.C: Victoria can appropriate with her own consent in writing as both PR and beneficiary
options.D: Appropriation to a personal representative is prohibited by s.41 AEA 1925

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: AEA 1925 s.41 grants PRs power to appropriate assets in satisfaction of a beneficiary's entitlement with the consent of the beneficiary (or their guardian/attorney). However, where the PR is also the beneficiary, they cannot give consent to their own appropriation — this would be a conflict of interest. Court approval is required, or the other beneficiaries must consent. This rule prevents a PR from using the appropriation power to benefit themselves without independent oversight.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Gerald Crane is executor of Colonel Ashworth's estate during the administration period. He wishes to invest estate assets in a speculative cryptocurrency while awaiting distribution. Which is correct?

options.A: Gerald can invest in any asset during administration as PRs have absolute ownership
options.B: During administration, PRs hold as trustees and are subject to TA 2000 investment duties including the standard investment criteria
options.C: PRs are not trustees and have no investment duties — only distribution duties
options.D: Gerald can invest speculatively if the will authorises it

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Although PRs hold legal title to the estate during administration, they do so in a fiduciary capacity and are subject to the Trustee Act 2000 investment provisions (Part II) during the administration period. They must apply the standard investment criteria (s.4 TA 2000): suitability of the investment and need for diversification. Speculative cryptocurrency investment would likely breach these duties. The TA 2000 applies to PRs to the extent they have investment functions during administration.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Gerald Crane as executor pays a debt of Colonel Ashworth's estate that was statute-barred. He does not take a s.27 Trustee Act 1925 advertisement. A creditor with a valid claim subsequently appears. What is Gerald's personal liability?

options.A: Gerald is personally liable for the unpaid creditor's claim as he paid a void debt and failed to advertise
options.B: Gerald has full protection from the statute-barred payment and is not liable if he distributes assets after a proper s.27 advertisement
options.C: Gerald is liable for paying the statute-barred debt but has no further liability as the assets have been distributed
options.D: Gerald must refund the statute-barred payment but is protected from later creditor claims regardless of advertisement

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A PR who pays a statute-barred debt voluntarily may do so, but this may constitute a devastavit (breach of duty) if other creditors go unpaid. Crucially, a PR who distributes the estate without taking s.27 TA 1925 advertisements loses the protection that section provides against unknown creditor claims. Gerald is personally liable to known and unknown creditors whose claims he has not satisfied. The paid statute-barred debt reduces the estate available to pay valid creditors, compounding his personal exposure.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Gerald Crane wishes to obtain a clearance certificate from HMRC before distributing Colonel Ashworth's estate. Under which provision is this available and what does it certify?

options.A: IHTA 1984 s.239 — certifies that no IHT is outstanding on the property specified in the certificate
options.B: IHTA 1984 s.256 — certifies that all IHT on the estate has been paid
options.C: TMA 1970 s.12 — certifies that income tax is satisfied
options.D: AEA 1925 s.27 — certifies that all debts have been discharged

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IHTA 1984 s.239 allows a person liable to pay IHT to apply for a certificate of discharge. The certificate is property-specific — it certifies that HMRC are satisfied that the IHT attributable to the property specified has been or will be paid. It does not give blanket clearance for the whole estate. Once issued, the property is discharged from HMRC's charge (s.237 charge). Gerald should obtain section 239 certificates for specific assets before distributing.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's estate includes agricultural land valued at £1.2 million. The executors cannot realise sufficient cash to pay all the IHT on the land immediately. What instalment option is available?

options.A: IHT on qualifying instalment property can be paid in 10 equal annual instalments beginning 6 months after death
options.B: IHT on land can be paid in 5 equal annual instalments with interest accruing from the date of death
options.C: IHT on agricultural land must be paid in full within 6 months of death with no instalment option
options.D: IHT on qualifying property can be deferred for up to 2 years pending sale

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IHTA 1984 s.227 provides that IHT attributable to qualifying 'instalment property' (which includes land, unquoted shares, and business property) may be paid in 10 equal annual instalments. The first instalment is due 6 months after the end of the month of death (the normal IHT payment date). Interest accrues on unpaid instalments on business or agricultural property only if the instalment is late. If the property is sold, the outstanding IHT becomes immediately payable.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Following Colonel Ashworth's death, his family wish to vary the intestacy distribution so that Lady Ashworth receives a life interest in the estate rather than an outright legacy. What formalities must the arrangement comply with to achieve both IHT and CGT read-back?

options.A: The variation must be in writing, made within 2 years of death, contain a statement that IHTA 1984 s.142 applies, and if CGT read-back is wanted, also elect under TCGA 1992 s.62(6)
options.B: The variation must be in writing, made within 6 months of death, and approved by the court
options.C: The variation must be a deed, made within 2 years, and all beneficiaries must receive independent legal advice
options.D: The variation must be in writing, made within 2 years, and all beneficiaries whose interests are reduced must consent

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: For IHTA 1984 s.142 IHT read-back: the variation must be in writing, made within 2 years of death, and the instrument must contain a statement that s.142 is intended to apply (from Finance Act 2002 amendment). For TCGA 1992 s.62(6) CGT read-back: a separate election must be included. All beneficiaries whose entitlements are being reduced must consent. Option D identifies the consent requirement correctly but misses the s.142 statement. Option A is the most complete correct answer.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's will disposes of his investment portfolio and cash but is silent on his classic car collection. He leaves a wife (Lady Ashworth) and a son (James). How does the car collection pass?

options.A: The car collection passes to James as next of kin
options.B: The car collection passes under partial intestacy; Lady Ashworth takes the personal chattels absolutely under AEA 1925 s.46
options.C: The car collection falls into residue under the will
options.D: The car collection is distributed equally between Lady Ashworth and James

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Where a will does not dispose of all assets, partial intestacy arises and the intestacy rules apply to the undisposed-of property. Under AEA 1925 s.46, where the deceased leaves a spouse and issue, the spouse takes personal chattels absolutely (s.46(1)(i)). Classic cars are personal chattels within the statutory definition (AJA 1982 s.55(1)(x)). Lady Ashworth therefore takes the car collection absolutely under the partial intestacy rules, regardless of the will's other provisions.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth (UK domiciled) leaves £500,000 to his wife who is domiciled in France. What is the IHT position on the spouse exemption?

options.A: Full spouse exemption applies as marriage confers domicile of dependency
options.B: The spouse exemption is capped at the NRB (£325,000) for transfers to a non-UK-domiciled spouse
options.C: No spouse exemption applies as the recipient is not UK-domiciled
options.D: The spouse exemption is limited to £325,000 unless she elects to be treated as UK-domiciled for IHT purposes

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Under IHTA 1984 s.18, the spouse exemption is unlimited for transfers between UK-domiciled spouses. However, where the transferee spouse is not UK-domiciled, IHTA 1984 s.18(2) limits the exemption. Under FA 2013, the limit was increased from £55,000 to match the NRB (£325,000). However, s.267ZA (inserted by FA 2013) allows a non-domiciled spouse to elect to be treated as UK-domiciled for IHT purposes, which would restore the full exemption. Option D captures both the cap and the election remedy.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Lady Ashworth received £200,000 from Colonel Ashworth's estate (on which IHT was paid) and dies 18 months later leaving the same assets. IHT is payable on her estate. What relief is available?

options.A: No relief — IHT is charged in full on each successive death
options.B: Quick succession relief under IHTA 1984 s.141 reduces the IHT on Lady Ashworth's estate by a percentage of the IHT paid on Colonel Ashworth's transfer
options.C: Double taxation relief reduces the IHT to nil as the same assets have been taxed twice
options.D: Taper relief under IHTA 1984 s.7 reduces the rate of IHT payable

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: IHTA 1984 s.141 provides quick succession relief (QSR) where a person (Lady Ashworth) receives property from another person (Colonel Ashworth) and IHT was charged on that transfer, and Lady Ashworth then dies within 5 years. The relief is a percentage of the IHT previously paid: 100% (within 1 year), 80% (1-2 years), 60% (2-3 years), 40% (3-4 years), 20% (4-5 years). At 18 months, Lady Ashworth's estate gets 80% credit. The relief reduces (not eliminates) the IHT on her estate.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Gerald Crane as executor cannot locate a beneficiary named in Colonel Ashworth's will after exhaustive searches. He wishes to distribute the estate. What order should he seek and what is its effect?

options.A: A Bona Vacantia order — directing the legacy to the Crown
options.B: A Benjamin order — directing distribution on the basis that the missing person has predeceased; protects the executor from personal liability if the beneficiary later appears
options.C: A Tomlin order — staying proceedings pending the beneficiary's appearance
options.D: A freezing order — preserving assets until the beneficiary is found

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A Benjamin order (from Re Benjamin [1902]) is a court order that permits an executor to distribute on a stated assumption — usually that a missing beneficiary has predeceased — without waiting indefinitely. It protects the executor from personal liability if the assumption proves wrong and the beneficiary later appears. The beneficiary's rights against the other recipients (who received the share) are not extinguished — they may have a personal claim. The order does not resolve beneficial ownership; it authorises distribution.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth (UK domiciled) dies leaving a bank account in France. Which law governs the succession to the bank account?

options.A: French law as lex situs — the law of the place where the asset is situated
options.B: English law as the law of the domicile — movables are governed by the law of the domicile
options.C: EU Succession Regulation (Brussels IV) — habitual residence governs
options.D: French law as lex fori — the law of the forum of the dispute

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: In English private international law, succession to movable property (including bank accounts, which are choses in action) is governed by the law of the deceased's domicile at death — the lex domicilii. Succession to immovable property is governed by the lex situs (the law of the place where the land is situated). Colonel Ashworth is domiciled in England, so English law governs the succession to his French bank account. Note: the EU Succession Regulation (Brussels IV) applies in EU member states but not in the UK post-Brexit.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth's will was read to him by his solicitor Gerald Crane in the presence of Victoria. James challenges the will arguing Colonel Ashworth did not know and approve the contents. On whom does the burden of proof lie and is it discharged by due execution?

options.A: The burden lies on James to prove lack of knowledge and approval; due execution raises no presumption
options.B: The burden lies on those propounding the will (Gerald/Victoria) to prove knowledge and approval; due execution gives rise to a rebuttable presumption that the testator knew and approved the contents
options.C: Knowledge and approval is presumed conclusively from due execution; James cannot challenge
options.D: The burden lies on both parties equally under the evidential burden

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Knowledge and approval is a requirement for valid testamentary execution separate from capacity and intention. The burden lies on those propounding the will to prove it. However, where a will is duly executed and the testator had testamentary capacity, a rebuttable presumption arises that the testator knew and approved the contents: Barry v Butlin (1838). The presumption can be rebutted by suspicious circumstances (e.g. the will was prepared by a substantial beneficiary). Due execution raises the presumption but does not conclusively prove knowledge and approval.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Gerald Crane as executor wrongly pays Victoria's legacy of £50,000 to James by mistake. James spends £20,000 on a holiday and retains £30,000 in his bank account. What can Victoria recover and from whom?

options.A: Victoria can only recover from Gerald personally as the wrongdoing executor; James has a good defence as a bona fide recipient
options.B: Victoria can recover £30,000 from James by equitable tracing into the bank account; she has a personal claim for £50,000 against Gerald; James has a change of position defence for the £20,000 spent on the holiday
options.C: Victoria can recover the full £50,000 from James as the estate assets can be traced
options.D: Victoria has no claim against James as he received money, which is not traceable

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Equitable tracing allows Victoria to follow the misapplied assets into James's hands. The £30,000 remaining in his bank account is traceable. James cannot claim as bona fide purchaser for value as he gave no consideration. For the £20,000 spent on the holiday, James has a change of position defence (Lipkin Gorman v Karpnale [1991]) — he spent the money in good faith changing his position. Victoria also has a personal liability claim against Gerald as a wrongdoing executor who misapplied estate assets (devastavit).

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Colonel Ashworth tells his solicitor Gerald Crane to destroy his will. Gerald tears up a copy of the will, not knowing that Colonel Ashworth has the original. The original survives. What is the effect on revocation?

options.A: The will is revoked as Colonel Ashworth intended destruction
options.B: The will is not revoked — physical destruction of the will itself (or burning, tearing, or obliterating) must be done by the testator or in his presence and by his direction
options.C: The will is revoked by Gerald's act as agent with authority
options.D: The will is revoked provided Gerald intended to destroy it

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Wills Act 1837 s.20 requires that for revocation by destruction, the will must be burnt, torn, or otherwise destroyed by the testator himself or by someone in his presence and by his direction. The destruction of a copy (not the original) cannot revoke the will. Gerald was not in Colonel Ashworth's presence, and the original was not destroyed. The original will therefore remains valid. This illustrates the formality requirements for revocation by destruction.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Gerald Crane of Crane & Pelletier is acting as solicitor for Victoria as sole executor of Colonel Ashworth's estate. After the grant, Victoria asks Gerald to distribute the estate before paying HMRC a known IHT liability. What should Gerald do?

options.A: Follow Victoria's instructions as the client — she is the executor and Gerald must act on her authority
options.B: Advise Victoria that distributing before settling the HMRC liability would constitute a devastavit and expose her to personal liability; decline to facilitate the unlawful distribution
options.C: Seek HMRC's consent before advising Victoria
options.D: Distribute as instructed but note the risk in writing to Victoria

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A solicitor cannot act on client instructions that involve assisting a client in conduct that is unlawful: SRA Code of Conduct, Paragraph 1.4 (not acting if instructions require the firm to act in breach of law). Distributing the estate before satisfying HMRC's IHT liability is a devastavit (breach of duty by executor) and potentially involves the solicitor in facilitating unlawful conduct. Gerald must advise Victoria of the legal position and decline to carry out the instruction. If Victoria persists, Gerald should consider whether to continue acting.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills and the Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.subtopic: Professional Conduct — Conflicts in Estate Administration
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Crane & Pelletier acts for both Victoria (executor) and James (residuary beneficiary) in Colonel Ashworth's estate administration. A dispute arises about whether a specific asset should be treated as part of residue. What must Crane & Pelletier do?

options.A: Continue acting for both clients, sharing all information between them
options.B: Continue acting for both but erect information barriers between fee earners
options.C: Cease acting for at least one client as there is a conflict of interest that cannot be managed; both clients should seek independent advice on the disputed issue
options.D: Seek mediation and continue to act for both pending resolution

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: SRA Code of Conduct paragraph 6.2 prohibits acting for two or more clients where there is a conflict of interest (a situation where the firm's separate duties to act in the best interests of each client conflict). Victoria as executor has duties to maximise the estate; James as beneficiary has an interest in what is included in residue. On a disputed classification question, their interests are directly opposed. There is a genuine conflict — not merely a 'substantially common interest' situation. Crane & Pelletier must cease acting for at least one client and advise both to take independent legal advice.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: What is the primary test used by English courts to determine whether a settlor has the necessary certainty of intention to create a trust?

options.A: An objective test: whether the settlor's words or conduct demonstrate an unequivocal intention to create a binding obligation on the trustee, regardless of the settlor's subjective state of mind
options.B: A subjective test: whether the settlor consciously and deliberately stated the word 'trust'
options.C: A strict formality test: whether the settlor complied with all documentary requirements set out in the Law of Property Act 1925
options.D: A commercial reasonableness test: whether an ordinary prudent person would consider the settlor intended to create a trust

Trusts.explanation: Per Knight v Knight (1840) and confirmed in Paul v Constance [1977], the test is objective.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: In Re London Wine Co (Storage) Ltd, why did the court hold that the beneficiary could not trace into a bulk store of wine held by the company?

options.A: Because there was uncertainty as to which specific bottles belonged to the beneficiary when they were not separated from the general bulk
options.B: Because the wine was stored as a commercial product rather than as trust property with formal documentation
options.C: Because the beneficiary failed to register a charge over the property
options.D: Because the trustee company was insolvent

Trusts.explanation: Re London Wine Co [1986] established that certainty of subject matter requires that the trust property be clearly ascertained.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A settlor declares a fixed trust over shares in favour of 'the children of my sister.' At the time of the declaration, the sister has three children, but may have more in future. Which test governs whether the trust is valid?

options.A: The 'is or is not' test from McPhail v Doulton
options.B: The complete list test from IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust, requiring the trustee to be able to make a complete list of all beneficiaries at the moment the trust is declared
options.C: The commercial purpose test
options.D: The reasonable exercise test

Trusts.explanation: Fixed trusts must satisfy the complete list test (IRC v Broadway Cottages [1955]).

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A settlor writes a letter stating 'I hereby hold all my shares in ABC plc on trust for my nephew.' The settlor does not deliver the shares, execute any deed, or deliver the letter. Two weeks later, the settlor revokes the letter. Which principle determines the effectiveness of the trust declaration?

options.A: The trust fails because the settlor did not execute a formal deed of trust
options.B: The trust is ineffective because the settlor has not done everything within their power
options.C: The trust is valid as a declaration of self as trustee; no further conduct is necessary once the words of trust are recorded in writing
options.D: The trust fails because the settlor later revoked the document

Trusts.explanation: In Milroy v Lord (1862), the court held that a trust can be constituted by declaration of self as trustee.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A settlor orally declares a trust of land for the benefit of their child, without any written evidence. The child relies on the declaration and takes possession of the land. Is the trust enforceable?

options.A: No, because section 53(1)(b) of the Law of Property Act 1925 requires a declaration of trust in land to be evidenced in writing, and this requirement cannot be waived even by part performance or reliance
options.B: Yes, because the child's part performance and reliance on the declaration satisfy the equity
options.C: No, because oral trusts of land are prohibited at common law
options.D: Yes, if the oral declaration was made as a solemn declaration in front of two independent witnesses

Trusts.explanation: Section 53(1)(b) LPA 1925 is absolute: a declaration of trust in land must be manifested and proved by some writing.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A settlor has executed the share transfer form and delivered it to the company secretary. The company secretary delays registration for two weeks. Is the trust constituted?

options.A: No, because the legal title remains in the settlor until the company registers the transfer
options.B: No, because the delay in registration means the settlor has not complied with the requirements for constitution
options.C: Yes, under the principle in Re Rose [1952] that the settlor has done everything within their power
options.D: Yes, but only if the company eventually registers the transfer within a reasonable period, making the trust effective from the date of registration, not the date of execution

Trusts.explanation: Re Rose [1952] established that equity will assume a transfer is perfected once the settlor has done everything in their power. However, the trust becomes constituted only upon registration.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: In McPhail v Doulton [1971], the House of Lords held that a discretionary trust is valid if the trustee can determine what about any given person?

options.A: Is this person one of the few beneficiaries who will definitely receive a distribution from the fund?
options.B: Is or is not this person a member of the class of beneficiaries described in the trust deed?
options.C: Could this person reasonably expect to receive income or capital in the sole discretion of the trustee?
options.D: Is this person a beneficiary who the settlor clearly intended to favour?

Trusts.explanation: McPhail v Doulton [1971] established the 'is or is not' test for discretionary trusts. Option C captures this practical test.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A settlor executes a share transfer form and delivers it to a friend but makes clear the settlor will later deliver it to the company for registration. The settlor then dies before registration. Which principle applies?

options.A: The transfer is ineffective because the settlor did not personally deliver it to the company
options.B: The transfer is ineffective because the settlor retained control by indicating the settlor would later deliver it
options.C: The transfer is effective under Pennington v Waine [2002] because the friend's acceptance makes it unconscionable to deny the transfer effect
options.D: The transfer can only be effective if the company later registers it

Trusts.explanation: Pennington v Waine [2002] does not permit a one-sided, incomplete transfer where the settlor retains control. The settlor's statement indicates retention of dominion.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A trust deed states the purpose is 'to relieve poverty amongst retired teachers and their families.' Under the Charities Act 2011 section 3, is this charitable?

options.A: No, because poverty relief for a defined occupational group cannot qualify
