Trusts.stem: Gerald Crane transfers shares to Northgate Couriers Ltd as trustee 'to hold on trust for purposes to be declared'. No valid trust is subsequently declared. Who holds the beneficial interest?

options.A: Northgate Couriers Ltd holds beneficially as the trust was never constituted
options.B: Gerald Crane retains the beneficial interest under an automatic resulting trust as no valid trust was declared
options.C: The beneficial interest passes to HMRC
options.D: Northgate holds on a purpose trust for Gerald's general charitable objects

Trusts.explanation: In Vandervell v IRC [1967], the House of Lords held that where a settlor transfers property to a trustee but fails to effectively dispose of the equitable interest (because the trust declared is void or the declared trust fails), the equitable interest results back to the settlor automatically. The critical principle (from Lord Wilberforce) is that equity does not permit an equitable interest to be in suspense or to have no owner — it must reside somewhere. Since no valid trust was declared, the equitable interest results back to Gerald. This is an automatic resulting trust by operation of law.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: Colonel Ashworth (UK domiciled) creates a Cayman Islands trust to hold French property, seeking to avoid French forced heirship rules for Victoria and James. What is the likely outcome under English private international law?

options.A: The trust is effective — English law permits settlors to choose governing law freely
options.B: The French forced heirship rules apply to the French immovable property under the lex situs principle; the trust cannot override them
options.C: The trust is governed by Cayman law as chosen by the settlor; French law is irrelevant
options.D: UK law alone governs as Colonel Ashworth is UK domiciled

Trusts.explanation: In English private international law, succession to immovable property is governed by the lex situs — the law of the place where the land is situated (French law for French property). French law includes forced heirship (réserve héréditaire) which entitles children to a fixed share of the estate. Inserting a trust layer does not circumvent this — French courts will look through the trust structure and apply the mandatory provisions of French law to the French immovable property. The EU Succession Regulation (Article 35 — ordre public) also protects the forced heirship rules for EU states.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: Gerald Crane committed a breach of trust 7 years ago. The beneficiary Victoria discovers it only now. Is her claim time-barred?

options.A: Yes — the 6-year limitation period under LA 1980 s.21(3) has expired
options.B: No — there is no limitation period for breach of trust against a trustee who was fraudulent
options.C: The 6-year period applies but runs from when Victoria discovered (or ought reasonably to have discovered) the breach
options.D: No — breach of trust claims have a 12-year limitation period

Trusts.explanation: Under LA 1980 s.21(3), a 6-year limitation period applies to most breach of trust claims — running from the date of the breach. On the facts, the breach was 7 years ago and the period has expired. However, s.21(1) provides that there is NO limitation period for: (a) fraud by the trustee; or (b) recovery of trust property or its proceeds still retained by the trustee or converted to the trustee's use. On the facts, no fraud or retention is indicated — the basic 6-year period applies and Gerald's defence will succeed. Option C incorrectly applies the knowledge-based discovery rule (which applies to fraud under s.32 LA 1980).

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: Rachel Okonkwo as a trustee receives a secret bribe of £50,000 from a third party as an inducement to favour their investment proposal for the trust. She deposits the money in her personal account where it earns £5,000 interest. What is the trust's claim?

options.A: A personal claim for £50,000 as the bribe
options.B: A proprietary claim over the bribe and any profits made from it, as Rachel holds it on constructive trust
options.C: No claim — bribes cannot be recovered once mixed with personal funds
options.D: A personal claim for £55,000 (bribe plus interest)

Trusts.explanation: In Attorney General for Hong Kong v Reid [1994] (PC) and confirmed for England in FHR European Ventures LLP v Cedar Capital Partners LLC [2014] (SC), a fiduciary who receives a bribe or secret commission holds it on constructive trust for the principal. The proprietary claim (not merely personal) means the trust can claim the £50,000 bribe and any profits made from it (the £5,000 interest), and can trace the money into substitute assets. The proprietary nature of the claim is crucial if Rachel is insolvent — the trust takes priority over unsecured creditors.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.subtopic: Duty of Impartiality — Life Tenant and Remainderman
Trusts.stem: The Ashworth Family Trust gives James a life interest with remainder to Victoria. The trust holds rental property generating high income. Victoria argues the property should be sold and reinvested for capital growth. How should the trustees approach this conflict?

options.A: James's income interest takes priority as current beneficiary
options.B: Trustees must act impartially between the life tenant (James) and remainderman (Victoria), balancing income and capital returns
options.C: Victoria's remainder interest takes priority to protect long-term trust value
options.D: The trustees must follow the court's direction and cannot decide independently

Trusts.explanation: Trustees owe a duty of impartiality (or even-handedness) between beneficiaries with different interests — particularly the life tenant (who benefits from income) and the remainderman (who benefits from capital growth): Nestlé v National Westminster Bank plc [1993]. The trustees must balance the interests fairly — not sacrifice the remainderman's capital position for income, nor deprive the life tenant of income for capital growth. The 'total return' approach under TA 2000 allows trustees to adopt investment strategies that balance both interests and make appropriate allocations.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: Diana Pelletier and Ravi Sharma are beneficial co-owners of a house held on trust. They cannot agree whether to sell. Which court has jurisdiction to order a sale and what factors does it consider?

options.A: The Chancery Division under TOLATA 1996 s.14; it considers the purposes of the trust, welfare of minors, and interests of secured creditors
options.B: The Family Court under MCA 1973 — the property must be dealt with as matrimonial property
options.C: The County Court has exclusive jurisdiction over co-ownership disputes
options.D: The Chancery Division under LPA 1925 s.30 — the old statutory regime

Trusts.explanation: TOLATA 1996 s.14 gives any person with an interest in property subject to a trust of land the right to apply to court for an order relating to the exercise of the trustees' functions. Under s.15, the court must consider: the intentions of the settlor (or person who created the trust); the purposes for which the property is held; the welfare of any minor who occupies; and the interests of secured creditors. LPA 1925 s.30 was the old regime (now replaced by TOLATA). Where the parties are in a relationship, the court may also consider cohabitation legislation.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: James Ashworth, a beneficiary, was told about a proposed breach of trust by trustee Victoria and raised no objection. The breach occurred and caused a loss. Can Victoria rely on James's acquiescence as a defence?

options.A: No — acquiescence is never a defence to breach of trust
options.B: Yes — if James had full knowledge of the facts constituting the breach and acquiesced with full knowledge, equity bars him from bringing a claim
options.C: Yes — any failure to object within a reasonable time bars a claim
options.D: No — only written consent by all beneficiaries can bar a breach of trust claim

Trusts.explanation: A beneficiary who with full knowledge of the facts consents to, concurs in, or acquiesces in a breach of trust cannot subsequently sue the trustee for that breach: Re Pauling's Settlement Trusts [1964]. The defence requires: (1) the beneficiary had full knowledge of all material facts; (2) the beneficiary was of full age and sound mind; and (3) the beneficiary acquiesced voluntarily without undue influence. Merely failing to object without knowledge, or objecting without result, does not constitute acquiescence. James's failure to object with full knowledge may bar his personal claim.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.subtopic: Constructive Trust — Knowing Receipt vs. Dishonest Assistance
Trusts.stem: Crane & Pelletier receives £200,000 from Gerald Crane (a trustee) to pay legal fees. The firm has constructive notice that the funds are trust money paid in breach of trust. The firm provides legal services worth £200,000. What liability does the firm face?

options.A: Knowing receipt — the firm is liable to account for the full £200,000 received
options.B: Dishonest assistance — the firm is liable for all losses caused by Gerald's breach
options.C: The firm has a change of position defence as it provided services in exchange; knowing receipt liability is discharged
options.D: No liability — professional services rendered in good faith are always protected

Trusts.explanation: Knowing receipt requires: receipt of trust property, in a fiduciary capacity context, with sufficient knowledge that the property was transferred in breach of trust. Even if established, the change of position defence (Lipkin Gorman v Karpnale [1991]) may be available where the recipient has detrimentally changed their position in good faith reliance on receipt. If Crane & Pelletier provided legal services worth £200,000 in good faith (before or without realising the funds were trust money), they have a change of position defence to the extent of the services rendered. The 'constructive notice' element requires further analysis of the specific knowledge.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: Colonel Ashworth creates an express trust for Victoria and James but does not include a power of revocation. He later changes his mind and wishes to revoke it. Can he do so?

options.A: Yes — a settlor can always revoke a trust during their lifetime
options.B: No — once a trust is completely constituted and made without reservation of a power of revocation, it is irrevocable; the beneficiaries' interests are vested
options.C: Yes — provided all beneficiaries consent under Saunders v Vautier
options.D: No — only the court can revoke a trust

Trusts.explanation: Once a trust is completely constituted and the settlor has not reserved a power of revocation, the trust is irrevocable. The beneficiaries' interests have vested and the settlor has parted with the property. The settlor cannot unilaterally revoke: Paul v Paul (1882). Option C (Saunders v Vautier) applies to the beneficiaries terminating the trust — not the settlor revoking it. Victoria and James (as beneficiaries of full age with vested interests) could exercise Saunders v Vautier rights, but that is their choice, not Colonel Ashworth's.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A trust is established 'for charitable and benevolent purposes in Wharton Magna'. Is this a valid charitable trust?

options.A: Yes — benevolent purposes are effectively the same as charitable purposes
options.B: No — the word 'and' requires all purposes to be charitable; 'benevolent' is wider than charitable and may include non-charitable purposes, making the trust void
options.C: Yes — the court will sever the non-charitable purpose
options.D: No — charitable trusts cannot operate at a local level

Trusts.explanation: A charitable trust must be exclusively charitable. 'Benevolent' purposes include purposes that are morally praiseworthy but not legally charitable (e.g. giving to kind neighbours). In IRC v City of Glasgow Police Athletic Association [1953] and Chichester Diocesan Fund v Simpson [1944], the courts held that 'charitable or benevolent' (or 'and') makes the trust non-exclusively charitable — the trustees might apply funds for non-charitable benevolent purposes. The disjunctive (or) clearly fails; the conjunctive (and) is also fatal if benevolent extends beyond charitable. The trust fails as non-charitable.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.subtopic: Solicitor as Trustee — Acting for Trust and Beneficiary
Trusts.stem: Rachel Okonkwo of Crane & Pelletier is a trustee of the Ashworth Family Trust. James (a beneficiary) asks Crane & Pelletier to advise him personally in a dispute with the other trustees about the exercise of a discretion. What should Crane & Pelletier do?

options.A: Act for James — the firm's loyalty is to all beneficiaries equally
options.B: Decline to act for James personally — there is a conflict between the firm's duties to the trust (of which Rachel is trustee) and to James as individual client in litigation against the trust
options.C: Act for James with Rachel recusing herself from the file
options.D: Apply to the court for permission to act for both the trust and James

Trusts.explanation: SRA Code of Conduct paragraphs 6.1 and 6.2 prohibit acting where there is an own-interest conflict or a conflict between two clients. Rachel as trustee owes fiduciary duties to all beneficiaries including acting impartially. Crane & Pelletier acting for James personally in litigation against the trust (of which Rachel is a member) creates an irreconcilable conflict: the firm could not properly act in James's best interests against the trust while Rachel (a partner) has duties to the trust. Even an information barrier would be insufficient where a partner holds the conflicting fiduciary duty. Crane & Pelletier must decline and advise James to seek independent advice.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: Gerald Crane as both trustee and senior partner of Crane & Pelletier causes the trust to instruct the firm at standard commercial rates without any express authorisation in the trust instrument or informed beneficiary consent. What are the consequences?

options.A: The fees are valid — professional trustees are entitled to market-rate remuneration under TA 2000 s.29
options.B: Gerald has made an unauthorised profit as trustee; the trust can recover the fees paid to the firm; Gerald and the firm hold the profits on constructive trust
options.C: The fees are valid provided they represent fair market value
options.D: Gerald must account only for the profit element above his personal costs

Trusts.explanation: The no-profit rule prohibits a trustee from using their position to make an unauthorised profit: Keech v Sandford (1726). Causing trust funds to be paid to the trustee's own firm is a classic unauthorised profit — Gerald profits through the firm's receipt of fees. TA 2000 s.29 (which would authorise remuneration) requires agreement in writing by the other trustees, not mere instruction of the firm. Without authorisation (trust instrument or beneficiary consent under Saunders v Vautier), the fees are unauthorised. The firm holds them on constructive trust for the trust. This also engages SRA rules on conflicts — identical analysis to the conduct question.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Purpose and application of the Rules
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, which of the following best describes the primary duty of a solicitor when holding client money?

options.A: To keep client money separate from office money in a designated client account
options.B: To invest client money to generate returns for the client
options.C: To pay interest on all client money held for more than one month
options.D: To deliver a monthly reconciliation statement to every client

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 2.1 of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 requires solicitors to keep client money separate from office money in a designated client account. This is the foundational duty underpinning all other Rules.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: To which of the following does the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 apply?

options.A: Only to the managing partner of an SRA-authorised firm
options.B: To all SRA-authorised bodies and any person who holds or receives client money in connection with regulated legal services
options.C: To barristers regulated by the Bar Standards Board
options.D: Only to firms with client account balances exceeding £250,000 per annum

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The Rules apply to all SRA-authorised bodies and to any person (including paralegals and non-solicitor staff) who holds or receives client money in connection with regulated legal services.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Which of the following is the correct definition of 'client money' under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?

options.A: Money paid by a client in response to a delivered bill from the firm
options.B: Money held or received by a firm from or on behalf of a client in connection with regulated legal services
options.C: Only money paid by the client directly to the firm; third-party payments are excluded
options.D: Money received as a fixed fee earned in full on receipt

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Client money is money held or received by a firm from or on behalf of a client in connection with regulated legal services. Critically, this includes money received from third parties on behalf of a client.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: What is not client money (office money, out-of-scope)
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm delivers a bill for £2,500 to its client on 1 June. The client pays £2,500 on 3 June in response to that bill. Into which account must the firm pay this money?

options.A: The client account, as it is a payment from the client
options.B: The office account, as it is payment in response to a delivered bill
options.C: The office account initially, then transfer to client account after 14 days
options.D: Either account, as the firm may choose its accounting treatment

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Money paid in response to a delivered bill is office money — it belongs to the firm from the moment of receipt and must be paid into the office account.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A client sends a firm £3,000 'on account of costs.' No bill has been delivered. The firm issues a bill for £1,500 on day 10. How should the firm account for the payment?

options.A: Transfer £3,000 to the office account on day 1
options.B: Hold £3,000 in client account; transfer £1,500 to office account on day 10 after bill is delivered; hold remaining £1,500 in client account as residual balance
options.C: Transfer £3,000 to office account immediately upon receipt of the bill
options.D: Keep £3,000 in client account indefinitely as it is described as 'on account'

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: A payment on account is client money until a bill is delivered. On day 1, the £3,000 is client money and must be held in the client account. On day 10, when a bill for £1,500 is delivered, that amount may be transferred to the office account.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm receives a single cheque for £4,000. It comprises £2,000 for anticipated disbursements and £2,000 as fees against a delivered bill. What is the correct treatment?

options.A: Pay £2,000 to the office account and £2,000 to the client account immediately
options.B: Pay the full £4,000 to the client account, then transfer £2,000 to the office account once the split is confirmed
options.C: Pay the full £4,000 to the office account and transfer £2,000 to client account later
options.D: Hold the full £4,000 in a suspense account until the client clarifies the split

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: This is a mixed receipt. The protective default is to pay the entire mixed receipt into the client account if the split is not immediately clear, then transfer the office element out once identified.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A defendant's liability insurer pays £50,000 damages directly to the firm's client account on behalf of the claimant client, without the client instructing the insurer. Is this client money?

options.A: No, because the payer (the insurer) is not the firm's client
options.B: Yes, because it is money received on behalf of a client in connection with regulated legal services
options.C: No, because it is paid directly to the bank account, not received by the firm in person
options.D: Yes, but only if the client has given prior written consent to receipt

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Money received from a third party on behalf of a client is client money under the Rules, even though the payer is not the firm's client. The key test is whether the money is held or received on behalf of a client in connection with regulated legal services.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A matter concludes and the firm holds £300 in client account that has not yet been returned to the client. What obligation arises?

options.A: The firm must hold the money indefinitely as it may be required in future
options.B: The firm must return the client money promptly, or take steps to locate the client and return it
options.C: The firm may transfer the money to the office account to cover partner drawings
options.D: The firm must pay interest on the balance at the statutory rate

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The SRA Accounts Rules require a firm to return client money promptly once it is no longer needed for the purpose for which it was held. If the matter has concluded and a balance remains, the firm must take active steps to return it without delay.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm holds a residual client balance of £40 for a former client it cannot locate despite reasonable efforts over 18 months. The firm applies to the SRA for permission to pay the balance to charity. Which statement is correct?

options.A: The firm may keep the money as fees owed by the client
options.B: The firm may pay to charity with SRA permission after exhausting tracing steps, provided records are kept
options.C: The firm must pay to the Legal Services Commission without seeking SRA permission
options.D: The firm may pay to charity only if the client is deceased

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Where a residual client balance cannot be returned because the client cannot be located, a firm may apply to the SRA for permission to pay the balance to an SRA-nominated charity, provided all reasonable steps to return the money to the client have been taken.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: What is not client money (office money, out-of-scope)
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A court awards damages to the claimant, and the defendant pays the compensation directly to the claimant's personal bank account (not through the firm). Must the firm account for this money under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?

options.A: Yes, because it relates to a legal matter the firm handled
options.B: No, because the money never passes through the firm's hands and thus was never 'held or received' by it
options.C: Yes, but only if the claimant later asks the firm to manage the funds
options.D: No, because the defendant is not the firm's client

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The SRA Accounts Rules apply only to money 'held or received' by the firm. If money is paid directly to the client and never passes through the firm's accounts, the Rules are not engaged.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm receives £5,000 from a client on 1 June. The money must be paid into the client account. By when must payment be made to satisfy the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?

options.A: Within 7 days of receipt
options.B: Without delay; the money must be in the client account by the end of the business day of receipt or the next working day
options.C: Within 14 days of receipt
options.D: Within 2 weeks of the month following receipt

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 2.1 of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 requires client money to be paid into a client account 'without delay.' The SRA interprets this as meaning payment must be made by the end of the business day of receipt, or the next working day if receipt occurs late in the day.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm receives a cheque for £6,000 made out to 'the firm as client account' for conveyancing disbursements. The cheque bounces 5 days later. What has occurred?

options.A: No breach, as the cheque was not cleared
options.B: A breach of Rule 2.1, as the firm treated cleared money as having been paid into client account when it had not
options.C: A breach of Rule 4.1, as the firm failed to ensure the client account is held at a bank or building society
options.D: No breach if the firm discovers and corrects the error within 14 days

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The firm must ensure money is actually received and cleared in the client account, not merely that a cheque has been presented. If a cheque bounces, the money was never in the client account. This is a breach of Rule 2.1.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm receives £2,000 from a client for anticipated disbursements. What entry is made in the client's ledger account?

options.A: Debit: £2,000 (to reflect money paid in)
options.B: Credit: £2,000 (to reflect money received for the client)
options.C: Debit: £1,000; Credit: £1,000 (to split the entry)
options.D: No entry, as the ledger is only used for fees, not for client money

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: In the client ledger, money received from or on behalf of the client is recorded as a Credit. The client ledger reflects the client's account — from the client's perspective, a credit represents money the firm is holding for them.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm pays £1,500 from the client account to discharge a client's mortgage on their behalf. What entry is made in the client's ledger account?

options.A: Credit: £1,500 (to record the payment)
options.B: Debit: £1,500 (to record money paid out on the client's behalf)
options.C: Credit: £1,500, then Debit: £1,500 (offsetting entries)
options.D: No entry, as the payment was made from the client account, not the ledger

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: When money is paid out from the client account on the client's behalf (a disbursement), the entry in the client ledger is a Debit. Debits in the client ledger represent money paid to or for the client.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Rule 8 of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 requires reconciliation of client account. How frequently must this be done?

options.A: At least once per calendar year
options.B: At least once every 5 weeks
options.C: At least once per calendar month
options.D: At least once every 2 weeks

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 8 requires that the client account be reconciled at least once every 5 weeks. The reconciliation must compare the firm's accounting records with the actual balance held at the bank.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm's reconciliation on 10 June shows the client ledger total as £45,000 and the bank statement balance as £44,500. What should the firm do?

options.A: Accept the variance of £500 as within normal tolerance
options.B: Investigate the discrepancy immediately to identify the cause and correct it
options.C: Report the discrepancy to the SRA within 7 days
options.D: Request the bank to issue a revised statement

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Any discrepancy between the client ledger total and the bank balance must be investigated immediately. Even small variances indicate either an error in the accounting records or a missing transaction.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: When interest must be accounted for
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, when must a firm account to a client for interest earned on money held in the client account?

options.A: Only if the interest exceeds £100
options.B: When interest is received or accrued (subject to de minimis thresholds)
options.C: Only if the client specifically requests it in writing
options.D: At the end of each calendar year in the annual statement

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 5 requires a firm to account to the client for interest received on money held in the client account. A de minimis exemption applies — currently, the SRA guidance indicates that no account need be given for interest below £50.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: When interest must be accounted for
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm holds £25,000 in client account for 4 months. The bank credits interest of £45 to the account. What must the firm do with this interest?

options.A: Retain the interest as office money, as it is below the £50 de minimis threshold
options.B: Account to the client for the interest, as it is money received in connection with client money held
options.C: Pay the interest to the SRA
options.D: Deduct the interest from the client's final bill as a credit

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The firm must account to the client for the interest of £45. Although the SRA de minimis guidance allows no account to be given for interest below £50, the interest of £45 is still client money and should be credited to the client's ledger account.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: When interest must be accounted for
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A client holds a substantial balance (£100,000+) in client account for 12 months. The client and firm agree in writing that the firm may retain half of any interest earned above the statutory rate. Is this permitted?

options.A: No, because Rule 5 does not permit the firm to retain any portion of interest earned on client money
options.B: Yes, as long as it is agreed in writing and the statutory rate is exceeded
options.C: Yes, but only if the client has been given independent legal advice
options.D: No, because interest on client account may only be dealt with by the SRA

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 5 permits a firm to account to the client for a fair and reasonable sum of interest, or such amount as agreed. If a client agrees in writing to allow the firm to retain part of the interest, this is permissible.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Which of the following must be paid into the firm's office account?

options.A: Money received from a client as a payment on account of fees
options.B: The firm's own business money, fees against a delivered bill, and profit costs
options.C: Money received from a third party on behalf of a client for damages
options.D: All client money, which is then transferred to client account

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The office account is used for the firm's own business money, including fees that the firm is entitled to retain (such as profit costs or payment against a delivered bill).

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Transfers between client and office account
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm delivers a bill for £1,200 to a client on 1 June. The client had previously paid £1,500 on account on 15 May (before any bill was delivered). What is the correct accounting treatment on 1 June?

options.A: Transfer the full £1,500 to the office account
options.B: Transfer only £1,200 to the office account (matching the bill amount); hold the £300 surplus in client account
options.C: Hold the full £1,500 in client account until the client requests withdrawal
options.D: Deduct the fee from the payment on account and return the remainder immediately

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The £1,500 payment on 15 May is client money and must be held in the client account. On 1 June, when a bill for £1,200 is delivered, the firm may transfer £1,200 from client account to office account. The remaining £300 is a residual client balance.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm pays counsel's fees of £800 from the client account after the client has paid that amount into the firm. Is this treated as a disbursement from client money?

options.A: No, because counsel's fees are the firm's own costs and must be paid from the office account
options.B: Yes, if the client has advanced the money for that specific purpose, it is a client money disbursement
options.C: Only if counsel is an associate of the firm
options.D: Only if the client requests payment in writing

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: If a client has paid money into the firm for the purpose of paying counsel's fees, and that money remains unspent, it is client money. When the firm pays counsel from the client account, this is a disbursement from client money.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm instructs counsel and agrees to pay the fee of £500. Before the client has paid any money, the firm pays the fee from its office account. Later, the client pays £500 to reimburse the firm's costs. What is the correct treatment?

options.A: Credit client ledger £500 (money received); Debit office account when counsel was paid
options.B: Debit client ledger £500 (cost paid on behalf); Credit client account when payment is received
options.C: Hold the £500 in client account pending counsel payment
options.D: Transfer the £500 immediately to the office account as reimbursement of costs

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: When the firm pays counsel from its own resources before receiving the client's money, this is a business expense. The firm records a cost receivable. When the client pays £500 as reimbursement, this is received as payment of the cost and should go to the office account as office money (or be treated as satisfying the client's liability for costs).

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, when is an annual accountant's report required?

options.A: For all SRA-authorised firms, every year
options.B: If the firm has held an average of more than £10,000 in client money on any one day during the reporting year, or more than £250,000 in aggregate
options.C: Only if the firm has committed a breach of the Rules
options.D: Only for firms with more than 10 partners

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 12 requires an annual accountant's report to be filed if during the reporting year the firm has held client money with an average balance exceeding £10,000 on any one day, or more than £250,000 in aggregate.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm's reporting accountant discovers a breach of the SRA Accounts Rules during their audit — the firm failed to pay client money into a client account for 3 days. What must the accountant do?

options.A: Advise the firm informally and suggest remediation without reporting to the SRA
options.B: Issue an unqualified report stating the firm is compliant
options.C: Issue a qualified report identifying the breach and report it to the SRA
options.D: Issue a qualified report but allow the firm to decide whether to report to the SRA

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: If a reporting accountant discovers a breach of the SRA Accounts Rules, they must issue a qualified report. The accountant must also report the breach to the SRA. Professional reporting obligations override confidentiality.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A small firm with £8,000 average client balance per day does not file an accountant's report. It discovers it made an error 18 months ago — client money was temporarily held in an office account for 5 days before being transferred. Does the firm have an obligation to report this breach?

options.A: No, because the firm is below the reporting threshold and thus not subject to audit
options.B: Yes, any breach of the Rules — including holding office money in client account — must be reported to the SRA by the COFA
options.C: Yes, but only if the client suffered loss
options.D: No, de minimis breaches under £1,000 are not reportable

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The absence of a requirement to file a reporting accountant's report does not exempt a firm from the SRA Accounts Rules themselves. All breaches of the Rules must be reported to the SRA by the firm.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm uses a designated client account at a bank. The account title is 'ABC Law Firm — Client Account.' The firm also holds separate designated accounts for two specific clients with substantial assets, each titled 'ABC Law Firm — Client Account [Client Name].' Are the separate accounts permissible?

options.A: No, the Rules require a single pooled client account for all clients
options.B: Yes, separate designated accounts for individual clients are permissible if properly recorded and reconciled
options.C: No, separate accounts create confusion and are prohibited
options.D: Yes, but only with SRA written permission

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 4.1 permits a firm to operate a single pooled account serving all clients, or to use separate designated accounts for individual clients. The key requirement is that the account must be at a bank or building society and properly designated.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm receives £3,000 from a client on 5 June, pays £800 to counsel on 8 June from the client account, and on 10 June delivers a bill for £1,500. The firm then transfers £1,500 from client account to office account. What is the client ledger balance at the end of 10 June?

options.C: £3,000 (no change until the client pays the bill)
options.D: £1,500 (only the balance on the bill matters)

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The client ledger records: Credit £3,000 (money received on 5 June); Debit £800 (counsel paid on 8 June); Debit £1,500 (amount transferred to office account on 10 June). The net balance is £700.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm's reconciliation on 30 June reveals: Client ledger total £50,000; Client account bank statement balance £49,500. Investigation shows a cheque for £500 was drawn on 25 June but has not yet cleared. After clearing, what will be the reconciliation position?

options.A: £50,000 (bank balance will match the ledger)
options.B: £49,500 (the cheque has been recorded in the ledger but not yet cleared by the bank)
options.C: £50,000 (once the cheque clears, the bank will match the ledger figure recorded on 30 June)
options.D: £49,500 (uncleared cheques are not reconciled to the ledger)

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: On 30 June, the firm correctly recorded the cheque as a debit in the client ledger, but the bank has not yet cleared it. This is a timing difference. Once the cheque clears, the bank balance will become £49,500, matching the accounting records at that point.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm discovers it mistakenly held £600 of office money (deferred fee income) in the client account for 8 days before transferring it to the office account. The fund was adequately secured and no loss occurred. Must the firm report this breach to the SRA?

options.A: No, because no loss occurred and the money was recovered within 14 days
options.B: Yes, any breach of the Rules — including holding office money in client account — must be reported to the SRA by the COFA
options.C: Only if the amount exceeds £1,000
options.D: No, de minimis breaches under £1,000 are not reportable

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Any breach of the SRA Accounts Rules must be reported to the SRA, regardless of whether loss occurred or was promptly remedied. The Rules are strict liability. There is no de minimis exemption for breaches.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier receives £5,000 from James Ashworth as a payment on account of fees before any work is done. Under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, how must this money be treated?

options.A: It can be paid into the firm's office account immediately as it is an advance payment for fees
options.B: It must be paid into the client account as it is client money — not yet earned
options.C: It can be held in either account at the firm's discretion
options.D: It must be held in a separate designated client account in James's name

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Under SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 2.1, client money includes money received from or on behalf of a client in connection with legal services, including payments on account of costs. A payment on account of fees received before the work is done (or before a bill is delivered) is client money and must be held in the client account. The firm cannot transfer it to office account until a bill is delivered or a proper transfer is made under rule 4.3. The obligation to use a designated account only applies where held for a specific purpose exceeding 8 weeks.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Diana Pelletier sends a cheque of £10,000 to Crane & Pelletier for a property purchase. The firm receives the cheque on Monday. What does 'promptly' require under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?

options.A: The cheque must be paid in the same day
options.B: The cheque must be paid in within 2 business days
options.C: The cheque must be paid in the next business day
options.D: Promptly means without undue delay — the SRA does not specify a precise number of days

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 2.3 requires firms to pay client money promptly into a client account (unless a permitted exception applies). The Rules do not define a specific number of days for 'promptly' — the SRA's guidance indicates it means as soon as reasonably practicable, usually the day of receipt or the next working day. In practice, most firms aim to bank on the day of receipt or the next working day. The word 'promptly' is a principle-based standard, not a precise day count.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier receives £250 from Rachel Okonkwo for court fees. The firm wishes to avoid opening a client account for this small sum. Is there an exception permitting this?

options.A: No — all client money must go into the client account without exception
options.B: Yes — rule 2.2(c) permits a firm to make a payment to a third party on behalf of a client without first putting the money in client account, if it is impractical to do so and the amount is small
options.C: Yes — rule 2.2(d) allows firms to hold client money in office account for amounts under £500
options.D: No — only bills rendered can be held in office account

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 2.2 sets out exceptions to the client account obligation. Rule 2.2(c) allows a firm to make a payment to a third party on behalf of a client without routing the money through a client account where it is impractical, for example where the money is received and paid out on the same day (same-day transactions). This is the most applicable exception for small court fee payments received and immediately disbursed. Rule 2.2(d) permits firms to hold client money in office account in certain regulated situations. The key is whether the payment is immediate.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier receives £3,000 from Victoria Ashworth for conveyancing fees (billed). What are the correct ledger entries?

options.A: DR Cash account (office) £3,000; CR Victoria Ashworth office ledger £3,000
options.B: DR Victoria Ashworth client ledger (client side) £3,000; CR Cash account (client) £3,000
options.C: DR Cash account (office) £3,000; CR Office receipts ledger £3,000
options.D: DR Cash account (client) £3,000; CR Victoria Ashworth client ledger (client side) £3,000

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Once a bill is delivered and the client pays the billed amount, the payment is office money (not client money) — the firm has earned the fee. The entries are: DR Cash account (office) £3,000 (the firm's bank account is increased) and CR Victoria Ashworth office ledger £3,000 (reducing the debt owed by Victoria on the office side). This is a standard double-entry recording the receipt of office money. Client account ledger entries are only used for client money held on behalf of the client before billing.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Transfer from Client to Office — Rule 4.3
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier has completed work for Hassan Nazari and delivered a bill for £2,000. Hassan's client ledger shows a balance of £3,000 (client side). What must the firm do to transfer the £2,000 to office account?

options.A: Transfer £2,000 directly — no notification to Hassan is required once a bill is delivered
options.B: Transfer £2,000 under rule 4.3 promptly after delivering the bill; give Hassan notice of the transfer if the bill is not paid
options.C: Obtain Hassan's prior written consent before any transfer from client account
options.D: Apply to the SRA for permission to make the transfer

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 4.3 permits a firm to transfer from client to office account the amount of costs once a bill has been given or a written notification of costs has been provided. The transfer should be made promptly. No prior written consent from the client is required — the right to payment arises on delivery of the bill. However, the firm should give the client clear information about costs and the transfer. The remaining £1,000 stays in client account as it still belongs to Hassan.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier pays a barrister's fee of £1,000 plus VAT (£200) on behalf of James Ashworth. The firm is VAT registered. What is the correct accounting treatment for the VAT?

options.A: The full £1,200 is a disbursement — all paid from client account
options.B: £1,000 is a disbursement from client account; £200 VAT is an office expense
options.C: The firm reclaims the £200 VAT as input tax; £1,000 is charged to the client
options.D: Whether VAT is a disbursement or a firm expense depends on VAT treatment: if the firm is the principal for VAT purposes, the £200 is a firm cost; if the firm acts as agent, the £1,200 is a disbursement

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The VAT treatment of disbursements depends on whether the firm is acting as principal or agent. Where the firm is agent (the expense is the client's direct liability — e.g. court fees, barrister's fees where the client directly instructed), the full amount including VAT is a disbursement paid from client account and passed on to the client as a disbursement on the bill. Where the firm is principal (the expense is the firm's own liability), the VAT is the firm's input tax and only the net amount is billed to the client. Barrister's fees instructed by the firm on behalf of the client are typically treated as disbursements with the full amount from client account.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier is holding £500,000 for Ashworth Developments Ltd in connection with a large property transaction expected to complete in 3 months. Should the firm open a designated client account?

options.A: Yes — a designated account is mandatory for any sum exceeding £50,000
options.B: No — all client money can be held in the general client account
options.C: Yes — a designated client account is required where a client requests it or where the firm considers it necessary to safeguard the client's interests given the amount and duration
options.D: No — designated accounts are only required for sums held for longer than 12 months

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 7 requires firms to consider whether interest should be paid and on what basis. A designated (or separate) client account is one held in the client's name, generating interest specifically for that client. The firm must consider whether a separate account is needed based on the amount held, the length of time it will be held, and the client's interests. £500,000 held for 3 months would typically generate material interest — the firm should open a designated account or pay the client a sum equivalent to interest. There is no fixed monetary threshold in the Rules.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier holds £50,000 for Ravi Sharma in client account for 4 months. The firm pays no interest. What is the position under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?

options.A: The firm has no obligation to pay interest on client money
options.B: The firm must pay interest to Ravi if it is fair and reasonable to do so in all the circumstances — the amount and period are factors in this assessment
options.C: The firm must automatically pay the Bank of England base rate on all client money
options.D: Interest is only payable if Ravi makes a specific written demand

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 7 requires firms to pay clients a fair sum in lieu of interest or to account for actual interest earned. Whether a payment is required depends on whether it is fair and reasonable in the circumstances — relevant factors include the amount held, the length of time held, and the prevailing interest rates. For £50,000 held for 4 months, it would typically be fair and reasonable to pay interest. The firm should have a written policy on interest (rule 7.1). Failure to pay interest where required can amount to misconduct.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Rachel Okonkwo as COFA of Crane & Pelletier is reviewing the firm's compliance with SRA Accounts Rules 2019. How frequently must client account reconciliations be carried out?

options.A: Daily
options.B: Weekly
options.C: At least every 5 weeks
options.D: Monthly

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 8.3 requires the COFA (Compliance Officer for Finance and Administration) to ensure that client account reconciliations are carried out at least every 5 weeks. The reconciliation compares the firm's client ledger balances with the actual bank statement balances. The 5-week maximum interval ensures prompt identification of discrepancies, shortfalls, or errors. A daily or weekly reconciliation is good practice but the minimum required by the Rules is 5 weeks.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier's client account reconciliation shows that the total client ledger balances are £200,000 but the bank statement shows only £195,000. What is this and what must the firm do?

options.A: A credit balance — the firm can recover the excess from clients' matters
options.B: A client account shortfall — the firm must immediately pay in monies from office account to make good the shortfall and investigate the cause
options.C: A timing difference — the firm should wait for outstanding transactions to clear
options.D: A reconciliation error — the firm should adjust the ledger balances

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: A shortfall arises where the bank balance is less than the total of client ledger balances — the firm does not hold sufficient money to meet all its obligations to clients. Under SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 6.1, a firm must ensure that office money is not used to make payments out of client account — and where a shortfall arises, it must be remedied immediately by topping up the client account from the firm's own office funds. The cause must be investigated. A shortfall may indicate misappropriation, dishonesty, or accounting error — serious regulatory breaches.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier receives a cheque for £5,000 from a client comprising £3,000 fees (billed) and £2,000 on account of anticipated disbursements. How should the cheque be allocated?

options.A: The whole £5,000 must go into client account
options.B: The whole £5,000 must go into office account
options.C: £3,000 goes to office account (billed fees); £2,000 goes to client account (on account of disbursements); or the whole cheque can be banked into client account and £3,000 promptly transferred to office
options.D: The cheque must be split into two separate payments before banking

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 4.1 deals with mixed receipts. A mixed payment contains both office money (the billed fees) and client money (the disbursement on account). The firm can either: (1) split the payment — £3,000 to office, £2,000 to client account; or (2) pay the whole into client account and promptly (within 14 days under old rules, as soon as practicable under 2019 rules) transfer the office element (£3,000) out. The client money element (£2,000) must not be paid directly into office account.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: After completing Hassan Nazari's conveyancing, Crane & Pelletier cannot return £75 remaining in client account to Hassan as he cannot be located. What options does the firm have under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?

options.A: Transfer the £75 to office account immediately as the matter is complete
options.B: Hold the £75 indefinitely in client account until Hassan is found
options.C: After reasonable attempts to return the money and a reasonable time has passed, the firm can apply to the SRA for permission to withdraw the residual balance, or donate it to a charity with the SRA's agreement
options.D: Pay the £75 to the Legal Aid Agency as unclaimed client funds

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 2.5 of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 addresses residual balances. A firm cannot simply transfer unclaimed client money to office account. After making all reasonable attempts to return the money to the client (documented searches, letters, etc.), and after a reasonable period, the firm should contact the SRA for guidance. The SRA may give permission to withdraw the balance or direct it to a charity. The firm must keep records of all steps taken. Simply leaving the money in client account indefinitely or transferring to office are not compliant approaches.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier is considering using a third-party managed account (TPMA) for one client's matter rather than its own client account. What rule governs this and what does the client need to know?

options.A: TPMAs are not permitted under SRA Accounts Rules 2019
options.B: Rule 11 permits the use of a TPMA with the client's prior informed consent; the client must be told they are no longer protected by the firm's client account safeguards
options.C: TPMAs can be used without client consent as they are regulated by the FCA
options.D: Rule 11 permits TPMAs only for sums below £10,000

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 11 specifically deals with third-party managed accounts. A firm may hold client money in a TPMA (an account operated by an authorised third party such as a licensed escrow provider) as an alternative to its own client account. The firm must obtain the client's prior written informed consent, explaining that: (1) the money will not be in the firm's client account; (2) the SARS protections (Compensation Fund backstop, SRA oversight) will not apply in the same way; and (3) the risks involved. No monetary threshold applies.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Rachel Okonkwo as COFA discovers that a fee earner has been using client money to pay office expenses over several months. What must Rachel do?

options.A: Deal with it internally — dismiss the fee earner and make good the client account
options.B: Report the matter to the SRA promptly as it is a material breach of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019
options.C: Report to the police only — the SRA need not be involved unless criminal proceedings result
options.D: Notify the affected clients only and make good the client account

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Under SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 8.7, the COFA must ensure the firm reports to the SRA any material information about the firm's compliance. SRA Code of Conduct (Firms) paragraph 3.9 requires firms to report to the SRA promptly if they have a reason to suspect that the firm or anyone in it is or has been involved in a serious breach of the regulatory requirements. Using client money to pay office expenses is a serious breach (misappropriation) — it must be reported to the SRA promptly. Internal remediation alone is insufficient. The SRA's Compensation Fund may be implicated.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: When must Crane & Pelletier obtain and deliver an accountant's report to the SRA under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?

options.A: Annually — regardless of the level of client money held
options.B: Only if the SRA specifically requests one
options.C: The firm must obtain an accountant's report for each accounting period if it holds or receives client money; it must be delivered to the SRA if qualified (containing a finding)
options.D: The accountant's report is no longer required under the 2019 rules

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Under SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 12, a firm that has, or has had in the accounting period, an obligation to hold client money must obtain an accountant's report for that period. The report is prepared by a qualified accountant (Reporting Accountant) and must be delivered to the SRA only if it is 'qualified' — i.e. it contains a finding of a material breach or departure from the rules. An unqualified report is retained by the firm but need not be delivered to the SRA. This is a significant change from the pre-2019 regime of mandatory annual delivery.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: On completion of a residential property purchase for Victoria Ashworth, Crane & Pelletier transfers £400,000 to the seller's solicitors. Victoria had paid £400,000 into the firm's client account. What are the completion ledger entries?

options.A: DR Victoria Ashworth client ledger (client side) £400,000; CR Cash account (client) £400,000
options.B: DR Cash account (client) £400,000; CR Victoria Ashworth client ledger (client side) £400,000
options.C: DR Victoria Ashworth office ledger £400,000; CR Cash account (office) £400,000
options.D: No entries needed — transfers between clients' accounts are netting transactions

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: At completion, client money is paid out of client account to the seller's solicitors. The accounting entries record: (1) the reduction in the client ledger balance for Victoria (DR Victoria Ashworth client ledger — client side £400,000, reducing what the firm owes Victoria); and (2) the corresponding reduction in the client bank account (CR Cash account (client) £400,000, recording the money leaving the bank). DR records reductions in liability (client owes firm nothing more); CR records reduction in the asset (bank balance decreases). Standard double-entry for a client account payment out.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Gerald Crane is a solicitor and sole principal of Crane & Pelletier. He wishes to make a withdrawal from client account to pay a personal debt. What is the position?

options.A: Permitted — as sole principal he has authority over the firm's accounts
options.B: Not permitted — client money can only be withdrawn to make payments to or on behalf of the client, to pay professional disbursements, or to transfer to office account for earned fees
options.C: Permitted with the agreement of the COFA
options.D: Permitted if the amount is less than £500 and repaid within 7 days

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 5.1 specifies the permitted purposes for which client money may be withdrawn from client account: to pay it to or on behalf of the client; to carry out the client's instructions; to pay disbursements on behalf of the client; or to transfer to office account for earned fees after billing. Withdrawing client money for personal debts is not a permitted purpose — it is misappropriation of client funds, a serious regulatory breach that may constitute dishonesty. Gerald's status as principal provides no authority for this.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier needs to pay a £15 photocopying charge for James Ashworth's matter. The charge is on the firm's list of agreed disbursements to be recovered from the client. Where should this be recorded?

options.A: Paid from client account as a disbursement on James's behalf
options.B: Paid from office account as an office expense — then billed to James on the final invoice
options.C: Paid from petty cash and reimbursed from client account
options.D: Not charged to the client — photocopying is not a recoverable disbursement

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Office expenses (including photocopying, printing, and similar overheads) are incurred by the firm and billed to the client as part of the fee or as a recoverable expense on the bill. They are paid from office account — not client account. Client account is only used for money belonging to the client or held on behalf of the client for specific purposes. The firm incurs the photocopying cost, pays from office funds, and recovers it on the bill. Paying from client account for the firm's own expenses would be using client money for office purposes — a breach of the Rules.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Who Can Operate Client Account — Rule 8.1
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier employs a non-solicitor accounts manager to sign client account cheques. The manager is not regulated by the SRA. Is this permissible?

options.A: No — only solicitors can sign client account cheques
options.B: Yes — any employee can operate client account if authorised by the firm
options.C: Yes — non-solicitor managers can operate client account provided appropriate supervision and oversight are in place, and the firm's principals remain responsible
options.D: No — the COFA must personally authorise every client account transaction

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 do not restrict who may operate client account to qualified solicitors. Rule 8.1 provides that the authorised body (the firm) must ensure its client account is operated properly. Non-solicitor staff can operate client accounts under appropriate supervision. The firm's managers and the COFA remain responsible for compliance. Adequate supervision, clear authorisation protocols, and oversight are key. The firm's principals and the COFA have personal regulatory responsibility even when day-to-day operations are delegated.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Money Laundering — Interaction with Accounts Rules
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier receives £100,000 cash from a new client for a property purchase. The source of funds cannot be verified. What should the firm do?

options.A: Accept the funds into client account — the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 does not prohibit cash receipts
options.B: Refuse to accept the funds and report a suspicious activity report (SAR) to the National Crime Agency if there is suspicion of money laundering
options.C: Accept the funds but limit any transaction to £10,000
options.D: Request a signed declaration from the client about the source of funds before accepting

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 and Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds Regulations 2017 impose obligations on law firms. Large cash receipts (particularly in property transactions) are a high-risk indicator of money laundering. If the firm suspects the money may represent the proceeds of crime, it must not proceed (tipping off prohibition) and must submit a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) to the National Crime Agency. Accepting the funds could constitute money laundering under POCA 2002 ss.327-329. The SRA Accounts Rules cannot override the criminal law obligations.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Bill of Costs — Delivery and Entries
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier delivers a final bill to Pat Hollowell for £4,000 plus VAT (£800). What are the correct ledger entries on delivery of the bill?

options.A: DR Pat Hollowell office ledger £4,800; CR Office costs £4,000; CR VAT account £800
options.B: DR Cash account (office) £4,800; CR Pat Hollowell office ledger £4,800
options.C: DR Pat Hollowell client ledger £4,800; CR Client account £4,800
options.D: No entries until the bill is paid

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: On delivery of the bill (before payment), the firm records the debt owed by the client. The correct double-entry is: DR Pat Hollowell office ledger £4,800 (the debtor account — Pat now owes the firm money); CR Office costs (income) £4,000 (recognising the fee income); CR VAT account £800 (recognising the VAT liability to HMRC). When Pat pays, the entry is: DR Cash account (office) £4,800; CR Pat Hollowell office ledger £4,800 (settling the debt). The bill creates a debt — entries are in the office ledger, not client account.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier receives a £10,000 payment on account from Victoria Ashworth at the start of a matter. The firm later terminates the retainer having done £6,000 of work (billed). What happens to the remaining £4,000?

options.A: The firm retains the full £10,000 as reasonable costs
options.B: The £4,000 unearned balance must be returned to Victoria from client account
options.C: The £4,000 can be retained in client account indefinitely
options.D: The firm can apply £4,000 to any other matter for Victoria

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: A payment on account is client money held on trust for the client. Once billed and earned, £6,000 can be transferred to office account under rule 4.3. The remaining £4,000 remains client money and must be returned to Victoria. The firm cannot retain unearned client money after termination of the retainer. This is client money held for a purpose that no longer exists — it must be returned promptly under rule 1.2 (client money belongs to the client). Retention would be misappropriation.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Holding Money as Stakeholder vs. Agent
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier holds a deposit of £50,000 as stakeholder in a property transaction for Ashworth Developments Ltd (buyer) and the seller. The transaction falls through. What is the stakeholder's position?

options.A: The stakeholder returns the deposit to the buyer as it is their money
options.B: The stakeholder pays the deposit to the seller as it is earnest money
options.C: As stakeholder, the firm holds the deposit for whoever becomes entitled under the contract; it cannot release the deposit without agreement from both parties or a court order
options.D: The stakeholder is free to return the deposit to whichever party it considers most deserving

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: A stakeholder holds money on behalf of both parties to a transaction — they are neutral custodians. Unlike an agent (who holds for the principal alone), a stakeholder cannot release the deposit without either (1) agreement from both parties; or (2) a court order determining entitlement. If the transaction falls through, there will often be a contractual dispute about whether the buyer or seller is entitled to the deposit (e.g. whether there has been a breach by buyer or seller). The stakeholder must not take sides. Releasing to one party without agreement or court order would be a breach of duty.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Gerald Crane is a trustee of the Hollowell Family Trust and uses Crane & Pelletier to administer the trust. The trust holds funds in a bank account. Do the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 apply to these trust funds?

options.A: No — trust funds are governed by trust law, not the SRA Accounts Rules
options.B: Yes — money held by a solicitor in their capacity as trustee in a controlled trust is subject to the SRA Accounts Rules 2019
options.C: No — only money received in the course of legal practice is regulated
options.D: Yes — but only if the trust funds are commingled with client account money

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, a 'controlled trust' is a trust of which the solicitor (alone or with others) is a trustee and has control or co-control of the trust money. Money held by a solicitor as trustee in a controlled trust is treated as client money for the purposes of the Rules. It must therefore be held in the client account (or a designated account) and the full protections of the Rules apply. This ensures trust money held by solicitor-trustees has the same safeguards as other client money.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Rachel Okonkwo submits a SAR to the NCA regarding suspicious funds held for James Ashworth. Before consent is received from the NCA, James calls demanding the funds be released. What must Rachel do?

options.A: Release the funds — the client has an absolute right to their money
options.B: Obtain a defence against money laundering (DAML) consent from the NCA before releasing the funds; do not tip off James that a SAR has been submitted
options.C: Tell James a SAR has been submitted and await his response
options.D: Release the funds but note the suspicious activity on file

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Where a solicitor submits a SAR, POCA 2002 s.335 requires the solicitor to obtain consent from the NCA (or wait for the 7-day 'moratorium period' plus any further extension) before proceeding with the transaction. Proceeding without consent could constitute money laundering. Critically, POCA 2002 s.333A prohibits 'tipping off' — telling James that a SAR has been submitted (or that the NCA has been notified) could alert him to the investigation, which is a criminal offence. Rachel must give an innocent explanation for any delay (e.g. internal compliance procedures) without disclosing the SAR.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier receives a bank transfer of £15,000 from a third party (not the client) for a personal injury settlement on behalf of Diana Pelletier. What is the correct first entry?

options.A: DR Cash account (office) £15,000; CR Office income £15,000
options.B: DR Cash account (client) £15,000; CR Diana Pelletier client ledger (client side) £15,000
options.C: DR Diana Pelletier client ledger (client side) £15,000; CR Cash account (client) £15,000
options.D: DR Cash account (client) £15,000; CR Third party account £15,000

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Settlement money received from a third party for a client's benefit is client money — it belongs to Diana, not the firm. The correct entries record receipt into client account: DR Cash account (client) £15,000 (the firm's client bank balance increases) and CR Diana Pelletier client ledger (client side) £15,000 (recording that the firm now owes Diana £15,000 from client account). The DR/CR convention in legal accounts records the cash account as an asset (DR = increase); the client ledger as a liability (CR = increase in what the firm owes).

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier bills Donna Pearce £10,000 for a straightforward will that took 2 hours to draft. The partner in charge knows the fee is significantly above the market rate. What is the principal professional conduct concern?

options.A: Overcharging is permitted where the client has agreed the fee in advance
options.B: The firm must ensure its costs are fair and reasonable; a clearly excessive fee may breach the SRA Code of Conduct requirement to act in the client's best interests and to behave with integrity
options.C: Overcharging is a matter for the client to challenge through the Legal Ombudsman only — it does not engage professional conduct
options.D: The fee is valid provided a bill is delivered and a costs information letter was sent

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Code of Conduct (Solicitors) paragraph 1.2 requires solicitors to act in a way that upholds public trust and confidence in the profession. Paragraph 7.1 requires fair and transparent information about costs. Charging a manifestly excessive fee for simple work raises conduct concerns about: failing to act in the client's best interests (para 1.4); lack of integrity (para 1.2); and potentially taking unfair advantage of the client. The client can challenge through the Legal Ombudsman and can apply for a detailed assessment of the bill. The solicitor may face regulatory investigation.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Rachel Okonkwo is appointed as COFA of Crane & Pelletier. What are her primary responsibilities under the SRA's regulatory framework?

options.A: To personally sign off every client account transaction
options.B: To ensure the firm's compliance with the SRA Accounts Rules 2019; to record and report to the SRA any material breaches; and to take appropriate action to remedy compliance failures
options.C: To maintain the firm's client account in her personal name
options.D: To conduct the annual accountant's report personally

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The COFA (Compliance Officer for Finance and Administration) is appointed under SRA Authorisation of Firms Rules rule 9. The COFA must: (1) ensure the firm's compliance with the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 and any conditions attached to the firm's authorisation; (2) record all failures to comply with the Rules; and (3) report promptly to the SRA any material breaches. The COFA need not personally sign every transaction — the role is oversight and compliance. The COFA is personally responsible for these obligations and can face regulatory sanction if they fail to discharge them.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier is a litigation firm that never holds client money — all costs are paid in advance and all damages are paid directly to clients by third parties. Must the firm maintain a client account?

options.A: Yes — all SRA-regulated firms must maintain a client account
options.B: No — a firm that never holds client money is not required to maintain a client account, though it must have a written policy and procedures in place
options.C: No — firms can opt out of the SRA Accounts Rules entirely if they wish
options.D: Yes — the firm may receive client money unexpectedly and must be prepared

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The SRA Accounts Rules 2019 apply to firms that hold or receive client money. A firm that has a client money avoidance policy (confirmed by rule 2.2 exceptions and specific practice management) and genuinely never holds client money does not need to maintain a client account. The firm must have clear procedures and a policy confirming this approach, and must be able to demonstrate compliance. If the firm unexpectedly receives client money, it must bank it promptly in a client account (which may need to be opened). This position is specifically acknowledged in the SRA's guidance to the 2019 Rules.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Gerald Crane takes on a new client matter for Alek Kowalski but gives no information about costs at the outset, then delivers a bill for £8,000 without prior warning. Which SRA obligation has Gerald breached?

options.A: None — costs information is only required for publicly funded work
options.B: SRA Code of Conduct paragraph 8.6 — solicitors must give clients the best possible information about costs at the outset and as the matter progresses
options.C: The Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012
options.D: The Consumer Rights Act 2015 — costs must be agreed in advance as a term

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: SRA Code of Conduct for Solicitors (2019) paragraph 8.6 requires that solicitors give clients the best possible information about likely overall costs (and how they are calculated) at the outset and updates as the matter progresses. This is reinforced by paragraph 7.1 (fair and transparent information) and the Transparency Rules. Failing to give any costs information before delivering an unexpectedly large bill breaches these obligations, may entitle the client to challenge the bill (detailed assessment), and can result in a complaint to the Legal Ombudsman and SRA investigation.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Crane & Pelletier acts for Ravi Sharma purchasing a flat. On the day of exchange, the firm receives £25,000 deposit from Ravi and simultaneously pays a 10% deposit of £35,000 to the seller's solicitors (the firm had bridged the gap from office funds). What is the correct accounting sequence?

options.A: DR Cash (client) £25,000; CR Ravi client ledger £25,000; then DR Ravi client ledger £35,000; CR Cash (client) £35,000; and separately record the office bridging
options.B: DR Cash (office) £25,000; CR Ravi office ledger £25,000; pay deposit from client account
options.C: Receive the £25,000 into office account; pay the £35,000 from client account; reconcile on completion
options.D: No accounting entries until completion

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Ravi's £25,000 is client money — it must go into client account: DR Cash (client) £25,000; CR Ravi client ledger £25,000. The £35,000 deposit paid to the seller's solicitors is also client money being paid out: DR Ravi client ledger £35,000; CR Cash (client) £35,000 (noting the client account is overdrawn for Ravi's matter by £10,000). The £10,000 shortfall bridged from office funds must be recorded separately (DR Ravi client ledger £10,000 — from office; CR Cash (office) £10,000) and recovered on completion. Using office money to fund a client account shortfall must be temporary and recorded.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
